titre: Top 10 Most BRAINDEAD Champions to CLIMB FAST in SEASON 16
transcription: If you want the most amount of LP possible for the least amount of work, then this video is going to be for you, as these are the 10 easiest champions to climb with in season 16. Now, to make it on this list, there's only two requirements that we have. The champions have to be ranked S tier or above and have a very low barrier to entry, making them the perfect picks for anybody to just pick up and start climbing on straight away. Because at SkillCap, we believe league shouldn't be as complicated as literally everybody makes it out to be. Climbing isn't about learning crazy strategies or the theoretical macro nonsense where you're expected to just pause the game, analyze 20 different factors, and then make the perfect decision after that. It's ridiculous. Now, although stuff like this, it does sound great in theory, let's be honest about it. None of it actually ever works in your games, does it? That's because the ELO you're playing in is a completely different game, and we get that. Which is why we focus on teaching you practical fundamentals for every role that's not just easy to apply, but are proven to get you climbing and get you climbing fast. as we do what nobody else does. Take players just like you. Find out what's holding you back from climbing and build our content from the ground up around what actually gets results for the average player. And we're so confident that this works. We back it all up with a rankup guarantee, which means climb at least five divisions while you are actively using our service or we'll give you every penny back, plus a 30-day refund change of mind policy on top of that. There's literally zero risk to doing this today. So, access your exclusive discount code using the link in the description or pin comment below and start your climb today. So, starting off, we have our first top lane pick. A champion that was already amazing last season, but has somehow only gotten stronger with the top lane roll quest, that of course is Dr. Mundo. If you like being so insanely tanky that players basically tickle you with their damage while also being able to completely one-shot them at the same time, yeah, that's not balanced. The main weakness of Mundo has always been his early lane phase. But in season 16 with the new changes to home guards, being forced to take a bad recall is so much less punishing than it used to be, as you can just get back to lane so incredibly fast that you barely lose anything nowadays. Not to mention, even in his more difficult matchups, you can just look to sit back, farm with his Q, and focus on surviving. And even then, if you hit a few cleaver on your opponent, you're going to often end up out sustaining and even out poking him anyway. When Dr. Mundo truly comes online, though, is after you pick up a few health items. Basically, your goal is to get warmogs as fast as possible, but to do this, you need the health requirement. So what the top Mundo players are doing is immediately rushing three giants belts and then build into warmogs at which point you become completely unstoppable regardless of your matchup as you can just take any trade any poke and just heal up from your warmogs at which point you want to take over the lane and push for turret plates which Mundo takes extremely well especially with the new demolish as without a doubt he is one of the best split pushing and sidelane tanks in the entire game which again is a strategy that only got stronger this season and even when you grew True. Mundo is still one of the best tanks in the game right now. Sure, you might not have any hard CC, but between your insane tankiness and surprisingly high damage with him, you're a nightmare for teams to deal with. Now, for some quick tips. Although Mundo's kit is pretty straightforward, there's a lot of players who misuse his W. Now, how this works is that there's two parts to his W. When you first use it, for the first 0.75 seconds, he stores up to 95% of the damage dealt as this gray health that you see right here. Then the second part is you can either let it run out and heal for 50% of that or if you reactivate it while near a champion, you're going to heal for 100%, not that 50%. Which means you ideally want to press it when you know you're going to take a big chunk of burst and then reactivate it while they're still in range. Next is that once you reach level 16, your ultimate gains a new effect, increasing the initial healing and healing over time based on how many champions are nearby. And lastly, your E is not only an auto attack reset, but also a great tool to both harass and push waves with in lane. As if used to last hit to a minion will swat it forward, dealing damage to everything that it passes through. Now, for runes, pick up grasp, demolish, and then either second wind versus range or bone plating versus melee and overgrowth with inspiration as your secondary for free boots so you can reach your warmogs faster and biscuits to just help with the early lane phase. Now, when it comes to items, rush those three giants belts into Warmmog's armor first, then build into heart steel. From there, you're going to have some options. Titanic Hydra for more damage, spirit vis, or dead man's versus AD. Rounding out your build with items like overlords, thorn mail, randuons, or even jack show. As for our next top lane pick, we have a champion I'm willing to bet not a single person expected here. So, a little bit of a surprise for you. Singed. Now, before you go start flaming me in the comments here about how Singed isn't easy, as you need to know all about proxying and mind games where you flash on the spot, flip somebody over a wall and teleport to a ward. No, you don't need to do either of these. Last season, Singed got one of the biggest buffs imaginable that has not only made him far easier to pick up, but when combined with the new season 16 items has made him the undisputed highest win rate top laner across practically every single ELO bracket. Now, what changed here is that now Singed Q essentially executes low health minions, making it so you always get the last hit as long as they're poisoned. This previously was not the case, [music] and in large part why proxying on Singed became more or less mandatory, as last hitting was just so incredibly abysmal, but when played like a normal laner, Singed is deceptively strong right now. as not only is his wave clear insane, it's really difficult for most top laners to interact with him or even do anything about it as you need to either match his wave clear while not getting hit by his poison or look for an all-in at which point Singed well just kind of does singed things and just flips you away, you know. Then after that, once you get to the mid late game, he just transitions into a great high damage, very annoying AP tank. And you can just go to a side lane, push out a couple of waves, and then just group up with your team or even look for a flank and just run around everybody, poisoning them all and slowing them with your riy eyes, creating a ton ton ton of pressure, all while being practically immortal thanks to your ultimate combined with protoplasm harness. This item, it's absolutely insane on Singed right now as the healing it provides scales off your resistances, which Singed gets him bulk from his ultimate. Finishing off the build with either Force of Nature versus AP, Dead Man's versus AD, Randuins versus Crit, or Rift Maker for some extra damage. So, everybody already knows how easy and strong Nasis is as a champion. despite being completely useless early on. You just farm up with Q, but then mid to late game comes around and you're suddenly a complete monster. Only why you'd never see him in a video like this is that he's actually surprisingly difficult to play as. Yeah, you just end up getting zoned and falling massively behind if you don't know what you're doing. However, players are finally starting to realize they've been playing him in the wrong lane this entire time. Jungle Nasses has been taking over and completely removes his biggest weaknesses, letting him scale practically for free as now you get up to 80 stacks from a single clear of your jungle. And with the addition of failites, it's not only harder to invade you, but much harder for junglers to gank in general. Nasis has some of the strongest ganks in the game thanks to his W. And as we know, the longer the game goes on, the stronger you get, basically putting your opponents on a timer. Now, for runes, grab this fleet footwork page with inspiration as your secondary for free boots and approach velocity, which helps massively for getting onto targets. Item wise, you can either rush Trinity Force if you want some damage with Protoplasm Harness second, which is yet another reason this pick is so strong due to the heal scaling off the resistance Nasis gains from his ultimate, or instead of that, you could just opt for a more tanky route with Protoplasm first into Iceborn Gauntlet. Rounding out your build with Jack Show third, and then either Spirit Vis, Randons, or Frozen Heart, depending on what you need. But if you don't want to do that and you're looking to have more impact in the early game, our next pick is a champion every jungle main looking to climb should be abusing right now. and that is Zen Sha and is living proof that sometimes the simplest kits do end up being the strongest. Zen's early game has always been basically unmatched. 1v ones or small skirmishes, his consistent damage and healing make him absolutely insane. But even then, he's one of the strongest and easiest to execute early game gankers out there as you can just walk into a lane, dive in with your E, or hit W first to extend the range, then follow up with a Q auto reset for a quick burst of damage and knock up, making him fantastic at securing leads and pressuring the map, especially when combined alongside the jungle tech Zen mains have been abusing, consisting of starting raptors, doing red into kugs, and then basing for a long sword before heading to wolves, which enables him to get to scuttle on time with a long sword advantage, giving you insanely high potential to snowball right from the start of the game. And if you're interested in learning that clear for Zen as well as many other champions, be sure to check out our newly updated jungle clear course where you can learn the fastest possible world record jungle clears for every meta champion, including helpful subtitles with everything you could ever need to shave up to 10 seconds off your next clear. But Zen's biggest weakness has always been falling off late game. However, in patch 26.03, 3. This definitely was addressed and Zen gains some huge buffs to both the scaling on his passive and E attack speed, making his late game far stronger now and enabling Zen to scale surprisingly well into the mid and late game, dealing a ton of damage and with his ultimate still remaining incredibly hard to kill. Now, for runes, we suggest this conquer rune page with free boots and cosmic insight as your secondaries. For items, start red smite and rush sundered sky as your first item. In most games, you can go cleaver second. But if the game's going to be a little bit slower, you could instead go for Titanic Hydra second to improve your clear speed and make you a little more tankier. Either way, you're going to want Cleaver third. From there, round out your build with typical bruiser items like Death's Dance, Guardians Angel, Sterox, or even Endless Hunger. All right, moving over to mid lane. Now, our first suggestion is going to be Malahar. Now, if you're somebody who struggles with wave control, trading, or maybe your mechanics just aren't really what they used to be, Malahar is going to be the perfect champion for climbing this particular season. And what makes him so effective is his ability to just completely shut down his lane opponent, no matter what champion they are. All of this while still just chilling horizontal straight in your chair like a lazy bum. as once you get through the first few levels, all you need to do is walk up to a wave, press E and W, and you are done and free to do whatever the heck you want to do. Be that base, look for a roam, whatever it is, while your opponent is just perpetually stuck in lane, spending the whole game answering the minion waves you're constantly pushing. This not only completely nullifies every lane opponent, keeping you incredibly safe, but gives you all of the priority and pressure on the map to move around and help with whatever shenanigans your solo Q teammates are trying to pull off. And of course, once you hit level six, you can outplay everybody by just pressing R, making your Romes gank setup absolutely amazing. Now, what's crazy is that even as the game progresses, Malahar remains super strong as you can continue to just quickly farm waves and build massive CS leads over your opponent. And once you have riy eyes, your team fighting is insane as you can spread ease and just slow the entire enemy team while they slowly die to your damage over time while always having the ability to just press R on anybody trying to dive your backline. Now for runes, comet does slightly outperform airy as your keystone with manaflow, transcendence, and gathering storm, taking free boots and triple tonic as your secondary. Now, as for your build, rush Blackfire Torch into Leandre and then Riyze, rounding out your build with Death Cap and Zas. And speaking of outplaying your opponents with your R. Nobody does this quite as well as our second midlane pick, Annie. Annie has always been the champion you think about first when you talk about easytoplay mid laners, but for the past few patches, she's basically been non-existent in the meta. It's kind of crazy. However, after her recent buffs from last patch, she's back and back with a vengeance. Riot massively increased her Q damage and reduced her E cooldown. And this is a huge deal because E has always been the ability you maxed last. Now Annie's E sits at a flat 10-second cooldown at all ranks, which means you're now able to get massive value from it. Whether you're using it for the movement speed to get back onto the map, get in range, or to generate an extra passive [music] stack for a stun, Annie can now do it all way more frequently. Now, although Annie's lane phase [music] is typically seen as pretty weak, this isn't actually true, as she has the second highest base auto attack range in the entire League [music] roster, making her extremely strong in lane if you're constantly looking to poke your opponent with Q's and autos. But undoubtedly, Annie's biggest strength does come in her ability to set up ganks, looking [music] for big engages and burst down squishy targets with her ultimate. and she thrives at roaming around the map and helping her team in skirmishes, which should be your number one goal on Annie. You want to be as active on the map as possible. Now, as for the rune page, don't be baited into going into electrocute. And triple tonic is [music] great as not only does Annie gain huge value from it at level six, but as she's incredibly reliant on both Q and W, the elixir of skill helps her get an extra point in W earlier into the game. As for the build, grab [music] malignance as your first item to get the lower cool down on your ultimate. Then work towards storm surge. This two item core is going to basically give you all the damage you need to one-shot carries. At which point, we're going to want to transition into items that help keep your damage relevant outside of just dealing burst. And this is [music] why you're going to want Riyize and Leandre, both of which get applied by your tippers, giving you perma damage over time and an annoying slow to help clean up fights, all while finishing up your build with death cap. All right, moving over to ADC. Now, our first champion is one that, man, I'm going to be honest, we [music] really try to avoid putting in these lists because she just ends [laughter] up being in every single one of them. But honestly, not putting her here, it'd be a huge complete injustice to the game. [music] We're of course talking about Misfortune for any bot laner looking for an easy ADC to pick up. Nobody comes even remotely close to Misfortune. She's one of the few ADCs that require very minimal mechanics to pull off and pull off well. And her damage is absolutely crazy. as with just an auto into a Q, you can completely chunk, if not one-shot, most squishy targets. Now, as for lane, last hitting is super simple with her passive. W is going to make positioning, avoiding damage, and just generally getting around the map back to lane effortless. And her Q makes her trading pattern super strong and easy to understand. Then, once you get into team fighting, [music] as the bulk of your damage comes from your R, all you need to do is focus on positioning and finding good angles. Plus, with the season 16 roll quest and the combination of cashback and triple tonic, she's able to hit her item spike so much sooner compared to other champions, meaning you end up always ahead of the curve, keeping your damage ridiculously [music] high at all points of the game. Now, for the build, we highly suggest going lethality [music] in the majority of games with a ghost blade rush into collector and then i.e. and Lord Dominic's rounding out with rapid fire [music] cannon and GA. Also, remember you don't need Berserker Greaves, so pick up either swiftness boots, steel caps, or merks instead. [music] coupled with this first strike rune page with mana flow band and gathering storm as your secondaries. Now our second pick is more of a [music] conventional auto attackbased marksman, but arguably the easiest of them all, Jinx. And I get it. I know what you're thinking. Jinx does sound difficult on paper due to being an immobile marksman. Her stats and low elo though they really don't lie. And she's overall just one of the most dominant, easy to pick up and consistent marksmen out there patch after patch after patch. What makes Jinx easier than most other marksmen is that although she's a hyper scaler, she lacks the weaker lane phase [music] that most scaling champions really have to deal with. As with her fishbones, you're able to gain the push in basically every lane. [music] Then with push, you can just swap to minigun and take advantage of the free attack speed, weaving in fishbones autos on your opponent for some free poke. Then in the late game, even as an immobile carry, Jinx is much safer than most, as not only does her E do a great job at self-deeing, but just her pure range and rocket form allows you to stay safe for most of the fight, at least until you get that first reset and completely take over the fight. But to make sure you do that, here's the build that you're going to have to follow. After the buff to hexoptics, [music] it has a massive 54% win rate compared to the 51% on either Yall or Kraken. and buying Phantom Dancer into IE right after has over a 60% win rate as a three item core. And from here, you're then going to want to pick up Lord Dom's or Mortal Reminder. And then opt [music] for items like GA, Blood Thirstster, or Mercurial Scimitar to round out your build. Then for runes, Lethal Tempo with Presence of Mind, Bloodline, and Cutdown, opting for the ADC staple of Triple Tonic, and Cashback as your secondary to compound the extra gold from your quest. Last up, let's cover our recommended support picks for this patch. In season 16, enchanters are stronger than ever. And our first suggestion and arguably the strongest enchanter of all is Nami. Nami is a champion that does have an incredibly high skill ceiling, yet also has one of the lowest and most effective skill floors in the entire game, making putting in the work to play her well worth it. And what makes Nami so incredibly strong and easy to play right now is how insanely versatile she is. You basically fit every single team composition and it's a great blind pick as she brings so much to every team she's in. You've got great engage and disengage with your ult and movement speed buffs, amazing sustain or poke in lane through your W, damage amp and catch potential with your E and Q, and overall just solid peels. You can play with AD or AP champions. Basically, Nami does everything you want from a support and she does it all really, really well. And despite being an enchanter, her damage and playmaking potential is extremely good still. During lane, you should definitely be looking to play fairly aggressively. Ideally, your ADC is going to move up for trades, and you use your E for extra damage while you follow up with a W to heal them, which will then bounce onto the enemy for even more poke. But let's be honest with that, that's probably never going to happen. So, you instead, every time you have mana flow band ready, E yourself, walk up, auto, and then W and use the movement speed to back [music] off, and you're going to come out ahead in the trade. Truthfully though, the hardest part about learning Nami is going to be landing your Q. But the biggest tip we can give you on landing that Q is to ideally always look to slow the target with your E first before attempting to land it. Otherwise, use it as a follow-up off other CC, like your own ultimate, but even then, it's always easier to land your Q to peel rather than to set up. For your support item, Dream Maker is the go-to in most games, but you could definitely opt for Solstice Slay in the more difficult matchups. then work towards Imperial Mandate as your only must-have item due to its synergy with your E. And from here, build Echoes of Helia and Moonstone, rounding out your build with Dawncore. Now, for a top tip here, as Nami does have amazing AP scalings, it's also going to be great to buy an early Dark Seal and look to upgrade it to Magis if you have stacks. As for runes, grab this airy page and then pick up magical footwear and jack of all trades as your secondaries. This just allows you to hit your item spikes a little bit faster while also getting some free ability haste and AP. However, if you're somebody who doesn't enjoy enchanters and you want a little more playmaking, a little more roam potential, yet you struggle to find success on some of the more difficult champions like Thrash or maybe even Pike, you have to be playing Maokai right now. [music] Almost every engage support in the League roster is reliant on hooks, skill shots, or well-timed engages to be impactful right now. But not Maokai. He is completely brain dead with his entire kit being point-and-click. Just land your W onto the enemy ADC, walk through them, and use your Q knockock to knock them back into your ADC, making you ridiculously hard to deal with in lane if you have an aggressive ADC. As all it takes for you to get a kill is for you to get in range of your W. Then transitioning into the mid and late game, Maokai does continue to be one of the best engage supports in the entire game. As by using your ultimate from off angles, you can set up fights or look for picks or even use it for a tool to disengage if you have to. Plus, with his saplings, getting and controlling vision is effortless, as you can just throw a sapling in every bush before you move up to ward, keeping you safe throughout the game. For items, Solstice Slay is going to be a no-brainer here. Then, for your first item, grab Trailblazer, which helps you get in range for your W, as well as helping your team follow up your engage. After that, grab Bandal Pipes as Maokai is one of the best users of the item due to his abundance of CC in his kit, at which point you can either go lock it by default or grab Knights Vow to keep a single fed carry alive. then rounding out your build with again either Locket or Knight's Vow or personal survivability items like Protoplasm. Now, as for runes, Aftershock is the go-to with Font of Life and then either Bone Plating versus Melee or Second Wind versus Range Supports and Overgrowth. Then, as Maokai is so reliant on Flash to engage, grab Cosmic Insight in the Inspiration tree, coupled with Biscuit Delivery for a little more sustain. And there you have it, the 10 simplest and easiest champions to climb with in season 16. If you're looking for more information on how to play any of these champions or builds featured in this guide, be sure to check out our Smurf commentaries page, where our challenger coaches walk you through every stage of the game step by step. And that's on top of our full library of expertly curated courses, all updated for season 16, which are designed for players of all skill levels, whether you're a beginner or a seasoned veteran, giving you everything you need to climb this season, which you can gain access to all of it right now by using the exclusive discount link in the pin comment or description below. Thank you all so much for watching. Good luck with your ranked climbs, and we'll see you in the next one.

titre: The Five WORST Champion Designs Ever Made | League Of Legends
transcription: Hey everyone, welcome back to another League of Legends video. I've been making content for this game for over half a decade now, and in that time I talked extensively about champion design, from good champion design to bad champion design and everything else in between. One that I don't think I ever went over though, is a topic on the absolute worst main champions ever in the history of League. The two that I made so far focused entirely on what was in the game at the time, and while spoiler alert, I do plan to include one of those champions today, if we were to compare them against every single champion now and since time immemorial, relatively speaking, I think we have it a lot better these days. >> [music] >> League is a very old game with an over 15-year tenure of live service. It's been a part of the growing video game industry through every stage of its evolution. In fact, titles like League are a good way to see just how far video game expectations have become, where stuff that used to be permissible way back in the day would be an affront to humanity now. But just how offensive were they though? We're going to find out today. I'm going to be going through five of the absolute worst designed champions of all time, taking not just the current champion pool, but their past iterations as well. While this video won't be ordered, if I had to decide on who deserves the number one spot, I don't think many people would disagree with my choice being old Poppy. A small fragment of players however have a bit of nostalgia for old Poppy due to her humorously polarizing gameplay, and satirically they might be inclined to call me dumb for saying that, but I would hope that the majority of us have common sense, [music] and I think we can all agree she would be a contender for bottom five champion designs without question. For that matter, Poppy's old design was so irredeemable that if you were to compare the two versions of her, they may as well be entirely different champions. Old Poppy was the same in terms of class and goal as current Poppy. She [music] too was a tanky fighter that typically ran one or two bruiser items before going full tank. In game, her primary goal was to single out a member of the enemy team, and try to beat them down as well. The way she went about it however, couldn't be any more divergent from the current Poppy. Current Poppy is one of the most skill expressive tanks in the game, being noticeably harder to master than your traditional Malakai, Malfi, Leona, and such. Old Poppy may have been one of the simplest champions in the game. She was also one of the most degenerate. There's no other way to put it. This girl was a stat checker to end all stat checkers. Valiant Fighter caused any instance of damage over 10% of her current health to happen strength for the remaining access. A singular function, but a powerful one. It basically made Poppy impossible to one shot because if, for example, she had 1,000 HP and you did 500 damage to her, that attack would only do 300 because it would do 100 damage and then the remaining 400 would be cut in half to do only 200. Keep in mind, this was current health. So, say Poppy was at 300 health and you did 500 damage to her, she would only take 265 damage because it would be 30 and then the extra 470 would cut down to 235. In the mid to late game, you'd find Poppy at low HP somehow not dying thanks to her passive. It reduced more [music] as time went on by being a percentage and then became more and more effective the lower HP she was in light of it measuring based on her current health. If you wanted to kill her, you had to consistently DPS her, not try to burst her in a single round. Just one problem. Paragon of Demacia gave her bonus AD and armor anytime she dealt or took damage, giving her a line of defense against any sustained physical damage, or in other words, AD carries. So, she had protection against burst damage and consistent DPS. [music] None of those, however, were as bad as her ultimate, diplomatic immunity. How it worked is that she would apply a debuff to an enemy champion that caused her to deal bonus damage against that champion for a period of time. Additionally, Poppy would become immune to all effects not originating from that champion for the duration. She would not only ignore all damage dealt to her during that time, but any effects whatsoever, including crowd control and things of that nature. >> [music] >> The intent behind her ultimate was to put her into a one V one scenario where it was just her and her opponent. Sure, the deck was stacked against her opponent on account of Poppy doing more damage against them, but at least it would be an honest one V one fight, right? That would be the case if Poppy likewise couldn't do anything to anyone except the person [music] she was targeting. Unfortunately, life is never fair. Well, what ends up happening is that Poppy would often mark someone else on the enemy team before charging after her intended victim. Said victim would then proceed to get ass six ways to Sunday by a 500 move speed 380 invincible crowd control and debuff immune Poppy. I'm being serious. You legit couldn't touch her, making your only option to run away. This champion had the most toxic experience of all time, not just on playing against her, but playing as her. Poppy was universally regarded as one of the worst champions in the early game due to having no wave clear, no sustain, no neutral game, absolutely nothing. She was practically guaranteed to lose lane, but if you were patient and lucky enough come late game, you'd be staring down one of the strongest champions of all time. [music] Poppy would be damn near unkillable one V ones courtesy of her passive and W and her ultimate would amplify her DPS against you, making her impossible to duel. And in team fights, the ADC or mid laner couldn't [music] do anything except pick a god and pray that they can run away from her for 3 seconds without dying. It was just an awful experience for both teams cuz on your team, you had a dead weight top laner and on the enemy team, you had a ticking time bomb that would ruin your life if you didn't finish your game quickly enough. People about current Poppy for being too oppressive or tanky while doing so much damage, but trust me, you do not want her past iteration. Old Poppy was traumatizing in more ways than one. Now, speaking of degeneracy, we have another champion whose past iteration is nothing like what he is now and in a good way, Sion. Although old Sion was degenerate for an altogether different reason than Poppy. To her credit, each of old Poppy's abilities made sense in the context of each other. [music] It's what they led to that ruined the experience for everyone around her. Sion, by comparison, was a lot more mellow in that he didn't force you into a damned if you do, damned if you don't situation. Rather, his kit just didn't make sense at all. It was as if they had a jar full of random individual abilities they stuck their hand in five times and out came Sion. The only way to explain would be to go through each ability one at a time. First, passive, Feel No Pain. Sion had a 40% chance to reduce auto attack damage taken by a flat amount. Now, to be fair, in his defense, that's how passive abilities were back in the day. Usually some kind of stat-related effects as opposed to entire playstyle [music] forming things we have now. Even among his peers though, Feel No Pain was a really lossy ability that didn't scale whatsoever. It was by all accounts an infinitely worse old Poppy passive, and it didn't really have any correlation with his active skills. Cryptic Gaze was his Q, a point-and-click range attack that did magic damage and stunned enemies. In a vacuum, this is one of the most powerful abilities of all time. A point-and-click 300 base plus 90% AP 550 range ability with a guaranteed 1.5 second stun on [music] it. It's an Annie Q that always stuns. Broken. Okay, let's see what else he has. Functionally, Death's Grasp is Soul Furnace, a shield that explodes if he manages to hold. Only difference being [music] that it doesn't have the health stacking passive nor health ratios. From his Q and W, you might infer that Sion's a weird AP major bruiser of some sort, right? Well, no. Because his E and ultimate are physical. Enrage is a toggle ability that gives Sion bonus AD in exchange for paying a health cost on basic attacks. However, anything he kills while this toggle is active permanently grants bonus health. There's the Soul Furnace passive, it's just not his E. Okay, so now he's supposed to be an auto attacker. Driving that point home was Cannibalism, a long-lasting steroid ultimate that gave him bonus attack speed and an insane amount of lifesteal while causing his attacks to heal nearby teammates. On one hand, [music] his Q and W would convince you to build ability power, while on the other, his E and ultimate wanted you to build AD. >> [music] >> That aside though, when looking at all of Sion's abilities together, other than the lifesteal helping to refund his E cost, nothing in his kit makes any sense with each other beyond their individual merit. [music] I wasn't kidding when I said it's like if you stuck your hand in a jar of random abilities and put them together. It's not that Sion was a toxic champion to deal with, he was just a really thoughtless champion. All he could do was stun you and then walk up and hack you to pieces. He wasn't a tank, nor was he a particularly reliable juggernaut. You might be able to find a few clips here and there of him humorously life stealing Fallen Laser because of his ultimate, but otherwise this guy was a mess. Mind you, any one of his abilities wouldn't be half bad to have in the modern day. [music] I can imagine how broken cannibalism would be on an AD carry for instance, but when put together they created a really boring one note stat checker. Okay, let's take a break from old stuff and fast forward a few years into the future. [music] At one point many would have given the title of worst design champion of all time to Yuumi despite having been released in 2019, which by now would be almost 7 years ago. What the Existentialism aside, contrasting from Poppy and Sion's past iterations, Yuumi has a very clear glue line connecting all of her abilities and she plays rather well with all of them in tow. For what she tries to do in game, she has the perfect [music] means of doing so. It's what she tries to do in game that calls her design integrity into question. Risk management is one of the main aspects to mastering enchanter supports. How well you can position yourself to be within range of your team to buff and peel them while also keeping yourself out of harm's way so as to not get focused on by the enemy team. Inconveniently, there's no Geneva Convention laws in League. People can and will try to kill the medic. The issue with Yuumi is that she fundamentally overrides that dynamic by allowing her to attach onto an ally and become untargetable, enabling her to provide the full extent of her buffing and supportive utility without exposing herself. More pressing those are the type of buffs offered by Yuumi when she first came out were essentially force multipliers, wherein she herself would contribute no pressure. Instead, she funneled all of her strength into her companion, not just combining their strengths but magnifying them even further. As anyone who's played Pokémon knows, a level 100 Pokémon can sweep a full team of level 80s [music] or even 90s. Most of that is due to Yuumi offering an increase to just about every possible stat [music] in the game. She gave them a shield, a long range projectile that can slow enemies, aka chasing heart, bonus adaptive force, which would then be reciprocated back to Yumi to make her boss stronger. Healing, attack speed, and movement speed on her E, and then a massive AoE root on her ultimate. The presumed counterplay for Yumi was that if you killed your partner and she had no one to dash to, she would die shortly after, rewarding the enemy team with a two for one. However, [music] Yumi would power up her partner to such an extreme extent that they were the closest thing we had to a literal one V nine champion. Having lived through the experience myself, [music] you don't know the meaning of helplessness until you find a pre-rework Yumi attached to a six-item Hecarim or Kayn by 25 [music] minutes. Truly a force powerful enough to rival season three Kassadin. In essence, it was a catch-22. In order to shut down the carry, you had to kill Yumi, but in order to kill Yumi, you had to shut down the carry. >> [music] >> While she's no longer the same game-breaking support she used to be, I still don't understand why she's in the game. Her mere existence fundamentally infringes on a core balancing factor of the very class that she represents. Moving on though, we're going to dive back into old school territory and discuss arguably one of the dumbest champions in the game, who's pretty much a combination of old Poppy and Sion. He poorly constructed set of abilities that could only do one thing properly, but if you couldn't handle that one thing properly, there was next to zero counterplay against it. That would be Yorick. Yorick is League's primary summoner class, a guy who fights by spawning minions to fight for him. Unfortunately for old Yorick, the champion design team took that a bit too literally by making him do nothing but spawn minions over and over and throw them at you like Angry Birds. All three of his basic abilities had the exact same effect. He would deal damage to the target and then spawn a ghoul that would mindlessly attack the first thing it saw. Omen of War empowered his next auto attack and spawned two ghouls that gave him bonus movement speed while it was alive. Omen of Pestilence was an area blast that slowed enemies and spawned a ghoul with a slowing aura around it. And lastly, Omen of Famine was a point-and-click damaging tool that healed Yorick for a portion of the damage dealt, spawning a ghoul that could heal him with every auto. If it wasn't [music] apparent, the entirety of his consisted of mashing Q, W, and E off cooldown to relentlessly throw ghouls at you over and over. There was nothing else the guy could do. [music] He had no engage, no crowd control, no combos, or anything. All he could do was dead ass keep sending ghouls at you and hope it would achieve something, which usually devolved into a battle of attrition. I mean, there is his ultimate, but that's basically the champion in a nutshell. He would gradually wear you down in lane over and over unless you were one of the rare few with lane sustain back in the day, and then in the mid to late game, there was more of the same thing. Continuously spawn ghouls and let them throw themselves at you in the hopes that that would do something. No play making potential, just 30 minutes of chucking bodies at you. Omen of Death was about the only interesting thing from his kit because he could actually use it on one of his teammates as well. For all intents and purposes, it was a combination of Shaco's Hallucinate and Renata Glasc in one. An extremely powerful ability, especially if you had like a super fed AD carry, make no mistake, but it was on a champion who couldn't really use it all that well on himself cuz he had nothing else going for him. If memory serves, old Yorick was I believe the lowest play rate champion of all time in League, and it's not hard to see why. People straight up forgot this guy even existed half the time. Like, he perennially had a sub 0.5% pick rate for his entire life before getting reworked. It was a cool premise for a champion, executed horribly even by the standards of old League of Legends. And now for our final worst designed champion in League history, [music] I'm going to give it to Tryndamere. Might be a bit contentious, in fact, I know you guys are spamming about a dozen other champions in the comments, but hear me out. I chose Tryndamere for one simple reason. He's not aging very well. Tryndamere is a product of old champion design just like Poppy, Sion, and Yorick were, and yet they haven't really done much of anything to make him better. Even though there are other old champions, Tryndamere doesn't really stand out in a meaningful way. The entirety of his gameplay hinges on getting lucky through crits, at least until he gets 100%, and making himself unkillable to buy him time to auto attack you. He's basically a slightly less degenerate version of old Poppy, which is still really freaking degenerate. Pragmatically [music] speaking, he is viable, but only because they pumped him full of stats to get there. The only real skill expression left in his kit after they made his W guarantee a slow is fury management. Everything else is pretty one-dimensional. The community doesn't complain about him all that much compared to say [music] like a K'Sante because thankfully it's easy to counter play Tryndamere in lane and in fights. But that's all the more reason why I think he's one of the worst designed champions in the game's history. He's basically the only champion who banks on getting lucky to beat you early game. And then afterwards it becomes a black and white situation of him walking up to you and mashing right click until you die or he does. I'm aware other champions are like that as well, Jax mastery and so on. But for those champions at the very least they have aspects of nuance and skill expression to them. Whereas Tryndamere gets a metric ton of free stats and literal death immunity to make up for how little he can influence the outcome otherwise. I really hope he's the next target for the VGU project after Shyvana. His gameplay aged quite terribly. He was bad when he first came out all those years ago and it's only gotten worse over time. If you have to pump a champ full of artificial stats to keep them viable, that just goes to show you the kit itself is a problem. >> [music] >> Alrighty, that concludes my video on the five worst designed champions in the history of League. Again, I recognize there's a hell of a lot more than the five I chose right now. I would put Malzahar, K'Sante, and post-rework Akali on there as honorable mentions, but we only had time for five at the moment. So let me know in the comments down below what your five picks would be and [music] in the meantime if you enjoyed the video, I would appreciate it if you'd like to like and subscribe. Don't forget to follow me on Twitter at Farstar and join my Discord server and check out my other champion discussion videos if you haven't yet. But till next time, thanks so much for watching and I look forward to seeing you again soon. Take care. >> Oh.

titre: How to Choose Your Champion in League of Legends - Everything You Need to Know About the Draft
transcription: Si tu ne sais jamais quoi pique et que tu as l'impression d'avoir perdu des games avant même qu'elles aient commencé, c'est parce que tu ne sais pas draft. Et je vais t'expliquer comment procéder en cinq étapes, quel que soit ton rôle ou ton ho pour mettre toutes les chances de win de ton côté. Tu vas commencer par faire les choses dans l'ordre et en première étape, tu vas définir ta champion pool. C'est très simple, tu vas passer par une phase de test et jouer des champions qui tu penses pourrai te plaire. Mon conseil, c'est qu'au début, si tu as un objectif de monter en ello, tu joues des champions 1 à 5, on va dire, avec des gameplays à peu près similaires. Pour commencer, par exemple, si tu aimes les mages, tu peux jouer Syndra, Oriana et Luxe. Les trois sont de la même classe de champion, mais peuvent courir différents types de draft. Syndra va avoir un gros potentiel de play en solo. C'est un mix entre un mage et un assassin et est plus agro que les autres. Oriana quant à elle est un excellent blind peak. Ça rentre dans toutes les compositions. Excellent team fight et a une grosse phase de line. Et enfin, Luxe est très safe. Ça permet de hold des games pendant très longtemps et ça a un gros potentiel de catch et une très bonne tenue de line et un midgame. Ainsi, tu as trois champions qui ont un gameplay relativement similaire. Donc ça ne te demande pas beaucoup d'efforts et d'adaptation de switch de l'un à l'autre et tes chambres vont te permettre de t'adapter à plusieurs matchup et différents types de draft. Tu peux évidemment jouer des classes différentes, c'est-à-dire des mages que tu peux switcher avec des assassins ou bien des AD mid, des bruiseers et cetera. Tant que tu aimes le champion et le gameplay, juste ça risque d'être un petit peu plus long d'atteindre un niveau similaire sur des matches si de base tu es un joueur d'assassin. Maintenant ça y est, tu as sélectionné ta champôle de 1 à cinq champions et maintenant tu vas passer par une étape clé qui est de déterminer ton niveau individuel sur chacun des champions que tu as sélectionné. C'est l'étape la plus importante de toutes les étapes quant à la réussite de ta draft. Tu vas noter chacun de tes champions sur 10 en terme de skill individuel. C'est le critère le plus important surtout à Baelo parce que tu dois être bon et savoir jouer ton champion. Donc admettons que tu joues Oriana, Syndra et Silas. Tu te mets 9/10 sur Oriana, 7/10 sur Syndra et 5/10 sur Silas. Si contre la compo adverse Oriana est un 7/10, Sidra un 8/10 et Silas un 9/10 et bien tu ne vas pas pixilas parce que tout simplement tu es trop nul dessus comparé à tes deux autres champions. Même si les broken contre la compo adverse, tu auras beaucoup plus de chance de win avec Oriana ou Syndra que tu maîtrises bien mieux. De manière générale, pour te donner une idée, si jamais tu as un champion qui a deux points sur 10 de plus qu'un autre, ça va quasiment être tout le temps worse de le pic Overload champion. Les compositions d'équipe, ça importe vraiment peu à bao et c'est seulement quand tu commences à atteindre le platine, l'émeraude et le diamant et au-dessus que ça commence à avoir un réel impact. Donc te prends pas la tête avec les compositions, te prends pas la tête à vouloir changer de champion, pique le meilleur champion dans tel ou tel draft. La métrique la plus importante, c'est que tu connaisses ton champion, ses matchup et que tu saches bien te débrouiller avec. Une fois que tu as ta champion de pool et que maintenant tu as noté tes champions sur 10, la trème étape c'est de draft par rapport à ton match-up direct dans un premier temps. Essaie de toujours t'assurer un bon matchup pour toi avant de penser aux compositions dans leur globalité. Les compos, ça a un impact vraiment minim queue avant au moins le diamant et le master. Ce qui va primer c'est ton early game, t'assurer une bonne line pour que tu a des ressources et la capacité à avoir de l'impact grâce à cela. Essaie donc toujours d'avoir le counter peck, surtout si tu es solo liner et de voir au moins ton matchup direct avant de pick. Dans le cas de figure où tu es blind peck, tu vas simplement t'orienter vers le champion de ta poule qui est à la fois un bon blind peck et un champion que tu maîtrises très bien. Imaginons le cas de figure où tu es 9/10 sur Yazuo, 9/10 sur Kiana et 8/10 sur Oriana. Il y a très peu d'écart de niveau entre tes trois champions, mais Oriana est de loin le meilleur blind, donc tu vas te diriger vers elle. Si en revanche tu étais seulement 6/10 sur Oriana, tu aurais pris l'un des deux autres parce qu'avec un tel écart de niveau, ce serait ta maîtrise du champion qui va primer. Si maintenant tu as l'info sur ton matchup direct, tu vas me dire comment est-ce que je détermine si mon champ est fort ou non. Bah ça, il y a pas de secret, c'est surtout de l'expérience, de la pratique. Tu vas essayer de comprendre les sorts et les CD de vos deux champions. Par exemple, pourquoi est-ce que Oriana est forte contrari ? Elle a plus de dégâts, plus de range, plus de CD, un wave player à peu près similaire et une meilleure phase de lait. Oriana est donc par définition un excellent liner alors carry est un champ qui est design pour les roms et les scrimish. Ce qui veut dire que si tu es du côté de la Oriana, tu vas plutôt chercher à jouer la phase de la line et le 1v1 alors que Harry a tout intérêt à ignorer la line, à jouer le push, essayer de clear les waves avec son coup spel et de décal. Donc Oriana va battre Harry à niveau égal. Il faut que tu penses aux forces faiblesse de tes deux champes à chaque phase de draft. Mais c'est toujours pas suffisant pour arriver à bien gérer une phase de draft. Parce que là, même si tu as un champion que tu maîtrises, même si tu as fait ta notation sur 10 et que tu sais quels matchup sont bien ou non, plus tu montes, plus il va falloir que tu comprennent ce que font les compos adverses. Tu sais que tu dois piqu un champion que tu maîtrises, que tu aimes et que tu sais draft pour t'octroyer un bon matchup, mais maintenant il faut que tu t'adaptes à la compo adverse et à la tienne qu'on va voir dans la 5e partie. Plus tu vas pic tard dans la draft, plus tu auras d'infos sur la compo adverse et plus tu pourras t'adapter. Essaie donc de piquer le plus tard possible le plus souvent peu importe ton rôle parce que je te rappelle qu'en Solou queue, il y a que toi qui compte et c'est toi qui doit faire la différence et non tes alliés. Quand c'est ton tour, réfléchis à ce que veulent faire les champes de la compoverse. Si par exemple en face, tu vois qu'ils ont déjà sélectionné Nocturne, Kenen et Z, tu vas essayer de prendre un bon chambre contre Z tout en tenant compte que leur compo est clairement axée sur l'engage et très fort pour tuer des squishies mobiles. La game risque d'être très chaotique. Donc tu évites de jouer luxe là-dedans. Si tu joues des mages only, tu vas plutôt partir sur un champion qui peut se self pil du genre Victor. Il est très bon pour recevoir une tel engage et un bien meilleur chambre contre Z et Nocturne que Luxe. Autre exemple, si en face tu vois Darius, Viego, Rise, Samira, Nautilus, là tu peux partir sur Syndra parce que tu as un matchup simple. En plus, il y a que des loweng en face que tu peux repousser. Le pic est littéralement gode, mais ça suffit pas forcément, surtout si tu approches du diamant ou du master, à réussir ta phase de draft. Parce que tu peux très bien piquer un champion qui est très fort contre la compo adverse mais qui va très mal avec ta compo et donc tu auras quand même du mal à t'exprimer. La dernière étape pour réussir ta phase de draft, c'est de comprendre ce que veut faire ta composition. Tu dois comprendre l'identité de ta compo. La base des bases, c'est d'arriver à avoir une balance de dégâts AP AD. Ici, encore une fois, si tu ne joues que des AD ou que des AP, c'est toujours mieux d'être sur un confort que tu maîtrises parfaitement en full AD ou full AP, plutôt que d'aller jouer quelque chose que tu ne maîtrises pas du tout, juste pour la compo, je te rappelle, c'est de la solo queue, tu passes avant l'équipe. Si jamais tu as une compo avec Soraka, Jinx, Trundle et Graves et qu'en face ils ont Kane, Akali, Keissa, Lux et Trinda, Vex est un très bon exemple, très forte contre la compo adverse parce que beaucoup de dash de squishy et un bon matchup. Mais vex, c'est pas ouf avec ta team puisque tu as zéro followup, pas de CC pour t'aider à toucher ton R et ta compo veut clairement jouer les 5v5 front to back et le scaling avec Trundle en speed push. Dans ce cas-là, tu vas plus t'orienter sur un gros match contrôle avec du wave clear qui rentre plus dans la composition du genre Oriana ou Rise et qui reste très bien contre la compo adverse. Tu peux même piqu une le blanc si jamais tu sais la jouer. Le champ est god dans ses drafts. On arrive à la fin de la vidéo. Maintenant, tu as toutes les cartes en main pour ne plus perdre de game à la draft. retient bien lors d'importance dans lequel j'ai évoqué les différents points et pense à lâcher un petit j'aime et t'abonner si la vidéo t'a plu et on se retrouve dans une prochaine vidéo. [Musique]

titre: How to Choose Your MAIN Champion in Season 14! - Beginner's League of Legends Guide
transcription: in today's guide we're going to help you find your main Champion with over 160 different Champions to pick from in season 14 it can be a bit overwhelming trying to choose one but my promise to you is by the end of this guide we'll have narrow it down to a select few Champions that you'll love to play whether you're a new player returning player or just looking for a new champ this guide has got you covered and once you lock in your main if you want to supercharge your improvement then head on over to skill cap.com we have the largest collection of Premium courses tailored for every single role take our course on getting diamond in 30 days over 35 star ratings from satisfied customers saying how this one course alone is worth their entire membership or take our course on the best way to Jungle in Solo CU Perfect festar reviews with users saying how this showed exactly why they were stuck at their rank the best part you can try all of this out completely risk-free if you don't rank up while actively using skill cap you get your money back no questions asked so click the link in the description below to get the rank you've always wanted this season all right so starting us off we have top Lan known for attracting Summoners who just want to be left alone while they bash their opponent to pieces and it's no surprise that's why the most popular class of Champions in this role are what's known as Fighters The Fighter class contains Champions that have a nice balance of both damage and tankiness and there are three fighter subclasses will go over first are the lane bullies these are champions who are strong early and fight for Lane dominance right from level one constantly looking to trade and fight in Lane this can be pretty fun since who doesn't want to start the laning phase immediately crushing their opponent as a general rule though the stronger you are in the early game the harder you're going to fall off in the late game this means aggression and generating early leads and snowballing is going to be expected of you but the bigger the risk the bigger the reward when executed well you'll find yourself getting satisfying early wins Garen and Pantheon are the two piics we recommend trying out second are the split pushers these are champions that will scale and become hyper carries as the game progresses they're kind of the reverse of the lane bully where instead of going aggressive and dominating the early game you'll be dominating the late game one distinct Hallmark of these Champions is the main way they apply their power it's going to be through sticking in a sid Lane by themselves not grouping with their team think of this as the Lone Wolf class you want to take things that your own pace and end the game in your own way nases trinere and Jack are great picks for those trying out this subass for the first time and for the third and final fighter class are the Jack of all trades instead of being very strong in the early or late game these are champions who just kind of good at all phases they are typically a bit more useful in team fights as well compared to the other fighter subclasses these are great champions for those who don't like to feel restricted or forced into a certain play style due to the champion they're playing snowballing early scaling for late team fighting or split pushing they can all be viable depending on the game you're in this can also be a great starting point for those new to Top Lane as a whole since you can end up using these Champions to find what kind of play style you enjoy and then later switching to one of the bullies or split pushers once you realize that's how you want to play Mizer and set are the recommended starting point for those new to the subass now maybe you're someone who doesn't want to role play as the main protagonist of the story and instead wants to be the unkillable raid boss they have to defeat well that's where the always needed tank class comes in the tank class has three sub classes to pick from first are the offensive tanks These Are the Champions that that are looking to land those big epic engages That Swing fights in their favor whether it's something a bit smaller like catching out a champion and locking them down for your teammates or making a huge play with your high impact ultimate in a lot of ways you're kind of like the battle commander as you'll be deciding when your team goes in if that sounds appealing then we recommend trying out Mal fight and or the second subass are defensive tanks these are champions that are looking to protect their teammates with their big body rather than crush the opponent now don't get me wrong defensive tanks still have ways of engaging and making picks but overall their design is done in such a way that you'll more often be using your abilities to help peel enemies off of your carries essentially instead of being proactive and pulling the trigger on a big malate ultimate you're more reactive waiting for the perfect timing on your alt to save a teammate so if you like the idea of being a tank but don't like the pressure of having to engage these are likely the champions for you Poppy and Shen are the picks we recommend for newcomers and our final tank subass are the fighter tanks these are champions who typically have lower impact ultimates but in turn are stronger in smaller skirmishes they aren't as relying on their teammates for following up as they not only tank damage but dish it out as as well these are perfect for players who like the idea of playing a tank but still want to be able to carry without having to rely too much on their teammates Dr Mundo and volibear are two great starter champs for the subass now maybe you like Top Lane itself but Fighters and tanks aren't the Champions you enjoy playing well this is where the ranged carry class comes in it has two sub classes the first are the ranged attack damage carries these actually play a lot different in Top Lane compared to when you play them in a different role like bot or mid since so many top laners are melee playing one of these adcs means you'll actually be extremely strong in the early levels often able to bully opponents with your range advantage in fact despite some of the champions in the subass being typically associated with scaling late game like vars and vean you actually will have more in common with the lane bully fighter subass with the ranged Advantage you have early there is an expectation for you to generate a lead if you fall behind or go even in most matchups that means you may just be run over once the enemy hits level six and gets access to their ultimate as you'll be too squishy to survive this subass is perfect for players who love playing range carries but hate having to play them with a teammate in bot Lane they're also great for players to enjoy a mechanically demanding Champion as positioning and kiing is key if you're curious and want to try out the subass we actually recommend starting with Quinn as they have a lot less mechanical Demand on the player compared to the others now standing beside the ranged 80 carry is the ranged ability power carry in function these are very similar to the ADC class we just went over however there's one key difference the AP carry will typically bring a lot more to team fights therefore for players who enjoy playing the Champions found in the subass but dislike having to play them in their more traditional role mid where they can often face assassins and longer range Mages that they may find to be unfun kenon is the one we recommend trying out and finally we have our final top Ling class the Specialists these are champions that are so unique they simply don't fit into any of the other traditional molds typically these Champions have a small but dedicated fan base around them and if youve tried out several other classes of Champions and found them to be a bit too vanilla and boring while then trying out champions from this subass may be what you've been looking for all along moving on we have the jungle roll within jungle the fighter class is the most prevalent providing a good mix of damage and tankiness and it has five sub classes first are the early game Fighters these are champions that will look to aggressively gank Lanes early and Snowball the game if you want to start impacting the game as soon as possible then these are the junglers for you the main downside is you typically won't be super impactful in team fights and will fall off as the game gets later zenzo serves as a great introduction to this play style second are the midgame fighters these are more well-rounded Champions that are often giving up a bit of power in the early game in order to have a bigger power Spike once they get their ultimate at level six you'll often be farming a bit more than ganking in the early game because of this but are rewarded by having a stronger mid game and team fighting ability nocturn is a great started Champion for this play style third are the late game Fighters there aren't too many in this group but your primary focus will be farming up with weaker gangs than the others even when you hit Level six you'll typically be more damage focused so think of less of a big ultimate that swings a fight and more just shredding down the enemy with high DPS Master E is the starter champ that we recommend for the subass next are the fighter Mages these are ability power spellcasters who are more tanky than your average Mage instead of a typical attack damage fighter play style of jumping into fights to tank and damage enemies the fighter Mages have to be a bit more strategic with when and how they use their abilities their abilities will often have more crowd control than the other Fighters so there can be a bit more of an emphasis on Landing that big alt or ability to make a pick on an enemy rather than just straight diving in and Stat checking both Fiddlesticks and Lilia serve as a great introduction to this play style and finally our fifth subass the fighter tank these are similar to The midgame Fighter subass except they give up some of the damage to become more tanky this is a great choice choice if you're the type of player who leans more towards enjoying being a tank rather than a damage dealer but doesn't want to fully commit to a pure tank poppy is a great place to start next we have the assassin class hiding inside fog of War only to pop out and One-Shot targets if that sounds fun to you then you should look to main an assassin jungler do be aware though usually assassin junglers will lean slightly more on the weak side in the early game running straight into a fighter class champion level three and expecting to win probably won't work in your favor however if you just strategically pick your spots you'll only need a few kills to start snow balling the game two great Champions to try out to see if you like this play style are kha'zix and Evelyn and if instead of one-shotting people you want to be unkillable well then the tank class is for you tank junglers are typically equipped with a lot of crowd control this means they may not be as strong as fighters in the 1v1 but they still have strong ganks not only early on but also once they Spike level six the tank class is not only great if you like well the tank archetype found in most games but also if you want a more relaxed chill approach to the game this is because there's no pressure for you to snowball as you'll always be useful whether you're ahead or behind this is due to you having really high impact ultimates and abilities that can always swing fights amumu and Ramis are both great for first-time tank players and then we have the true Mage junglers unlike the fighter Mages these guys usually have a complete lack of tankiness however they make up for it with a massive amount of AP damage you'll typically have good scaling and team fighting but be a bit on the weaker side in the early game it's similar to the Assassin play style with less of an emphasis on having to snowball through kills often just with good farm and keeping Pace with the rest of the game will be enough for you to have high impact in fights we recommend trying out this play style with brand as carus and Talia can be very mechanically taxing in comparison and then we have our 0 carry junglers there are only two that fall into this category but they've always been consistently strong through League's history these Champions are actually quite unique as they not only scale extremely well but they also have very strong early games due to their range Advantage so if you enjoy the role of jungling while also the play style of an ad0 carry then definitely give one of these Champions a try and finally we have our jungle specialist with only one Champion falling into the category Iver ivern is basically a jungle support but it's just so unique you can't really just label him that alone for most players it only takes one game of Iver to know whether you love him or hate him however I would say if you're a player who loves the traditional support archetype in games while also enjoying the jungle role then Iver was likely designed specifically for you moving on we have the midlaners which is mostly dominated by spellcasters or just High burst champions in general Mages are the most popular class having four different subclasses to pick from the first is the burst Mage these Champions are all about catching that one squishy Target out and deleting them from the game in one spell rotation so if you like the idea of blowing up your opponent in a split second then these are for you both Annie and Ari serve as the two best champions for becoming familiar with the subass next is the control Mage these are less about one-shotting opponents and more about controlling parts of the battlefield with either crowd control AOE damage over time or all three if you like slower paced more strategic fights then these are the champions you want to main Nivea or aelan Soul are great starter picks as the others can be quite mechanically demanding the third subass are battle mat these are typically Champions who have lower range and get a bit more close and personal with the opposition they're usually a bit tankier than the other Mages but not by that much in order to survive in the close quarter fights their abilities will grant them crowd control healing or immunity rather than just phase tanking damage Vladimir Swain rise and copia are more about outputting consistent DPS in the mid-range while malahar and landre have more burst damage and are more about making picks we recommend trying out malahar or Vladimir to get a taste of each type of battle mage and the fourth and final Mage subass is the artillery mage these Champions typically outrange every other champion on the rift their abilities typically are skill shot heavy and so having good aim is key to playing them well often you'll either be poking enemies down from a distance or Landing your one crowd control ability to make a pick and burst someone down Lux is definitely the most approachable for players new to the subass the second most popular class of midlaners are the Assassins since a lot of squishy Champions are played in mid lane along with mid being the center of the map giving you great Rome opportunities assassins will find a lot of success played here they could be very feast or famine though with so much burst damage in the midlane roster often you'll be in very skill-based matchups where either you or the opponent will get a kill and snowball early if you're new to the style of play and we actually recommend starting out with either cadin or Talon as they're much more beginner friendly then we have the 80 carry mids what's interesting is with Mages and assassins being so prevalent in mid lane it actually leaves open 80 carries to bully those matchups early on before they get items needed to come online so if you enjoy playing 80 carries but just like how when you play them in bot Lane there's a lot of waiting to scale to become relevant well then you should should try one of these midlane 80 carries you keep in mind though they can be volatile when you snowball an early lead you'll dominate the MAV but if you fall behind expect a lot of gray screens due to the high burst damage found in mid Tristana and corki are great picks if you're trying out this class for the first time and then there's the fighter class these are midlaners who typically are a bit tankier while doing more DPS rather than just pure burst these are perfect for people who have tried some of these champions in Top Lane but found it a bit too boring with having to face a lot of tanky Champions that neutralize the lane since there are are so many squishy champions in mid you'll often have more of an opportunity to get kills and snowball they also typically play a lot like an assassin while being a bit more forgiving since he won't be One-Shot as much Diana and yon are what we recommend trying out and finally we have the midlane Specialists these are champions that are so unique they don't really fit into the other traditional classes for that reason they're pretty polarizing and usually after one game you'll know whether you love them or hate them heading down to the bot Lane let's look at our 80 carries or as Riot calls them marksmen marksmen have five subclasses the first being the lane bullies these are adcs that have a strong early game and will be looking to crush their opponent right from level one now like other roles stronger early game Champions usually are weaker later but it's a bit different with Marksman in general ad0 carries have good scaling regardless at least in comparison to other roles so just don't get the wrong idea you can pick a lane bully and it won't mean that you end up completely useless in the late game two Champions we recommend to beginners wanting to try this out are Tristana and Caitlyn next we have the hyper carries these stand opposite to the Lane bullies these are champions who are typically weak or early but have some of the best scaling in the game if you're someone who prefers to chill in the landing phase and look to make an impact in team fights instead then these are the picks for you for beginners we recommend Jinx and Zia then we have the utility Marksman these are champions who use crowd control to swing fights in their favor don't get it twisted though they can still do massive amounts of damage instead usually what they're giving up is mobility in their kits in order to have that crowd control if you like the Marksman class but hate having to wait for your teammates to initiate fights well these picks are for you we recommend starting out with Ash if you're a beginner and then we have the mobility Marksman this subass of adcs are typically very slippery and hard for opponents to pin down they can be incredibly fun to play but usually demand a high mechanical skill in order to Pilot effectively if that sounds up your alley then we recommend trying out Lucian or Ezreal then we have the final subclass of marksman the Jack of all trades only civer resides in here and it's due to the fact that she's kind of good at everything but doesn't excel in any one thing in particular well except for wave clear if you're new to The Marksman class as a whole and feel a bit overwhelmed civer is always a good pick to fall back on and then we have the skirmisher class these are champions that usually have shorter range than most marksmen and instead of raining down damage from range prefer to get into the thick of things they'll often have one key defensive ability you have to use well in fights in order to lplay opponents in close range if getting in close to brawl with opponent sounds appealing to you then we recommend trying out kaisa or Samira and finally we have the Mage class these are ability power carries and can be good picks if you enjoy bot Lane but dislike Marksman and prefer the play style of a mage in general Mages in bot Lane typically have a we weaker early game in comparison to traditional Marksman however they do have a powerful mid game Spike and late game scaling the two Champions we recommend starting out with are ziggs and Swain and finally we have the support role so let's break down the different Champions you have to choose from beginning with the tank class which has two subclasses first are the offensive tanks these are champions who look to engage and catch out opponents or land that Big Al to set up a winning team fight in Lane you're typically waiting for that moment where an enemy overextends than jumping on them to burst them down with your 80 carry that sounds appealing to you the Nautilus is a great Champion to try out first second are the defensive tanks these are champions that are still tanky but instead of engaging on the enemy carries you're more concerned about protecting your own Rah is a great pick to try out to see if you like this play style and then we have the catchers these Champions are similar to the offensive tanks but they want to make picks on the enemy with powerful crowd control spells however overall they're usually squishier but as compensation get more damage in their kits Blitz crank is our recommendation if you're trying this class out for the first time moving on we then have the enchanters These Are the Champions usually most associated with a support role with heals and shields their main concern is protecting their carries and keeping them alive sraa and Jana are both fantastic picks for trying out this style of champion now if doing damage is more appealing to you well then you'll want to try out the artillery Mage class these are supports that constantly poke enemies down in lane and often have one crowd control ability that if they land can let them one shot squishy Champions their abilities also typically scale a lot more with items than other traditional supports Lux is who we recommend trying out for beginners now if you like the sound of of doing damage but prefer to burst rather than poke from distance you should be looking into the burst Mage class these Champions have less range than the artillery Mages but usually either have more upfront burst or more AOE damage in their kits letting them team fight better brand and Zyra are both great choices for trying out this class and if you still want to do damage but don't like playing squishy characters well you should look into the battle mage class there's only one Champion found here Swain but he's actually a great option for those who want to do more damage than a tank while not getting one- shot by enemy carries and then we have the 80 carry class of supports as crazy as it sounds there are a few adcs that can be played in the support role Ash does the least damage of the three but brings a lot more crowd control than the others twitch is basically just pure damage and Senna is a nice mix of damage utility and healing and shielding to protect your teammates our recommendation is to start with Senna if this play style sounds appealing to you and finally we have the support Specialists these are champions that are so unique they don't really fit into any other class for that reason they can be polarizing and usually after one game you'll know whether you love them or hate them all
