titre: Why Are Some Champions Useless In Pro Play?
transcription: Have you ever dominated a game with Master Yi or Teemo and wondered why you never see them in pro matches? Despite certain champions being wildly popular and even strong in solo Q, they are rarely if ever picked in professional League of Legends. To understand the recent years, we should note that this isn't a new phenomenon. Historically, plenty of champions that terrorize solo Q have struggled in proplay. Mastery and Nasus are classic examples. They torment low elo players, but have had little success in professional play. Buffing them to be viable on stage would risk making them unstoppable menaces for the average player. This fundamental balance dilemma has existed for ages. Riot often leaves certain pub stompers a bit undertuned for coordinated play rather than overpowering them for everyone else. By 2020, this gap was firmly in place. Competitive metas favored champions with crowd control, mobility, or team fight utility attributes our niche champions often lack. Yet, the story from 2020 onward is not just these champs are bad and stay bad. In fact, the last few years have seen dramatic shifts in items, meta, and champion updates. There were moments when it looked like a few of these solo Q superstars might break through in proplay only to fall back into obscurity. Let's dive yearbyear tracing how proplay priorities evolved and how our cast of outcast champions fared or failed in the evershifting meta. 2020 had a relatively stable competitive meta. Pro team stuck to champions that offered reliability. strong early game junglers like Leon or Olaf, safe scaling mages and ADCs and tanks or fighters in top. In such an environment, champions like Mastery or Trindomir had virtually 0% pick or ban presence in major regions. The drafting philosophy in pro play was all about wave control, objective setups, and team fight impact. A split push specialist with no CC or a snowball reliant jungler with no early gank pressure was seen as too risky. Teams had limited practice time and gravitated toward the known good picks as one coach described with only a few effective scrim games each week. You certainly won't be ripping up the draft recipe and starting from scratch to test a fringe pick. In other words, why spend precious hours practicing Teemo top when you could refine your or Camille that you know will work? The result throughout 2020 champions like Teemo, Shako, Yei, Garen, Velkos were essentially pub legends. Only Amumu, famously a low elo powerhouse jungler, was completely unseen in pro play. The last time Amumu had even been mentioned in a competitive context was a punchline until Riot decided to intervene, foreshadowing for 2021. But in 2020, pro play was all about consistency. A cheese pick might pop up in regional leagues or minor tournaments. But at Worlds 2020, you wouldn't find a single Teemo or Master Yi. If a pro even hovered these champions in draft, it was usually as a joke to get a rise out of the audience. To solve that problem, right? Remember, the rise was banned in game one. It was the Lulu in the second game. So, Tristana Lulu was draft Faker. I swear that's a grin. I Season 11 in 2021 introduced mythic items, shaking up the game's item system and opening the door to new strategies. For a brief time, these changes narrowed the gap between solo Q and pro play power for some champions. In solo Q, players discovered deadly builds. For example, Master Yi abusing the new dust blade of Drathar for invisibility resets or Trindamir benefiting from Gale Forc's dash to cover his old weakness of no gap close. But did pros spite on these changes? Not immediately. Early 2021 pro play still favored champions that could use mystics like Gorrinker or Everrost to great team effect. Think Renekton, Udier, Azir, etc. Our off meta heroes were still mostly sideline. However, by summer 2021, a curious trend emerged. Trindir mid. Yes, the perennial split pusher was being played in mid lane by high elo players, leveraging gale force or kraken slayer and the buffed lethal tempo rune to dual squishy mages. Bruiser itemization was incredibly strong at this time, way stronger than mage items. As one analyst noted, Trindir with items like Gorrinker, Nvori Quickblades, Infinity Edge could outstat almost any mid laner. His ultimate gave him survivability and his scaling was monstrous. So if he survived lane, he'd practically carry the game singlehandedly. This solo Q success did not go unnoticed by pros and teams in scrims. The mid trinumir strategy was brewing beneath the surface in the leadup to Worlds 2021. At the same time, Riot made a bold move to address Amumu's absence from the highle play in patch 11.17 back in August 2021. Amumu got a mini rework. Next up, we have a small rework to Amumu. Like, he's actually good in most elos, but he's bad in like, you know, midplat and higher into diamond to masters into pro play. He's unplayable, right? Thing is a mumu nuggets two charges of Q. And this is the entire changes that matter. His Q bandage toss now had two charges among other tweaks. Lead game designer Jivie Sidu explained the goal was to make Amumu better in higher MMRs since he had a huge performance disparity between low and high elo. In other words, Riot acknowledged Amumu was garbage in proplay and tried to fix it by giving him more engage power. This immediately suggested a roll shift. Amumu could be played as a support with dual bandage engages, pairing his alt with a strong AoE carry. The World Championship 2021 was the real testing ground for these developments. And indeed, we saw the competitive debut or resurgence of some of these champions. Peace side, hey, look, maybe you decide to try and take one of them away and then you go towards it. But we will finally get the sad mummy onto the screen. It will be the curse, the sad bullet time down to that bottom side. I thought they'd never pick Amumu. It's just been so long and I've been so excited to see this one now. And it's all about that Wombo combo. Amumu support was highly anticipated going into Worlds. Many analysts predicted the combo of Amumu plus Misfortune whose bullet time could devastate cursed targets as a meta strategy. We are seeing level six is being picked up now. You do not have access to the cannon barrage, but death mark the hero's entrance and of course the curse of the sad bullet time. They land it. They're going to go in and the AoE is good. Violet will pick himself up the kill on the first blood. This was the first pro presence for the sad mummy in years. One stat he hadn't been seen as a support in top competition since 2014. The two charge Q gave him a reliable engage follow-up on paper at least. However, reality was harsh. Amumu ended playins with only 27.3% win rate. Teams quickly learned to exploit his weaknesses after he dove in. He was a sitting duck. As Inven Global reported, the champion looked very strong, or so it seemed before playins. Once on stage, teams had a hard time accounting for his weaknesses, particularly how squishy he was if the all-in failed. By the group stage, Amumu saw three bands but zero picks as teams largely abandoned the pick. The experiment showed that even with buffs, a champion needs to fit the meta's demands. And in worlds 2021, engaged supports like Leona or more durable picks like Braum were preferred over the fragile mummy. Trindir mid likewise made waves during Worlds 2021, especially in the group stage. Trindir became a hot pocket pick in mid lane. Utilized by star players like Chovi and Perks. The meta at Worlds featured a lot of scaling mages like Oriana, Synindra, and Corki, who had a strong late game but weak early laning and no crowd control. Trindir was the perfect spoiler for those picks. He could bully them early with his crits and sustain, and they had little CC to stop his engage. It reached a point where analysts noted Trindir was too good to be ignored given the item advantages. In fact, during World's 2021 playins, Trindir was picked five times with a 60% win rate in those games. A staggering success that caused teams to start banning him. Even the legendary Faker was practicing it. More on him soon. Suddenly, a champion that had been a meme in proplay for years was a priority pick or a ban in a world championship setting. It was a culmination of perfect conditions, overtuned items as discussed, a favorable mid-lane meta, and pros finally putting in the scrim time to master a champion previously outside their comfort zone. Amumu's low win rate meant teams lost confidence in him after the initial trials, and Trindir Surge would soon be checked by Riot and Meta Shifts. Nonetheless, these moments were significant. They prove that given the right patch or strategy, even garbage champions can spike in pro play. The key is usually a cause and effect chain. Riot changes something, buffs new items, solo Q players find success, pros take notice and practice it. If it counters the current meta, it appears on stage. We saw this with Trindir and Amumu in 2021. Next, we'll see how 2022 continued this story, especially for Trindir, and how other champions like Velcos and Master Yi fared. The year 2022 started with a competitive meta still influenced by late 2021's developments. In spring 2022, Trindir Mid actually became meta in some regions. This is something nobody would have believed a couple years prior. Over in Korea's LCK, Trindir reached an astounding 79% presence in spring 2022. Essentially, first pick or first band material. Even Faker, initially skeptical, gave it a go. In an interview in January 2022, Faker explained his reasoning after picking Trindir mid into Corki. Corki lacks crowd control and doesn't have a strong laning phase, making him pray for Trindamir. This strategic insight shows how pros weigh a champion's value. Trindir wasn't picked because he's inherently amazing, but because he exploited a specific meta trend. Tier 1's coaches and Faker deemed that risk worth it. And it paid off. Faker nabbed a triple kill in that game and Tier 1 extended an undefeated streak. Yeah, Barrel has spotted him out here as the tower is going to fall down. Great grand entrance is going to connect onto Pic, but he has the alt to keep himself alive. Faker now gets underneath the turret immediately. Has to use the alt, but it does not matter. That's a triple kill. It's worth noting that Faker's Trindameir pick was his 70th unique champion in pro play, highlighting how exceptional it was to see trend on a champion list alongside standard mages and assassins. Hello pros and analysts commented on this emerging trend. For instance, former pro score predicted that Amumu support would definitely be played at Worlds. He was right, but said, "I'm not sure about Trindir. It might be played, it might not." Implying it was a gamble that depended on performance. That gambled paid off in early 2022. Trindir was everywhere in solo Q and frequently seen in pro drafts. A true crossover hit born from the confluence of item strength and meta adaptation. However, League's balance team didn't sit idle. The durability update in mid 2022 patch 12.10 dramatically changed the landscape. All champions got tankier, reducing bursts across the board. Overall champions gained about 15% more health and about 0 to 10% more durability through armor and MR, which combines to a roughly um 15 to 28% durability increase in champions. Okay, so champions take 15 to 28% more damage incoming before they will die. This made it harder for champions like Trindamir to secure quick kills and it gave team oriented comps more breathing room to focus him down during his ultimate. Additionally, Riot issued by summer 2022 Trindir's presence fell off in pro play as the meta shifted to more stable mid laners, Ari, Talia, etc. And the corgi trend waned. The Trendir mid chapter, while exciting, was relatively short-lived. A perfect example of a champion spiking briefly due to meta, then dropping once conditions normalize. What about Amumu in 2022? The sad mummy enjoyed a bit of renaissance in early 2022, riding the momentum from worlds. In regional leagues and MSI, a few teams played Amumu support as a niche engage pick, especially paired with aggressive early ADCs. We saw it with Kalista in some cases where Amumu's double Q practically guaranteed the Ren stacks would be cashed in. But as new patches rolled out, other engage supports like Nautilus and Leona remained more popular. They could tank more damage and had better laning phase. Amumu started to fall back out of favor. By Worlds 2022, he was no longer a contested pick. Other supports like Yumi, Enchanter Meta, and Nautilus dominated the support picks. Still, Amumu's brief return proved that a welltimed buff could at least inject a long-forgotten champ into the meta, even if just temporarily. Meanwhile, Mastery in 2022 mostly stayed a persona nongrada in proplay, but not for lack of trying from Riot. Early in 2022, patch 12.5, Riot gave Yi a mini rework or buff. Master Yi is getting a reshape. Basically, the TLDDR is Wuju style and Kraken Slayer and other on hit effects now apply during alpha strike. The result was explosive. Master Yi's win rate in solo Q shot above 55% overnight as players found a new onhit build that wasn't affected by the nerfs to Dusk Blade. He was overpowered to the point of warping low elo games. Riot had to issue an emergency hot fix within days, nerfing Yi's base health and damage scaling. After those nerfs, his win rate plunged below 45%. This incident is telling. It shows how afraid Riot is of a world where Master Yi is actually meta. The champ can take over games against uncoordinated teams. So any buff too far must be quickly dialed back. From a pro-play perspective, even during that brief OP window, no one pulled out Yei in competitive matches. Why? Because coordinated teams still have the tools to handle him. They will draft point-and-click CC like Scarnner or Landra and make his life miserable. The risk of picking Yi, a champion with zero hard CC and purely single target damage on a competitive stage far outweighed any potential surprise factor. One Reddit comment succinctly joked about champs like Yei or Velcos. These picks bully inexperienced players, but pro players just aren't going to get hit by all skill shots, then line up to get Penta killed. They will find ways to negate his strengths. In Yi's case, pros know how to not give him the 1v one duels he wants. They'll collapse on him as a team. Thus, Master Yi remained absent in proplay through 2022, even has he continued to be a solo Q favorite with a healthy 50% win rate and 5% pick rate in platinum plus ranked. What a Velcos, the laser launching eye of the void. Velcos has actually had sporadic showings in pro play over the years as a niche counterpick, typically as a support into heavy tanks or mid lane into certain matchups. But from 2020 to 2025, his appearances were few and far between. In solo Q, Velcon support maintained a respectable win rate around 52% with a small but dedicated player base, two to 3% pick rates in platinum. Plus, his kit offers immense longrange damage and even true damage to melt tanks, which sounds useful for coordinated play. The problem is reliability. Lensing cause full combo requires hitting skill shots, and pro teams are experts at dodging and using fog of war to avoid telegraph skill shots. Any mistake on Velcos, and he's a sitting tentacle with zero mobility to escape a collapse. That's why we seldom see him unless a team has practiced a poke composition to perfection. For the vast majority of pro games, if a team wants AP poke, they opt for a safer champion like Ziggs or even Nazerath, who at least has longer range, or they just avoid that style altogether. The risk of doing negligible damage if you miss your spells is too high when everything is on the line. By the end of 2022, the drafting philosophy in pro play had further solidified around consistency and flexibility. Champions that could be flexed to multiple roles or that provided engage or scaling insurance were heavily favored. Drafting is a zero sum game. If you lock in Master Yi, you've not only hardpicked a champ with clear counters, but you've also signaled your win condition. Ye must hard carry and surrendered any engage potential from that role. It's a huge strategic cost. That's why we often saw teams prefer a weaker early champ with more utility over a pub stomp carry. For example, pro junglers would rather pick Trundle to pillar and stat steel for the team or Poppy to stop dives even if Master Yi might outdamage them in theory. The priorities of pro play, vision control, objective setups, team fight coordination just don't align with what Master Yi or Teemo offer. Heading into 2023 and 2024, Riot continued to iterate on the game. New champions entered the frey and a few underused champions received reworks. One notable update was the comprehensive Aurelion Soul rework in 2023, which transformed another longtime never seen in Pro Champion into a late game scaling monster. Initially, Aurelion Souls rework made him dominate solo Q. Much like our earlier Yi example, he shot above 55% minates in mid for a bit. There was buzz that he might appear in pro play due to his infinite scaling fantasy, but pros recognized his exploitable early game and most teams kept him shelled. This was yet another case reinforcing the theme. A champ can be overtuned for solo queue, but if he has clear early weaknesses or requires too much setup, pros shy away. For a roster of champions in question, 2023 2025 didn't bring much renaissance. Trindir largely stayed out of pro meta after his 2022 moment. Teemo remained the ultimate niche pick. Though it's worth noting Riot hasn't forgotten him. In late 2024, Riot pushed a small Teemo rework patch 14.20, aimed at giving him more late game utility and even viability in the jungle. They improve his poison scaling, increase his shroom burst, and revamp his stealth to allow more aggressive ambush plays. The patch notes optimistically suggested this could make him a more attractive option for both casual and pro players. It's too early to tell, as of early 2025, if pro teams will take that bait. Most analysts remain skeptical. To work in pro, Teemo would need to fill a unique niche that another champion can't do better. His signature skill, blinding darts, primarily counters auto attack reliant champions. In a tournament draft, why pick Teemo to blind when you could pick, say, Malight to completely shut down an AD carry with an ultimate and provide engage. The cost benefit still isn't there. Perhaps if a future meta revolves almost entirely around immobile ADCs and split push sidelane fights, some big brain coach might pull out Teemo top as a surprise. But even then, it would be an edge case. As of now, Teemo has zero pro games recorded in top leagues in the past few years despite the tweaks. One area where pros occasionally experiments with off-meta champs is scrims or practice games. There's a saying that anything can work in scrims. Teams often test wild strategies behind closed doors. We've heard rumors of mastery e comps or garin yumi comps stomping scrims. However, there's also scrim secrecy. If a team truly finds a sleeper OP strategy, they'll hide it until a crucial match. But more often than not, these offmeta trials in scrims don't consistently succeed, so they never see the light of the stage. A tier one coach explained that all of the scrim games teams play, only a couple are actually productive practice. With that limited effective time, teams rarely devoted to mastering a fringe pick like Gee or Shako. It's seen as inefficient when they could be scrimming meta champs. There's also the factor of champion pools and player comfort. Pro players specialize in certain styles. By 2025, the top players have thousands of hours on meta champions. For a player to pick up a champion like Master Yi, which they haven't seriously played since maybe their rookie days, is a big ask. Occasionally, a player is known as a one trick or has a pocket pick. For instance, a support player who spams Pike or Brand in solo Q might be more inclined to bring it out in pro. But if no pro player seriously plays Teemo or Yi in their spare time, it's unlikely they'd bust it out under the high pressure, highstakes scenario of a tournament. Pro play often sticks to the adage, practice makes perfect. Teams would rather run a proven composition they practiced 50 times than a surprise pick practiced five times, even if the latter has a theoretical edge. Finally, consider patch specific risk. Competitive play usually runs on a specific patch for each tournament stage. If a champion gets a big buff on the very next patch, teams might note it, but not incorporate it until they have more time. Sometimes a champion becomes strong in solo Q on patch X or Y, but the tournament is still on X and X or teams don't feel ready to use it on short notice. A good example was the Master Yi mini rework we discussed. It happened just after a major patch lock, so pro teams largely ignored it since they anticipated the nerves and didn't want to invest in a soontobe fixed outlier. By the time the next tournament came, Yei was nerfed and off the table again. After examining the last five years, the pattern is clear. Certain champions are rarely picked in proplay, not because they can't ever be strong, but because their strengths don't align with proplay's priorities, or they carry crippling weaknesses that coordinated opponents exploit. Champions like Trindamir, Teemo, Master Yi, Vulcaus, Amumu, Shako, Garen, etc. often lack one or more of the musthaves for a competitive pick. Safe laning, team fight impact, crowd control, versatility, or consistency. Even when they become overpowering in solo Q, pro teams will respond with refined strategies and Riot usually intervenes with balance changes. As we saw, Trendamir had his shining moment when conditions were just right. And Amumu briefly found a place through a direct buff. But sustaining that presence is tough. The meta in proplay is like a living organism. It quickly digests oneoff surprises and moves on. By 2025, none of the champions we discussed have permanently broken into the competitive rotation. They remain by and large solo Q kings and queens, but competitive poppers.

titre: Ranking EVERY CHAMPION From EASIEST To HARDEST - Season 16 - League of Legends
transcription: In today's video, we're going to be ranking every single champion from easiest to hardest per season 16. There are two different ways in which you can approach a difficulty ranking, either ranking by skill floor, which is how difficult a champion is to pick up and find a baseline level of success with, or skill ceiling, which is how difficult it is to master a champion to their limits. For the sake of this video, we're going to be rating based on skill ceiling. So, which champions are the most difficult to truly master in League of Legends? We're going to be covering each role and place champions in four unique tiers. The first tier will be the easy tier, and you'll find the champions with the lowest mastery curve in this tier. Second tier will be medium, third tier will be hard, and then all the way up to the expert tier where you'll find champions that are almost impossible to actually master, even with thousands of games played. And remember, if you're learning new tech, broken builds, or macro and wave control strategies from videos like this one, you're already behind. By the time something hits your feed, it's either about to get patched or everyone already knows about it and knows how to play around it. That's why whenever our challenger players discover new tech, broken builds, or game-winning strategies, they post it immediately in our new meta updates course, weeks before you'll ever see it on YouTube, Reddit, or TikTok. For instance, AP Varus top lane was broken from the very first patch of the season, but the average player didn't discover that until a few weeks later, while SkillCapped subscribers were notified before everyone else. As a SkillCapped subscriber, you're also getting access to our newly updated essentials courses covering everything you need to master season 16 from fundamentals to advanced macro, or even our bronze to diamond series where we document hard stuck players using only our content to climb through every rank. And if that wasn't enough already, every single week our challenger players upload 10 full game commentaries breaking down exactly how to carry games on your chosen champion in your Elo, explaining every decision in real time. Best of all, you can get access to all this and more completely risk-free. As if you don't climb at least five divisions while actively using our service, then you get every penny back. Check out the link in the description below for an exclusive one-time discount completely risk-free today. So, we're going to start off with top lane, and then work our way down to bot, and first let's have a look at the top lane easy tier. So, there's not a whole lot of complexity when it comes down to these champs as they have very basic trading patterns and not a whole lot of ways in which they can optimally play fights. For example, Nasus doesn't have multiple combos that he has to use depending on the situation. He literally just boxes his opponents with his Q, slows them with W whenever it's up, and tosses E down when it's available. To master Nasus is more about your general game knowledge and being able to split push effectively that takes the champ to another level and not so much anything specific from his kit. You could argue that Malphite may deserve a slightly higher difficulty ranking than the other picks we have here just because there is a decent ceiling in regards to target selection with his R in team fights. I'm sure we've all had that Malphite player on our team who just whiffs his R on nobody or alts the tank instead of the carry. So, mastering him may be slightly more difficult than the likes of Garen, Nasus, or Mundo. However, we still think it's relatively easy compared to the majority of top lane picks. For our medium-tier top lane champions, this is where the majority of the champions lie as most of them are in and around that middle ground. So, take Urgot for example. Definitely not all that difficult to master, but when compared with someone like Nasus, there's more nuance that goes into playing an optimal Urgot. Positioning in fights so that you can get maximum DPS with your passive, use your R at the correct time and on the correct target, toggling W on and off in different situations for max DPS. So, even though it's really not that hard to find a baseline level of success with the Urgot when you first pick him up, actually getting the most out of the champion's kit will take you a bit more time than the easy tier picks. Same thing goes for Sett. The way in which you combo with Sett is pretty basic, but stuff like knowing when to activate your W in a fight, using W flash to one-shot carries, and choosing which target to R in a grouped up team fight all make Sett slightly more difficult than easy tier. The hard tier is where we get into champions who have more advanced mechanical aspects of their kits or very unique elements that no other champs have. Rumble is a good example of a champ with a more unique element as he's got his passive heat that he needs to play around. Although, you could probably first time Rumble and be able to play him at an impactful level, really mastering the champion to its limits is not easy. Maximizing Rumble's heat so that he receives max DPS while overheating at the correct time in fights is a skill that takes a while to learn. If you don't manage your heat correctly, then you'll either be missing out on loads of damage or completely put yourself out of the fight by overheating. Singed is a really good example of a champion with a lower skill floor, but a higher skill ceiling. Since Riot gave Singed the minion execute if his Q poison is ticking on them, it's made it way easier to find a baseline level of success with Singed. Just look at his win rate in bronze and silver. He's constantly the highest win rate champ for those ranks, which goes to show that it doesn't take a super skilled player to win with Singed. At the same time though, actually mastering Singed to his limits is a lot harder than it may look. You've got the ability to proxy on Singed, and knowing how to do this effectively to avoid certain matchups is really key to maximizing the champ's limits. Proxying is even more powerful in season 16 as well, because if the enemy tries to stop you, then they lose progress towards their role quest, whereas you do not. So, it's a really important skill to learn on Singed. And then of course, we have picks like Irelia, Riven, or Fiora, who have so much outplay potential due to the many ways in which they can approach fights. For example, with Irelia, target selection is super important. The timing in which you choose to use your stun is really key. Hitting a multi-man R and then dashing around to get resets from Q so that you can dodge skill shots is extremely difficult. So, from a mechanical standpoint, many of these champs have very high ceilings. And then we've got League's newest champion, Zeri, placed in the hard tier, but you could argue that he should be in medium. The reason we have Zeri placed in the hard tier isn't because he's very difficult mechanically, but because of how playing around his passive stacks is going to change with every game and every fight. Choosing the correct moment to dive into fights, knowing exactly how many passive stacks you're going to get if you dive in during that moment, and just overall maximizing the potential of Zeri's passive from game to game has a pretty high skill ceiling. And then for the expert tier for top lane, we've got three champions being Yasuo, Kled, and Gangplank. There's so much that goes into playing an optimal Yasuo as being able to team fight efficiently if you don't have any knockups from your teammates is really hard to do. Optimal wind wall usage in fights, dashing through minions as fast as possible to close the gap on targets, all the unique combos that he can use depending on the situation. There's really just limitless amounts of things you can do to improve at Yasuo, and truly mastering him is incredibly hard. Kled takes a spot in expert tier, not so much because of the fact he's difficult mechanically, but because of his unique mounted and unmounted forms and the fact that taking each form to its limits has a really high ceiling. Knowing exactly how much damage you need to deal to remount, knowing if you have enough health to win a 1v1, you just need a lot of games on Kled to really understand what you can and cannot do in different situations, so he's super hard to master. And then for Gangplank, his triple barrel combo alone is one of the most difficult combos to learn in League. Using his barrels to their absolute limits is something that very few players can do, and it's just such a unique ability. So, there's no transfer of skill from playing top lane as a whole. You have to play so much of Gangplank himself in order to get the most out of him. Moving on over to jungle now, if we start off with the easy tier, we have a bunch of different champs. Rammus is your prototypical easy tier jungler as he doesn't have any crazy combos. The way in which he looks to approach a fight isn't going to be much different in each game, and his clear is not hard to do optimally. If Rammus is the easiest of easy, then someone like Nocturne you could argue for a slightly higher difficulty rating, but placing him in medium still feels too high. With Nocturne, your spell shield does have of a skill ceiling saving it for an impactful spell in a skirmish can often be more optimal than just spamming it as soon as you enter a fight. Target selection with Nocturne R is important, but you could honestly say that for most dive junglers, so it's not something that's unique to him. The simplicity in the way you look to combo your spells is something that a lot of these champions have in common. There aren't multiple different ways in which you can combo with Mundo, you just toss your Q and follow with E when you get in range. Trundle just smacks enemies with his Q. Amumu is always Q for engage followed up by E. So, even if you play a ton of games on these picks, there's not massive room for improvement to many other junglers. The medium tier for jungle is going to feature champs who are still on the easier side, but often have one aspect in their kit that makes them slightly harder to master. Master Yi is a good example because even though he's relatively easy mechanically, the way in which you choose to use your Q and W during fights is something that has a bit of a skill ceiling. You don't always want to use your Q right as you enter a fight because saving it to dodge a key spell can be way more impactful. For example, if you are skirmishing against an Echo as Master Yi and the Echo throws down his W, then you ideally want to wait to use your Q for when Echo's W stun is coming down so that you can dodge the stun entirely. Just little things like this to where depending on the enemy comp, you're going to shift how you play, which gives Yi a higher skill ceiling than the easy tier picks. Warwick is another champ who a lot of players may be surprised is not in the easy tier, but because of how Warwick gains in power to lower heat drops on health, it provides a ceiling to a skirmish that isn't easy to master right away. You've definitely got to get your games in on Warwick to understand his limits in skirmishes and how powerful he becomes when he's on lower health. The hard tier for jungle is going to consist of a mixed bag of champions that are either more difficult to master mechanically, they have unique ways in which they clear their jungle, or they have a unique part of their kit that no other champion has to offer. Kindred is one of those picks with a unique part to her kit as marks are something that no other jungler has to focus on. Kindred's marks just makes it so that she has this separate mini game that she's got to focus on and it really adds to the skill ceiling of the pick as you already have to be focused on so much as jungler, but this just adds a whole 'nother layer. And then just being a ranged jungler in itself gives Kindred a higher skill ceiling than most junglers because you've got to be able to position well and kite effectively to be most impactful on the champ. Bel'Veth is a jungler whose skill ceiling is mainly derived from the mechanical skill required to master her. The fact you've got four dashes with Q means you have so many different ways in which you can use the spell in fights. Choosing which way to dash, whether you should be saving a dash or not, understanding exactly how much ground you can close with each charge are all things that will take you quite a bit of time to optimize. And then for the jungle expert tier, we have four champions being Ivern, Elise, Nidalee, and Viego. So, starting with Ivern, Daisy micro-management is one of the most unique mechanics in the game and has a really high skill ceiling. Because of this, you're going to need to spend a ton of time on Ivern in order to reach his max potential. For Nidalee, the fact that she is so reliant on landing a skill shot to be most effective makes her really difficult to master. Playing mechanically perfect in skirmishes is just so difficult with Nidalee that even with thousands of games played, you'd be hard-pressed to have an absolutely flawless game. Elise, with her ability to tower dive so effectively early on, gives her quite a high skill ceiling. Knowing how many tower shots you can soak for your team, recognizing a dive play in the first place, and then executing on it with your teammate is not easy to master in the slightest. And then for Viego, you literally need to know what every single champion in the game does in order to play him optimally. If you dive in on a Vel'Koz, end up getting a kill, pick up your passive, and are now playing as Vel'Koz, if you don't know how to angle his Q to extend the range and finish off a kill, then you can just miss out on a potential game-winning kill. Viego may have the highest skill ceiling for any champion in the entire game because of his passive. Moving on to mid lane, the easy tier is going to consist of champs who have very linear ways in which they can approach fights. If you want to go in with Fury, then you're always going to use R on your target, follow with Q and E, and then use W to reposition. There isn't anything complicated about that, and it adds for a very low skill ceiling. Same thing with Annie, the majority of fights you're just looking for that R stun on a priority target, and will follow up with your Q and W. Nothing too complicated at all, and the only thing you may change from time to time is whether or not you lead with your R or just simply use Q or W if you feel like R would be overkill. Even if you put thousands of games in on Malzahar, often times the most optimal thing for you to do in a fight is going to be to simply press R on a fed carry. Whether you have two games or a thousand games on Malz, the player with two games isn't going to play any differently if Yasuo is diving on them in a late game fight. So overall, there's just much less skill expression from these champions, and you can't exponentially increase your skill by putting in thousands of games. For the medium tier mid laners, many of these champs are actually pretty easy from a mechanical standpoint, but tend to require more game knowledge in order to get the most out of them. Kassadin is a good example of a champ who doesn't require any crazy combos to be effective, but knowing how to play macro in the mid game is a lot more important on someone like Kass than it is on Malz or Annie. With Malz and Annie, the most optimal thing for you to do in the majority of games is to group up and team fight, but with Kassadin, his side lane threat is incredible. So in order to master him, you need to know how to play through the side lane effectively. Veigar is a relatively easy champion to master, but when compared to the easy tier champs, there's a bit more that goes into mastering him. Optimal E placement is something that even Veigar players who have hundreds of games on him won't use absolutely perfectly in skirmishes. Everything else about the champ is pretty linear, but E usage is going to take you a good amount of games to master. Most of these champs are similar to Veigar where they have one part of their kit which takes a good amount of time to master, but everything else is pretty simple. For the mid lane hard tier, this is where we get into champs who have very unique elements to their kits or champs that have many different ways in which they can approach fights. Orianna is a good example of having a unique element in her ball tracking. Being able to keep track of exactly where your ball is in a fight, constantly repositioning it to zone, and having it in the correct spot to maximize the potential multi-man R are all things that take a good chunk of time to master. The decision to use E on a teammate as opposed to keeping your ball on the ground also has massive implications on later game fights and it can often be really hard to choose which is most optimal. Twisted Fate is a champion who has a pretty low skill floor but a very high skill ceiling. The way in which you choose to use your ultimate in the mid game is a skill that takes a while to learn. You need to be so aware of what's happening on the entire map at all times while also staying focused on the lane in front of you. So that alone gives TF a higher skill ceiling than most mid laners. Expert tier for mid lane is going to revolve around four champions being Vel'Koz, Akshan, Yasuo, and Azir. For Akshan, what makes his skill ceiling incredibly high is his E. Akshan E is one of the most unique spells in the entire game and truly mastering how to use it the most optimally around all sorts of terrain will take you hundreds of games. Vel'Koz's Q is one of the highest skill gap spells in the entire game. All the different angles in which you can use the spell to hit an enemy gives you so many different ways in which you can approach fights. There are just so many kill opportunities awaiting for Vel'Koz throughout the game if he can hit an angled Q, but it really depends on how mastered the Vel'Koz player is as to whether or not they will be able to capitalize on that kill. Purely from a mechanical standpoint, Yasuo is one of the most difficult champions to master in League. There are so many different combos that you can use with Yasuo depending on the situation and choosing the correct one at every single moment is extremely difficult to do. And then for Azir, he could honestly be an expert plus tier on his own as there's just so many ways in which you can approach fights which gives him a very high skill ceiling. The fact that Azir has the ability to either dive the backline himself or sit in behind his team and be a safe DPS threat gives you many options to think about. You always have to be pondering whether or not now is the right moment to try and dive in and catch out the enemy squishy, and then actually executing on him mechanically is a whole another story. Just micro-managing Azir's soldiers and moving them around in the optimal spot with Q during fights also adds to Azir's infinite skill ceiling. Heading down to the bot lane now, if we have a look at the easy tier for ADC, we've got Miss Fortune, Sivir, and Smolder. MF is a no-brainer in this tier because regardless of how many games you have played on the champ, you're looking to do the same thing in every fight, which is press R. Pressing R in the correct moment and at the correct angle does take a bit of time to learn, but relative to all other ADCs, Miss Fortune is one of the easiest champions to master. Smolder is also quite easy to master because he has his point-and-click damage coming out of Q, and it's really just the way in which you position throughout a fight that's going to differentiate an average from a mastered Smolder. Since a lot of Smolder's damage comes from weaving in his point-and-click Q, it makes his skill ceiling a lot lower than the majority of ADCs who are very reliant on auto weaving to deal max DPS. And then Sivir is pretty simple to master because she literally has a spell within her kit that makes positioning way easier thanks to her R. So, while many other ADCs can get punished really hard for making a small misstep in a fight, it's very easy for Sivir to correct those while she also has the spell shield from her E. So, getting the most out of her in a team fight is easier than on a lot of other ADCs. Medium tier for ADC is going to consist of pretty straightforward champions who are a little more reliant on auto weaving and positioning than the easy tier champs. With MF and Smolder, the optimal way to play them in fights often doesn't revolve around constant auto weaving, and with Sivir, you don't need to worry as much about positioning because of your R movement speed. Jinx, Tristana, Ashe, Kog, and Twitch are all super reliant on immaculate auto weaving to play them optimally. While their positioning in fights is also super key, minus Trist since she has the gap closer. Varus and Corki are a bit more skill shot reliant than most ADCs, so even though they aren't too complicated, it will take you more time to master them than our easy tier picks. For Kog'Maw and Twitch, we did consider placing them in the hard tier, but the only thing that's truly difficult about them is positioning well in fights as they aren't very hard to play from a mechanical standpoint. If we transition right over to the hard tier, we find picks like Zeri and Xayah who, relative to Twitch and Kog, require way more mechanical skill to master while also requiring excellent positioning in fights due to their shorter ranges. Jhin is a very unique ADC in the sense that he's very skill shot reliant with his R and the way in which you use your R in fights has a very high skill ceiling. Playing mind games with opponents by holding your R charge for longer to force them into a predictable pattern, holding R for if your teammate has some CC they're about to use, just choosing when to R in a fight as opposed to auto attacking, are all things that provide Jhin with a more exponential mastery curve. Nilah, Samira, Kai'Sa, Vayne, and Xayah are all relatively shorter ranged ADCs, so this magnifies the importance of positioning in fights even more than on most ADCs. In medium tier, we had Jinx, Twitch, Kog, and Trist who all have range enhancers in their kits, which [music] makes mastering them a bit easier. Nilah and Samira in specific are such unique ADCs, and being shorter ranged, the timing in which you decide to go in during a fight is so much more important. Mastering team fighting with Nilah and Samira is just so much harder than most ADCs because of the fact that you have to be so close up in order to be most effective. And then rounding things out with the expert tier, we've got Kalista, Draven, and Ezreal. So, Ezreal being entirely skill shot reliant for the majority of his DPS, along with all the different angles that you can find in fights by repositioning with E, makes him a champ that is nearly impossible to master. watch some of the best Ezreal players in the world play him and then you go into your solo queue games and watch an Ezreal on your team, that's going to say enough as to how hard he really is to master. Kalista, with her passive hop mechanic, is always going to have an extremely high skill ceiling. Just getting used to this mechanic alone is going to take you a ton of games, and then mastering it within skirmishes, hopping in the correct direction, and kiting perfectly in fights is really difficult to do. And then Draven, with his axe mechanic, has a very steep mastery curve. All other ADCs just have to track their their in fights, but Draven has to do this on top of tracking where his axes are landing. So, it literally adds another layer of focus that no other ADC has to deal with. So, if we round out the video by looking at the support role, we've got a handful of champs in the easy tier. When it comes down to hook supports, I don't think it's much of an argument to say that Nautilus is the easiest. The thing that lowers Nautilus' skill ceiling compared to other hook supports like Thresh, Blitz, and Pyke is the fact the hit box on his hook is super wide combined with the fact that he can just point and click R from range on an enemy. And then once the CC from R lands, he gets to follow with a guaranteed hook. This is their bread and butter combo for Nautilus in skirmishes as R followed up by Q takes no skill to use it all and it's often the most optimal combo to use. Yuumi is a bit of a weird one to rate on this list because her skill floor is really low, but one could argue that her skill ceiling is actually higher than some of these easy tier picks. Things like hopping off your ADC to block damage in skirmishes or using R at the correct moments and at the correct angle in fights aren't actually that easy to grasp right away. So, we could definitely see the argument for her being a medium tier skill ceiling support. Medium tier is where the majority of support champions are going to fall as most of these champs just have slightly higher ceilings than the easy tier picks. It's not like Braum is all that more difficult than Nautilus, but stuff like hitting Q's consistently, using W on minions to set up plays, Q flashing to find picks, and timing E usage in fights to block the most amount of damage are things that provide a somewhat higher skill ceiling. Braum just has a little more that he has to be thinking about during skirmishes as it's not just, "Oh, I look to land my CC on a priority target." and more so, "Okay, I've got to land my CC, but then I also have to focus on being in the right position to block damage for my team." In a vacuum, Sona is an enchanter that may seem very simple and her skill floor isn't that high, but using her passive most optimally in fights is what leads to her having a higher skill ceiling. Same thing with Lulu, if you just throw all your spells down on your carry in a fight, then you're going to perform just fine, but it's optimizing whether you save W to polymorph a dive threat or use it on your carry and choosing the correct decision in a fight that leads to Lulu being a little more difficult than someone like Nami or Lulu. The hard tier for support is going to consist of champions that tend to have a lot more play making potential in their kits, which leads to them being more difficult to master. Blitz, Renata, Rakan, Neeko, and Pyke all thrive off of being able to recognize small windows within a skirmish and then immediately diving in to punish the enemies missteps. This alone leads to them having higher skill ceilings because you need a pretty high level of game knowledge in order to get the most out of them. For instance, the best play for a Lulu or a Soraka in a team fight is almost always going to be to sit in behind their carries and peel for them. There often isn't anything more optimal for them to be doing, so this takes a lot of mental burden off you. With someone like Rakan or Renata though, they not only have the ability to dive the back line with flash plays, but they also have great peel strength. So, understanding how to get the most out of their kits for each specific team comp that you're playing into is not easy to master. Nami is an enchanter that you could argue should be in the medium tier instead of hard, but there is quite a big gap between an average and a mastered Nami player. Bubble optimization and correct R usage in fights is something that the majority of average Nami players fail miserably at getting right, and the ceiling of using both spells correctly is definitely quite high. So, Nami would be one of those picks where we think her skill floor is quite low because you can still achieve a baseline level of success with her by just using W and E on teammates, but the skill ceiling of Nami is higher due to Q and R optimization. And to round out the video, the expert tier for support is going to feature Taric, Thresh, Vel'Koz, Bard, and Elise. So, what makes a lot of these champions more difficult to master is that they have at least one spell in their kit that has an infinite skill ceiling. Vel'Koz, Bard, and Taric are these three champs as Vel'Koz Q is one of the most difficult spells in the game to master. Bard with his R and even his Q provide a massive ceiling while Taric has his ultimate. Taric's ultimate is one spell that is so difficult to min-max in a fight because choosing the correct time to use the spell is really difficult. One second too late and your teammate could die, but one second too early and the enemy team just retreats as they don't want to fight with Taric R coming down. Actually using Taric R to its limits in every single fight is pretty much impossible, so the skill ceiling to the champ is infinite. For Thresh, he's a support who has so many ways in which he can approach fights. Not only does he have a skill shot that he has to land, but he's got to decide whether or not he should be sitting back and peeling for his carry or trying to dive in and catch out the enemy carry. Maximizing Thresh's kit requires more game knowledge than most supports because of his ability to shift how he positions and what his priority is in fights based on compositions. If you're an ADC main, I'm sure you've all had that one Thresh on your team who just constantly tries to dive in and find hook plays while in reality he should be sitting back more and giving you lanterns so that you can play up more aggressive as a fed ADC. And then for Elise, she's a super unique support because her early game roam power and dive strength is unmatched by most supports, which requires more split-second decision-making. Choosing the correct times to roam, recognizing dive plays, and then executing on the play are all things that you need to get good at to master Elise, which gives her a very high skill ceiling. All right, guys, one last thing, our rank up guarantee is insane. It's like signing up for the gym and getting a refund if you don't get ripped. That's how confident we are in Skillcapped. We obsess over making the best guides with top players, rigorous testing, and top-tier video editing to make your climb easy. If you're ready to level up, visit skillcapped.com and see the difference. So, there you have it, everyone. Those are our rankings of the most difficult champions to master for season 16. Thank you all so much for watching. We'll see you in the next one.

titre: A character design hot take for every single League of Legends champion

champion and now i'm done i've done a character design hot take about every single league of legends champion sometimes in multiple parts because even on a short channel i cannot keep things brief anyway some people asked if i'd make a compilation video collecting all of them so i did and here it is i guess good god that was a lot of shorts [Music] aatrox has a really solid i have no mouth and against his surroundings desperate to find some way to either end the world or end himself but his design doesn't really express the horror of his existence instead he looks like a somewhat painfully generic big red angry demon man that you might see in any generic fantasy setting and i think going hard on body horror showing his body as a flesh puppet attached to a possessed sword that is continuously drinking blood to remake its physical vessel which is what he is would have made him truly unique riot unfortunately stuck too close to the generic original when they remade him he has a great concept but he ends up falling a bit flat my grade c subscribe to this channel for more character design hot takes or head on over to my main channel to get long form videos that discuss these subjects in detail ari began her life as a stereotypical succubus seductress draining the spirit from her victims but over time evolved into a story of addiction she's addicted to the draining but understand her own nature and addiction her design uses the same cliche boring standard hot fantasy babe body that every other hot fantasy babe in every game uses especially in league of legends which kinda sucks but since ari is meant to be a seductress at least it makes some sense as an expression of her character history her base design and outfit are broadly speaking fine but the darker more feral version they've created for the ruined king game is to me much more appropriate to the current state of her story and who she is as a character my grade b subscribe to this channel for more
