titre: Yunara Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Yunara’s been doing her job for a long time, but centuries of standing guard in the spirit
realm have only sharpened her resolve And her aim In game, Yunara is an ADC for ADC’s with a focus on core marksman skills
like spacing and orb blocking She's all about ramping up power and
unleashing it at exactly the right time So pour yourself some tea
and brush up on your ancient Ionian It's time for the Yunara Champion spotlight The time has come to prune the tree Up first Yunara’s passive, Vow to the First Lands Her critical strikes
deal extra magic damage And... no that's it that’s the entire passive Yunara’s Q is Cultivation of Spirit She gains stacks of unleash
by attacking enemies. At max stacks, Yunara can activate Q to- You guessed it, unleash for a few seconds She gains attack speed, bonus
damage on hit and deals splash damage in a small area with each attack.
Unleash stacks decay quickly out of combat so Yunara wants to stay in the fray
to stay at her strongest Next, W is Arc of Judgment Yunara sends a swirling prayer
bead out, which damages and slows enemies as it travels It slows down any time it hits a target,
then lingers and expands at its terminus, continuing to damage enemies
who don't get out of the way For E, Yunara activates Kanmei’s Steps,
which grants her a quick burst of move speed to chase down
enemies, reposition, or dodge skill shots. Now let's focus on her ultimate. Transcend
One’s Self. With R, Yunara taps into her full power, entering a
transcendent state for several seconds That enhances all of her basic abilities
for the duration Her Q, Cultivation of Spirit
instantly activates and empowers her attacks for as long as she's transcended,
which is a long duration upgrade Her W, Arc of Judgment becomes Arc of Ruin Instead of a swirling prayer bead,
she fires a beam of spirit magic that slows and deals
high damage in a line Finally, her E Kanmei’s
Steps, becomes Untouchable Shadow She gains a short dash instead of movement
speed, and the ult cast resets the cooldown. She can dive out of danger
or right into the fight. When Yunara transcends oneself,
her enemies don't have a prayer Like many other AD carries, Yunara wants to play safe until she finds
the right moment to get aggressive She's truly powerful with a fully stack Q.
If Cultivation of Spirit is ready to activate, it might be a good time to fight. An even
better time to fight? when her ult is up. Her mobility is pretty limited
most of the time, so opponents want to lock her down
before she goes all super Kinku mode Positioning is key here All that potential power means nothing
if she doesn't live long enough to use it Yunara is meant for you
ADC purists Her ramping power might feel familiar
if you like playing Ashe or even Zeri Basically, anyone who likes decimating
enemies with a relentless barrage of autos Find out what more than 200
years of Spirit Realm experience
looks like with Yunara The Unbroken Faith Perseverance, patience, providence

titre: Naafiri Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Once a powerful force of chaos
and corruption, Naafiri was imprisoned in a dagger
and lost to the sands of Shurima... until now. She’s found terrifying new freedom
in a pack of dune hounds, possessing them in a never ending hunt. With canine and Darkin abilities at her disposal, her howl brings a horde of teeth,
claws, and blades, and they're hungry. Welcome to the Naafiri Champion Spotlight. "It’s lonely at the top of the food chain." Naafiri’s passive is We Are More. Naafiri passively spawns mini-me packmates that 
mirror her attacks and assist her abilities. Aw, what a good girl! Hitting champions with abilities, or last hitting anything,
reduces the cooldown of a packmate spawn. As Naafiri levels up, her spawn rate
and maximum ca-pack-city will increase. Each packmate helps Naafiri
last hit occasionally, has its own health, and can eat skill shots,
but not your homework. Thanks to Darkin corruption, Naafiri
isn't just the leader of her pack, she is them, all at once. Unlike other champions who command
their lowly minions or just go it solo, Naafiri and her packmates
truly play as one. She's got that dog in her...
and another dog, and another. And they love to make “fetch” happen. What a mean girl. Naafiri’s Q fires Darkin Daggers 
which damage and bleed all enemies hit. executing minions and small monsters
if they’re brought low enough. Packmates will swarm the first champion hit
in a deadly dogpile. "Tastes like terror." Naafiri can recast this ability
within a short window. If it hits a bleeding enemy, the remaining
bleed will be dealt instantly, plus missing health damage. And if the target is a champion,
Naafiri will heal. This ability gives the Naafiri quite
the edge in most lanes. She's got range
advantage over many other assassins. And of course, everyone tastes delicious
after a second dagger hit. When Naafiri is ready to roam in search of larger meals,
the minion execute will help her push quickly. Let us expand our feeding grounds! Mobility is an assassin's favorite treat. With Hounds’ Pursuit Naafiri briefly
channels before dashing to an enemy. Her packmates pounce after her
dealing extra damage for each puppy pal. Naafiri will chase her enemy within in a wide range. If an enemy Ezreal or LeBlanc
is trying to slip out of trouble, she won't give up that easy. But if her target gets very far away,
there's no hounds allowed. Thanks to this ability, Naafiri is the top dog when it comes
to diving onto squishy targets. But be careful. Other champions can block the impact
of her charge. Plus, while she's channeling her packmates
go untargetable, which leaves her open to all kinds
of pesky skill shots. Naafiri’s E, Eviscerate, lets her dash a short distance,
damaging enemies in her path. When she lands Naafiri explodes
in a swirl of steel. Her packmates are recalled and healed to full,
which is useful for leashing them out of a pinch. Naafiri can combine this dash with her W
to cover a lot of ground quickly, expanding her hunting grounds
to unsuspecting lanes. But take caution around champions with lots of CC
because they're looking to play dog catcher. Once Naafiri pops
both dashes to engage, she can't escape until someone's diced up. With a mighty howl Naafiri runs
wild with her ultimate, The Call of the Pack. In a flurry of blades and borks, she spawns bonus packmates to assist her. Each packmate also gets a health
and move speed boost, kind of like my dog when it’s dinnertime. Naafiri gains attack damage, out of combat
move speed, a burst of vision, and a shield when she
first attacks a champion. On her first takedown all those effects are refreshed,
making her paws-itively lethal. Of all the buffs on the Naafiri’s ultimate,
don't underestimate the vision boost. The better to see you with, my dear. It's a shortcut to creating the perfect moment
for exercising your killer instincts. If you reveal an enemy in your headlights,
like a fed ADC, use W to lock onto them and rush them down
with your full horde of hounds. Sigh, it's a dog eat dog world. Naafiri was born from the concept of a beginner assassin,
the ideal champion to learn the class. She's kind of like a Pantheon or Zed
who wants to get ahead quickly, then chomp down across the map
to grab an early win. For most assassins, the difference between a big play
and a fumble is sniffing out when to pick a fight. Managing your mini-mes and fighting around them 
is what sets good Naafiri players apart from the pack. Stubborn Aatrox, so driven to die. Creating the perfect conditions to go in for the kill 
is essential for any master assassin. After the Naafiri’s lessons, any pup
can grow into the pick of the litter. With the full force of her vicious vessels,
Naafiri reigns at the top of the food chain. Help the Hound of a Hundred Bites hunt down
her next meal at the links below.

titre: Most POWERFUL Characters in League of Legends Lore Explained
transcription: Fiddlesticks. Fiddlesticks is the primordial demon of fear. He is not a spirit or a ghost. He is the concept of fear itself. He is probably one of the oldest beings on this list, being born when the first breath of life screamed. He is known as the first of 10, the 10 being the 10 primordial demons. They are some of the most mysterious beings in Runeterra. Other demons like Evelynn or Atakhan have certain goals or desires, whereas Fiddlesticks is just a force of entropy. He can manipulate space and manifest anything that someone fears. Apart from this, there are no actual feats that show how powerful Fiddlesticks is. He hasn't killed any gods, Darkin, or aspects. There is one feat though that I find really interesting. In the game, Fiddlesticks has unique interactions with every champion. His voice lines are references to something that the champions fear. For example, when he interacts with Garen, this is what he says. GAREN, BROTHER, >> [groaning] >> PLEASE DON'T [clears throat] HATE ME. The reason why he says this is because in Demacia, mages are despised. They are usually executed or thrown in prison. Garen's uncle was also killed by mage, so Garen hates mages. The conflict here comes from Garen's little sister, Lux. He loves her more than anything, but she was born a mage. So, with this, we can see that Fiddlesticks knows what everyone fears the most. So, the voice line that I find particularly interesting is the one that he has with Kindred. Let's listen. Gray man. We will cover Kindred separately later in the video, but for now, the gray man that Fiddlesticks mentions refers to death, Kindred's initial form. Death comes for everyone, and the thing that it fears the most is being alone. If everyone dies, then death has no choice but to be alone. Let's listen to one of Kindred's voice lines now. Lamb, tell me a story. There was once a pale man with dark hair who was very lonely. Why was it lonely? All things must meet this man, so they shunned him. Did he chase them all? He took an axe and split himself in two, right down the middle. So, he would always have a friend. So, he would always have a friend. The significance of Fiddlesticks' interaction with Kindred is that it shows us that even death has its own fear. This means that the extent of Fiddles' power can also touch death in a way, something that no other character on this list can do. Lissandra. Lissandra is the ice witch. She lives in the Freljord and is thousands of years old. She has two sisters, Avarosa and Serylda. At one point, Lissandra was attacked and blinded by Volibear. This, however, was a blessing in disguise. Lissandra was no longer able to see, but was able to access the dream plane. This is how she established a connection with the watchers. These are extremely powerful extra-dimensional beings from the void. Lissandra was able to make a deal with them. In exchange for granting her and her sisters immortality, they would prepare Runeterra for the arrival of the void. Her sisters thought that this is actually not the really good deal. If the watchers came, they would destroy the world. There's no point in being the ruler of nothingness. So, they asked the demigod Ornn for a favor, to build a prison for the watchers. This is how the Howling Abyss came into existence. In the end, Lissandra also thought it's not a good idea to bring the watchers into the world, so she betrayed them, sealing them in true ice under the Howling Abyss. She now keeps watch over the watchers, ironically, and is preventing their release and the end of the world. So, what are Lissandra's powers? Well, she is a true iceborn, which makes her immortal and able to resist any type of cold. She is also capable of wielding and manipulating true ice. True ice is magical and can never melt. Lissandra is also a powerful and knowledgeable witch. She can enslave other beings with her will and enter their dreams. This way, she can manipulate memories and even rewrite the history that people remember. This may not sound like much, but she can enter the dreams of the watchers, which is no small feat. Speaking of the watchers, the true power that Lissandra holds is that without her, all of Runeterra would get destroyed by them. She now seeks to find the heart of blue, a mysterious artifact that can help create more true ice and keep the watchers at bay. Bard. Bard is the wandering caretaker. Bard has existed since before the creation of the universe. He is a wandering celestial who drifts like a note in an endless song. To some, he is the great caretaker. To others, he is a strange omen wrapped in floating bells and laughter, but his purpose never changes. Preserve balance, protect creation, and intervene only when the cosmic itself falls out of tune. He appears in places where reality grows unstable, where forgotten relics hum with too much power, or where reckless mortals awaken forces they cannot contain. Bard does not warn, negotiate, or threaten. He simply acts. A simple gesture or a quiet adjustment, and fate itself shifts back into harmony. Bard also has meeps that follow him around. These are celestial spirits that were created from the residual inspiration used to create the stars. They carry his tools, alter the world in his direction, and guide those who stumble into his path. Entire cultures have myths about Bard, the spirit who fixes what should not break, the wanderer who mends the stars, the protector who comes before disaster. Bard is not a warrior or a conqueror. His strength is quiet, cosmic, and precise. He can warp reality and avert catastrophes before mortals ever realized they were moments away from extinction. If Fiddlesticks is fear incarnate, then Bard is balance. Where the universe falters, Bard appears. When the balance is restored, he simply drifts away, humming the eternal song only he can hear. Kindred. Kindred is the living embodiment of death in Runeterra. Not a villain, not a savior, but the inevitable balance that everyone must face. They appear as a duo entity, Lamb and Wolf. When a soul reaches its final moment, Kindred offers a choice, acceptance or denial. If you come willingly, Lamb's arrow will end your life swiftly and peacefully. If you deny though, Wolf will hunt you down violently. Kindred's presence weaves into the mythologies of many cultures. To some, they are poetic and inevitable. To others, terrifying and unavoidable. Kindred does not kill indiscriminately. [clears throat] Instead, they respect the natural order, closing the cycle of life and death. They are drawn to souls who are meeting their end and those who are desperately trying to flee from it. When someone meets their end, whether on a battlefield or from a dying heart, Kindred arrives. When Lamb's arrow is loose or Wolf's jaw snaps shut, the soul moves on. And in the act, Kindred maintains the balance of life and death across Runeterra. Power-wise, Kindred's dominion is absolute. Death is not a power to be challenged indefinitely. In a world of war, magic, and chaos, Kindred stands as the ultimate constant. Soraka. Soraka is the star child. She is a celestial being who abandoned immortality to walk among mortals. She saw suffering and needless pain in the mortal world, and rather than judge from above, she descended to heal it herself. Villages tell stories of a gentle horned healer arriving on moonlit nights, leaving peace and life in her footsteps. To the dying, she is hope. To the desperate, she is light. To the wicked, she is forgiveness, even when they do not deserve it. Unlike other celestials, Soraka does not impose order or chase grand cosmic goals. She cares about the small things, a wounded traveler, a starving child, or a lost warrior who has forgotten kindness. Her compassion is her power, and it radiates like a quiet star in the darkness. Her magic is pure restoration. Each time she heals, she uses her own celestial essence. This is why in game, she loses health whenever she heals someone. Beneath her gentleness though, lies something vast. She has seen worlds rise and fall, watched stars be born and die. Still, she chooses to care for fragile mortal hearts. Soraka's strength is not destructive, it's transformative. She changes people by showing them a better way. In a world of gods, monsters, and endless war, Soraka stands as a reminder. Sometimes, the greatest power is simply choosing to heal. Aurelion Sol. Aurelion Sol is the star forger. Long before Runeterra ever existed, Aurelion Sol was sculpting the heavens. He is a celestial born in the beginning of creation. He can create stars and form entire galaxies. To him, stars are not miracles. They are art. Even though we know him as a big celestial dragon, his true form is something unimaginable that cannot be described. Once upon a time, Aurelion Sol returned to one of his earlier creations, but noticed that a world he had not created was orbiting it. This world was Runeterra. So, who created Runeterra? Well, that's a topic for another video. Let me know in the comments if you'd like to see that. Anyway, the aspects of Targon invited him to Runeterra to guide its inhabitants. Flattered by the offer, Aurelion descended in the form of a dragon. The Targonians named him Sol after their sun. To thank him for honoring their request, they gave him a golden crown. As soon as he touched it, though, he realized that it was not a gift, but a prison. The crown was imbued in magic so powerful that even he couldn't fight against. Now, with Aurelion Sol's power in their hands, the Targonians used it to channel power into a man-made structure, the Sun Disc. Aurelion Sol decided that when he escaped, he would take revenge on the aspects and destroy Runeterra for detaining him. To anger the Star Forger is to challenge the cosmos itself, and the cosmos rarely loses. Zoe. Zoe is the aspect of twilight. She looks like a little girl, but is actually thousands of years old. She was born as a human and chosen by the aspects to be one of their hosts. As the aspect of twilight, she heralds change. She embodies the moment between what was and what will be, a moment of upheaval. Just like in the real world, twilight is the moment before the day turns into night. Her arrival often marks turning points in history. Old orders crumble, new threats awaken, and cosmic forces shift. Essentially, if you see her, it means that something big is about to happen. Zoe is also chaotic and childish. She is friends with Aurelion Sol, and even though he is one of the most powerful beings in the universe, she treats him as a puppy. She can create miniature stars and blast them into her enemies. She can also teleport and travel between dimensions. Behind her playful grin lies undeniable truth. The universe bends around her whims. She is both harmless and utterly terrifying, a paradox wrapped in sparkles and laughter. Volibear. Volibear is ancient, older than mortal kingdoms, older than Freljord tribes. He is storm made flesh, the primal fury of nature incarnate. To many, he is the thunder's roar, the great storm, the primal god of war and survival. Legend says he and his demigod kin shaped the land long before mortals walked it. With a swipe of his claws, he carved fjords. In a titanic battle against the magma serpent, he forged rivers and mountains from its blood and corpse. For countless generations, the tribes of the Freljord worshipped him, offering blood and strength. Volibear led them into war, equipped with runic armor forged by his brother, the Divine Smith. But time and civilization softened many. Fields replaced hunts, walls replaced wild roaming. Volibear saw this as decay, weakness born out of comfort. He cast aside armor, discarded restraint, and embraced pure elemental rage. Now, he calls for a return to the old ways. He seeks to strip civilization away and resurrect the primal spirit of the land. To him, strength, blood, and struggle are what makes life meaningful. Civilization is a cage. Wilderness is the truth. In terms of power, Volibear is as close as Runeterra gets to an avatar of nature's fury. Storm magic, godlike strength, and near immortality so long as the old beliefs endure. The stronger the belief in Volibear, the stronger he gets. He does not just fight armies. He embodies unstoppable force, a tempest given form, and brings war into the bones of the land itself.

titre: K’Sante Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
K’Sante is a proud monster hunter of the top lane who transforms from a defensive tank to a lethal skirmisher. The city of Nazumah looks to him
for his courage, resilience, and deadly power. He is both protector and apex predator. Welcome to the K’Sante Champion Spotlight. "I'm done holding back!" K’Sante is all about finding the perfect moment
to prove your strength. So let’s start with his ultimate, All Out. Cast All Out to target an enemy champion. If they collide against a wall,
K’Sante knocks them through it. He’ll pursue his enemy to the end of their path, dealing damage and stunning them. If K’Sante’s prey doesn’t hit a wall, they’ll be knocked back a short distance instead, taking less damage. Whether his cast hits a wall or not, 
K’Sante shatters his ntofos to turn them into sharp blades, entering All Out form at the end of his charge. While he’s All Out, K’Sante gains attack damage
that scales with his armor and magic resist. He’ll also gain omnivamp, scaling with his health. But be careful, because all that power comes with responsibility. In All Out form, K’Sante is no longer a tank. He loses a large chunk of health, armor, and magic resist. All Out form expires after a brief period,
and K’Sante will be ready to tank once more. He can also re-cast his ultimate to exit the form early. K’Sante’s passive, Dauntless Instinct,
marks enemies that are damaged by his abilities. His next attack will consume the mark,
dealing bonus damage based on their max health. In All Out form, attacks against a marked target
will deal even more damage, scaling with K’Sante’s bonus armor and magic resist. K’Sante’s Q, Ntofo Strikes, slams his ntofos in a line, damaging and slowing all enemies hit. If this ability hits an enemy, K’Sante will gain a stack. At two stacks, his next cast of Ntofo Strikes will fire a shockwave, knocking all enemies toward him and briefly stunning them. In All Out form, Ntofo Strikes will no longer slow,
but its cooldown is reduced. "I will push them back, alone if I must." Use K’Sante’s W, Path Maker, to begin charging a dash. Release to dash in a line, knocking enemies to the end and stunning them. During the charge, K’Sante is Unstoppable and takes greatly reduced damage. Damage and knockback duration will increase with a longer charge. When K’Sante enters All Out form, Path Maker’s cooldown is refreshed. K’Sante gains even more damage reduction,
and his charge speed and dash speed will increase. Enemies will also take more damage, but they will not be knocked back, so be sure to knock ‘em out instead. Use K’Sante’s E, Footwork, to dash to an ally or target location. K’Sante will gain a shield that scales with his max health. When you can, it’s best to save your side-steps for your friends. Footwork’s range is much longer when cast on a teammate,
and K’Sante will grant the same shield to them as well. You can use Footwork to jump to allies that are over walls, but your untargeted dash won’t go the distance,
unless you’re in All Out form. Ntofo Strikes is your one-two-three punch for activating his passive damage. If you’re able to attack between each Q cast,
your opponent will crumble like the desert sand. Against stronger laners like Fiora or Darius,
K’Sante needs to play smart. Don’t let pride become your downfall.
Use Path Maker and Footwork to stay out of reach. Once K’Sante’s powered up, his mid game is pretty flexible. He’s a capable match for any 1v1 if he’s ahead,
so he’s free to split push and roam the map as needed. When it comes to teamfights, your primary job as K’Sante
is to protect a large group of very important people. Your team, of course. Stick with your back line and peel, and save your ult for when the best defense is a good offense. That’s your moment to go All Out on your prey. "I went All Out, you did not." All Out relies heavily on finding the correct positioning, since its impact is much greater when used against a wall. Use Path Maker and Footwork to back your enemy into a corner. But remember, transforming is a double-edged sword. Make sure you land in a good place to take the weights off,
or the hunter might become the hunted. A good leader knows the difference
between when to act and when to lie in wait. K’Sante will really shine if you’re up for a challenge
of decision making and mechanical mastery. From protecting his allies to tearing through the enemy:
he can do it all. Can you? K’Sante, the Pride of Nazumah, is here to finish every fight.
Learn his lessons at the links below.

titre: League's Original 40 Champions: Then and Now | League of Legends
transcription: when League of Legends released in 2009 a bundle of 40 different Champions came along with it 14 years down the line many of those Champions were drastically changed whether it being titre: Choosing a role in League of Legends
transcription: hello everyone as you may know picking a main role in league is one of the most important decisions a player can make having a main role in League means you dedicate yourself to learning all the ins and outs of a lane and mastering the class of Champions that get played there the most while it's not necessary to being a good player it does help to have a role you're best at that you can always fall back on I'll go over each role starting at the top of the map and talk about some of the role's responsibilities most common Champion types and some pros and cons keep in mind that some of this is based on my own opinions and experiences and is subject to change over time due to balance changes and shifts in The Meta not to mention the new season is starting in just a few weeks so take it with a pinch of salt all right so starting off in Top Lane top has a very and I mean very broad selection of Champions ranging anywhere from your usual tanks and bruisers like Mal fight and Darius to something as frustrating as vain or radiant virtue Karma there's no shortage of matchups for Top Lane and learning them all will take a long time but all the same it can be very rewarding for some players when it comes to Top Lane there really are no limits to what Champions you can play but what limits you as a laner is your ability to control minion waves and know when and where you're needed for example you say you're playing yoro Yori has a very strong Lane presence and simply can't be ignored meaning that his goal is to farm efficiently and push Lanes in order to keep pressure on the enemy team this is obviously a different play style from someone like Kenan whose job is more or less to hold down the lane until he's needed in team fights because of his strong ultimate a lot of what's expected from you as a top laner is often dictated by what Champion you pick making it very diverse and open-ended rooll let's go over the pros and cons and you'll notice that most of the pros and cons for each role go hand inand with each other or seem like a double-edged sword meaning that something seen as an upside for most players can also seem like a downside to others so keep that in mind going forward one top has a wide selection of Champions to pick from if not the entire cast you can basically play whoever you want and build whatever you want two there's no shortage of matchups to learn and improve your mechanical skills in game sense three Top Lane is an island so to speak meaning that you get solo EXP experience and are typically a higher level than the rest of your team to add of this you usually don't get ganked quite as much as the other Landes since there's only one objective on the top of the map obviously this is going to change with the addition of void grubs next season but we will talk about that another time now for the cons and you'll see what I mean about the double-edge sword one because of the wide variety of Champions you will often find yourself leaning not just against Champions you've never faced but also different builds it's this lack of consistency and fairness that make the lane so frustrating also a lot of your typical Top Lane Champions are considered Lane bullies and tend to snowball very fast when ahead making leaning phase pretty hard two while being an island can be nice it also means that your lane often gets ignored since there's only one real objective on top side there isn't always a good reason for your jungler to show up when you need help this also means that team fights are typically going to happen on the bottom side of the map and without teleport you won't be participating in them very often at least not until mid to late game three your success as a top laner mostly comes from fundamental skills like last hitting map awareness and Wave Control sometimes even with these skills perfected you find yourself against a champion that simply ignores him and finds a way to win regardless so if you want a role that's very isolated from the rest of the game and has basically no limits to what you can play Then Top Lane is a great place for you it allows you to focus on your mechanical skills and matchup knowledge without all the banter of showing up for team fights and objectives in the early game just keep in mind that there are a lot of unfun and sometimes unfair challenges that you will have to face often time without the help of your team all right so moving on to Jungle and man I feel bad for people that may in this role jungle has been through hell and bad with the amount of balance changes and updates to objectives it's had For Better or Worse junglers clear monster camps for Golden experience while finding opportunities to gang Lanes in order to get their teammates ahead as well as secure important objectives like Rift Herald Elemental Drakes and baron Nasher I'll try to keep this part short but I'm going to briefly explain what these objectives do and why are they so important so bear with me starting off with elemental Drakes Drakes spawn in a pit on the bottom side of the map and there are currently six types of Elemental Dres as well as the Elder Drake each Elemental Drake will give your entire team permanent stats when slain that more or less correspond to the element Elder Drake on the other hand only spawns after one team has slain four Drakes and instead grants you and your allies a short-term buff that executes enemies below a certain Health threshold making it a great way to close out games moving to the top side of the map we have the rift Herald or Shelly Rift heral spawns in the baron pit at 10 minutes and despawns at 20 she respawns twice and drops the eye of the heral which can be picked up by you or a teammate whoever holds the eye gets an empowered recall until they consume it which will then spawn Shelly who will run down the nearest Lane and deal mass of damage damage to any structures on the way this is a great way to either get your laner back into the game or push them even further ahead lastly we have Baron Nasher Baron spawns at 20 minutes and will respawn every 7 minutes after being killed taking down Baron is a team effort due to its high amounts of health and damage but will reward the team with a short-term buff that grants attack damage ability power and an empowered recall while also buffing nearby minions empowered minions are incredibly hard to kill which basically means your team becomes an Unstoppable pushing machine all right now that that's out of the way let's go over the pros and cons one one jungling in concept is relatively easy you clear camps gank and get objectives without the worries of last hitting wave management or trading with your laner two there's a good selection of Champions that can be played in the jungle that can suit your play style making it a great role for Flex picking to better help your team comp even if you don't have a lot of mechanical skill three you have an insane impact on the turnout of your games and have a lot of opportunity to dominate a match whether it's through ganks getting fed or just securing objectives now for the cons one everything is your fault and your team hates you that's putting it lightly if anything goes wrong whether it's your mid laner dying to a gank or you miss a Smite one time your team will not hesitate to Flame you two a lot of your golden experience in the current meta comes from killing camps meaning that even if you're fat and you have a bunch of kills you can still easily fall behind in levels if you don't manage your clear properly ganking too often or getting counter jungled can send you on a downhill spiral three the outcome of a lot of games very heavily depends on the jungler securing objectives is statistically one of the most important parts of a game about 62% of one games are ones where the the winning team secure the first Drake while it may be a pretty simple and laid-back role the importance of objectives is Paramount jungle definitely takes a specific kind of person and hopefully that person has their chat turned off for their own sake it doesn't take a lot of mechanical skill or Mastery of specific Champions to do well and with enough practice and game sense you can easily influence the outcome of your games even if you don't get credit for it all right it's time to talk about the star child of the rift in the most popular Lane by far mid lane mid is quite literally the center of the game boasting a selection of high skill cap champions typically being assassins Mages or something in between mid is a place to show off your mechanical skills in a high stakes 1 V one being in the center of the map means that you have the shortest route to everything whether it's objectives team fights or ganking SID Lanes mid lane Champions are usually high damage snowballer that win games by getting fed one-shotting carries and having a lot of map presence a lot of this impact is affected by your ability to communicate to your team when the enemy laner is roaming and counter roaming when needed when fights break out men jungle are usually the first ones to respond making them incredibly important to the outcome of a match with that said let's get get right into the pros and cons one mid is by far the most skill expressive Lane and can be very rewarding for players that like racking up kills and making big plays two mid has plenty of matchups to learn and some of the most fun and intuitive Champions to Master Three even if you aren't winning your lane you still have a big impact on the match one good roam or team fight can easily slingshot you back into the game cons one you will probably end up facing a lot of Hardcore one tricks that have millions of Mastery points on yaso Katarina yon Etc and Justice off as you stomp your lane you will probably end up getting stomped as well two there are a ton of different gank paths for mid lane making it very high risk especially if you don't have good map awareness three mid is the most likely to get counterpicked most mid laners want to pick last to avoid getting counterpicked since it has some of the most severe consequences so if you're looking to show off your skills and rack up your KD then mid would be the place to do it it's great Lan to master new Champions perfect your fundamentals and make game-winning plays now very similar to Mid let's talk about the wannabe star child ADC simply but the ADC job is to do as much DPS as possible from the safety of the back lines the role of ADC can be a little vague since you aren't required to play a marksman some people like to play artillery Mages or other kinds of Champions and that's not to say it doesn't work but most of the time a marksman is the right way to go due to their squishy nature Marksman kind of act as a glass Cannon having low mobility and dealing damage from afar while being very prone to getting one- shot by assassins and mages makes their positioning extremely important one wrong step and you can be taken out of a team fight in a matter of seconds leaving your team at a disadvantage marksmen have the benefit of doing most of their damage through Auto attacks alone which means they don't have to rely on coold Downs or Mana so as the name is say their primary function is to carry games pros of playing ADC one you have the potential to do the most damage in the game through very simple means most marksmen are very uncomplicated and easy to pick up for newer players since most of their damage comes from Auto attacks two playing ADC and actually carrying a game is a very gratifying feeling that just doesn't quite feel the same in other lanes at least in my opinion three the cast of marksman all have pretty unique and diverse play styles there's almost certainly a marksman for everyone to enjoy even if you don't usually play ADC don't forget you can always take your favorite Mages to bot Lane like zigs or velot cons one ADC is stuck in the super position of being the most and least important role in the game you have the potential to go 10 and0 and completely carry a game while having the exact same potential to go 0 and 10 and do absolutely nothing for a whole match two marksmen are some of the squishiest and least capable champions win alone despite being fed you will still lose a 1 V one to most Champions including supports not only do you have to rely on another person but you have to Lane against two other people it's very chaotic and someone usually dies before 5 minutes with all that said being an ADC main myself I think it's a role worth trying at least once it's paced a lot differently than the other lanes and has a very unique form of skill expression to the nature of marksman a lot of your performance depends on positioning and knowing your limits while relying on your team to carry you when you need it last and certainly not least we have support these poor players fly so far under the radar when it comes to getting Credit in games and usually the only time they're talking talked about is when their ADC is Flaming them that being said support is definitely the most accessible role for newer players since the responsibilities are very simple support your team and Ward that's pretty much it higher ELO Sports can make worlds of a difference in your game and you probably wouldn't notice luckily there are varying levels of skill expression for sport champions depending on how you like to play there's enchanters engage tanks Pickers Mages even assassins there's really no limits to what you can play as support as long as your ADC isn't running down mid lane then you're doing something right pros of playing support one since you have a pretty small impact on your games your job is pretty easy don't get me wrong supports can make or break a game in certain cases but most of the time you just get to sit back and relax with little room for error two most support builds are pretty simple and don't need to be changed around too much especially considering how complicated building items in League can be most of your utility and Buffs that you provide to your team come from your abilities and item passes making it hard not to be useful in some way three there's a million different ways to support whether you want to sit back and poke heal and Shield or be in the front lines diving turret at level three cons one you have little impact on your games when compared to the other roles no matter how well you do you will rarely be able to carry as a support you're extremely dependent on the rest of your team doing well in order to win games two something that I personally see as a con is the shift in the support meta over time in most games people would sooner pick serth Fela or any other kind of damage forward Champion rather than pick someone with high utility and healing which can make leaning phase pretty punishing for you and your ADC three just like ADC you're stuck in a lane with another person that you have to rely on unfortunately I think support players have it worse in this situation if your carry isn't doing well then there really isn't much you can do except play it safe until Ling phase is over so if you're new to the game and looking for an easy way in or if you just typically find yourself playing support in most games and it's a nice cozy role that definitely goes underappreciated don't let the cons deter you from playing it because whenever you need a break from solo lanes and want to take it easy it's always nice to play support even if it's not your best role all right well that's about everything I wanted to cover in this video and I hope some of you found it helpful if you agree disagree or think I left anything out put it in the comments below I'd like to know what you guys think anyways thanks for watching and I hope to see you in the next [Music] [Music] [Music] one

titre: Ranking EVERY CHAMPION From EASIEST To HARDEST - Season 15 - League of Legends
transcription: In today's video, we're going to be ranking every single champion from easiest to hardest. The video will be split up role by roll, and champions will be placed among four different difficulty tiers. Easy, medium, hard, and expert will be the four tiers that a champion can be placed in. How difficult a champion is can be interpreted in a few different ways. So, for the sake of this video, we're going to be focused on rating champions based on how difficult they are to master. So, in other words, which champs have the highest skill ceiling. So, an easy champion will take you very few games to fully grasp, while an expert champion will take you a larger sample of games before you can reach maximum potential. If we were to ask you if a certain champion is difficult to master and your answer to that is no, then the champion would likely be in easy tier. If your answer is kind of, they probably slot into medium tier. A yes answer would have them in hard, and an oh definitely answer would put them in expert tier. If you don't see a champion on the list for a certain role, then it's probably because they aren't played at a high enough rate. Champions who have play rates below 0.5% in a specific role won't be included in the video because it just wouldn't make sense to include things like Yumi top lane or Jonno jungle. And it's no secret that League's ranking system is designed to trap you. It pulls you in with those games where everything clicks and you carry hard only to balance it with frustrating matches that feel unwinable from the start. This cycle keeps you grinding, hoping for change, but never quite breaking through. We're the only service in this industry that has invested millions of dollars into identifying what actually works at every tier. What you're going to find in the easy tier are champs who don't require very much complex decision-making to play them optimally. Everything is pretty linear about these picks as nothing they do is really going to surprise you. If you think about it like this, that lack of outplay potential is something that makes a pick easier to master. There aren't any crazy combos that you can use or flashy plays that you can execute with these champs. So, mastering them doesn't take that much time. Of course, there are still some subtle things that you can do on these picks to express your skill, but relative to all other top lane champs, you don't need that many games to master them. So, for our medium tier top lane champions, we have a ton of different picks, as most champs are actually pretty easy to master, but just have one or two different nuances in their kits that make them a bit harder than the easy tier picks. So, for example, Set is a pretty straightforward champion for the most part. He doesn't have any crazy combos and his skill shots aren't that difficult to land. But getting used to the timing in which you use your W with set is something that will take a bit longer to master. With someone like Nasis who's in the easy tier, you're never worried about holding a spell for longer so that you can deal more damage or positioning in a specific way so that you can hit your spells. You just run up to the enemy, bonk them with Q, and that's pretty much all you got to do to maximize his potential. One champion you may be confused as to why he's in the medium tier and not in easy is Trindir. So your argument could be that both Nasis and Trinameir just auto attack their opponents to death. So, how is Trundameir more difficult to master? Well, Trinameir's R is what pushes him into the medium tier and separates him from someone like Nasis because it's going to take you a while to truly be able to minmax using the spell. The dive potential at level six is incredible with Trindir if you have the enemy chunked out, but if you don't know the exact amount of damage you deal against a certain matchup, then you can either be missing out on free dives or completely messing them up. With Nasis, you don't need as much pinpoint accuracy in terms of knowing your damage limits to get the most out of them. So, looking at the hard tier for top lane, most of these champions have more unique kits that will take you longer to learn. Warwick is a pretty good example of a difficult champion to master, but an easy champion to find a baseline level of success with. There's nothing mechanically too difficult about Warwick, as the majority of his damage is point-and-click. However, optimizing his passive is something that cannot be learned quickly at all. Warwick's passive makes him a more unique champion in the sense that he gets stronger when he drops lower on health. So, this incentivizes you to walk a very fine line. Knowing how much damage you deal and how much you can heal at certain levels and in certain matchups is something that will take a ton of games to master. To really get the most out of a top lane Warwick, you need to have extensive knowledge of your matchups. So, it's impossible to really master him without putting in hundreds of games. Rumble in the hard tier may come as a surprise to some, but in terms of true mastery, Rumble is more difficult. Correct passive usage with Rumble and actually mastering your heat usage with the champion is what puts him in the hard tier. Using R optimally is also something that's not easy by any means with Rumble and is a part of his kit that you can always look to get better at. Rumble R is kind of like bard R to where there's so many different angles and targets that you can look to use the spell on in fights. So actually using it in the most beneficial way possible is quite difficult to do unless the entire enemy team lines up for you in a choke point. And then moving on to the expert tier for top lane. These are the most difficult champions to master for the role. For Casante, the data heavily backs up the champion being more difficult to master as his one-trick win rate is 8% higher than his average win rate. Using W at the most optimal times, procking vitals in the most efficient way, and being able to succeed with Fiora in team fights are not easy to master in the slightest. Ambessa not only has a very unique kit due to the fact that she dashes after each spell usage, but her skill ceiling is quite high due to using energy and needing to consistently weave auto attacks between spells to get the most out of her passive energy refund. Correct R usage is also a more difficult aspect of Ambessa as it's going to throw you into the enemy backline. So, you need to understand your limits or else you'll just end up ining by using the spell and going in too deep. With Aurelia, she's not only mechanically intensive, but it's really difficult to master team fighting with the champion, especially if flash is down. Riven is kind of similar to Ambessa in the sense that your mechanical skill has to be on point to get the most out of her, as the different combos and auto attack weaving you need to execute during fights will take a while to master. Just being able to execute Gangplank's triple barrel combo consistently is something that makes him so difficult to master. And then Jace is a very feast or famine top lane champ who's got two different forms and is relying on hitting a skill shot to win trades. So you've got to be really skilled at Jace to make him work for solo queue. So making our way into the jungle now for the easy tier. We are going to have a bunch of less mechanically demanding champions who don't require any crazy decision-making to find success with. So, it's just more variance with Master Yi than someone like Trundle. So, let us know where you would rate Y on your difficulty list because there's definitely an argument to be made for him to be placed in the hard tier. So, looking at the hard tier for jungle, we have a bunch of assassins. Assassin junglers are going to be more difficult to master than most fighters because of how much more important each decision you make on them is in a fight. You get punished so much harder for misplaying on an assassin than you do on a fighter because in general, assassins are way more squishy. So, in order to really master assassin junglers, your game knowledge and decision-making needs to be on point. If you're super far ahead on a fighter jungler, then you can get away with so much more. But even if you're fed on an assassin, you can't just dive in all willy-nilly and expect to 1v3 fights. You have to be a lot more calculated with how you execute on assassins. Sho is a champion that we are torn between whether or not he should land in hard or expert tier. All the different tricks that you can use with his boxes give him a higher skill ceiling than most junglers. At the same time though, mechanically in a skirmish or when you are ganking preix, Shico isn't all that difficult to execute. So that's why we don't feel he's quite worthy of an expert tier rating, but the different mind games you can play with his R and the overall optimization of his boxes make him a harder champ to master. Ivvern is a champ some people may look at and think is quite easy to play, but this is where it depends on what exactly you mean by that. You can definitely be pretty effective on Ivvern with little gains by just throwing your shield on carries and fights, but that would be in reference to skill floor. And in this video, we're rating based on skill ceiling. Maneuvering Daisy around in fights is such a unique mechanic and one that takes a very long time to learn. So, the ceiling of Ivvern is pretty high, which lands him in the hard tier. So, having a look at the expert tier for jungle, you either need superb mechanical or macro knowledge to truly master these champs. With Elise, you need both strong micro and macro skill in order to play her optimally. As coordinating early dives, form management, repel timings, and cocoon accuracy are a few things that make her skill ceiling higher than most junglers. Even though nowadays it's not crazy to see solo Q players pull off insect kills, for a completely new player, it's still a more advanced combo and mastering Lee is going to take them way longer than the majority of junglers. It's mainly the macro side of things that slots Kindred into the expert tier, as properly playing around marks is not an easy task. In solo Q, you will see so many Kindred players completely throw games to greed for marks. So knowing when you should invade and when to just call off the mark requires a high level of game knowledge. By no means is Kindred easy mechanically either though, as kiting around and spacing well in fights along with using R in the correct moments are pretty difficult to master. Niti is similar to Elise in the sense that you need to utilize two different forms correctly. And then Niti being incredibly reliant on hitting a skill shot to be effective makes her super difficult to master. So for mid lane now, starting with the easy tier, these are champions we are confident you can pick up and master in a very limited number of games. None of these champions have any advanced combos, and it's pretty easy to be effective with them in fights. Mal and Annie, both having the point-and-click CC, make them really simple to execute mechanically, which is why they are so good for newer players because you can focus much more on improving your game knowledge at a faster rate. With Morgana, the way she plays lane is so simple as you just spam your W out to push, so less variance with the champ makes her quite easy to master. Nefiri is by far the easiest assassin mid laner in the game as she doesn't have nearly as demanding of a kit as the likes of Akali, Zed, or Katarina. With Vaggar, he has a very simple win condition and stacking up as much AP with his Q as possible. And his early skirmish really isn't that great. So, you can just heavily focus on farming and reach the mid to late game where you'll be oneshotting squishies with a Q into R combo. And then Malight isn't really played all that much as a mid laner, but he can be pretty effective in the role without needing a ton of games as you have one goal with Mal in every game, and that is to use your R on a priority target in a fight, which gives him a super easy game plan. And then for the medium tier midlaners, these champions are going to have something extra compared to the easy tier picks that you've got to focus on, which makes them a bit more difficult to master. So for Mel, she's a relatively easy mid-lane champion, but she is still a skill shot reliant champ, and her W is a spell that takes some skill expression to use at an optimal level. In order to use Mel's W most effectively, you need to have the game knowledge of what every spell on the enemy team does and how impactful each spell is. A new Mel player who's more inexperienced may just spam her W when champs are running at her. But if there's actually no threat of being caught out by CC or if the enemy has a more telegraphed CC ability, then holding W for a specific moment can be really vital. Pantheon can deal the majority of his damage from point and click. So why is he not on the easy tier? Well, in order to play Pantheon at his potential, your macro has to be a lot better than the easy tier picks. Since you have the semi- global with Pantheon's R, it just gives you more options as to how to play out the game. And choosing the correct time to use your R is something that will take a while to master. Just the literal placement of a Pantheon R in a fight is something that many newer Panthan players struggle with because it has a longer cast time. So you really have to get good at predicting where the enemy is going to be a few seconds later. Pantheon is also much more early game reliant than picks like Annie or Malahar. So your matchup knowledge and being able to win lane consistently on him is very key to winning with the champ. So, for the hard tier mid-lane champs, many of these picks either have some sort of unique mechanic in their kits, heavy reliance on macro play that you'll need to learn, or they're more punishable if your mechanics are not on point. Twisted Fate is a champ who, if we did a poll as to how difficult he is, there would likely be a wide range of votes. Reason being is that by no means is Twisted Fate hard to play from a mechanical standpoint in fights, as you're almost always looking to just pull out a gold card and stun up a priority target. It all comes down to Twisted Fates R, which makes him a more difficult champion to truly master. There are so many different ways in which you can use Twisted Fates R and knowing how to exploit the spell to its fullest potential is not easy. Should you be split pushing and then looking to flank with R? Should you simply use R to provide vision for your team as a key objective is spawning? Should you use R to backdoor the enemy team while they are trying to fight for Baron? This last one is something that really requires a deeper understanding of the state of the game. Because if you know for a fact that your team cannot contest an objective and all five of the enemy team members are there, often times just using R to start pressuring a side lane and trading towers for that objective is going to be the most positive outcome. Auction is a good example of a champion who has a more advanced unique mechanic in his kit that will take time to master. Auction's E is a spell with so much skill expression because knowing where to attach your E to that wall to get the most out of the spell in a skirmish is something that is extremely difficult to actually master. Oriana may not seem that difficult to master, but correct ball management in a team fight is not easy to do. Knowing whether you should throw your ball on a teammate or keep it on the ground is super difficult to grasp. Understanding the max range of your ball and spacing accordingly is also very key as oftent times newer Oriana players won't even realize that their ball is snapped back to them and they'll end up pressing R on top of themselves. We have just two champions in the expert tier for mid lane and they are Azir and Yasuo. Being able to play Yaso at his potential is something that very few players can actually do. Team fighting with the champion if your team does not have a single knockup is very difficult and it takes a lot of practice to be consistently impactful in those types of games. Mechanically, there's a lot to master on Yasuo as using E to escape through jungle camps, getting the most out of a wind wall in a fight and outplaying scenarios where you can dash through minions to dodge skill shots all require a high level of mastery after a team fight is over. Even if you end up coming out on top, there's usually something throughout that fight that you could have done differently when looking back at it. So, this makes for a very hard champ to master. On the macro side of things, being able to control your aggression with Yaso is something that many players struggle with. When you get a lead on Yaso, you can feel invincible. Being able to close the gap by using E through minions and constantly looking to threaten. But identifying when it's best to sit back and when you want a full send really differentiates an average from a mastered Yasua. Patch after patch, if you look at data for Azir's one-trick win rate compared to his average win rate, he's always got one of the largest gaps. It's usually around a 7% gap, meaning master Azir players are able to achieve a 7% better win rate than the average Azir. So why is this the case? Well, Azir has the ability to completely shift the way he plays fights depending on the situation. You can play him like an assassin and dive the enemy backline, or you can be sitting back and get DPS off from range. Most of the mid-lane champions don't have these different options as they are either wanting to always play from range if you are a mage or always dive a priority target if you're an assassin. So being able to identify how to play Azir in a team fight to get the most out of him and then mechanically executing on that adds a layer of skill expression that no other mid-lane champ has. Secondly, a lot of Azir's worth comes solely from his DPS value in fights and proper micromanagement of his soldiers. Optimal positioning and constantly weaving auto attacks to deal max DPS is not an easy task in the slightest. Most mid lane champs are relying on playing around spell cooldowns, so they have moments of downtime in fights, but Azir can have constant uptime in a fight due to his soldiers. So, you have to be so much more skilled mechanically to make him work. So, if we move on to the ADC role now, we have just three champions slotted into the easy tier being Civer, Misfortune, and Ash. So, with all three of these champs, there isn't a whole lot that separates a good player from a mastered player. For Misfortune, the way to be most effective in a team fight is by finding a multi-man R. And because of that, it's mainly just how you position in a fight that's going to dictate how impactful you are. Pressing R on a CCed enemy target takes no skill at all. And if we were to show you a clip of a high elo MF playing a team fight versus a low elo MF, it'd be way harder to spot the difference compared to an ADC that we have in the hard tier like Samira or Kai'Sa. With ADCs, of course, positioning is going to be vital regardless of who you're playing. So, it's mainly going to come down to how advanced or unique an ADC's kit is, which will dictate how hard they are to master. And Civer, Misfortune, and Ash all have very simple kits. For our medium tier ADCs, a lot of these champs are very simple to play mechanically, but there's usually more intricate decision-making that needs to be made in order to find maximum success. Let's take Twitch for example. Super easy champion from a mechanical standpoint as you're just auto attacking to deal DPS. But it's the way in which you can approach different situations with Twitch that make him more difficult than the easy tier champs. With Twitch's stealth, he has the option to either look for a catch play before a team fight even starts or just sit back and wait for his team to engage. Deciding when you open up from stealth is also something that takes a while to learn. So, the more options you have and the more ways you can play out of fight with a champ makes them harder to master. And this is why Twitch isn't in the easy tier despite being really easy from a mechanical standpoint. Tristana is kind of the same story. With Ash, Civer, and Misfortune, you don't have a gap closer. So, you don't even have the option to dive in and assassinate a target. With Tristana, you do have that option. So, very much like Twitch, even though Trrist is super simple mechanically, you have to make more difficult decisions on a macro level in order to reach her full potential. These hard tier ADCs just take things one step further as many of them have a much more unique style of play. Ma and Samira in specific both have completely different kits compared to all other ADCs as they actually want to play up in melee range to be most effective. So, this adds a whole new layer of skill expression because you have to be able to identify when it's best to sit back in a fight and when it's time to dive in. Almost every other ADC in the game is probably in a bad spot if they are up in melee range. But that's not the case with Nila and Samira. So, it's a whole different mindset shift with champs like Vain, Lucian, and Kai'Sa. The fact that they have dashes adds a layer of optimization that none of the easy tier champs have to worry about. Is it most optimal to dash forward in a fight? Should you be kiting to the side or straight back? Should you be holding your dash to dodge a skill shot? There are a lot of different decisions that you can make on Lucian, Vain, and Kaisa. So, choosing the correct one in all situations is quite difficult to do. And then for our expert tier for ADC, we've got three champs being Ezreal, Draven, and Kalista. So, with Draven, he's got a completely unique kit with his axe juggling and passive stacks. Being able to position well in fights while also catching axes is something that no other ADC in the game has to worry about. Your deaths are so much more punishing when playing Draven as well because losing passive stacks can make or break a game. Because of this, things like jungle tracking and map awareness are so much more important on Draven as you want to be aggressive on the pick, but controlling that aggression and playing up in the correct spots is often difficult for most Draven players to do. For Ezreal, he's the one champ in the game where it's incredibly easy to tell if the player is actually mastered on the pick or not. Being a skill shot reliant ADC, truly mastering Ezreal is really difficult to do. If you watch any pro Ezreal player and then compare that to the Ezreal players you see in solo Q, it's a complete night and day difference. There's a reason that the gap in win rate between Ezreal one tricks and the average Ezreal is a whopping 9%. The ADC who has the largest gap in win rate between average and one trick players is Kalista, up at 11%. No other ADC in the game has the same mechanic that you find on Kalista's passive. So, the fact that you can hop after every auto attack makes the skill ceiling of Kalista incredibly high. After each auto, you have to make a decision on where the best place to jump to is, and optimally choosing that spot every single time is pretty much impossible. Kalista's rend mechanic with her E also requires a high level of mastery because knowing the exact amount of damage it's going to deal based on what items you have and what targets you're hitting is also pretty impossible to actually master. So, if we move on to looking at support for the easy tier, we have Melio, Yumi, Tomkench, Maai, Nautilus, and Leona. Mastering these supports won't take you very long at all as there aren't that many ways to express your skill on each of them. None of them have any complicated combos or different ways to sequence spells depending on the situation. So, they are very straightforward. With Nautilus, you're going to do the exact same thing every time you hit a hook on a priority target. Tom Kench is just Q into W every single time if he's looking to engage. Same thing with Maai. W into Q and then R is your breadandut combo. And there's nothing to really overthink on these picks. The less thinking you got to do as to which combo would be most optimal in different situations, the easier the champ is going to be to master. And you can play these supports close to optimally with half of your brain turned off. If we move on to the medium tier, someone like Karma has many more decisions that she has to make because of her R. There's not just one combo that is most optimal for all situations. So, this adds a layer of champion mastery. Brandon Zyra may have a bit higher of a skill floor than a lot of other supports due to being more skill shot reliant, but in terms of how difficult they are to master, they actually aren't that hard. Sure, you've got to hit your skill shots, but how you approach a fight with Zyra doesn't really change that much depending on the situation. In a team fight, you're just looking for that stun with E to then follow with your R and spamming your Q out whenever it's up. So, in terms of ability sequencing, Zyra is not all that complicated. Saraka is a support that some people may argue to be an easy tier, but there's a pretty big difference between how a mastered and an average Saraka player navigates the laning phase. Minmaxing your Q usage in lane with Saraka is something that is relying on matchup knowledge. And you'll see mastered Sarraka players hit a ton of Q's in lane and provide great pressure for their ADCs. While an average Sarraka will just sit back and be more of a heal bot. For someone like Lulu, you may wonder why she's in medium tier while a support pretty similar to her and Melio slots into easy tier. With Lulu, it just comes down to the fact that there are more ways in which you can optimally use your spells. Should you throw your W on your ADC or save it to polymorph a diving enemy champ? Should you use your E for damage in lane or put it on your ADC? There's more complex decision-making that goes into playing a good Lulu, so it's going to take you a bit longer to master her. Blitzcrank is a support that we could see people arguing between medium and hard tier, but we have him slotted into medium. Don't get us wrong, the skill floor of Blitz is pretty high. So, if that were what the video was based on, he would likely be in hard tier. Since we're rating based on how difficult a champion is to master, though, which is in reference to skill ceiling, there's not actually a ton of different ways that you can express your skill on Blitz. The only thing that's a little more difficult is deciding whether or not you should run straight up into the enemy and lead with your E first or look to hook from range. But other than that, you don't have many complex combos that you have to execute with blitz. A good way to look at this is that if we showed you a clip of a challenger blitz and a bronze blitz in a team fight and both of them landed a hook, it would be hard for you to actually tell the difference between the players because both of them will look to hook into E every single time. Making our way over to the hard tier for support, a lot of these champs have very unique ways in which they can approach different situations. Velka's Q alone has so much skill expression due to the different angles you can launch it out at and being able to calculate what angles to throw it at in different situations takes a lot of mastery. Trick's R is such a difficult ability to use optimally and a spell that even one trick TK players won't always use at the best moment in a fight. Nico has her passive which can be so deadly if used to its potential. But due to it being such a unique mechanic, you're going to have to spend a lot of games to really master it. And then for our expert tier support champions, we've got only three of them and they include Thresh, Bard, and Sho. So, these are three of the highest variant supports in the game, as when you get one on your team, you're either going to want to pull your eyes out or spam ping them after they make a game-winning play. Bard is arguably the highest skill ceiling for any champion in the game because of how difficult it is to use his ultimate optimally. I'm sure we've all had that bard player on our team who single-handedly loses your team the game because of a bad alt. So, there's just such a big gap between an average bard and a mastered bard. What makes Thresh more difficult to master compared to other hook supports is the different ways in which you can play out fights. Thresh W usage is what really differentiates an average Thresh from a mastered Thresh player. And because of Thresh's W, playing forward and looking for a hook on an enemy carry isn't always how you want to approach a fight. How you position in a fight with Thresh is so much more important than many other supports because knowing when to back off and be more of a lantern bot versus when you should be up in the middle of things applying CC is something that sets him apart from a difficulty perspective. And then for Shako, he's such a wildcard support pick because unless you are really mastered at the champ, your ADC is probably going to hate playing with you. Navigating laning with Shako is so unique because he doesn't offer a whole lot of presence. So, you really have to capitalize on your windows of opportunity. Your macro play needs to be on point in order to master Shako because a lot of his worth is going to come down to placing his boxes in hot spots during the midame. And then our management in a fight is something that has such a high skill ceiling as using it to bait enemies and having him thinking your clone is actually you makes the champ really obnoxious, but it's not easy to do. All right, guys. One last thing. Our rankup guarantee is insane. It's like signing up for the gym and getting a refund if you don't get ripped. That's how confident we are in SkillCap. We obsess over making the best guides with top players, rigorous testing, and top tier video editing to make your climb easy. If you're ready to level up, visit skillcap.com and see the difference. So, there you have it, everyone. That is our season 15 rankings of every champion in the game from easiest to hardest. Don't flame us too hard in the comments below if you don't agree with some of our selections, but definitely let us know if you'd swap anyone around. Thank you all so much for watching, and we'll see you in the next one.

and 25 minute Mark the super minion spawns after you destroy an inhibitor but why can't you randomly roam minions start spawning at 1 minute and 5 Seconds and every 30 seconds after that a wave spawns is why if you start roaming while not being extremely in front you can lose your lane really quickly what about other roles how do they earn gold the jungler has to kill their Farm unless it gets stolen then they get nothing support can get these small blue circles around them which basically says if you attack a minion when it has less than half of its Health you share with your teammate if he's nearby I recommend sharing Cannon minions as they give the most gold out of any minion and can be crucial to winning the game in the early stages you also earn gold by doing objectives destroying plates Towers or killing someone what are plates in the first 14 minutes of the game the health of the towers are divided into plates getting one plate gets you money you can also earn gold from Wards either by destroying them or pinging them I recommend that usually 2,000 gold that you go back home and buy items so that you're stronger how to learn a new Champion let's say you want to learn Brier you first watch a three-minute guide on Brier to understand her abilities and what the champion does if Brier seems interesting enough then you can buy her and in League there's a game mode called training where you can practice with Champions I'm going to show you how to practice you go to the control panel and level up until you're level six so you can test out all abilities then you teleport yourself into the middle disable the minions so that they won't bother you then spawn an enemy practice dummy to test out your abilities you can also disable cooldowns and have infinite Mana you can test out different combos but the three minute guide will probably tell you what combos to do one thing that most beginners do is learn a new Champion plus the game which makes it extremely difficult to learn this game this game is highly strategic so I recommend you learn two Champions at maximum three you like and just play them after you feel comfortable with your Champion you can then focus on the Strategic part of the game I recommend you watch uus who play the same Champion but at a higher elos you can learn and improve be careful however there are some YouTubers that do troll picks and make it seem like that the troll pick is op but the reason why it's op is because they're either playing in Iron lobbies or it's most likely staged if you copy their troll pick you will most likely lose now how to win your games you know that by destroying the Nexus no matter how badly you're losing you win but how do we get to the Nexus and destroy it you should focus on getting towers and objectives objectives can be the deciding factor if you win or lose this game is Progressive which means you'll get the outer Towers the inner towers and so on but you don't have to get all the towers you could get all the towers at the Top Lane and destroy the Nexus without ever taking any other Tower if you go into a tower without minions then you will get attacked by the tower even if minions are there you can still get attacked if you attack an enemy these two towers respa after 3 minutes of being destroyed the Inhibitors can also respa after 5 minutes of being destroyed if you destroy only one or two then in each lane only one super minion will spawn if you destroy all three then in each lane two super minions will spawn per wave if you're in a game you can go into settings by pressing escape click on quick cast all so that all abilities instantly get used when you cast it then go to abilities and Summoner spells and put it all to shift plus the original letter this is so that if you use shift plus an ability you can still see the range of your spell go go to interface and in show names above health bar pick Champion name as you will get confused from the many skins enable show spell cost so you can see how much each spell costs without hovering over it scroll down and enable show neutral cams so that you can instantly see if objectives are on the map if you don't want any toxicity then disable chat from the enemy and Ally team if you press Zed you can unlock your camera to move it freely instead of it staying locked to your character I haven't seen a single I talk about this next thing even though it can make or break your games the mental if your mentality sucks then you will also be bad at the game but how do you improve your mental for everyone it's different but what works the best is not giving them any attention because the second you start talking to teammates or enemies that are toxic they will know that they got under your skin thus they will Target you making it harder to maintain your cool another great tip for not losing your mental is to stop caring about your rank because you will win more if you constantly think about your rank you are more likely to rage when bad things happen in your games if you enjoyed my guide Please Subscribe like and join my Discord I'm streaming every single day on Twitch and YouTube and I'd
