titre: Milio Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Milio is a playful enchanter support
who brings warm, fuzzy feelings
wherever he goes. He's mastered a form of 
fire magic that can heal instead of hurt, creating the adorable fuemigos
 from his eternal flame. Now he's off to Ixtal’s capital to pass the ultimate test
of greatness, making his beloved
family proud. Welcome to the Milio Champion Spotlight. "C’mon fuemigos! We got a lot of work to do!" Milio’s Passive, Fired Up, gives his friends
a little extra fuel. When Milo’s abilities
touch an allied champion, their next attack or ability will deal bonus damage scaling
with their attack damage. The target enemy will also
burn for a few seconds. The burn doesn't stack,
so reigniting it will just refresh
the duration. Milio wants to be the very best, but 
he always has time for fun. His Q, Ultra Mega Fire
Kick, launches a ball of flame that knocks back an enemy. If it hits, the ball will bounce behind them and explode dealing damage
and slowing in an area. The ball bounces extra far
when it hits minions or monsters. Line it up when you're
sure to score a goal. "Score!" Cast Milos W, Cozy Campfire, to create a zone that follows
the closest teammate. The zone heals over
time and applies Milios passive every few seconds. It also boosts
ally attack range for a percent of their current range,
increasing with ability level. These effects apply to all
allied champions in the area, so gather round the campfire, no song required. Recast the ability to 
have the zone follow someone else. Milio loves to help
others and makes new friends wherever he goes. Use
his E, Warm Hugs, to protect an ally granting
a shield and move speed. Warm Hugs holds two charges
so the fuemigos can spread the love
or stack it up. "My fire might look scary but don’t worry, it’s totally safe." Milios Ultimate, Breath of Life,
unleashes purifying flames. Allies in the area are healed, and cleansed, removing any crowd control effect
except for airborne. Allies will also gain tenacity
for a few seconds after. "Your welcome!" In lane, Milio wants to play safe
and bide his time for team fights. But he does have some poke
tools in his backpack, backpack! His range is short,
but the move speed from Warm Hugs can keep you safe
when you run up and auto. Ultra Mega Fire
Kick will bounce on hit. So catch enemies off
guard from behind minion waves. Score consistently
and you'll drive your opponents un poco loco. "We did it! Yeah!" All of Milio’s abilities
apply Fired Up when they touch allies, and those empowered attacks
can run surprisingly hot. Cozy Campfire grants Fired Up
three times over its duration. Weave in two casts of Warm Hugs
and you'll get up to five passive procs
on a single ally. Try this out in 2v2 fights
to make sparks fly. Milio is fast and hard to lock down,
but he's squishy and the knockback on 
Ultra Mega Fire Kick is his only peel tool. If you waste it, Milos out of gas
against any assassins or other divers,
it's also single target so groups of enemies
can quickly burn him out. And remember,
you can't cast your ultimate while you're affected by hard
crowd control. Milio’s flame may be eternal,
but stay out of reach or you'll be 
quickly extinguished. "But I want to help my family..." In the late game, Milio's team fighting powers
really flare up. Stick to a group of allies
to catch the most smoke for your fire. "I’m just warming up! Get it?" Cozy Campfire is great for healing all your friends
and at higher levels the attack range boost shines
even brighter. Use it to grant massive buffs
across multiple teammates, whether you're torching turrets or late game objectives. "I’m pretty strong.
I’ve been training like... all day!" With Breath of Life, there's
no waiting on a miracle. Its area of effect
cleanse can turn any fight. Cast it in a pinch, and you'll be out of there
before you can say “Flameo!” The bonus tenacity will also help
keep your team out of the frying pan. "Tag! You’re it!" Milio can't wait to share his
fuemigos with new amigos. So what are you waiting for? Pick them up and they'll be
sure to warm your heart. Join the Gentle Flame on his
adventure at the links below. "Let’s go fuemigos!"

titre: Top 10 Most BRAINDEAD Champions to CLIMB FAST Before the Season Ends!
transcription: With there being less than a month before the season ends, time is running out to hit your end of season goals. So, today we're breaking down 10 champions that are basically free LP being ridiculously strong and stupidly easy to play. If you want fast, effortless wins and a guaranteed shot at climbing this season, you need to start abusing these picks right now. And let's be real, nothing is bold enough to ever guarantee anything. Dating apps can't guarantee matches, gyms can't guarantee gains, coaches can't guarantee ranks. As if they did, they'd all go bankrupt overnight. But at SkillCapped, we've offered the same guarantee for over a decade. Climb at least five divisions while actively using our service or get your money back, no questions asked. Because if you don't see results, we believe you shouldn't have to pay, which is why at SkillCapped, we do the hard work for you. Finding the strongest S-tier picks, builds, and strategies before anyone else and teaching you exactly how to use them through our in-depth commentary page. Then, to make you a complete player, you get access to our full fundamentals library built by elite coaches like Rog and Chippys who teach you the specific skills you need to climb in your rank. And once you've built those fundamentals, you'll see how it all comes together in our bronze to diamond series, where you can watch real players just like you use those same lessons to rank up fast, step-by-step, game-by-game, giving you the complete blueprint you need to climb this season. Use the link in the description to get a special one-time discount and supercharge your climb today. Starting off, we have our first of two top lane picks, Sett. Sett is somehow going completely under the radar right now, currently having some of the highest win rates in low elo out of any champion in the top lane. And best of all, he's one of the easiest champions to just pick up and do well on. Thanks to his passive, it gives him huge early game damage, enabling you to beat almost every top lane champion straight from level one. On top of that, trading in lane is super consistent and straightforward. Just wait for your opponent to walk up to last hit, land your E for the stun, and then just beat down on them. If they try to trade back, you immediately start stacking grit, giving you a massive shield and the ridiculous true damage from your W, practically guaranteeing you'll win any trade you take, especially if you can hit that sweet spot. Sett's only real weakness is being kited, making ranged top laners fairly difficult for him to connect to. But even then, thanks to his inherent sustain, it's very hard to push him out of lane, giving you plenty of time for them to misposition, at which point if you ever get on them, especially once you have Stridebreaker, it's as good as over. Even outside of lane phase, Sett excels as not only is he a great split pusher [music] with amazing one v two potential, but he is fantastic in team fights, where unlike most bruisers, he isn't solely relying on finding flanks in order to get squishy targets. As you can just ult the front line and use them as a way to get to the squishy [music] targets position at the back of a fight. The hardest part of mastering Sett though is definitely when it comes to using his W, as not only do you want to try to as late as possible to maximize the damage, but it's also fairly easy to dodge. So, here are some tips. First, if you can, try to hold off on using your E early into fights or trades, as if there's multiple champions or minions nearby, landing the stun will guarantee the true damage on your W. Second, practice the flash W. Sett can flash at any point during the W animation, which enables you to reposition the damage and catch enemies by surprise. For build, Stridebreaker is the perfect rush item as it gives you both extra wave clear [music] and stick potential. Grabbing Overlord's Bloodmail second, which synergizes amazingly with Sett's W. you can grab Sterak's if you're grouping and want some extra durability and damage, Hullbreaker if you're playing more in the side lanes, or Black Cleaver, which can also be a good option either as third or second item against high armor targets. Finishing your build off with either more bruiser items like Spear of Shojin or situational tank items depending on what you need. As for runes, a great all-purpose page is this Conqueror one with Last Stand, taking Shield Bash and Revitalize as your secondaries. But if you want something a little more exciting, then look no further than the newest addition to the Rift, Zeri, where even after the recent nerfs, he is still one of the most overloaded top laners we've ever seen. And by understanding just a few simple mechanics, you'll be able to completely one v nine your games with Zeri, as even though his kit looks simple, Zeri actually needs to play lane a little more calculated. This is because in a straight one v one, you'll almost never fully stack your passive, which is where most of your power comes from. So, early on, you can't play for long all-ins, and this is where a lot of players go wrong. Instead, dominating lane is all about poking with Q and E and taking shorter trades. Something you might not know is that most auto-enhancing abilities extend your attack range, and Zeri gets two of them with his Q. This gives him a surprising amount of easy, reliable poke in lane. Another key tip is getting the most value out of your E by hitting the sweet spot, similar to a Darius Q. But there's a reliable trick for this. That sweet spot lines up perfectly with Zeri's 175 auto attack range, so you can auto or even Q, then dash in place with E for a super fast, high damage trade. Undoubtedly though, where Zeri shines the most is when it comes to small skirmishes or team fighting, where especially in the mid to late game, he is an absolute monster. Your goal in every team fight is the same, get your passive stacked as fast as possible, as this not only gives you that crazy boost to your attack damage, but your access to your passive revive. So, don't be afraid to hit the front line or just whatever you can, then once you have your stacks, you can look to try and threaten the enemy carries. For build, Zeri has a few options for his first item, the standard being a Trinity Force rush, which is just great all around. But against ranged top laners or hard to stick on team comps, Stridebreaker comes out slightly ahead. Whereas against tanks or high armor targets, you can opt for a Cleaver rush. From there, regardless of first item, go Sundered Sky second and then finish off your build with bruiser items like Death's Dance, Sterak's, Overlord's Bloodmail, or even Guardian Angel, because who doesn't like having three lives? Enchanters do obviously help him scale and keep him alive during his early lane phase, whereas engage supports help him connect and stick on targets where the damage from his ultimate and E get twice the value due to most fights being two-v-two [music] as well as giving you twice the healing from your R as you're typically going to be able to hit two players every time. Now, for the build, we suggest this phase rush page with Nimbus Cloak and Ghost Flash to help you connect and stick on targets with legend haste and cut down as your secondaries. Item-wise, Stormsurge is going to be a great rush item here as combined with Sorc Shoes gives you a ton of early flat pen to completely one-shot squishy ADCs in the bot lane once you unlock your ultimate, at which point you can go Liandry's and then either Rocketbelt or Void Staff into Deathcap to scale into the late game. It's also great to pick up an early Dark Seal as with your W immunity, you can protect the stacks very, very easily, giving you the option to upgrade to a Mejai's depending on how the game goes. But if mages in the bot lane aren't your thing and you want an easy ADC to play this season, you need to be spamming Smolder. Most players have an outdated misconception about how he's supposed to be played as when you think about Smolder, you think passive, AFK farming, just praying to scale the late game, right? But Riot has slowly shifted more of his late game power towards the early game. Yes, your strongest spike is still obviously 225 stacks when your Q is fully upgraded. However, this isn't nearly as crucial as it once was, and rather than just sitting in lane praying to scale, the best Smolder players have started playing way more aggressively early on because trading and poking champions actually gives you way more stacks than just AFK farming minions. This lets you hit that 225 spike a whole lot sooner, have way more agency, and take over games far more consistently. So don't be afraid to scrap early with your support or at least try to poke with auto Q or W as much as possible. And to make sure you can do that properly, you want to be taking this [music] comet rune page coupled with a Hubris rush as the early dirt gives you a ton of fighting power while the early Hubris stacks also provide amazing scaling into the mid and late game. Basically, she's kind of great all around. Now, to round out the build, go Essence Reaver second and Less Hunger third, and then around out with LDR and IE, finishing up with a Guardian Angel if it goes late. But moving on to support now, with Nami finally being reeled in after all those recent nerfs, there's one enchanter that is still drowning the rest of the competition, and luckily for us, he's one of the easiest to play, Milio. All around, Milio is just the complete package. You've got amazing trade potential in lane with the shielding, movement speed, and extra damage from your passive when using E. Your W is one of the best all-in tools in the entire game as it gives a stream of consistent healing, constant passive procs, and even range to everybody inside, allowing your ADC to chase down anybody. Yet you are still one of the safest enchanters in the game as you can reliably deal with engage supports thanks to the knockback on your Q. The only issue is everybody builds him completely wrong. Rather than opting for this rune page, Manaflow Band, and Scorch, you instead want to swap to Axiom Arcanist and Gathering Storm. We go into full detail as to why on our builds page, so go there after this video, but both Scorch and Manaflow are reliant on hitting your Q to proc, which in lane, you typically want to save for disengaging. Whereas Milio's R is one of the longest cool downs in the game and benefits massively from the extra healing and cool down reduction Axiom provides. And Gathering Storm is honestly just kind of a no-brainer here as you're not exactly picking Milio to poke players with Q in lane, now are you? Now, for items, although typically everybody does rush Echoes of Helia, ever since its rework, it's typically not the best anymore and it relies on you constantly being able to auto and land your Q to stack up and get value from, which isn't always possible, which is why if you have an ADC that benefits from attack speed, which is most outside of like Smolder, Jhin, and Corki, you instead should go Ardent Censer. Outside of that, the rest of your build consists of Moonstone, Redemption, and Dawncore to get the maximum amount of healing and shielding. But while Milio is easy to pick up, he definitely isn't the easiest to carry and for sure team reliant. So if taking matters into your own hands is kind of more of your style and you want the perfect support to roam, look for engages and play make on, look no further than our next and final champion, Braum. See, there's one issue with most engage supports. They're honestly deceptively hard to play. Not only do you need to first find the engage, but you need to know when you should or shouldn't go in. Yet what makes Braum so great is that he sort of sits in that middle ground between an enchanter and an engage support. You can stay by your team, keep them alive with the insane resistances from your W, block damage and spells with your E, and provide a ton of peel through constant passive stuns, but you also have way more agency to look for picks with Q and set up team fights with R. So you have all the benefits and carry potential of an engage support just without most of the drawbacks and the hardships. The only real mechanic that you have to master Braum is that a bad Braum is going to W to their target and then use E once they arrive. A Braum main is going to E during that leap as doing this unlocks Braum's hidden combos. As if you just W to an ally, you can't use R or Q until you land. However, if you press W, then immediately use E, you can actually Q or R during the leap. This not only makes your Q come out faster, but completely hides the animation as well. Now, for your build, grab this guardian rune page here with either biscuit delivery and cosmic insight in the harder matchups or free boots and approach velocity in the easier ones. Item-wise, upgrade your support item to Solstice Sleigh as you can proc this effortlessly with your Q and alongside Font of Life provides a nice little boost to healing. Then go Bami's Cinder first item into Locket, Knight's Vow, and then Portal Plasma Harness. So, do you want a deeper look at any of the champions featured in this guide? Well, then you have to check out our Smurf commentary where our challenger experts break down exactly how to play each and every one of them step-by-step. And that's on top of our full library of expertly curated courses all updated for season 16, designed for players of all skill levels. Whether you're a beginner, you're hard stuck, you're a seasoned veteran trying to push in for the Apex tiers, we give you everything you need to climb this season, which you can get an access to right now by using the exclusive discount link in the pinned comment or description below. Completely risk-free as it's all backed up by our rank up guarantee. Climb at least five divisions while actively using our service where you get every penny back. Thank you all so much for watching this video, man. Good luck with your rank climbs and we will see you in the next one.

titre: Noxus: The Land Of Selfish Champions | League of Legends
transcription: so far in this series we've gone over three different groups of nations vandal cities yordles demacia and ionia and there's one thing shared among them their way of life up until now i have covered only factions that conduct a more or less peaceful society keeping to themselves and finding relative prosperity in their own ways vandal city has been described as an enchanted land beyond the material realm a place actively described as the paradise by those looking from outside demacia has enjoyed a long stretch of nourishment in their insular yet self-sustaining and protected walls ionia's various provinces are rich with history and culture each assimilating the land and its inhabitants to sculpt out their civilizations all of them have established themselves in harmony both internally and externally at least until current events that's all about to change with this month's episode as noxus is a land whose people handled their political and societal affairs with a more cold-hearted and self-serving group than the previous three it's a place that all of us are no doubt aware of with several champions having reputations that precede them for better or worse mostly worse we just went over ionia whose candidates have faced heavy scrutiny for their ironic lack of balance and while the same opinion may potentially be had here as well i actually believed that beyond its supposed derelict moral code there's a strange almost paradoxically noble creed they follow one that is fair and dispassionate to all regardless of circumstance throughout summoner's rift and runeterra's lore noxus has made a name for themselves as the nation whose champions have only one goal in mind to achieve victory by any means necessary these guys do not mess around so it's with great excitement that for today i present to you noxus the champions that play to win really quickly though i want to spend a moment to shout out today's sponsor you gg almost all of you should know by now it's one of the premier tierless platforms and my personal favorite one to use but they also have a desktop application for overwolf now so alongside the normal stuff from their website they have all sorts of different overlays for chance select in-game and post game chancelog lets you search up player statistics for your team and as per usual you get to set builds and runes for whatever champion you're gonna play in game you have a host of different data panels like player card and info or in-game performance benchmarks as the game progresses also they have a few conveniences jungle timers are one of them that way you get to track when things are about to respawn even more than just dragon baron and red and blue buffs once the game is over you get your post game metrics including lp earned per champion and match so you know which champions you do best on and how you're doing in terms of mmr i'm a huge fan of you dai chiji and myself i have their tearless bookmarked whenever i need to look at matchups or whatever champion i should be banning so if you want to make use of it as well head on over to the link on screen which is also in the description but thanks again to uwg for sponsoring the video noxus is a powerful empire that casts a long and dark shadow outside of its borders they're seen as brutal expansionist belligerent perhaps barbaric yet those who delve deeper than his stratocratic appearance will see an unusually inclusive and welcoming society where the strengths and talents of his people are respected fostered and cultivated with the turbulent history filled with constant aggression and conflict noxians had to fight an uphill battle enduring countless attacks from outside forces time after time their perpetual fight for survival is what gave rise to their fervent and almost neurotic appreciation for strength and power in whatever form that took be it physical magical concrete abstract force or wits that belief is what they subscribe to and what they expect each and every person to understand it doesn't matter where you come from your social standing background homeland or material wealth anyone from the lavish to the destitute has equal opportunity to thrive and succeed here for how hard and pragmatic and unsympathetic the land of noxus is they have a surprisingly romantic view on society considering how humans often create hierarchies and chains of command your upbringing and place of origin especially during the feudal and medieval times would often shape your entire life power was a prerogative of those who already had power we can even see this in their rival country demacia their strong sense of order and honor is what made it such a commendable world power but their strict and rigid governance inhibits social and economic mobility if you were born a peasant you most likely stayed a peasant essentially noxus has no place for the weak if you possess merit and drive you can achieve anything you set out to do here but if you don't you'll be cast aside not unlike that of a meritocracy it may seem heartless and a bit cruel but it does mean that a person can't rise to power or greatness without earning it by their own hand a kid born into a noble family will still have to put in the same grueling work and effort as someone in the slums now despite their world views the empire does have a strong governing body in the spirit of checks and balances rather than one individual standing at the top the land is run by the tropherics a triad of equal leaders each representing one of the core principles of strength might vision and guile the principle of might which is currently led by darius manages the entire military the principle of vision championed by jericho swain is the main face of the government for the empire while the principle of guile whose representative is merely dubbed the faceless is much less conspicuous one could ascertain them as the nation's intelligence network that deals with more clandestine matters under the table affairs if you will although many theories speculate that leblanc is the head of the faceless given her role as the matriarch of the black rose whose primary directive bears a lot of similarities to the faceless according to a tweet made by ryze scathlock sk  i'm not sure how to pronounce that but anyways leblanc is not the faceless so perhaps the real leader is someone will never know due to their never ending quest for more power and more knowledge and more expansion noxus is no stranger to stepping over others for their own benefit they have launched several campaigns against neighboring lands demacia ionia shurima and so on not just as a means to acquire sustenance to their homeland in light of their poor supply of natural resources but also as a way to prove to the world that their way is the right way it's a very imperialist approach to geopolitics make of that what you will on sumner's rift noxus may very well have the most selfish roster of champions in the entire game rather than protect their allies they endeavor to crush their enemies whatever defensive or collaborative measures they take are to only further their own ambitions their sense of ethics and grey morality is a stark contrast to the largely black and white good as good bad as bad ideology of demacia if you remember from that episode i brought out that demacians are known for being very honorable fighters choosing to engage their opponents in head-on confrontation as opposed to subtle future duplicity to gain the upper hand for noxus anything goes really however it's divided into three different subcategories to reflect the different facets of its fighting styles noxus does have its own fair share of straightforward in-your-face type champions in fact they embrace that kind of fighting style to an almost obsessive extent one key distinction between the two that you can probably glance at already is that noxian champions are almost entirely geared towards offense this can be observed mostly by comparing the two generals dairies versus garen garen's w courage provides a huge defensive boost in combat while perseverance reflects his desire to fight only when necessary properties that are absent within darius conversely darius is a tool that breaks the neutral game in the form of apprehend drawing his opponents within striking distance of him while hemorrhage offers a staggering boost of raw physical might incentivizing him to stay in combat as much as possible for as long as possible it's safe to say that while garen is far more durable in most cases darius is capable of outputting way more damage and is widely considered the more aggressive of the two though not to say garen can't deal his fair share of damage everyone else draven riven mordekaiser scion clad all have very attack focused mechanics any form of protection they come with is typically to bolster their advantage state not mitigate their disadvantage for example mordecai's indestructible provides him a massive shield based on damage taken and damage dealt with the latter given much more in other words he gets more defense doing more damage stein as well he's one of the few champions of the shield that also deals damage generally it's safe to say that noxus has a greater propensity for offense than their rival counterparts if you were to look at each group on screen i think the majority of us would be more terrified of noxus assuming they're on equal footing furthermore they have an unusually large number of champions whose kids explicitly deal damage not very much defense or utility to speak of and once again those defensive tools are usually only to make it easier for them to do damage as opposed to ward off enemy threats they really embrace the idea that the best defense is a strong offense after all there's no need to worry about survivability if you kill your opponent before they kill you right physical pressure isn't the only thing they excel at while demacia may be averse to the usage of magic noxus welcomes it having a respectable number of mages each with their own unique ways to rip you to shreds not only that but they employ a good number of assassins in their roster who are known for dispatching high profile targets both in lore and in game i'm sure no explanation is necessary for those two as a nation there's certainly a force to be reckoned with you not only have to contend with the empire's military prowess on the front lines but their myriad of ways to destabilize and undermine you from within vladimir elise cassiopeia and leblanc gain much notoriety for not exactly being the most honest fighters and you can see this through their gameplay as each of them has an ability that cheats you out of a clean kill or straight up locks you out of using options no matter what category there's one thing all noxians share they don't care about anyone but themselves barring rel no champions on screen have any semblance of team-based utility whatsoever what this means is that they're very self-sufficient and don't depend on team compositions as much as that of other regions naturally it would be convenient for swain to have teammates with crowd control so he can make liberal use of ravenous flock but it isn't a requirement for him to accomplish this task he can do it on his own additionally prior to rel noxus had no support champions they were the only major established region in all of runeterra to not feature support for over a decade noxus allegiances with others are only under the basis of personal gain as a result they're only willing to cooperate with other champions if it makes practical sense hud's ultimate creating a draft current that speeds up allies isn't made out of the kindness of his own heart it's merely a byproduct of his actions that just so happen to conveniently benefit his team it may sound like a coincidence but i think it was done intentionally in that way remember noxus is a meritocracy it places an emphasis on the individual not the collective though not in the same way as ionia everyone's worth is judged purely by what they can achieve in either strength or smarts for them it's not about enlightenment it's about survival they care only about themselves they fight only for themselves because that's the only way they can live at one point talon was ordered to assassinate katarina by her own father for failing to complete her mission it's a dog-eat-dog world over there these people are willing to turn against their own comrades if they gain something from it strength is the be-all end-all in noxus if you're strong you live if you're weak you die so noxus has long been a target for discussion when it comes to how healthy their gameplay is for those on the receiving end true to their nature they're exceptionally proficient at overwhelming you through sheer force or oppression and with such an offense oriented kit they're some of the best to snowballing out of control yes i only get slandered a lot for having a bunch of 200 years champions but in my personal opinion noxus may not give the same kind of anime 1v1005 but they're most certainly good at basically not letting you play the game against any of their fighters you have a snowball's chance in hell of ever mounting a comeback if you give them so much as even a tiny lead darius mordekaiser clyde zion these guys can utterly dominate entire battlefields off of one single mistake because their kits are designed to beat the living out of you three times over if that's what it takes and facing them alone is almost suicide same thing with katarina talon leblanc draven samira and riven they get so much as one kill you might as well just give up as the adc cassiopeia vladimir and swain are amazing at harassing you from a distance without you being able to engage on them thanks to their myriad of disengage or all-in tools noxian champions are ludicrously frustrating to compete against because they can do their job so effectively with a very wide margin for error i'm not saying they're brainless and easy to play but since they were meant to function at 100 irrespective of circumstance it's really hard to force a mistake from them because they're always ready to fight at any possible moment irelia needs time to stack up her passive which requires minions the same with yasuo and jonah they need to get stacks of their q energy champions have a finite amount of actions they can take at any time noxus has 5 manalas champions they can scrap for as long as they please there's nothing holding them back from a fight so there's very little opportunity to catch them off guard thematically it is accurate i just spent an entire section of the video talking about how everyone has to sleep with a knife next to their bed but on the rift their consistency has led to many of them being quite unpleasant as opponents if you happen to be their next target usually when a champion snowballs like a monster it's offset by the inverse being a possibility as well ionian champions either 1v9 or turbo feed that's not true to the same extent for noxus which is why it feels so unfair to fight them seldom do you get to stop worrying about a talon at any point in the game he can be zero and five with only a prowess claw and still have enough bursts to 100 to zero and adc through immortal shield bow mordekaiser can have a rough laning phase but when he zaps you into the shadow realm he turns your own strength against you while you're deprived of your allies protection sion literally dies on purpose to win games fighting against noxians can feel tiresome to one person and perfectly fine to another depending on the types of champions you play as for almost all of them their power budgets are skewed in such a way that they can still take someone down with them something i noticed when looking through the list is that when facing a champion who is all about aggression it turns into a matchup scenario do you have the necessary tools to shut down that type of aggression if yes you win if no you lose why does renekton destroy riven because he has a point-and-click stun can match her tray combos and erase her shield why does clutter lose to fiora because she can repost his bear trap against him and their percent max cell true damage cancels out to carl's health advantage it takes very specific champions to be noxian champions in a way they're just as straightforward as demasians in the sense that they're all very easy to ascertain and defeat the question then becomes do you have those self-same champions as a vain player someone like mordekaiser darius and scion are easy pickings not so much talon and katarina i can't think of any other faction that embodies this dynamic more than noxus because no other faction is as hyper-aggressive as them that being said it begs the question whether or not their way of doing battle is truly the right way several of their champions have held strong in popularity for many years attesting to their appealing playstyles which is clearly encouraged by ride in light of the snowballing medics but it's not like they're entirely without counterplay either they're all good at shutting down one or two specific groups of champions while getting shut down themselves by another group sometimes a team composition just doesn't have the necessary tools to stave off noxian champions if you're playing adc and the enemy team has a talon while yours has no way to get him off you you don't get to play the game at all when i said these guys will do anything to win i meant it they will do whatever it takes to win and that includes not letting you play the game if you're playing garen lord have mercy on your soul if the enemy team has a leblanc you'll get kited for days some might say it's poor design others might say it's fair game no one from noxus can do everything by themselves they can be overbuffed to all hell and still have a very clear weakness which contrasts from ionia's roster who feels like they can do everything at the same time noxians have the highest probability of snowballing games leading them to appear unstoppable so i don't know to what extent riot should avoid these types of rock paper scissors match-ups should an assassin like katarina grind to mobile mages and marks into dust unconditionally or should there exist a way for her to still lose even with a type advantage so to speak although at that point the discussion shifts away from the nation in particular and more about the ideal competitive landscape that leagues should have it does make for an interesting challenge though to create someone who embraces the notion of attack attack attack zion's rework was one of the most confounding designs for a tank he can stand toe-to-toe with other vanguard brutes like malphite nautilus and leona but dishes out deceptively high amounts of damage through unique interactions like decimating smash and unstoppable onslaught i'm curious about what would happen if we ever saw a noxian enchanter we don't have one of those yet for them either at the end of the day many people consider them to be the villains of runeterra seeing as how they're invading other lands and their own domestic affairs are very ruthless and cutthroat but like i said earlier it's almost refreshing to know that the only thing stopping you from achieving victory is yourself with their emphasis on offense and aggression they naturally reward players who are proactive players who take matters into their own hands and don't ask for permission to accomplish what they set out to do that could possibly contribute to why rhythm means have a bigger ego than a brain anyways let me know your own thoughts on noxus and his champions in the comments section below for now if you enjoyed the video please be sure to leave a like and subscribe consider following me on twitter joining my discord server and checking out my previous episodes on ionia demacia and yordles if you haven't yet until next time though thank you all so much for watching and i look forward to seeing you again soon take care the world may fall to darkness but noxus will remain [Music] [Music] [Music] you

titre: Stat Checkers: The Most Broken... Yet Fair Champions | League of Legends
transcription: part of me thinks everyone who watches this video will subconsciously feel conflicted about the topic because it's secretly everyone's guilty pleasure yet no one really wants to admit it since it's a rules for the even not for me type deal but hey guys welcome back for today's episode i was thinking about champion groups i haven't gone over yet but are in sore need of discussion and i have yet to talk about stat checkers who i did mention multiple times in several videos the league of legends community prefers to complain about overloaded champions a lot these days myself included while i don't believe complexity is a bad thing it shouldn't exist in a champion with the sole focus of trying to meet a quota examples of complex champions that i feel aren't loaded with senseless amounts of fluff would be someone like talia takes a good understanding of movement prediction and spatial awareness to play her well but there's nothing in her kit that feels like it was shoehorned in there it's also important to talk about the other end of the spectrum though champions whose kits are too simplistic to the point where there's not much you can do to increase or decrease the extent of their pressure outside of number adjustments but surprisingly when those champions are paired up against their antithetical counterparts their properties don't cancel each other out it just amplifies them even further today i want to discuss the presence of stat checkers and how they managed to be both broken yet fair at the same time stat checkers are a breed of champions who play in such a way that there are no mechanics to work around in other words fighting them devolves into whichever player has the bigger stick hence the name stat check or stat stick ordinarily the concept of stat tracking only matters if both sides are using one but in league of legends most of the time only one player has to while mechanics do matter a lot for optimizing a champion well the inherent limitations and the way the game is designed allows for the likes of a felios and gwen to coexist with garen and mundo see in super smash bros while one character has inherent advantages over the other the game is designed in such a way so as to place greater focus on the players operating the fighter rather than the fighter themselves you can play pyro and mithra joker or pikachu and get completely destroyed by a ganondorf because the ganon player is just better than you conversely in a turn-based rpg like pokemon outside of edge cases like sturdy paint split nose pass there's almost zero chance a level 1 pokemon can beat a level 100 you could be the best pokemon player known to man and lose to a five-year-old since you have no way to overcome the sheer deficit and stats between pokemon league is somewhat of an anomaly in this case it doesn't have the same twitch based movements as a fighting game like smash but it doesn't have the same static calculations like pokemon it's sort of in the middle the goal of a game is to acquire items and levels at a faster rate than your opponent and though it doesn't happen all too often even late game monsters like kale and jinx won't be able to do very much against the 4-item renekton if they only have two that brings us to stat checkers these are champions whose entire game plan is to overwhelm you through sheer difference in numbers just like if they were a level 100 pokemon in theory any champion is capable of stack checking if they have six items while their opponent has only three but the ones in particular that i want to go over are the champions who basically embody it as a playstyle like all groups of champions there's no hard line in the sand for what is and what isn't a stat checker but there are two ways we can pinpoint them first the champion literally gains free stats to the leveling or usage of an ability or if they have an ability that directly interfaces with stats let's take a look at tryndamere battle fury gives some forty percent chance for free based on his fury bloodlust gives them bonus attack damage plus additional attack damage based on his missing health up to 85 mx rank and max conditions which is pretty insane considering infinity edge's 78d mocking shout debuffs all enemies within range dropping their attack damage by up to 80 at max rank as well three of his abilities either give him free stats or revolve around increasing and decreasing his and his opponent's stats by that logic gwen could be a stat checker considering her w gives her bonus armor and magic resist while skip and slash gives her bonus attack speed and range technically that is true but that's where the next condition comes into play most if not all of the champion's abilities have unconditional and more importantly unilateral effects what i mean by that is that the ability does one thing and one thing only take nasus his q is just bonus damage on the next auto attack his w is a point-and-click slow and his e is aoe magic damage to shreds armor and his ultimate is free hp armor and magic resist plus the magic damage aura going back to gwen she has three abilities that have multi-layered properties snip snip for instance functions on a charge system giving her more damage on her q based on how many auto attacks she did before and on top of that her attack is a sweet spot and a sour spot oh and stat checkers are almost unanimously melee ranged champs can't be stat checkers given that they have the element of range so they naturally have to be aware of spacing and distance management it's actually really hard to determine who is a legitimate stat checker and who isn't someone like tom kench would fit the criteria and i'm sure many people would agree but is he one of the same extent as mundo or tryndamere not sure if the champion has a skill shot does that invalidate them then that would mean neither mundo nor tryndamere are stat checkers that doesn't sound right either so to reduce as much ambiguity as possible i'll just pick a list and this is what we're going to work with for this video dr mundo garen jax malphite mordekaiser nasa's olaf trundle tryndamere and i think that's it a good number of them were in either the outdated or brainless champion videos so we're keeping them for this one too the interactions between the stat tracker and other champions are pretty interesting at a glance it may seem pretty ridiculous to give so much free power to someone actually let's calculate just how much these guys receive let's look at tryndamere again 40 crit chance 85 attack damage and a debuff that reduces attack damage by 80. based on the reference items for gold efficiency tryndamere's passive gives him 1600 bonus golden crit chance his q is almost 3 000 and his w is 2800 in fairness he only gets the max id from his q while his ultimate is active but that's pretty incredible over 7 000 golden extra stats not counting the rest of his abilities it's a little harder to calculate the other snack checkers because they have scaling elements but that sort of gives you a rough estimation of what to expect that brings us to the question why why do those champions need so much free stuff the answer is really simple without them they'd be completely unusable due to their one-dimensional nature and flat number scaling stat checker's abilities are notoriously bad objectively speaking as such that deficit needs to be made up with inflated numbers it references the basic convention of the speed and power balance if an attack is fast it likely doesn't have much power if an attack is a lot of power it shouldn't be very fast if you notice the running thing with stat checkers their abilities don't do a whole lot as an example trundle gets an insane amount of damage attack and movement speed from his q and w because the only thing those two abilities do combined is empower his next auto attack that's it if we put him up against one of the newer champs they can do a whole lot more with their q and w gwen has aoe percent health damage and true damage on her q and her w makes her untargetable zoe's q is a long range skill shot with incredible burst damage and her w can let her steal freaking sumner spells the implications for both of them are endless if gwen and zoe missed their q's they do absolutely nothing but imagine if zoe landed a paddle star on all 5 members of the enemy team gwen's w can theoretically dodge an infinite amount of damage crowd control anything or it won't help her at all conversely nasus and trundle's q and w's are very cut and dry they're only able to do one thing and one thing only nasus's q does have infinite scaling but the actual ceiling of the attack has a hard cap it can only damage one person and that's about it if we look at it this way there's only so much these guys can do at a given moment if that were the case it beggars believe that anyone would play titre: The BEST Champions To MAIN For EVERY ROLE In Season 16 - League of Legends
transcription: So, with a new season underway, if you really want to achieve your desired rank for this year, then the best way for you to get there the fastest is by locking down a few champions that you stick to, or in other words, main. Maining a champion means that it's going to be the pick that you play in the majority of your games. One tricking can get boring after a while, and even though it's the most optimal way to climb, maning a few champs can be a lot more enjoyable. So, if you can limit yourself to three main champions, then you can find a pretty good balance between not getting burnt out on one champ, but also having the potential to reach champion mastery. Most of the champions we feature in this video are going to require a more significant amount of mastery in order to play them to their potential. And this is why we are releasing this video at the very start of the season so that you have a ton of time to put into these champs if you choose to main them. So, we'll be covering three champions for every single role that are very powerful for season 16 and have a lot of solo carry power once mastered. And if you guys want to stay ahead of the meta for season 16, then SkillCap is the place to be. We've got a new course for this year that our challenger players will be working on, and it revolves around keeping you up to date with all the new meta trends. As soon as we discover something new, you can expect a video to go live within that course, breaking down exactly what we found. Whether it be a new build, strategy, or rune setup, Skill Cap members will be the first to know with access to this course. On top of this, we've got our weekly commentaries where our challenger experts walk you through how to carry out the exact rank you're stuck in. We always upload 10 brand new commentaries each week focused on covering the best champions in meta. And then of course, you'll get access to our entire catalog of premium courses as you'll be mastering wave control, vision, trading, and macro in no time. And the best part of it all is that we offer a five division rankup guarantee. Climb at least five divisions while actively using Skill Cap or get your money back, no questions asked. So, what are you waiting for? Click the link in the top right corner of the screen or in the description below to get ahead of everyone else for season 16. So, a champion who's become so much more enjoyable for this year and a really rewarding champion to main is Gangplank. Gangplank is now back to building full crit for season 16, and there are very few things in League that are more satisfying than landing a multi-man barrel combo in a team fight that crits for a mass amount of AoE damage. This is definitely easier said than done, though, as consistently hitting your barrel combos is what gives Gangplank a higher skill ceiling than most top laners. Since Gangplank is one of the best scaling top lane champions in the game, it makes learning your matchups and being able to get ahead in lane that much more important. If you can get to the point where you're able to solo kill your lane opponent on a regular basis, then the sky is the limit. And since top lane champions gain more experience this season through their roll quest, it's phenomenal for Gangplank because of the fact he scales so well. You can kind of do a little bit of everything on Gangplank as well as even though his team fight is where he really thrives. His ability to side lane and win through a split push is completely viable, too. So, you definitely may struggle a bit as you first pick up Gangplank, but don't let that deter you from chugging along, as he's one of the most rewarding, if not the most rewarding top laner once mastered. One top lane champion who's always going to be a great champion to main who's in a really powerful spot to start season 16 is Riven. If you want to play a champion who has the ability to outplay opponents in the 1v one, then there are few better options than Riven. Riven is one of those champs to where even if she's slightly behind her opponent and often doesn't matter that much as long as you're mastered at the champ. The mobility in Riven's kit allows you to outplay so many different situations as dodging a key skill shot or canceling an ability with her W stun can allow you to win fights that most champs wouldn't stand a chance in winning. Riven is one of the most devastating top lane champs in the game if given a lead because of the fact she has so much mobility. Riven's ability to chase an opponent down the long lane is incredible and her dive threat is top tier as well. So, if you get ahead of your opponent, then extending that lead is so easy when you're mastered the champion. Truly understanding your limits with Riven as you reach later game situations is probably one of the most difficult things to grasp. Riven can quite literally win 1v3 situations when she's ahead, but at the same time, you don't want to ape into a fight and just throw your lead if you're ahead. So being able to walk that fine line and knowing when you can be hyper aggro and when you've got to chill back a bit is something that will take you a while to learn, but once you've got it down, the pick becomes unstoppable. A top lane champion who always seems to be in a strong spot no matter what and who's become even better for season 16 is Kale. So Kale is a great top lane champion to main for a few different reasons. First off, this season's changes have been amazing for her because access to additional experience through completing the top lane quest means level 16 Kale is going to be a thing in a lot more of your games. Level 16 is where Kale really comes online because she unlocks bonus range and permanently gains the full effects of Zealus, which give her 30% attack speed and 10% movement speed. So, even though the season 16 quest edition has made it easier to reach level 16, mastering the champions is still very rewarding to ensure that you're getting there in the majority of games. Knowing how to play the laning phase out on Kale and learning your matchups is going to be the most important part of mastering her. Mechanically speaking, Kale isn't all that difficult as most of your DPS comes from autoatt attacking. So matchup mastery is what's really important to get the most out of her. And this just comes with spamming a ton of games because in some matchups you can actually beat the opponent straight up at level one. Whereas in other lanes you have to be more reserved. So learning your laning limits on Kale doesn't come easy. For such a strong scaler like Kale, if you can get to a point where you're consistently coming out of lane with a CS advantage, then that's where things get really silly as a Kale who's ahead in lane is set up to completely take over. A jungler we're really confident in recommending as a champion you'll get a ton of value out of maning is Fiddlesticks. Fiddle is one of those junglers who literally always remains in a powerful spot for solo Q regardless of the meta or season. Fiddle is always up above a 51% win rate, but since his play rate never gets too high, Riot is so much less likely to ever nerf him or make any changes. All the way from bronze to master tier, Fiddle's win rate does not dip below 50%. So, he's the perfect champ to spam for your rank climb. One of the core reasons to why Fiddle is so powerful for solo Q is because of the unique gank paths he can take along with the incredible team fight prowess he has. Your early game plan with Fiddle is pretty simple. Hard farm and reach level six as quick as you can. Once you get six, that is where everything opens up for the champ because with his R, he can look for tower dives or ganks from in behind walls that few other junglers have the ability to look for. Your counter ganks are absolutely insane with Fiddle 2. So, if you suspect the enemy jungler is going to try and gank bot, you can just chill out in the mid lane brush and get an easy three-man R if the enemies try to engage on your team. Because of how impactful Fiddlestick's ultimate can be, he's one of the few champs that can consistently win outnumbered fights if he's ahead. So, you get rewarded a ton for being proficient on Fiddle. For our second jungle selection, if you're someone who enjoys a more skirmish style jungler who can win through sheer 1v one domination, then Kha'Zix is a great pickup. Ka is an assassin jungler who thrives in his ability to isolate squishy targets and burst them out in an instant. His Q quite literally deals more damage if he uses it on an enemy who is at least 425 units away from another teammate. So, his main strength is catching off those targets who are split from their team. From a mechanical standpoint, you're not going to have a very difficult time mastering Kha'Zix. But, as with any assassin, it's really the decision-making and how you choose to approach fights that puts you on another level. Flanking, positioning in fog of war, and just being in the right spot at the right time are all really important concepts to master in order to get the most out of Kha'Zix. For example, if Dragon Soul is spawning in the next minute, then you shouldn't be on the top side of the map 1 minute before it spawns. Controlling vision on the side of the map where the next objective is spawning at least 1 minute before it spawns and then picking off squishy targets that try to enter late for vision is the most basic way to find great success on a jungler like Ka'ix. One of the most impactful junglers in the entire game when in the hands of a mastered player is Talon. Talon jungle is such a unique pick because he gets to do things that a lot of other junglers can't. Talon's E allows him to traverse over walls. So, his ability to invade and gank is top tier. Plays that may be high risk for most junglers end up being very low risk for Talon, and this just allows you to run away with games if you have a solid understanding of the champion. For example, many junglers would be going on a suicide mission if they invaded top side while both their mid laner and top laner are shoved under tower. Since Talon can escape by jumping over walls, he doesn't actually have to worry nearly as much as to whether or not his lanes have priority when deciding what to do. So, this means you have so many more windows of opportunity throughout the early game and are much less reliant on your teammates to impact games. Now, this does mean that if you play Talon, then you can't really use the excuse of your teammates being bad for not being able to make plays early on. So, that is one downside. Jokes aside, if you want a jungler who has some of the most reliable early game impact, then maining Talon for season 16 is a great idea. Moving on to our mid lane selections now. First up is a champion who's incredibly rewarding to main for season 16 being Cassidin. So with the removal of Akon and with the introduction of turret plates on tier 2 and inhibitor turrets, it's made a champion like Cassadin who wants to play through the side lane way more powerful. Cass was not someone who wanted to group up at 20 minutes and take a clown fiesta fight at Oticon to coin flip the game. Cass is a champion who's way better suited with a bit of a slower paced game where he can sit in a side lane, collect his farm, and scale into at least his level 11 where he has two points in R. Obviously, the level 16 spike is the massive one for Cass, but even just having level 11 is a very good spike in itself as well. So, prior to getting your level 11, you really want to focus on collecting as much experience as possible. If you're someone who enjoys winning through smart macro plays instead of outplaying in 1v1 scenarios, then Cass is the champion for you. Don't get us wrong, you can absolutely win 1v ones with Cass, and he even has the ability to 1v3 if he gets ahead and has his level 16. But your decision-making in the midame is what really differentiates a good Cass player from a great one. Another mid-lane champion who fits in perfectly for the way season 16 has changed the game is Aurelia. Aurelia is one of those champs who if given an inch can take a mile and this makes her phenomenal to main. What we mean by this is Aurelia is a champion who spikes really hard off early game items. Even just completing your vamp scepter and getting the life steal for early on in lane can make certain matchups extremely favorable for you. Blade of the ruin king spike is the massive one though. And if you can get to this item before the enemy mid has their item complete, you will roll them in the 1v one. So this means that learning how to play the early lane, understanding your matchups, and being able to consistently out CS your opponent is so much more rewarding with Aurelia than most other mids. But of course, doing all this takes a ton of time, and you can't just learn matchups without playing the game yourself. So, it makes maning Aurelia a must if you want to be able to reach her full carry potential. With Blade of the Ruin King and Hullbreaker completed, that's where you can flip a game in an instant with one kill while sidelaning. Hullbreaker providing that bonus damage to towers is amazing for this season because of the fact there's so much more plating gold to collect. The dream scenario for Aelia in the mid game is that you are splitting down bot, get a kill, and take the enemy's inner turret. You then reset, head up top lane, get another kill, which leads to another turret, and suddenly you've just had over a 2,000 gold swing in the matter of 3 minutes. Playing in the side lane can reap massive rewards for this season, so manning Aurelia and really exploiting that is a great strategy. And to round out the top three for mid lane, it's going to be a really strong solo carry once mastered being auction. So is definitely not one of those champs you can pick up right away and expect to win immediately. The skill floor and skill ceiling of Auction is higher than most midlaners. And this is why choosing to main him at the start of the season is a really good idea because you've got a lot of time to get good before the season ends. It's action's E that gives him an incredibly high skill ceiling as being able to use the spell to its limits is super difficult. Knowing which walls to use it on, how to cancel the spell at the right moment, or where to place your E on the wall for maximum distance are all factors that are really hard to get right when you've got to make split-second decisions. Something that's really underrated about action for season 16 is the fact that his invis from W allows him to run through the river without getting spotted by failite wards. This just makes his early game roam plays so much more reliable than other mids because the enemy will be lulled into a false sense of security with their failite ward down and you'll just show up in their lane for some easy kills. Getting top or bot ahead is super rewarding for this season 2 because of the fact that their quests offer up a lot of snowball power. So roaming in general is buff for season 16. Auction himself is an absolute menace with Elite as well because of the fact that he has stealth from W and the longrange gap close from E. So picking enemies off in the side lane is about as free as it's going to get on auction because of these two factors. An ADC who was great to main all throughout last season and who should remain really solid for this year too is Jinx. Jinx always seems to be left in a powerful state for solo Q. And on top of this, she's rarely ever banned. So it makes her one of the best ADCs to main. There are few ADCs who have as much team fight potential as Jinx because the immense power her passive provides. The ramping DPS potential with each passive proc is incredible and it makes the champ really rewarding if you can position well and contribute towards takedown. It's really your ability to position and knowing your limits when fighting depending on the enemy composition that's really going to take your jinx to the next level. Correct. Swapping between Gatling gun and fishbones is also something that can be more difficult to grasp at first, but is a really cool mechanic to help maximize DPS. All in all, if you want to man a hypers scaling ADC who has incredible team fight impact, then choosing Jinx is a no-brainer. An ADC that you cannot go wrong with maining for season 16 is Nila. Since Nila is a more niche ADC that doesn't get a ton of attention, she's always left in a more powerful state for solo Q. The reason Nila isn't played as much is because she's an ADC who has a completely different play style than most other ADCs. The majority of ADCs want to sit back in fights and choose the right moments to weave in autos. But Nila is the opposite in the sense that she thrives when diving head first into the enemy team. For Nila to be most effective, she wants to be in melee range, CCing the enemy with her R while blocking a ton of incoming damage with her W. So, if you're a player who likes to make things happen on your own and don't like the fact that most ADCs need to rely on their team to go in first, then you should highly consider manning Nila. At the very least, playing a bunch of Nila so that you have a general idea of how to play her and then pulling her out into specific enemy comps can suit you incredibly well. Nila's W hard counters auto attack reliant champions because when activated, she dodges all basic attacks for 2.25 seconds. In a later game situation, this 2.25 25 seconds is enough for you to 100 to zero a squishy champion without them even being able to deal a tick of damage back. A common situation where you'll find good value out of Nila is when the enemy team locks Yasuo or Yona because when they try to dive on you in fights, you can completely counter their damage by popping W. And then for our third and final ADC recommendation that you should main for season 16, we've got Twitch. So kind of like Nila, Twitch has elements to his kit that allow him to make plays that no other ADCs can make. The stealth mechanic from Twitch Q creates unparalleled pick potential and the ability to completely take control of a game on your own. It's all about being in the right spot at the right time with Twitch because if that opportunity arises where the enemy squishy champion is pushed up alone too far in lane, then he's the best ADC at punishing that with his assassin-like stealth. Just being able to create your own opportunities is something that makes Twitch really rewarding to main. He's the only ADC in the game to where as soon as you disappear from vision, the entire enemy team has to play more reserved or grouped up together, or else you'll be picking up free kills left, right, and center. And when it comes down to team fighting, the skill expression is endless. Choosing the correct time to pop out of stealth, choosing whether you want to try and flank from the side or sit in behind your team, and just possessing the ability to be creative with how you approach fights makes Twitch a completely unique character in comparison to other ADCs. So, if you put the time into getting really good at Twitch [music] and can exploit what his kit has to offer, then you'll be speedr running to high elo in no time. Looking at the support role now, if there's one champion who's always going to be worth maning for solo Q, it's Thresh. Thresh is always one of the more balanced supports in solo Q, hovering around a 50% win rate. But this is actually really good if you're looking to main him because you never have to worry about him dropping into an overly weak state. It's the playmaking potential that Thresh has to offer, which makes him a very strong champ in the right hands. Once you get enough games on Thresh and understand his limits, the carry power is better than the majority of supports. This is because you can impact games in so many different ways. Whether it be finding that game-winning hook on the enemy carry or saving your own carry from an enemy assassin by tossing [music] out a clutch lantern. The skill ceiling of Thresh is limitless as even after thousands of games, you'll always be able to look back at your recent game and ponder as to how you could have played a situation more optimally. Because of this, it's hard to get bored on Thresh. So, if you decide to choose him as one of your main champions, then we doubt you'll be disappointed. So, if you want a champion where your mechanics and decision-m directly translate into climbing and carrying from the support role, then Thresh is your answer. One of the most broken enchanters in the game when in the hands of a mastered player, which makes her perfect to main, is Nami. We really like Nami on this list because of the fact that she has not been rated lower than S tier on our tier list for the past year. Similar to Thresh, Nami always stays in a strong spot for solo Q, which is really important if you're choosing a main. The worst thing that could happen is you choose a champion to main, spam them for a few weeks, and then Riot guts them in the next patch. But with Nami, we're 99% certain that isn't going to happen. You can reach a pretty good baseline level of success on Nami without even spending too much time. But once you master her, then she becomes an elite solo Q pick. It's not hard to toss your E on your carries or heal them with W, but landing your Q consistently is what really differentiates an average Nami from a Nami main. Having the poise to hold on to the spell and using at the correct moments is not easy to grasp at first, but once you can limit the amount of wasted Q's, then the champ will seem unfair. Not only will you be providing your ADC with a mass amount of DPS by using your E and building mandate, but you'll be able to set up plays for them as well by hitting R and landing Q. So, you can impact games in many different ways. Nami is the complete package in terms of a solo Q enchanter. So, if you want a main and enchanter for season 16, she is our number one pick. And then rounding out the top three is one of the most powerful supports in the game when in the hands of a main being Blitzcrank. Blitz is one of the more high-risk, highreward champions. So, by maning him, what you will be able to do is lower that risk and increase the reward. A lot of Blitz's power comes from landing a hook, but it's what you do leading up to that hook that is the most important. You want to make landing your hook as easy as possible. So whether that means chaining the spell with one of your teammates CC spells, running right up to the enemy to hit them with your E first, or waiting for the enemy to be forced to walk into a choke point, there's a lot that goes into playing an optimal blitz. Getting to the point where you know how to approach fights most optimally is what's going to take the most amount of time to learn with Blitz. Once you can get your hook hit rate up, there's no other support in the game who offers as much instant impact as Blitzcrank. Whether you're ahead or behind, that one hook can completely shift the momentum of the game. So it's completely worth it to main blitz for this factor alone. In general, just improving your macro knowledge is really going to make your Blitz so much better, too, because vision control is so key to succeed with him. If you can control vision around an objective before it spawns and force the enemy team to [music] face check into you, then that's when Blitz is at his best. All right, guys. One last thing. Our rankup guarantee is insane. It's like signing up for the gym and getting a refund if you don't get ripped. That's how confident we are in SkillCap. We obsess over making the best guides with top players, rigorous testing, and top tier video editing to make your climb easy. If you're ready to level up, visit skillcap.com and see the difference. So, there you have it everyone. Those are three champions for every single role that are well worth it to main for season 16. Thank you all so much for watching. We'll see you in the next one.

titre: The "Kalista" Syndrome: Why Pro Play Is Addicted To These Champions | League Of Legends
transcription: At the time of writing this, MSI 2025 just finished and the competitive scene is beginning to prepare for the second half of the proseason. Thanks to the new fearless draft format wherein champions can only be selected once per best of series, spectators remarked on how refreshing it is to see tons of champion variety. And while I myself haven't been actively following proplay for quite some time, I watched a couple games myself and was surprised to see how varied picks and bands were, especially if the series went on to game four and five. All things considered, Fearless Draft has been a welcome addition to the game, and it's been wellreceived by most, if not the entire world. I don't think I need to explain why a tournament series of upwards of 40 champions is way more entertaining and dynamic than the same 10 or so being picked over and over again. With that said, there will still inevitably be a cluster of picks that are consistently found in every best of series you come across. Not in every single game anymore, mind you, but teams will still try to get their hands on whatever they perform best with. One observation has been made throughout MSI this year though that at this point has become a meme and it concerns the champion Kalista. Although at MSI, Kalista was chosen 10 times putting her squarely in the top half of the botan roster in pick rate for the event. But you may have noticed something interesting about her, her win rate, more specifically lack. Kalista played in 10 different series and failed to win a single game.
