titre: Zeri Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
﻿As Zaun’s living battery, Zeri uses her electric 
powers to charge herself and her weapons for any situation. Full of spark and energy, Zeri 
electrifies those around her when no one else will. Her foot is always on the peddle, speeding 
and zapping around the battlefield with ease. This current has a short fuse, but even against 
impossible odds, she refuses to give up. So get ready to swim with the sparks! Welcome 
to the Zeri Champion Spotlight. Zeri’s passive is Living Battery. 
When damaging enemy shields, Zeri shields herself for a 
portion of the damage dealt. Any shield Zeri gains grants her a 
brief movement speed boost. Zeri volts things up with her Q, Burst Fire. 
Rather than a traditional basic attack, Zeri fires a barrage of seven rounds that damages the first 
enemy hit. It has an incredibly low cooldown that scales with attack speed and no mana cost. Burst 
Fire will also crit and apply on-hit effects. For all intents and purposes, her 
Q is a basic attack skillshot. Burst Fire's passive turns her actual basic 
attack into an ability, meaning it applies spell effects like Rylai’s Crystal Scepter, 
but not attack effects like crits or Sheen. This ability can be charged by moving 
and casting Burst Fire, dealing massively increased damage and slowing at full charge.
It also deals extra damage to low-health enemies, making last-hitting minions a little easier. 
But be careful because attacks drain charge, delaying her access to that big 
chunk of bonus damage. Zeri’s W is Ultrashock Laser, an electric pulse 
that damages and slows the first enemy hit. When the pulse is targeted through terrain, it 
widens and extends into an ultra-ultrashock-ing laser that hits multiple enemies.
Zeri’s E is Spark Surge. After dashing a short distance, her next three salvos of Burst Fire will 
pierce, damaging enemies beyond the first. Attacks and abilities on enemy champions 
reduce Spark Surge's cooldown. Zeri can Surge across any terrain 
she touches no matter how wide. While skating over terrain, cast Ultrashock 
Laser for more voltage. Zeri’s ultimate is Lightning Crash, which 
unleashes an electric nova that damages nearby enemies. Hitting an enemy champion
grants Overcharge, which gives increased attack speed, bonus on-hit damage, and a 
chain-lightning effect on Burst Fire. Overcharge also stacks, providing increased
movement speed per stack of Overcharge. Attacks and abilities on enemy champions add stacks 
and extend Overcharge’s duration to ramp up the movement speed buff further, which lets her kill
enemies in a Flash… or without it! While you might be ex-static to attack, be 
cautious with excessive auto-attacking and draining down your charge. Instead, use abilities 
and kite enemy champions for a fully charged slow, then rail them down without a sweat. If you spot your team in trouble, Spark Surge is a great tool to swiftly join 
the fight. Target some terrain to skate over and right before you land, cast Ultrashock
Laser for an electrifying entrance. Being a literal battery has its positives, 
especially in rapid-fire moments. Lightning Crash early in fights and keep Overcharge 
stacks up with abilities and charged attacks. With all that extra movespeed, skating circles 
around the enemy team is a breeze. Even if a situation looks dire, Zeri can turn 
the tides with a multi-target Lightning Crash. With Overcharged speed, use Static Surge to 
quickly reposition yourself and provide Burst Fire support from a safer position. Like a 
familiar Shiv, chain damage among enemies and speed your way to victory. Lightning always strikes twice. Or three to seven times. Uncover more about 
the Spark of Zaun in the links below.

titre: Kindred: Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Welcome to the League of Legends champion spotlight, featuring Kindred, the Eternal Hunters. Wolf: "Have they forgotten us yet, little Lamb?" Lamb: "All still know us, though they try to forget." "Soon, we will remind them." Kindred is the manifestation of death on Runeterra, and can only be seen in life's final moments. Lamb, the compassionate half, offers a merciful passing to those who accept death, killing victims with a swift and true arrow. Wolf, Kindred's savage and brutal side, terrorizes those who run from death, chasing and devouring their souls. In-game, Kindred plays the role of a mobile marksman who thrives in the jungle. Wolf: "They know we'll come -" Lamb: " - yet they are never prepared." Kindred's passive is Mark of the Kindred. By clicking on a champion icon in the special bar displayed above Kindred's inventory, Lamb marks a target for death, though it does take a few seconds to arm. The mark does nothing on its own, but if Kindred kills - or helps kill - the marked target, they gain a permanent and stackable buff on their basic attacks. Lamb can switch targets every couple minutes, though once a marked target bites the dust, Kindred won't be able to re-apply the mark to them for a few minutes. It's not just Lamb who can apply Mark of the Kindred - Wolf randomly marks certain enemy jungle camps. As soon as Wolf's mark activates, it'll alert all players in the game, giving the enemy team plenty of opportunities to defend their chickens, rocks, or whatever else the big bad wolf is hungering for. Wolf sets his sights on a new target every few minutes. To prevent either team from gaining information they don't deserve, Wolf's marks don't disappear until several seconds after the camp's been cleared. While of course you want to farm marked champions to get a bunch of passive stacks, you can also play mind games with your opponents by marking lanes you're not planning on ganking to divert attention from your true target. Kindred's Q is Dance of Arrows, a short range vault that ends with Lamb firing an arrow at up to three nearby enemies. Despite it being a short range dash, Lamb can jump over walls with Dance of Arrows, making counterjungling much safer. Kindred's W is Wolf's Frenzy. Passively, Wolf's Frenzy builds up stacks as Kindred moves about the map. Once Kindred hits max stacks, Lamb's next basic attack heals them. Whenever Kindred activates Wolf's Frenzy, Wolf splits away from Lamb and claims a medium-sized area for a few seconds. Wolf fights independently inside this area, but will focus his attacks on whomever Lamb is shooting, but if Lamb leaves Wolf's Frenzy, Wolf will pack up shop and rejoin his wooly pal. Also, the cooldown on Dance of Arrows is MASSIVELY reduced while in Wolf's Frenzy, regardless of which ability you hit first. You'll always want to fight inside Wolf's Frenzy, so use the reduced cooldown on Dance of Arrows to stay in the zone and get yo damage on. Next up is Kindred's E, Mounting Dread, a heavy targeted slow that applies an additional debuff to its target. If Lamb's able to attack them three times in quick succession, Wolf lunges out to deal percent max health damage. Mounting Dread is great for starting your ganks - it's also really useful when you're facing tanky jungle monsters. Finally, Kindred's ultimate is Lamb's Respite, a large blessed zone that prevents all living things inside of it - ally, neutral, and enemy - from dying. Kindred casts Lamb's Respite on a target ally, blessing the ground beneath them, and while everything inside can still lose health, once they get down to about ten percent, they become immune to all further damage. Lamb's Respite only safeguards the lives who stay inside it, so as soon as you're pulled, kicked, bustered or headbutted out, well, you're gonna be eligible for death right away. Lamb's Respite lasts about four seconds, and as it ends, it heals up everything inside of it by a flat amount. Use Lamb's Respite to save vulnerable allies - or even yourself - from death. With smart timing, you can keep your low health pals ticking for longer while they whittle down
the enemy team. Kindred is a pretty complicated champion, combining the hard parts of marksmen, junglers, and even Bard. With that in mind, here are some neat little tricks to help you out in your first few games. Kindred's pretty squishy compared to most junglers, and - especially early on - can lose so much health to jungle camps that they'll be forced back early. But not if you're smart! Unlike pretty much every other jungler out there, Kindred can stay on the move and use abilities - especially Wolf's Frenzy and Dance of Arrows - to kite jungle creatures while they whittle 'em down. Just don't go so far out that you reset the camp back to full health. When fighting jungle monsters pay attention to their patience level right above their health bar. If it empties and turns red, they'll retreat and heal back up. It's like a backwards Gnar bar for not fighting. An awesome side effect of kiting jungle camps is that you'll also build up stacks of your W passive. Whenever it's maxed out, attack something nearby while you move to your next camp to heal up and resume stacking. Don't worry about fighting whatever you attacked - you don't have to - just carry on your journey with a little more health than you had before. Marking enemy champions is totally unique to Kindred, so it's pretty easy to forget to use it. Keep a constant eye out for enemies you can mark, and pay attention to the enemy jungle for opportunities to claim EVEN MOAR stacks. Here's your basic ganking pattern. Get in behind your opponent, tag them with Mounting Dread, activate Wolf's Frenzy, and enjoy your hilariously low Dance of Arrows cooldown. Though Syndra tries to set up a stun, being ranged has its benefits, and Lamb refuses to get kited. Getting Mark of the Kindred stacks is important, but don't make the mistake of thinking they're your only way to scale into the lategame. Risky invades without vision, or risky dives, will often just put you further behind, so farm safely until you spy a mark you know you can secure. Here's Kindred going off to collect a mark the right way: with friends. Because the enemy team will always know which monster Wolf has marked, they're much more likely to contest the camp. So bring your pals and expect a fight. Lee Sin apparently didn't watch the Champion Spotlight and makes the exact mistake we just cautioned against. Not only does Kindred pick up the Gromp and the Mark, but also a few hundred gold. Lamb's Respite saves everyone from death, so whoever wins the fight often comes down to who plays best around the end of the ultimate. Rengar is about to do Rengar things, so Kindred hits the big red button as he attacks. Irelia times the end of it and bursts Rengar to turn the fight around. From here, the heal at the end allows them to continue fighting and pick up two more kills. Let's try again on a larger scale. Braum flashes and misses his ultimate, leaving Kindred and Vayne totally exposed to the enemy flank. Lamb's Respite comes out just as Vayne's about to die, allowing time to regroup and whittle down the enemy front line. Right as it ends, the two Marksmen bolt out of range and kill off their assailants. Incredibly, none of Kindred's allies die in this fight. Thanks for tuning in to the Kindred Champion Spotlight. Please subscribe to the Riot Games YouTube channel and leave us your comments just below the video. Wolf: "Is this what it feels like to end?" Lamb: "I do not know, for this is not our end." Wolf: "Do others come back?" Lamb: "Those who do, wish they hadn't."

A summoner has disconnected. >> Got him. Rexi lives to fight another day. Didn't get to deal any damage because my entire goal in life was just to keep our team alive and engage. What a throw. Holy mother of froze. So close. Okay, everybody. You might be disappointed in me, but that right there was the last game of the day. After nine hours, I'm throwing the towel in the ring. I'm going to go chill, recharge my batteries. I will not be streaming tomorrow. I'll be back on Thursday. Thursday is going to be day 15 of the to set challenge. For now, thank you all for tuning in across Twitch and YouTube. Thanks for watching. Moderator, subscribers, shouts, and lurgers. Thank you for stopping by. Thank you for being here and giving me the opportunity to continue pursuing streaming as my full-time job. I greatly appreciate all of you. We managed to turn things around after some crazy crazy losses over the last couple of days, finishing on a high note, winning five of the last six games. Much appreciated. Shame we didn't win the Ramos game as well. It was a close one. Bit of a banger. But yeah, like I said on Thursday, we will go again. If you want to watch more content with me, go the extra mile to support me. Consider checking out my most recent YouTube video, Hyo streamer battle between me, Aloy, Brohan, and Akarin. Teemo jungle special, the usual, the classic. And uh yeah, I don't know. Enjoy the rest of your day. Have a great one. Make it fun. Make it memorable. Take good care of yourselves. Eat some lasagna perhaps. Have some ice cream. I don't know. Whatever floats your boat and makes you happy. And then I hope to see you back again on Thursday for more A to set action. It's been a pleasure everybody. See you soon. Peace.

titre: Sylas: The Unshackled | Champion Trailer - League of Legends
transcription: (Sylas narrating) Honor... Justice... Tradition... All shackles for the ignorant. After fifteen years in the dark... I was illuminated. I see a world without lords or peasants where every Demacian is a king. Where those who keep us in chains... have their power ripped from their grasp. Rise up, brothers and sisters! Whatever they bring, we'll match it! Crush the oppressors! Claim your birthright! And burn their kingdom to the ground! All we need to start the fire... is a single... spark.

titre: Nilah: The Joy Unbound | Champion Trailer - League of Legends
transcription:
Evils, ancient and deadly, exist in every corner of the world. They have made themselves comfortable. Taken what is not theirs,
 gorged on the suffering of others. I will face them all, and crush them. Lord of the Waters... grant me swiftness for this trial. Joy, unceasing and forever! That was fast. Should I have held back? The "Epic of Nilah" does not end here! I slay foes the size of mountains. You were... fine, I guess. Give me five, demon! OW! That was way too hard! I will face the great villains of myth, and live forever in song. Prepare for death! Now you know what you're up against. I am Nilah of the Seventh Layer. May you find joy in the world beyond. I hope the heavens are watching. I hope they're nervous.

titre: Bard: Mountain | New Champion Teaser - League of Legends
transcription: Find the stone! We’ll die… without it.

titre: Nilah Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Nilah is an endlessly joyful melee marksman
who makes waves with the strength of her allies. She steals power from the Demon of Joy, Ashlesh,
siphoning incomparable magic from his prison. With every step, her water whip slays monsters
that would destroy the world. So the legends say. Welcome to the Nilah Champion Spotlight. "Joy, unceasing and forever!" Nilah’s passive, Joy Unending, makes ally heals and shields more powerful on her, increasing their value by a small amount. When a nearby ally casts an ability that heals
or shields Nilah, or themselves, Joy Unending buffs both of them for the bonus value. See? Everyone’s happier when Nilah’s around. "Joy protect you!" Though she’s quick to crush foes, Nilah is a caring friend. When she last-hits a minion, she’ll whip up some bonus experience
and split it with the nearest ally. "Such happiness!" Use Nilah’s Q, Formless Blade,
to attack enemies with her water whip. Roll 3d10! Wait, no that’s not right... Formless Blade strikes in a line,
damaging all enemies hit. Hitting an enemy increases Nilah’s attack range
and attack speed for a few seconds, and her basic attacks will splash in a cone, dealing additional damage. Formless Blade has a short cooldown. If you hit it consistently, your sustained AOE will wash enemies away. "My waters cut deep." With Formless Blade’s passive, attacks and ability damage
against champions will ignore some of their armor.  and heal Nilah for part of the damage dealt. This effect scales with crit chance and can overheal,
granting a shield. Nilah’s W shrouds her in Jubilant Veil, where she briefly gains bonus move speed
and dodges all incoming basic attacks. She also takes reduced magic damage
to really rain on her enemy’s parade. Touching an ally champion hides them in the veil as well,
but they’ll be protected for a shorter period. With her demon-hunting responsibilities,
Nilah must be swift as a coursing river. Her E, Slipstream, lets her dash through a target unit,
damaging all enemies she passes through. Nilah can hold 2 charges of Slipstream at once, and she’ll always move
for a set distance, so get ready to go with the flow. Cast Formless Blade during Slipstream
to pull a wave in your path, dealing damage and activating your empowered attacks. Nilah unleashes a surge of power with her ultimate, Apotheosis, lashing her whip in an area around her. With a final burst, she pulls enemies in toward the center. Apotheosis heals Nilah for part of the damage dealt,
converting any excess healing into a shield. This effect scales with crit chance and is granted to nearby allies. 
After all, joy is meant to be shared. "Now you know what you're up against." In lane, longer-range opponents
can easily poke Nilah out, so rely on Joy Unending to keep you healthy
and gain extra experience. Nilah’s self-healing and armor penetration scale with crit chance, so you’ll need a few items before you’re really whipped into shape. When you’re ready to dive in, managing Nilah’s mobility
is key to giving your enemies whiplash. As a melee marksman, she has to be especially alert to stay out of hot water 
against tanky frontliners and bruisers. Slipstream has a long cooldown, but it holds 2 charges 
and targets allies as well as enemies. It’s a powerful tool for maneuvering
around a fight—if you use it wisely. Jubilant Veil can also ensure Nilah’s survival by blocking basic attacks, and you can even use it to protect your teammates in a pinch. There may be an “I” in “Nilah”
but there isn't one in “team”. She wants to be the star of every fight, but be sure
to stick with friends who can keep her alive. Without support, your tale might end with an H2-Oh-no! "Remember... my name..." Nilah’s true power lies in mid and late game teamfights 
when your foes are grouped up for cutting down. Activate Formless Blade’s passive as often as possible,
and you’ll topple the greatest of giants. Clump up enemies with Apotheosis, then use Formless Blade
to empower your attacks and deal tons of damage. Combined with ally heals and shields, you’ll slice through the enemy
like a whip-sword through butter. "Joyous!" With skill, grace, and a waterbending whip-sword,
the Joy Unbound will slay with a smile. Continue the epic of Nilah at the links below. "And how strong are you?"

titre: Irelia Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
This is Irelia, the Blade Dancer. Irelia uses the traditional dances of Ionia
to levitate an array of deadly blades. Her graceful movements may seem tranquil at first, but a furious tempest awaits anyone who threatens her home. Welcome to the Irelia Champion Spotlight. Irelia's Q is Bladesurge. She dashes to a target, damaging and applying on-hit effects to it, while healing herself a small amount. The damage is increased if the target is a minion or monster. If the target dies to Bladesurge, or was marked, we'll get into that in a second,  its cooldown is refreshed. Irelia's most iconic ability can now work
wonders in and OUT of a minion wave. Use it to dodge attacks, dive opponents, and snag kills. On your mark, reset, go! Irelia's E, Flawless Duet, is one way to mark enemies. She sends out a blade and can recast it to
send a second blade to another location. These blades will fly toward one another,
damaging and stunning enemies caught in the crossfire. Champions and large monsters are marked for Bladesurge resets. If you don't recast the ability within a few
seconds, the second blade will simply drop at Irelia's feet, triggering the damage and
stun as normal. Flawless Duet lets you strike at all swords of unexpected angles. Keep your patterns unpredictable to catch
enemies off-guard, out-trading them with the stun and a double-dash of Bladesurge. Irelia's ultimate is Vanguard's Edge. Irelia throws her blades, which scatter upon hitting an enemy champion, damaging whatever they hit. As with Flawless Duet, the blades will also
mark champions and large monsters. As the blades scatter, they form a very sharp
cage that lasts for a few seconds. The blade wall damages, slows, and disarms enemies cutting through it. Disarmed is a SORT of new debuff that prevents
enemies from basic attacking. Here Irelia's opponent makes a Graves mistake when he crosses the edge and Quickdraws a blank. Close, but no cigar. Use your ultimate to carve up a fight, splitting off a carry and also setting up a wide selection of marked targets. This is where you separate the good Irelia
players from the great ones. Use Vanguard's Edge for both the blade wall
and the marks, unleashing her ultimate in order to maximize both damage and dashing. Let's cut back to Irelia's W, Defiant Dance. On first cast, Irelia begins to channel her
will into her blades... losing the ability to move or attack but gaining significant damage reduction, and a stack of her passive, which we'll cover next. On second cast, Irelia lashes out, damaging
enemies based on the length of the charge. The channel can't be interrupted by crowd
control, so use it in the heat of battle to out-dance the damage you can't dodge... and
put the "dance" in "avoidance". You can also charge it up to secure the last
hit on your Cho-sen enemy. Lastly, Irelia's passive is Ionian Fervor. Hitting enemies with spells grants Irelia stacks of Ionian Fervor for a few seconds. Hitting multiple champions with a single ability
grants multiple stacks, and basic attacking extends its duration. Each stack adds bonus damage to her basic
attacks. At max stacks, this bonus damage is increased against shields, and Irelia also gains attack speed. Once the Blade Dancer is running a high fervor,
she can slice through shields like a hot knife through butter, making enemy defenses Irelia-vent. Here Irelia takes on the Titan of the Depths. How does she do in this match up? Give you a hint: Naut - a - loss. Irelia is most devastating when you're constantly
Bladesurging. Keep enemies locked up with Flawless Duet
while you flow freely from one step to the next, turning minions and marks into a sequence
of Q resets. Opponents that can't keep up won't make the
cut... Better nerf Irelia. When enemies try to trip you up, switch to
a well-timed Defiant Dance to guard against their attacks, even when you're about to be
stunned. Lead in with Vanguard's Edge, keeping enemies
close as you whirl around the fight in a dance that will leave them dizzy - and dead. GG well blade. Thanks for tuning into the Irelia Champion
Spotlight. Leap to the links below for more information
on the Blade Dancer.

titre: Talon: Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription: Bienvenue dans le Focus sur Talon, la Lame des ténèbres. Talon est un assassin au contact. Il utilise la furtivité, la vitesse et de nombreux effets de contrôle pour abattre ses ennemis, l’un après l’autre. Le passif de Talon est Cruauté. Talon inflige des dégâts supplémentaires aux ennemis ralentis, étourdis ou immobilisés. Diplomatie noxienne permet à la prochaine attaque de Talon d’infliger des dégâts bonus et un saignement à la cible. Cet effet de saignement inflige des dégâts et dévoile la cible pendant quelques secondes. Diplomatie noxienne réinitialise le timer d'attaque de Talon. Si vous l’utilisez juste après une attaque de base, cela permet d’infliger des dégâts conséquents en un court laps de temps. Ratissage projette une vague de lames qui reviennent tout de suite à l’envoyeur. Ces lames blessent et ralentissent tous les ennemis touchés. Coupe-gorge permet à Talon de se téléporter jusqu’à l’ennemi ciblé. Après la téléportation, la cible est réduite au silence et Talon lui inflige des dégâts supplémentaires pendant quelques instants. Assaut ténébreux est l’ultime de Talon. Lorsqu’il l’utilise, il projette des lames et devient invisible. Lorsqu’il est invisible, Talon profite d’un bonus à la vitesse de déplacement. Au bout de quelques secondes ou après avoir attaqué une cible, Talon réapparaît et les lames reviennent jusqu’à lui. Ces lames infligent des dégâts à tous les ennemis qu’elles touchent tant à l’aller qu’au retour. J’affronte Nidalee sur la voie du haut. En général, j’ai assez de mana pour un harcèlement constant avec Coupe-gorge, Ratissage et Diplomatie noxienne. Je m’occupe surtout des sbires, mais je l’attaque chaque fois que possible. Quand ses PV commencent à être assez bas, j’utilise Assaut ténébreux pour la rattraper.  Lorsque j’arrive derrière elle, Assaut ténébreux et Ratissage infligent des tas de dégâts. Paniquée, elle bat en retraite, je la rattrape avec Saut éclair et je l'achève en toute sécurité. Lorsque je repars, Lee Sin tente un gank. Regardez comment je m’enfuis. Je lui saute dessus avec Coupe-gorge quand les sbires arrivent, ce qui l’empêche de me ralentir. Ensuite, j’utilise Ratissage et je continue de courir. Je me place pour que les sbires lanceurs de sorts l’empêchent de toucher avec Coup résonant. Quand il se libère, je sais qu’il va tout lâcher, je m’eclipse rapidement sur la droite et je m’en sors indemne. Nous arrivons à un combat dans la jungle. Je saute sur Nidalee en lâchant tout, puis j’utilise Assaut ténébreux pour éviter tout risque de contre-attaque. Je vais ensuite sur Vayne et je la termine avec Ratissage quand elle s’enfuit. Dans la foulée, j’ai peur d’Alistar, donc je le marque avec le silence de Coupe-gorge et je vais tuer Lee Sin.  Nous revoilà dans un combat à la rivière. Alistar est pris pour cible et tombe rapidement. Nous poussons sur Kayle et quand je me fais repousser par Lee Sin, je poursuis avec Saut éclair. On achève Kayle, puis j’avance pour tomber sur Vayne. Je sais qu’on va gagner ce combat, donc j’essaie de marquer des points. J’utilise Coupe-gorge pour l’empêcher de s’enfuir, mais elle se sauve avec Saut éclair et je meurs. Nos adversaires font leur retour et éliminent certains de mes équipiers. Heureusement, Talon est particulièrement puissant quand ses adversaires ne peuvent pas utiliser leurs compétences en récupération. J’en profite pour sauter sur Nidalee qui a peu de PV, mais qui reste une menace en raison des dégâts qu’elle inflige. Je rate mon coup ensuite, en utilisant Fatigue sur Alistar au lieu de Vayne. Par chance, Jarvan l’étourdit et on les élimine tous les deux. Ensuite, j’avance sur Kayle qui n’a aucune chance de s’enfuir, grâce à la Fatigue de Jarvan. Lee Sin rejoint le combat, mais il se montre trop cupide en voyant Jarvan commencer à manquer de PV. Il le paye de sa vie et je réussis un quadruplé !  Comme Talon dispose d’un burst important, il est très utile sur des ganks coordonnés. Lorsque Warwick et Twisted Fate arrivent à portée, je saute dans l’action avec Coupe-gorge et je la tue avec Ratissage et Diplomatie noxienne. Lorsque vous jouez assassin, vous devez savoir jauger vos adversaires. Si vous faites le mauvais choix, vous y passez sans le moindre bénéfice. Là, j’ai pas mal harcelé Nidalee et je pense que je peux la tuer. Mais quand je fonce sous la tourelle, elle profite d’un soin de Soraka et utilise Fatigue, ce qui m’empêche de lui infliger assez de dégâts pour la tuer. Je ne sais pas si j’aurais pu utiliser Saut éclair pour filer, mais de toute façon, j’aurais dû savoir que sa Fatigue et le soin étaient utilisables. Talon dispose de super outils pour s’enfuir. Je me fais surprendre par Brand et Nidalee. Au lieu de paniquer et d’utiliser Saut éclair tout de suite, j’utilise Assaut ténébreux et je profite de la vitesse et de la furtivité pour filer. Nidalee me poursuit et je dois utiliser Saut éclair pour franchir le mur et me mettre à l’abri. Je pousse Nidalee jusqu’à sa tourelle et j’utilise la combo de burst de Talon. Saut éclair pour être à portée, suivi de Coupe-gorge. Je la touche d’abord avec Ratissage, ce qui me donne deux bonus aux dégâts. Ensuite, j’utilise Assaut ténébreux et j’attaque tout de suite, les lames la frappent deux fois tant que je profite du bonus aux dégâts. Je n’ai pas encore utilisé Diplomatie noxienne, mais j’ai déjà infligé plus de 500 points de dégâts en un instant. Assaut ténébreux peut être utilisé de nombreuses façons ingénieuses. Je vois Miss Fortune en mauvaise position au milieu. J’engage avec Coupe-gorge, suivi de Ratissage et mon passif me confère ainsi plus de dégâts pour Diplomatie noxienne. Je lance ensuite Assaut ténébreux et j’attends de récupérer. Warwick arrive pour aider à la tuer, mais j’aurais pu l'avoir avec la deuxième phase d’Assaut ténébreux et Diplomatie noxienne. Les assassins doivent aussi faire attention aux effets de contrôle de foule dans les combats impliquant du monde. Si sauter sur Soraka pour la réduire au silence avec Coupe-gorge semblait une bonne idée, c’était trop près de Warwick. Il me lance Contrainte infinie et m’empêche d’agir jusqu’à ce que j’en meurs. En runes, je prends des marques de pénétration d’armure, des sceaux d’armure fixe, des glyphes de résistance magique fixe et des quintessences de régénération des PV fixe. Cette configuration me permet d'avoir plus de présence sur ma voie, ce qui est le principal point faible de Talon. En maîtrises, j’ai 21/0/9, essentiellement des améliorations de dégâts physiques, avec la maîtrise de Fatigue et Saut éclair. Je maximise d’abord Ratissage, pour profiter des dégâts et du ralentissement améliorés, et parce qu’il me permet de farmer les sbires très facilement. Je monte ensuite Diplomatie noxienne, en mettant 1 point dans Coupe-gorge au niveau 3. Pour faire simple, j’essaie de monter mon DPS au maximum dès le début. Pour les objets, je commence par Bouclier de Doran. Si vous avez du mal en début de partie, une Lame de Doran ou deux peuvent vous aider le temps de récupérer des Jambières du berzerker. Ensuite, je prends Lame spectre de Youmuu le plus tôt possible et je fais la transition sur des objets de dégâts, comme Soif-de-sang et Lame d’infini. Il y a deux objets que j’estime indispensables pour Talon : Maillet gelé, pour ralentir les ennemis à chaque attaque et profiter de l’augmentation passive des dégâts, et Ceinture de mercure, qui est le seul moyen de supprimer Fatigue ; si vous êtes arrêté par les sorts des invocateurs ennemis, pensez à prendre cet objet. Merci d’avoir suivi ce Focus sur Talon. Pensez à vous abonner à la chaîne YouTube de Riot Games et laissez-nous un commentaire, juste sous la vidéo.

titre: Evelynn Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
This... is Evelynn, Agony's Embrace. Eve lures in her prey with the promise of
love... then subjects her victims to unspeakable torment. Welcome to the Evelynn Champion Spotlight. Evelynn's passive is Demon Shade. After leaving combat for a few seconds, Evelynn
is enveloped by Demon Shade. If Evelynn's health is low, she'll recover
it quickly while this ability is active. Starting at level 6, Demon Shade also grants camouflage. This makes Evelynn hidden from view to all except nearby enemy champions, control wards, and turrets. Evelynn's Q is Hate Spike. She lashes out, dealing damage to and
marking the first enemy hit. The target receives additional damage from
Evelynn's next few attacks or abilities. If the marked target is a monster, a significant
chunk of Hate Spike's cooldown will be refunded. Even if Hate spike misses, Evelynn can recast
the ability up to three times to fire a line of spikes through the nearest enemy or whatever
Evelynn is currently attacking. The spikes deal full damage to all enemies
beyond the first. Evelynn's W is Lust Dust. She temporarily curses a target champion or
monster. Hitting the cursed target with her basic attacks
or abilities will expunge the curse, damaging and significantly slowing the target, and
refunding Lust Dusts's mana cost. If Evelynn leaves the curse untouched for
a few seconds, Lust Dust will charm the target instead of slow and shred their magic resistance. If the target is a monster, the length of
the charm will be increased and the spell will deal even more damage. Casting Lust Dust does not break Demon Shade,
but it does show your opponent a little indicator that points to your location when the W charge
completes. Evelynn's E is Whiplash. She impales an enemy with her lashers,
applying on-hit effects and dealing damage as a percent of the target's maximum health. Evelynn is then granted a short burst of movement speed. Entering Demon Shade resets Whiplash's cooldown
and empowers its next cast. When casting Empowered Whiplash, Evelynn pulls
herself to her target, deals additional damage, and hits all enemies in a line through her
target. Evelynn's ultimate is Widowmaker. She unleashes her demonic energy, becoming
untargetable and cleaving enemies in front of her before warping backwards. This ability deals bonus damage to low-health
enemies. Targets vulnerable to the bonus damage from Widowmaker will have a marker above their head, but this
does not necessarily mean they'll die to it. Evelynn is very reliant on mana for her clear,
so it's a good idea to take blue buff early in your route. Evelynn's got an agonizingly weak early game. Her mana pool is limited, and before
she unlocks Demon Shade's camouflage, her ganks are very telegraphed. Before 6, she'll need extra help from laners
with crowd control to pull off successful ganks. Be mindful of how you use your Q when clearing
camps. Land the initial cast of Hate Spike on the
biggest baddie so your subsequent casts deal bonus damage to it. Try to maneuver the smaller critters in the
camp into a line to make the most of all that hate you've got pent up inside. Once Eve hits level 6, though, she becomes
a terrifying, invisible murder machine. Keep opponents on their toes and emerge from the
darkness when they've got no chance of escaping. Evelynn's ultimate, is great
for stealing, er... securing kills. But you can even use it to do a spooky drive-by
on dragon or baron. It's also a great clutch defensive tool for
evading enemy skillshots or getting over walls. Surprise opponents by aiming it backwards
to use it as a gap-closer. Try not to blow it too early, though once
it's gone, you've got no reliable way to get back in. Once you enter the late-game, Evelynn's job is
to end fights before they start. Look for clueless supports in the jungle to
evaporate, or just pop out of nowhere and 1v1 their squishies when they think they're safe. After dumping all your damage on your primary
target, it's ok and often optimal to weave in and out of combat to re-enable Demon Shade
and Empowered Whiplash. This way you'll get even more damage out of
it and regain your cooldowns to eviscerate your next target. Thanks for watching the Evelynn champion spotlight... Want to learn even more? Check out these links to submit to 
Agony's Embrace.

titre: Sett Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Sett is the Boss: a self-made, half-Vastayan,
half-Noxian entrepreneur and fighter running Ionia’s underground fighting pits. Not one to beat around the bush, Sett takes
matters into his own hands, relying on his fists to get ahead and get the job done. Sett’s get ready to Rumble!!! Welcome to the Sett Champion Spotlight. Sett’s got two fists, so of course he’s
got two passives. First, Pit Grit grants him health regen that
scales the more damage he takes, so he can take hit after hit. Second, Sett’s basic attacks alternate between
his left and right hands: a Left Punch deals normal damage, and a Right Punch comes out
faster and deals more damage. Talk about a mean right hook. With Knuckle Down, Sett briefly fires up his
movement speed when running towards enemies. His next two basic attacks then deal additional
damage. Use his Q to reset your auto attack, giving
you the chance to combo with a flurry of punches, up to four times in rapid succession. Left-right, Q, left-right again. Mudamudamudamudamudamuda! Sett’s W is Haymaker. He passively stores damage he takes as Grit,
which decays after a short amount of time. When activated after a brief windup, Haymaker
lets loose a giant punch that expends all his Grit, dealing true damage to enemies in
the center, and physical damage to enemies on the sides. You could say it’s a… Full Grit PAWNCHHHH! He simultaneously gets a rapidly decaying
shield, equal to the Grit expended. And… “His Grit. It’s over 90%! What 90%? Yes, 90%!” That’s what I’m saying! When Sett’s Grit hits 90% or higher, expect
some visual flair from the Boss to show off just how charged up he is for a fight. We doin’ this thing, or should we come back
in a few episodes… He’s killing it! Sett’s really been eating his vegetables
and his kakarots. Alright, alright, go cool off for a bit. Sett’s E, Facebreaker, grabs enemies on
either side of him and smashes them into each other, dealing damage and stunning them. If enemies are only on one side, Facebreaker
deals damage but slows them instead. Facebreaker works on enemy champions, minions,
and monsters. Now kiss. Sett’s ultimate is Show Stopper. Sett grabs a target enemy champion and slams
them down, dealing damage to the target and slowing all nearby enemies.The closer bystanders are to the impact zone, the more damage they take. If the grappled target had any bonus health, Sett deals a large portion of it as additional damage. When it comes to the ult, leave the small
fries and go after the big contenders with Show Stopper to deal the most damage. Sett can style and swag up Show Stopper in
different ways. Y’all ready fur this? *Music sounds*... Alright... Being the Boss is all about getting Sett
up for a good fight. Positioning and spacing is key. Sett has to be close enough that he can sprint
in with Knuckle Down for a one two punch, or position around the minions to wombo a
larger combo, grabbing minions and the opponent for a big stun and bigger damage. If his footwork is off, he whiffs his combo
and gets punished. Sett’s ult comes in handy with junglers
who want to try their luck ganking… or make ‘em talk to a wall for attempting to steal
Baron. For a truly G.O.A.T. (and V.A.S.T.A.Y.A.N.) play, stun tanky enemies with Show Stopper,
crashing them into the enemy AD carry. Then bash ‘em together with Facebreaker,
use the Grit you’ve stored to serve up a giant Haymaker, and pummel ‘em with Knuckle
Down. It’s pun-ches all the way. We’ve hit the end of this spotlight. Float like a speedy butterfly toward your
opponents and sting like a boss bee for the ol’ one-two. If you’re waiting for a finishing punchline… that was it.

titre: All 60 League of Legends junglers explained in 15 minutes
transcription: do you know what the ultimate goal is for all 60 plus junglers due to the vast number of choices and strategies in any given matter it can be quite overwhelming even for experienced players and so I've created this videos for junglers and laners to give you a quick overview of what you can expect from the jungle you're facing or playing and the first Champion we will begin with shall be amumu this is a tankier champion that utilizes an area of effect crowd control to initiate fights seeking to engage and lock down enemies from his team with this hugely big ultimate as well titre: Ivern: Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Welcome to the League of Legends Champion Spotlight, featuring Ivern, the Green Father. Once, long ago, Ivern Bramblefoot was a man— a pretty bad one. As a Freljordian conquistador, Ivern took what he wanted and killed anyone in his way. That changed the day he cut down a mysterious tree called a God-Willow. As it fell, the tree's magic flooded the cruel warrior's soul, leaving him with a love of nature and new appreciation for life. Now, by protecting the forest from those with evil in their hearts, the Green Father hopes to find atonement for his sins. Ivern's passive is Friend of the Forest. Ivern loves all the jungle's creatures, and would never harm them; Instead, he sets them free! By right-clicking any non-epic jungle monster, Ivern spends some health and mana to create a grove that then matures over time. Once the grove fully matures, right-clicking it again will free the camp's critters, who leave behind gifts of gold and experience. Ivern can free camps immediately after placing a grove by smiting it... ...Or by clicking on any camp that's already missing its biggest monster. As Ivern levels up, the Green Father can share the red and blue buff with his allies. When he frees the Brambleback or the Sentinel, they'll leave behind a delicious little copy of the buff that can be picked up by an ally. Similarly, if an ally takes out one of the buffs themselves, a copy is left for Ivern. You don't have to stick around near groves while they take root, but come back once they've matured and you can harvest them instantly with one click. Ivern's Q is Rootcaller, which slings a long root that damages and (no surprises here) roots the first enemy it hits. If Ivern or any of his allies try to attack the rooted target from out of range, they'll dash to their max attack range. In the centuries since Ivern became a tree, he's grown some very long-grass roots. The Rootcaller–>Basic Attack combo has enough range to let him red rover right over jungle walls. Ivern's W is Brushmaker. Brushmaker works on an ammo system, allowing Ivern to create several small patches or one  large thicket of brush at a time. Brushmaker also has a passive that grants extra magic damage and range to Ivern's basic attacks whenever he's in brush. The effect briefly lingers after leaving brush, so he can still throw some zingers for a few seconds. Brushmaker is all about mind games. You can set up sneaky traps, or just create complete mayhem in a lane without ever actually  prancing in for a gank. Poor Lucian. Ivern's E is Triggerseed, which places a protective seed onto an ally, granting a shield. After a couple seconds the seed explodes, damaging and slowing all nearby enemies. Triggerseed is great for buffing allies, but it's also Ivern's best tool for one-on-one duels. The ability's shield and damage turned this trade around for the Green Father, and the slow kept Cho in range for one final autoattack. Ivern's ultimate is Daisy! She's a big, happy sentinel who loves hugs and punching enemies. She's controllable with R or alt-right-click, just like Tibbers. If Daisy ever attacks the same champion three times in a row, she knocks up and damages all enemies in a line. Ivern is best at supporting other champions, but when he gets caught out Daisy is there to help. While Ivern jukes around to dodge Riven's blows, Daisy gets in a few punches, activating her knockup effect. Her body blocking kept the Exile at bay until Ivern could bait her into a 1v4. How root. Ivern's passive gives him access to jungle roots that just wouldn't work for other junglers. After prepping his own Blue Buff for later, he goes in for a level one invade of the enemy Red, Rootcalling over the wall to place a grove. He Smites to save a few seconds and quickly goes for a gank on Xerath before the poor Magus Ascendant has even hit level two. Mid flash down. Once Ivern hits six, Daisy makes the Green man's ganks a real hazard. While Daisy dukes it out with Ashe, Ivern safely avoids turret aggro by making sure that she goes in first. Even then, Ivern general lee avoids turret damage using his shield from Triggerseed. Three hits from Daisy secures the knockup on Braum, earning the final kill. In teamfights, Ivern shines as a long-range support, with basic attacks to back it up. He starts out this fight making a beeline for the brush to enable Brushmaker’s passive and focuses on Cho’Gath until he dies. Then he helps take out Cassiopeia with a well-aimed Rootcaller, giving his fellow tree-man a free dash to stick to her. As they switch to Nasus, Ivern casts Brushmaker at his own feet to re-enable his ranged bonus damage, helping send their final foe to the dog pound. Creating brush anywhere lets Ivern put opponents in some very uncomfortable situations... like this one, with five untargetable champions sieging an inhib turret, complete with a Blitzcrank grab. He shields Caitlyn as the siege begins and tags Thresh with a Rootcaller. Unfortunately this means he’s missing all his defensive tools for the rest of the teamfight, except for Daisy who puts a few hits in to help Caitlyn survive her battle with Garen. The brush hook alone would let any Green Father player say, “I’v-erned” this one. Thanks for watching the Ivern Champion Spotlight. If you've got a green thumb, there's more
Ivern news in the links below.

titre: Lane Bullies: The Most Annoying Champions? | League of Legends
transcription: at this point i have made quite a few videos talking about groups of champions and in each one you guys have more or less some pretty strong opinions no seriously some of you make me wonder who the hell actually hurts you i mean i hate yona but you guys wish for some really really nasty stuff anyways welcome back to another discussion video just a forewarning i'm recovering from a pretty bad cold so if i still sound congested i apologize but hope that won't detract from the video today's topic is going to be on another infamous group of champions lane bullies these champions have a complex dynamic among the player base on one hand they're a really easy and valuable conduit for making games quick and dirty but on the other they can have the same feaster famine issues as hyper carries for totally different reasons however your perceptions may be we're gonna dive into the meat and bones of the lane bully sub category not before we talk about today's sponsor though mobolytics it's a gaming assistant tool that acts as an extension on your league of legends client showing you a ton of information ranging from builds to matchups to player profiles even tips and strategies for the game everyone and their mother uses at least one statistics page like op.gg u.g.champion.gigi mobilefire but that requires you to tab out of your game and open up a browser page which can cost you time all of mobolytics functions as an overlay so you can get quick information on your teammates runes and builds you can even check out the enemy team to see if there are any dirty smurfs or perhaps someone on a losing streak that you can abuse to all hell something in particular i think is really cool is that it even has additional alerts pop up on screen such as when an enemy champion buys a big item it can show you current gold leads just to see how far ahead or behind you are which you would normally be able to only see in spectator mode but with this tool you can see it while you're in game so even if your team is down a few kills you might still be neck and neck a lot of information can be provided to you for your convenience so you don't have to open up a whole bunch of tabs you get all of it right on the spot and if i may speak plainly having used this myself i think this beats out any gaming assistant i've seen so far so check out mobolytics by clicking the link in the description it would support the channel a whole bunch but without any further delay let's get back into the video to start let's explain what lane bullies are the league wiki actually has a definition in their terminology page so i'll read that first a champion decide to win the laning phase against other champions they try to constantly harass the enemy laner and deny them farm making them fall behind most lane bullies fall off late game in order to compensate for their early game strength generally speaking that's a pretty valid description but it's slightly outdated for me elaine bully is a champion whose neutral game advantage and disadvantage in the laning phase carry less risk than that of their peers they don't necessarily have to be very threatening and some lane bullies can be valuable even if they don't get a lead common knowledge dictates you win lane if you have a higher score than your opponent such as getting a solo kill but there are other ways you can win lane for one there's priority it's been gaining more awareness as of late in light of the number of games that are decided based on which team wins the first 2v2 or 3v3 priority can be easily achieved without need for kills or even a cs advantage and can oftentimes snowball into greater results down the line those that do meet an ideal situation in lane with little to no risk to themselves are considered lane bullies that brings us to the next part what types of lane bullies are there believe it or not this subcategory has its own subgroup of champions there are three of them the first type is the win hard and end quick champions these are the ones most people think of when the term lane bullies pops in their head pantheon renekton jace lee sin draven usually physical damage dealers with harass that is hard to counter or trade patterns that beat out a majority of the cast they also tend to be very easy to pick up but can be difficult to master not always mechanically but in macro since these champions gradually lose out an effective combat pressure as time goes on you have to figure out how to make the best use of your time with hopefully the league you've acquired as most champions with decent scaling will be able to not only contribute more in team fights but in some cases they can solo 1v1 year later down the road pantheon embodies this role the most since grand starfall is a semi-global teleport he has the liberty to take ignite instead of teleport which gives him more kill pressure in lane so he can then roam to other lanes and get a snowball going but since the only thing that scales is his damage he can start to lag behind other divers and juggernauts who can bring more to the table to his credit the rework made him a little stronger in the mid to late game but not enough to put him on par with the likes of camille or darius in fighting strength second type is the play safe and scale gangplank gnar vladimir kayla and kennen these champions ironically have pretty lousy early games and can be easily snowballed on if played improperly however they're not without tools to survive they have at least one ability that lets them safely and consistently apply pressure and one ability that gives them an easy out if the situation ever turns south i'd wager these are the most disliked lane bullies because they're the safest to play making it super difficult to get a lead on them while they poke you out of lane and then they can eventually run circles around you in the mid to late game vladimir is the poster child for this group his q is a ranged ability that can deal damage and heal him giving him infinite sustain so long as he has something to suck on combined as the sanguine pool and face rush he becomes one of the most slippery champions in the game the only ones who can beat him are rushed down characters like cled irelia and riven who have enough mobility to stay on top of him and the dps far outstrips his ability to heal lastly we have anti-carries i just made a video on them not too long ago singed teemo quinn heimerdinger malzahar unlike the first two they're neither safe to play nor do they have a strong early game pragmatically speaking sometimes they might teemo has the same poke strength as gnar and gangplank but we all know the real reason people pick these champions each of them has something really good at shutting down the enemy carries and they're also really annoying to lane against because their neutral game and disadvantage states are so good that they force you into a lose-lose situation either sit there and gradually get shoved out of lane due to their superior wave clear and or poke damage or charge straight at them with a high probability that that's exactly what they want you to do and then you lose even more lane priority heimerdinger is fantastic at this his turrets can zone so many melee champions indefinitely and push lanes really fast if you try to dive right in their face you're going to eat a grenade and likely 3 supercharged turret beams at which point you've already lost the trade but it's not without cost anti-carry lane bullies are usually more vulnerable than the safe scaling ones because careless positioning or resource management can make them extremely easy targets since they're usually immobile it should come as no surprise that the one thing they all share in common is that they're not exactly the most enjoyable lane matchups for many people and when piloted by experienced players you as the opponent may very well end up spending the first 10 to 15 minutes trying to do what you can to not fall too far behind and even if you do somehow manage to hold the line to the best of your ability there's no guarantee you'll be able to turn the tables around because you might be dealing with the champion who can also outscale you so then what is the deal with lane bullies and why do so many people grow not decide that their enemy later is still locking one of them is the group as a whole overpowered or just annoying to deal with if you're looking for the shortest answer i can probably think of laning phase is the most important part of the game right now and in the early game any countermeasures that would easily be able to shut down these lane bullies are not available to you yet because well the game just started right if you look at it statistically how do you shut down those pesky win hard champions a combination of increased durability to survive their burst damage and enough dps to cut right back at them both of those require gold and levels even tanks can't do much against them until they pick up at least the tabbies and their first completed item how do you shut down safe scaling lane bullies by bursting them hard enough to where they can't walk up to you and poke you and some extra mobility or slows can help like stride breaker or prowlers claw guess what both of those require gold wow who would have thunk how do you shut down anti carries you don't just kidding you need gold gold gold gold money makes the world go around and in a communist society like league of legends okay okay what the hell was i smoking while writing this on a more serious note though the quick answer to why lane bullies feel like such a hassle to deal with is that no one has the resources to shut down their power in the early game because everyone starts out on a level playing field sure there are specific counters but most of the time those just happen to be other lane bullies for example you pick gangplank or quinn to screw over renekton or you pick temo and panty out to screw over darius there are a few other reasons i want to talk about though and they depend from group to group i mentioned before that lane bullies are champions who can take much less risk for more gain in the laning phase that generally means they're really easy to play in the laning phase most people see lame bullies the same way they do anti-carries champions with a playstyle specifically designed to dominate a certain section of the game meaning unless you also play a champion who can do that you're automatically starting out with a losing hand each champion has their own set of win conditions for jax it could be reaching his mid game power spike and split pushing to kingdom come for kaiser it could be playing as aggressively as possible in the early game to then snowball in team fights by assassinating the backline for talon it could be roaming around the map to pick off unsuspecting targets whatever it is they have to first get past the laning phase and lane bullies control the lane making it that much more difficult for other champions to get what they want out of it in that sense lane bullies and anti-carries are one in the same in that they excel at shutting people down typically players dislike champions that prevent them from playing the game when you're playing against the lane bully you have to essentially bend to their will if they're good you're not going to be allowed to do a whole lot and the openings you can capitalize on get smaller with each passing minute if you have any at all what's often frustrating is that sometimes the champion you're playing just doesn't have anything to deal with whoever you're up against why do you think there are so many lane bullies in toblin that lane is the best place for them to be since going first or second is night and day if you go first your opponent can counter pick you with a lane bully that your champion isn't equipped to take on and your jungler obviously doesn't want to gank you because it's not worth it this can occur in the bot lane as well how is kaiser supposed to meet her win condition against a draven who does twice her damage per auto attack worse yet your support is yumi and there's his nautilus it's extremely unenjoyable to have reduced or sometimes zero agency for the first half of the game purely by virtue of how the game is designed summoner's rift is engineered to have a laning phase where champions can pick up gold and experience through farming and securing objectives before transitioning into team fights there will never be a situation in the game where you can skip the laning phase like it or not you will be stuck with the lane bully it would be a whole other discussion if lane swaps were still possible lane swapping used to be really common in pro play because it allowed for teams to counter certain top laners which again fielded the most amount of lane bullies even those champions can't effectively lane against the combined strength of a bot laner and support though recent changes make this strategy almost impossible the bot lane has less durable towers and tower plates in first tower gold are also highly contested combine this with the super snowball estate the game is in and we've seen the highest presence of lane bullies ever in the history of league of legends and there usually is no need to worry about falling off in the late game because you can end the game in 20 minutes all of these factors together have made it so lane bullies went from being a risky way of choosing games to now a legitimate meta strategy although lane bullies are not all that much stronger than they used to be it's not like they're all 55 win rate but player perception towards them has exacerbated over time and with the way ride has reworked and buffed a number of them to remain somewhat relevant even in the late game it is an easy way to kill player morale when you see a super fed pantheon or darius or renekton after all they were tolerable before since you could just tell yourself if i could hold out until the laning phase is over then i cannot scale them and kick their ass as sweet sweet payback darius gang playing tom kench and pantheon were all really awful after three items because they were purely designed to win lane but now half of them don't even reach the super useless point until the fifth or sixth item while the other half actually scale pretty dang well now this even happens in the jungle of all places xinjiao and kasich's have been tearing through the rift because of their strong early game presence champions would normally consider pretty lame once the 5v5 has started taking place but they don't seem to care about it that much since everyone starts panicking and spreading out once the fighting starts anyway i may be generalizing there are some exceptions that are reasonable like gangplank because he's super squishy in the early game so you can probably one shot him with any diver or even juggernaut and i would also argue champions like singed and teemo are okay too but there are a few that are questionably safe relative to their scaling like gnar and cassiopeia that could then possibly lead to a balanced problem the best way to design a champion with a super strong late game is to obviously give them a subpar early game to require players to learn how to be patient and squeeze out whatever they can if you can successfully make it to the three or four items point that is when you start dominating the game since they're the textbook opposite of lame bullies it would make sense for the latter to have the opposite dynamic though as we know that's not always the case is it fine for champions to have an equally strong early mid and late game without any major vulnerabilities that depends on how the strength manifests darius would be an example he's strong no matter how short or how long a game goes but due to a straightforward and telegraph nature he never feels like a true 1v9 hypercarry threat only someone you have to respect if he's standing right on top of you not much else that and juggernauts are naturally countered by range champions and 99 of games have at least one of them i'm not entirely sure if the same can apply to a champion with high carry potential like tristana though she is an off meta pick her occasional usage and success in the mid lane is pretty alarming considering towards the late game she reaches the same long range as caitlyn and jinx is that considered fine for other groups like mannerless champions or hyper carries there's a very clear set of boundaries you shouldn't cross when designing someone to fit into either if you give carries too much safety that is a big no if you give mana less champions no consequence in their ability usage that's a big no but for lane bullies it's not really that simple elaine bully doesn't have to be someone with a strong early game and a weak late game it can even be someone with a weak early game who just happens to survive lane very comfortably that's what's annoying about them to so many people they're not impossible to beat nor do they have the same anime plot armor as 200 years champions but they're sure as hell not fun to play against because the options you have to win lane against them are more limited unfortunately i'd argue it does come down to luck did you pick someone who does have a way to stop your opponent's lane bully pick or is it a hard 70-30 matchup where you just have to play safe for 20 minutes what do you guys think do you believe their strong early game presence is justified or annoying it might depend on what role you play after all i'm pretty sure an 80 carry main doesn't care a whole lot about fighting renekton since they're not in the top lane and vice versa i'm sure top main doesn't care so much about going up against draven because they're not in the bot lane regardless let me know in the comments down below if you enjoyed it would be awesome if you could leave a like consider subscribing to the channel for more content like this join my discord server and follow me on my socials for some extra stuff and lastly check out my other discussion videos on champion groups if you haven't yet that's it for today though sorry again if i sound a little congested but thanks so much for watching and i'll see you again soon take care [Music] [Music] you

titre: Qiyana Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription: Qiyana is an attack damage burst assassin, who wields the environment to demolish any who may threaten her people. Focusing power through her blade the empress of the elements slashes, dashes, and grasses her way to the backline. The boulder the better, this is the Qiyana Champion Spotlight. Qiyana dashes a short distance and pulls one of three elements into her weapon. Water... ...stone... ...or grass. With an element equipped her attacks get stronger and her movement speed increases near the source of that element. Qiyana: If talent were an element perhaps I could throw some at them. In it's base state Qiyana's Q slashes an area dealing damage. When empowered with water, stone, or grass... ...her next Q launches an explosive elemental attack. Water roots enemies freezing them in their tracks. Stone crushes low health enemies with bonus damage. And grass creates a stealthed area lasting until Qiyana leafs. Qiyana: They'll never see me coming, what a shame Qiyana's Q resets when she draws a new element. If she uses an empowered Q she'll expend her current element and need to grab another. The empress deals bonus damage the first time she hits a unit. Equipping a new element resets the cooldown against all targets. Refreshing! Qiyana mows down here target dealing damage and dashinga a fixed distance towards them. That's it. That's the whole ability. Qiyana launches a shockwave knocking enemies back but dealing no damage. Don't take it for granite though. If the shockwave hits a wall... ...brush... ... or the river the element explodes after a delay stunning and damaging nearby enemies. Shockwaves that hit a wall radiate around the perimeter of the entire thing. Qiyana's high mobility and bust damage allow her to setup attacks for her teammates or single out and ice squishy opponents. Every fight is a blank slate. Improvise and swap elements to make enemies gravel at your feet. It's... elementary. Qiyana: They always said I was cold. This is your crowning moment. Master the elements take what's yours and win in a land slide. Kick grass and chew bubblegum. Water you waiting for?
