titre: Braum: Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription: Welcome to the League of Legends Champion Spotlight, featuring Braum, the Heart of the Freljord. Braum is a hero of the Freljord, a champion strongman famed for his selfless and legendary feats.  Once a simple goat herder,  Braum found an unbreakable door and uses it as a shield to protect man and creature alike on his journey through Runeterra.  In game, Braum is a tanky support with the ability to defend allies from a barrage of attacks  before hammering out his own immense crowd control abilities.  Whenever Braum attacks an enemy, he applies a stack of his passive: Concussive Blows.  If Braum dishes out four blows to the head, he deals a burst of magic damage and stuns his enemy.  For a brief period after, Braum's basic attacks against them deal extra magic damage,  although they won't receive additional stacks of Concussive Blows.  There are two things worth noting here: first, Braum’s allies can help trigger the stun with basic attacks and on-hit abilities.  Second, Braum can initiate Concussive Blows against multiple enemies simultaneously.  Braum’s Q is Winter’s Bite.  Braum punches his shield, sending out a frozen skillshot that damages and heavily slows the first enemy struck.  Winter’s Bite also applies Concussive Blows, giving him the opportunity to stun distant enemies.  Winter’s Bite is essential for gaining lane dominance.  Tag your opponents with it from range to give your marksman opportunities for great trades.  Braum’s W is Stand Behind Me.  A targeted ability that Braum can only use on himself and his friends, Stand Behind Me sends Braum leaping to an allied champion or minion,  giving him and his ally bonus armor and magic resistance.  Note that Braum will automatically stand between his target and the nearest enemy champion,  allowing him to effortlessly block skillshots with his awesome body. Seriously, check out those pecs.  Use Stand Behind Me whenever you’re separated from your allies.  Since it allows you to cross over terrain, you can keep yourself in a good position while buffing their defenses.  Unbreakable, Braum’s E, is his signature defensive ability.  On activation, Braum raises his shield in a target direction for a few seconds, blocking all damage from the first incoming attack or spell.  Any subsequent damage from that direction is reduced by a percentage while the shield remains active.  The most notable thing about Unbreakable is that Braum’s shield intercepts and stops all incoming projectiles,  even those targeted at allies behind him.  This allows Braum to tank damage and crowd control effects for his team.  Here’s one nuance worth pointing out: though Unbreakable doesn’t intercept non projectile abilities,  he does reduce the damage he takes provided his shield is facing the source of the damage.  For example, if Unbreakable faces the center of Zyra’s ultimate, Braum will take reduced damage, but he won’t prevent damage to his allies.  Braum can cast other abilities and move slightly faster while the shield is raised,  and keeps his shield pointed at the initial target direction regardless of his movement.  Finally, as awesome as Unbreakable is, it’s not awesome enough to block turret attacks or true damage.  Unbreakable can negate major area of effect spells for your teammates.  Save it for hard hitting ultimates to block off a barrage of attacks.  Braum’s ultimate is Glacial Fissure. Braum slams his shield into the ground, sending out a huge shockwave that knocks-up, damages and slows enemies in a line.  Enemy units immediately around Braum are knocked up significantly higher than enemies further away.  If there are no enemy champions immediately around Braum, the first champion in the shockwave is knocked up higher instead.  Glacial Fissure also leaves behind a large zone of ice that slows all enemies.  Glacial Fissure is great for disengaging from team fights. The ability creates a slow zone that will handicap your opponents, especially in the narrow corridors of the jungle.  Braum strikes his own destiny.  One of his many ability,  Stand Behind Me is an extremely versatile skill that has several offensive and defensive options when paired with the rest of his kit.   Let’s look at a few important plays you’ll need in order to become master of the mustache.  The first is blocking for an ally.  Determine what direction the most damage or crowd control is coming from,  then leap in to get between danger and your ally before using Unbreakable to disrupt the attack.  This is your basic defensive pattern for clutch saves, and requires good timing.  When assisting allies in a chase or aggressive play, use Stand Behind Me instead as a gap closer.  By choosing an ally closest to your enemy you can often put yourself in range to slow them with Winter's Bite.  Since the dash automatically places you on the side closest to your enemy, you’ll have an easier time lining up the skillshot.  You can also jump to allied minions, allowing for some tricky plays in lane.  Finally, if one of your friends gets caught out in a bad situation surrounded by enemy champions, Braum can come to the rescue.  Use Stand Behind Me to jump to the center of the action, then knock up and slow everyone around your ally with your ultimate.  This will give your team a chance to disengage, or allow other allies to step in and help if needed.  In lane, a successful Braum will constantly look for opportunities to stack his passive for a stun.  By tagging Janna with Winter’s Bite, he makes it easy for Quinn to follow up and pick her off with the bonus damage and stun.  Turning his attention to Vayne, he stacks Concussive Blows with another Q and basic attacks,  helping focus her down before she can land a killing blow on Quinn.  Braum is especially adept at helping allies survive ganks with his W and E.  As soon as Quinn is caught out,  he immediately uses Stand Behind Me to boost her defenses and help her survive focus fire from Pantheon and Vayne.  Note that Braum makes sure to stay behind Quinn while using Unbreakable so he can tank any attacks directed at her,  forcing Vayne to hit him instead as they retreat.  Braum’s Unbreakable completely negates damage from the first attack or spell that hits him,  so with careful timing you can effectively cancel your opponent’s most damaging attack.  Here, Braum blocks the gold card and the damage reduction gives him just enough defense to survive.  Braum excels when sticking close to his allies.  As soon as Twitch appears, Braum repositions to the front of his marksman, ready to not only intercept Twitch’s damage,  but also hit both enemies with Glacial Fissure. Rather than chase for kills, Braum sticks with Ezreal, and applies Concussive Blows to any of his targets.  When playing Braum, your goal isn’t so much to dive on the enemy as it is to keep your squishy friends unsquashed.  Stand Behind Me’s low cooldown allows Braum multiple chances to reposition and help allies in long teamfights.  In this example, Braum quickly finds himself singled out after committing to defend Garen.  He uses Stand Behind Me to jump to Miss Fortune, and although he dies, he helps MF pick up the triple kill.  A well-played Braum boldly throws himself in harm’s way to protect his friends.  When timed correctly, Unbreakable has the potential to shield allies against tons of damage.  With great positioning here, Braum turns a lethal AoE combo into more of a mild annoyance,  almost singlehandedly turning the tide of this fight.  Braum’s big heart needs a lot of protection.  Focus on health, armor, and magic resistance.  Tenacity is also especially helpful, since Unbreakable means Braum will be tanking crowd control meant for his allies.  Mix in cooldown reduction to maximize Braum’s own crowd control,  and you’ll have a heavy frontline specialist ready to defend against any amount of aggression or unexpected bear wrestling.  Thanks for tuning in to the Braum Champion Spotlight.  Please subscribe to the Riot Games YouTube channel and leave us your comments just below the video.

titre: Twitch: Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Bienvenue dans le Focus sur Twitch, Semeur de peste. Twitch est un carry AD à distance qui attaque ses ennemis avec Venin mortel et s'en sert pour les tuer. Que vous commenciez la partie en jungler ou en carry AD à distance, Twitch s'assure la victoire en attendant le bon moment pour assassiner ses cibles, ou en se jetant sur toute l'équipe ennemie. Le passif de Twitch est Venin mortel. Les attaques de base de Twitch l'appliquent jusqu'à un certain nombre d'effets cumulés. Chaque effet inflige des dégâts par seconde pendant quelques secondes. Embuscade permet à Twitch de se rendre invisible. Il est alors indétectable à l'œil nu. Mais la vision pure des Balises de vision, des tourelles et de l'Élixir de l'oracle peut le détecter. Tant qu'il est camouflé, Twitch a un bonus de vitesse de déplacement. Lorsqu'il cesse d'être camouflé, il obtient un bonus de vitesse d'attaque pendant quelques secondes. Il ne faut à Twitch qu'un court instant pour se camoufler, mais s'il est touché, le délai sera allongé. Prenez Embuscade tôt dans la partie et maximisez-la au niveau 13. Dose de venin projette un fût explosif qui ralentit et inflige deux effets Venin mortel à tous les ennemis touchés par l'explosion. Mettez un point dans Dose de venin assez vite et, si vous voulez utiliser le ralentissement pour tuer rapidement les ennemis, maximisez Dose de venin au niveau 13 au lieu d'Embuscade. Catalyse blesse tous les ennemis proches en fonction du nombre d'effets Venin mortel qu'ils ont cumulés. Catalyse n'inflige pas de dégâts aux cibles qui n'ont pas d'effet Venin mortel. Pour infliger autant de dégâts que possible, je recommande de maximiser Catalyse en premier. L'ultime de Twitch est Panique. Les attaques de base de Twitch traversent alors les cibles pour toucher tous les ennemis en ligne droite pendant quelques secondes. La cible devant vous subit les dégâts maximum, les ennemis subséquents des dégâts réduits jusqu'à un certain seuil. Chaque ennemi touché cumule un effet Venin mortel et tout autre effet à l'impact qu'a Twitch. En début de partie, Twitch devrait toujours être agressif et utiliser Venin mortel et Catalyse. Lorsqu'Alistar est sur Morgana, l'attaquer puis lancer Catalyse la contraint à utiliser Saut éclair pour se soigner. Avec Catalyse au niveau maximum et Dose de venin, le burst de Twitch peut infliger des dégâts assez étonnants. Evelynn a été repérée en sortant des hautes herbes, Twitch utilise Venin mortel puis Dose de venin et des attaques de base. Il la tue presque immédiatement avec Catalyse. Avec Embuscade, si Twitch est bien positionné, il peut surprendre les ennemis sur sa propre lane. Twitch va derrière un obstacle, utilise Embuscade et retourne sur ses pas une fois invisible. Twitch utilise le bonus de vitesse de déplacement pour rattraper Morgana avant la fin du camouflage et il la ralentit avec Dose de venin. Attaquer tout en se déplaçant permet à Twitch de faire des victimes faciles. Pour Twitch, la meilleure défense, c'est l'attaque. Après avoir repéré Orianna, Twitch utilise Embuscade pour faire une autre victime. Lorsqu'elle se retourne, Dose de venin et des attaques de base ajoutent des effets Venin mortel. Quand elle court, Panique permet à Twitch de rester à portée, la tuant ainsi avec Catalyse. Quand Morgana arrive, Twitch court mais il ne peut pas passer à côté de Xin Zhao sans le tuer. Entre deux attaques, Twitch s'éloigne de Morgana et fait un doublé alors qu'il ne lui reste que très peu de PV. L'un des pires ennemis de Twitch est la Balise de vision : elle le révèle alors qu'il est embusqué. Si Twitch vous pose des problèmes avec son camouflage, placez des Balises de vision dans les zones fréquentées. Au cours de la partie, vous pouvez sauter sur les ennemis qui farment seuls sur leur lane en attendant dans les hautes herbes et en utilisant Embuscade. Le Jalon de vision de Morgana n'a repéré qu'Alistar, elle s'aventure pour le tuer. Dose de venin et des attaques de base appliquent des effets Venin mortel, Twitch utilise Catalyse et fait une nouvelle victime. Une astuce avec Embuscade consiste à l'activer juste avant d'utiliser Rappel. Twitch sera camouflé pendant le Rappel, il sera totalement invisible le temps qu'il retourne à la base.
00:04:01.800,00:03:05.100
Assurez-vous qu'Embuscade dure assez longtemps pour couvrir la canalisation de Rappel. Être bien placé et utiliser Panique assurent à Twitch une victoire dans les gros combats. Xin Zhao saute sur Twitch, lequel utilise Saut éclair puis Panique et inflige des dégâts impressionnants aux deux ennemis étourdis, réalisant ainsi un doublé. Soyez prudent si vous affrontez des ennemis qui vous pourchassent ou vous empêchent de fuir. Alors que Twitch ralentit l'équipe adverse avec Dose de venin et inflige de lourds dégâts avec Panique, il est projeté en l'air, ralenti et étourdi, il ne peut pas s'échapper. La priorité de Twitch est de survivre et de s'occuper des dangers imminents, en se servant de Panique pour toucher les ennemis derrière sa cible principale. Twitch s'échappe avec Saut éclair et Embuscade, puis s'en prend à Renekton et à Evelynn. Après avoir tué l'assassin, Twitch peut tout à fait tuer ses ennemis à distance, faisant même un triplé. Jouez Twitch pour qu'il inflige de lourds dégâts et n'oubliez pas la résistance pour survivre au début. Pour les runes, essayez des marques de dégâts d'attaque fixes, des sceaux d'armure fixe, des glyphes de résistance magique fixe et des quintessences de dégâts d'attaque fixes. Pour les maîtrises, 21/9/0 avec toutes les augmentations de dégâts physiques dans Attaque et celles d'armure et de PV dans Défense. Pour les sorts d'invocateur, Embrasement, Saut éclair, Châtiment et Fatigue sont les meilleurs. Châtiment pour jungler, Embrasement ou Fatigue pour les joueurs agressifs et Saut éclair pour les plus prudents. Prenez les Bottes de vitesse et des Potions de soin puis deux Lames de Doran ou une Lame du prospecteur. Améliorez les bottes en Jambières du berzerker puis prenez la Lame d'infini et le Sceptre vampirique. Choisissez ensuite Danseur fantôme pour augmenter vos dégâts et votre mobilité, Dernier souffle contre les ennemis qui ont beaucoup d'armure et Soif-de-sang ou Épée sanglante pour augmenter vos dégâts en burst avec Catalyse. Prenez enfin Voile de la banshee ou Voile d'Odyn contre les mages qui essaient de vous tuer. Merci d'avoir regardé le Focus sur Twitch. Abonnez-vous à la chaîne YouTube de Riot Games et laissez-nous vos commentaires sous la vidéo !

titre: BEST Champions to CLIMB with in EVERY Role in Season 2026 League of Legends
transcription: Today we are taking a look at three of the best champions to climb with in every role in season 2026. So, if you're struggling to rank up and are considering picking up a new champion to play, hopefully this video can help you out. Starting off with the top lane and our first pick is going to be Irelia, who's had an amazing season so far and has posted very impressive success rates in both of the solo lanes. She is definitely more appealing towards higher ranks, but is one of those picks that can carry you higher than you've ever been before if you can learn to play her well enough and understand how to really abuse a lead with her. Her early kill potential with her passive can be the real key to unlocking her snowballing power and before you know it, you'll be diving your enemy on repeat, rapidly becoming out of control and when more enemies come to try and shut you down, you can just take them out, too. Then we have Ornn, one of the most consistent tanks in the game right now and the perfect addition to pretty much any team comp. His unique long-range engage potential opens up avenues to make your mark on any game and his kit has a surprising amount of skill expression, too, with bouncy combos to learn and all sorts of little tricks to outplay your enemies. So, with what seems like a straightforward tank is actually one that you can really feel yourself mastering and when his stats are as good as they are so far this season, there's really no reason not to play him. Realistically, everybody loves having an Ornn on their team. A big, solid tank in the top lane who's always useful, no matter what. Then we have Xayah, who might not have the easiest lane phase in the world, but when you get to the point where you have enough items and levels to do your thing, he feels so fun and undeniably strong. Xayah is seeing a lot more action in the jungle now, too, after his buffs last patch. So, if you do learn him, you can play him in both roles. He's really just all about going god mode in extended fights and taking on multiple enemies at once with his constant DPS and sustain. And if you pull off a big AoE ultimate in team fights, you can clean up entire teams of enemies with not too much risk due to his passive allowing you to get back into the fight again. Well, that's if your enemies actually have the ability to take you down first. Moving into the jungle next and first up we have the king of the assassins, Kha'Zix, who's having an amazing season so far, especially so in the last few weeks. Kha'Zix is just all about that isolation damage and running around the map leaping and stealthing around from enemy to enemy and one-shotting them without too much response. He just has a ridiculous amount of damage once he gets fed and with his ability to snowball so quickly, he ends up getting very fed pretty often. He's one of those champions that just feels purely designed for solo queue. He might not be the most effective when he gets behind, but with a few early kills under his belt, he can take over games and put them to bed before they even get started. Then we have Rek'Sai, who's also had an incredible season so far and her stats have been actually pretty ridiculous and she's maintained some of the highest win rate stats for any jungler all year so far. One of the best things about Rek'Sai is her ability to create unconventional gank paths to bypass wards and get behind enemies. And although she can sometimes feel a little bit reliant on flash to actually make those ganks work out, the main thing to think about is tracking enemies' flashes and summoners instead because a tunnel into your knock-up is pretty much free kills if your enemies don't have mobility. Then once you get those free kills, you can deal so much damage when you start snowballing. Rek'Sai has always been an underrated pick in the jungle. So, if you want free LP for a jungler that's never picked or banned, give her a go. Then finally, we have to talk about Xin Zhao because after his buffs last patch, he's now up there with the big guns. Now, Xin Zhao is very easy to play and his whole playstyle revolves around skirmishing and stat-checking other junglers. All this guy wants to do is get stuck in. He clears easily, he ganks effectively and when he gets fed, he gets pretty scary very quickly. Xin is one of those junglers you should always have in your champion pool for when the match-up calls for it because if you go against picks, let's say for example Rengar, you pick Xin Zhao and you remove Rengar's ability to play the game. Moving into the mid lane next then and first up we have Ahri, who also had buffs last patch and she was already popular and already strong and now she's basically one of the best mid laners in the game. Ahri is a super versatile champion and she can basically be picked into any team comp and she always has a way to come out on top into any match-up, too. She's got solid and consistent wave clear, she can push and shove and roam with her ganking power with her ultimate. She's also not too bad in one-v-ones and jewels if she consistently lands poke before dashing in with a charm and a good Ahri player can basically roam around the map, pick enemies off, rinse and repeat this process, getting super strong and can easily take over games very quickly. Speaking of taking over games, our next pick is Talon and he's definitely a lot more niche than some other mid lane picks and assassins in the meta right now, but if you can utilize his ability to completely decimate the map with his roaming power, you're going to get super fed and you're going to be jumping over walls into enemies, one-shotting them before they have a chance to react. Going against a good Talon player is extremely scary, so why not become that good Talon player yourself? You'll be rewarded in a lot of LP. Then we have a bit of an underrated pick in the mid lane, but one that just makes sense when you think about it in Vex. Vex's strengths are incredibly clear. She is the perfect pick against high mobility assassins, which are being played in abundance right now in season 2026. Her win rate in emerald and above is around 54% plus and despite that, she still has a pretty low pick rate. So, the general player base might be missing out on how strong she can be. She can easily shut down any attempts for her enemies to fight her with her fear on her W, which is basically impossible to miss. She has a ton of poke and wave clear and her ultimate allows her to get around the map and resets her way to easy wins. So, if you have been forgetting about Vex recently, here's a reminder, she's a great option to go for. Now, let's move down into the bot lane and of course everybody knows who I'm going to say first, it's Jinx, who's easily up there with some of the best champions in the entire game to play right now, not just in the bot lane. Playing Jinx is all about aiming for that power spike where you have enough items to start running away with games and I mean literally running away with games with that passive movement speed in team fights because all it takes is one kill to start farming resets and get the rockets on the go and depending on how many items you have so far and what support you have in your pocket, before you know it, you'll be focused on recording more pentakills for your YouTube career. There's a reason why Jinx has always been so popular because she's just so much fun to play and when she's this strong, why wouldn't you want to play her? Then up next we have Lucian, who has flown a little bit more under the radar compared to how broken he was at the start of the year. After the nerfs to Essence Reaver, people have forgotten just how good he can be. I mean, his pick rate is still pretty high, but nothing compared to the likes of Jinx or Kai'Sa. But when you're in the groove on Lucian, it just feels so rewarding to play him. You can easily poke and bully lanes in the early game and with a good support beside you, you can get super fed very quickly. And as long as you can manage his lower range compared to some other AD carries, as long as you know how to get in and deal your damage without getting blown up, it just feels so good carrying games on this champion. Then finally, we have Miss Fortune and everybody knows just what she's good at. It's all about burst, poke and then playing around her ultimate. Space gliding is not really a thing when you play Miss Fortune, but blowing up entire teams in just a few clicks definitely is. And I think that anyone can pick up Miss Fortune and play her pretty quickly. She's easy, she's fun and especially so at lower ranks, people just do not respect her damage whatsoever. All it takes is one clumped up, grouped up team fight in a choke point, one good ultimate and you're probably going to win the game. Finally, for support, our first pick to talk about today just has to be Thresh and what a season he's having so far. He was already good for the majority of last season and this year he's arguably even better. Now, I can genuinely talk about Thresh all day because of just how fun and creative and versatile he is. He's got hooks, he's got lanterns, he's got flays that you can use in all sorts of different ways and of course some nice AoE CC and a bit of extra damage with his ultimate. When you're in the flow when you're playing this guy, you're landing hooks, you're perfectly timing those flays to disrupt enemy engages, he just feels like the most perfectly designed support. You can make your mark on games both offensively and defensively equally and there's nothing better than saving teammates in clutch situations with the perfect lanterns. I will always think that Thresh is one of the most enjoyable and rewarding picks in the game you can play. So, if you haven't tried him yet or at least haven't tried him again recently, this season he really is a fantastic choice. Up next, we have Nami, who's always been known as the jack-of-all-trades support and for good reason because her kit is incredibly versatile and to be honest with how good she is right now, you could basically pick her in every single game and always have an impact. It doesn't matter who you're paired up with in the bot lane, it doesn't really matter who you're against or what your team comp needs. Nami will always be useful because she has everything you need in her kit. From perfectly timed bubbles and tsunamis with her ultimate to simply buffing up your AD carry or even AP carry's burst damage in the lane phase. She's got poke, she can bully lanes, she can sustain and as long as you can keep yourself alive with some good positioning, you just can't go wrong. Then finally, we of course have to mention Sona in this video because what a season she's had. Her stats have been crazy and she's just been winning a ridiculous amount of games throughout every single rank. It doesn't matter what region, what rank you are or what rank you're trying to get to, Sona will always be a good addition. Now, she might not be very difficult to play and she might not have quite as much skill expression as some other picks, the amount of impact she'll have on any game is without question. The buffs, the boosts and sustain and scaling just make her such a powerful pick. So, if that laid-back enchanter playstyle is the one you want to go for, make sure you try Sona if you haven't already. Well, there you have it, guys. That's three fantastic picks to play in every single role so far in season 2026. You can find builds and runes for all champions featured in this video by clicking the links in the description and we'll catch you all in the next video. Take care.

titre: Smolder Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Long ago
before the ruination ended their line. The Royal Family of Camavor revered Imperial dragons
as powerful and majestic creatures. So as one of the few Imperial dragons still alive today, 
Smolder is kind of a big deal. Or he will be at least as soon as he
masters flying, and breathing fire, and being big, and scary. You know, dragon stuff.
Smolder: "Sizzle!" The fiery fledgling is an ADC
who's all about scaling. He may seem like a tiny spark at first,
but he can grow into a late game inferno. So you ready to play with fire? Welcome to the Smolder Champion Spotlight. Smolder: "Go Smolder! Go Smolder!" Smolder’s passive is "Dragon Practice". Whenever he hits a champion
with an ability or kills a target with Q,
he learns a bit more about Dragoning and gains a stack. Each stack increases
the damage of his basic abilities, making them extra spicy
as he gets more practice. This means Smolder gets stronger
and stronger as the game goes on, so let him cook. Q is Smolder's "Super Scorcher Breath". He spits the biggest, awesomest fireball
he can muster at his target. Okay, maybe it's not that impressive yet,
but you just wait. [Draven laughs]
Smolder: "You started it!" As Smolder gains
more stacks of his passive, he enhances super scorcher
breath in three tiers. At Tier 1,
it deals AOE damage around the target. At Tier 2, more explosions
shoot off behind the initial fireball. And at Tier 3, the flame burns targets
for additional true damage over a few seconds. If they drop below a certain health
threshold in that time, they're toast. Don't forget that Smolder gains stacks
whenever he hits enemy champs with abilities, or kills unit with Q. Focus on last hitting
and poking opponents with Q, so he reaches those stack tiers
as quickly as possible. By the time late game rolls around, he'll be a teamfight
terror with tons of AoE damage. Breathing fire
isn't without its challenges, though. Like all that soot in your nose.
With W smolder sneezes out a fiery glob this blast damages and briefly slows targets and explodes
around any champions in the line of fire. And you thought your allergies were bad
[Smolder laughing] During laning phase, "Achooo!" is a crucial farming tool
for Smolder. He can use it to soften up a wave
and last it with Q for all the stacks. In teamfights,
it's a powerful AoE spell, hitting multiple champions
sets up explosions around each one, which scale
with his passive and ability rank. That late game
damage is nothing to sneeze at. Don't you just love his little wings? Well, they aren't just for princely charm. Smolder's E, "FLAP, FLAP, FLAP",
sends him into the air for a short duration where he ignores
terrain and gains, moves speed and vision. He'll bombard the closest enemy in range
with bursts of flame. Prioritizing the lowest health champion. Smolder’s E can help him
catch fleeing enemies and secure kills, but it's also his only escape tool. So fly with caution
or you may crash and burn. Smolder: "What did I do?" Behind every adorable baby dragon
is a super protective mama dragon. Smolder’s ult summons "MMOOOMMMM!" to teach a lesson
to any meanies picking on her son, a face melting, scorched earth
kind of lesson. After casting,
Smolder’s mom flies overhead, breathing down a wave of fire
and burning enemies to a crisp. Targets caught in the center
take extra damage and are slowed. The wave starts a little bit behind Smolder’s position
and continues over a long distance. Anyone who might be hit by the flames
will hear Mom's roar as a warning, so best run for cover if you can. Nothing burns quite like a mother's love. While the spell can help
smolder disengage if he's in a bad spot, his mom is best at starting fires. I mean, fights, especially
if the enemy team is grouped up. Slow them down with a center of Smolder’s ult,
then go full blast with the rest of his AoE damage. Smolders Dragon Practice stacks are the fuel for turning
this glowing ember into a roaring blaze. He needs to farm safely in the early game
to stack his passes and improve his Q before he's ready to take on the world. That means he'll need a more lane dominant support looking out for him
while he grows big and strong. Champions who can zone opponents off
will make great lane partners for his widdle kiddo. On the other hand, he may struggle
against lane bullies who can force him to retreat and lose out on stacks.
Without much Dragon Practice, his ability to trade is limited,
so enemies with lots of poke and engage tools are blistering
counter picks against Smolder. Where he really burns
brightest is teamfighting. Thanks to his powerful
AoE damage, roasting grouped up enemies is his specialty, but like most ADCs,
he needs to position wisely to stay safe. Fly in solo,
and you might just fizzle out. Smolder is designed to be a simpler marksman
with an approachable kit. So if you've been looking for a reason
to learn ADC, who burns hotter than 80ºC, consider this a warm invitation
to try the Fiery Fledgling. Likewise, if you enjoy marching with
their own escape tools like Ezreal or Zeri, or adorable forces of destruction
like Tristana and Kog’Maw, you may want to take smolder
for a test flight. And of course, if you love getting stacks
on stacks on stacks, looking at you, Veigar and Nasus fans, then the scaling damage should be
just what you're looking for. Phew, is it toasty in here or just me? I got to go cool off. Learn how to train your dragon
at the links below, and bring the heat
with Smolder, the Fiery Fledgling.

This just goes to show how strong a poke mage can be in the bot lane when your enemies don't actually know how to dodge. Bran support is just not really played very much at higher ranks, but in bronze and silver, he's one of the best picks you can play. He's a lot of fun to play, and you can carry super hard, too, as you basically just turn into another AP carry if you get ahead during the lane phase. So, spam those spells and enjoy watching your enemies burn into crispy little piles of LP. For gold, we have Sona with a 52.6% win rate. Now, Sona is just undeniably impactful just by existing. So many games you can see a bad pike or bad Thresh just whiff everything and do nothing. But with Sona, you don't really have anything to miss. And all you have to do is not int and you'll be super valuable to your AD carry and your team. She brings sustain, scaling, utility, and AoE crowd control. And in goal, with so many A rams and extended team fights happening from the 15minute mark until the end of the game, she'll bring so much to your team come. For plats, we've got Milio with a 53.3% win rate. Milio really has been shining recently, and he's pretty easy to play, but his utility is so strong. He makes all the popular AD carries and plat just better and much harder to punish. and enemy players here just don't respect what he brings in his kit. Medio is one of those picks that can turn any doomed fight into a win. So when your enemies overcommit, this is when he's at his best. In Emerald, we have the solo Q queen in Janna with a 52.4% win rate. Janna has been one of the most consistent picks to play in ranked for so many years now, and around Emerald seems to be where she's at her best. She can chill in lane in games where she needs to, like when she's partnered up with an unstable racist straven, or she can roam like a psycho and actually carry the map pretty hard instead. She can turn any team fight with a few clutch tornadoes. And her ultimate can outplay fights and dives more often than not. In Diamond, we have Nami with a 52.1% win rate, and she's been one of the best support picks in the game all season. Patches come and go, but Nami still stays on top, and that's been the theme of this year. And we could have put Nami for many of the ranks in this video. But in Diamond, she is very popular and super successful. From big bubbles, welltimed waves, or just boosting and buffing up her carries, she's fun, easy to play, and with good positioning and good timings can go from decent to delicious. Finally, for master tier and above, you know it, it's going to have to be Bard. With a 53.3% win rate, a good bard is just so much fun to watch with big clutch stuns in the lane phase, roaming, and creative plays with big ults and magical tunnels full of LP, and so many ways to impact games rather than just AFKing in the lane phase and hoping for the best. High elo bar players have been abusing his kit for many years now. And when he's as strong as he has been recently, there's just simply no better option to carry with. But also, no other champion can compare to how fun this chime chap can be. Well, there you have it, guys. That's some of the best champions you can play in every rank, and every role to end the season 2025 on a high. Don't forget to head to mobileitics.gg to see builds and runes and guides for every single champion, and we'll see you all in the next one. Thanks for watching, and as always, guys, good luck and take care.

titre: The BEST Designed Champions in League Of Legends
transcription: A popular topic of discussion on my channel entails criticism towards badly designed things in this game. Whether unfairly overpowered items to 200 years champions to abilities that cause more harm than good to reworks that may have unnecessarily cross the line. While they're certainly entertaining given how often people enjoy complaining about the game in its entirety, I feel like it would be disingenuous and unreasonably cynical of me to not acknowledge the good parts of the game every so often. In light of that for today, I want to pick five champions who, in my personal opinion, are excellent additions to the game for offering a strong balance of player agency, opportunity for counterplay, a clear purpose and role throughout all parts of the game from laning phase to the final team fight, and how well thought out the abilities are. Keep in mind, there are obviously more than five, but for brevity sake, I'm only going to talk about five of them. After watching, you're more than welcome to share your own personal list of best designed champions if you like, but without further ado, let's begin. The most absurd thing about Bard is that while public opinion towards him is generally quite positive these days, people hated him when he first came out. He was one of the least popular champions on release. In fact, having one of the worst initial launches of any champion based on memory, only around like a 4% pick rate, whereas most champions have at least 10. A lot of that had to do with them being arguably the most unusual support champion to come out back in the day. Atypical play styles are diamond dozen in this day and age, nearing the point where a quote unquote normal champion like Unara is the unicorn of the group. But Barton was one of the first in a new generation of supports to come out and his perman roaming play style coupled with his unique abilities made him an acquired taste. However, people have warmed up to him over the years and in retrospect many would consider him one of the best designed champions in the game. Bard is classified as a jack of all trades support, someone who can do a little bit of everything depending on the situation. He has decent damage output, especially towards the mid to late game when you stock up on chimes. And the whole incentive behind Traveler Skull makes it so bard can apply good pressure throughout the map without forfeiting too much in experience. Taxing the player's ability to find a clear balance between roaming and laning. Cosmic binding is one of the most clever ways to design a strong stun, lining things up to where Bard throws a queue at a priority target standing between him and a wall or other entity. On its own, it's a fairly harmless projectile that does passable damage, but through good positioning and awareness, it becomes a deadly hard CC tool, even stronger than that of a Lux Binding or Zyra roof. Awareness is ultimately the running theme of his entire play style. Each of his abilities require good awareness in game sense to be used to their fullest efficiency, giving Bard a very open-ended play style with a lot of potential impact. Caretaker Shrine serving as health packs for himself and his team. Magical journey opening up tons of creative entry points for him and his team to engage or retreat. and Tempered Fate being a potential team fight deciding ultimate if used correctly. I think that's what makes him so fun to play as well. All of his abilities can be used in offensive or defensive ways, making him one of the most versatile supports in the game. There's just enough guarantee of value to make him not completely useless. And at the same time, the reward Bard gets from his successful ability usage is significant enough to be satisfying while not completely shutting down the enemy team's options. Rarely if ever do I see someone complain about Bard outside of the occasional comment of him being annoying with his poke damage. Whenever I see an enemy Bard land a clean tempered fade, I have to give credit where it's due. The fact that he can do so much in a way that isn't just healing or shielding the team is why people respect him. Magical journey and tempered fate are awesome playmaking tools despite not doing any damage at all. Last but not least, he's a very stable champion. From what I remember, he usually hangs just above a 50% win rate, and there's nar need to buff or nerf him either due to results hinging on the player's decision-making, not how strong or weak his numbers are. All in all, Bard is one of the most ironically well-designed champions in League. Next champion is going to be Oriana. Though she may not quite be on the same level of eccentricity as Bard, longtime players can definitely appreciate what she brings to the table. The best I can sum up Oriana in a single word would be reliable. She's a reliable mid laner. Anytime I see an Oriana on my team conceptually, it's reassuring as she can be a good compliment for all manner of team compositions. Just like Bard, Oriana's got a bit of everything that can be used in a wide variety of situations. She has consistent damage output through command attack and dissonance, positioning her ball to attack enemies. Chaining her Q and W together leads to solid burst trade DPS. More importantly though, dissonance offers an electric field that speeds up allies and slows enemies down, affording Oriana, a modum of area control, which can be great for both parts offense and defense. The ball itself also exerts continuous pressure to the enemy team on account of the constant threat of command shock wave. One of the OG wombo combo ultimates that even to this day is greatly feared for what it can do. You'll find no shortage of clips that showcase how devastating it can be with the right team or honestly even just by itself. Oriana can use it to start a team fight engage, especially if the enemy team doesn't pay attention to where it is. She can follow up on a teammate's attack by attaching the ball to them for a wombo combo, or she can use it to buy her team time to disengage from a fight. All four of her abilities have applications in aggression and defense. Even her command protect as she can put in on someone like a J4 or Malight who's engaging to lead into the affformentioned followup shockwave. No matter what the situation, there's always something she can do to apply pressure to the enemy team or assist her own team, making her a very consistent champion. All the same, Oriana never feels unbearably oppressive to deal with. None of her abilities compensate for her weaknesses in a way that causes her to come off overpowered. She has just enough windows of vulnerability for poor execution to punish her, and her effective range isn't ridiculously high. Though she's considered a burst mage, her burst damage is on the lower end in exchange for all the other stuff that she has. One could also argue she's gotten power or feature craft over the years, one with the oneshot high mobility meta we're in. But the fact that she's consistently had good presence in proplay and solo queue across all ranks and given her low balance volatility outside of maybe an extra 5% AP or a few points of armor added or subtracted here and there. Oriana by all accounts is one of the most stable mid laners in the game. Just enough playmaking potential to feel good to play, just enough weaknesses to feel beatable by enough champions and just enough utility and agency to wear multiple hats for her team. She is more than deserving of a spot on this list. Everyone knows it wouldn't be a best design champions video without the golden boy of League of Legends, Jyn, who many would claim is the single best design champion in League. The sheer amount of time, attention, effort, manpower, and promotion that went behind this guy puts every other cast member to shame even 9 years later. I can't remember the last time Riot Games went above and beyond for this guy. Jyn is a testament to how good champions can get when designers have the freedom and resources to think everything through. From a thematic point of view, I love how they were able to engineer a functioning and practical kit while managing to adhere to the number four as a concept. Auto attacks counted to four with a fixed attack speed, but they compensated by making all of them fire at twice or even thrice the power of a traditional marksman. The four shot in particular being a built-in execute, no less. Dancing grenade bounces on up to four enemies, getting stronger on the next hit if it kills the target prior. Four enemies is a workable target cap for this kind of attack. Deadly flourish marks enemies for 4 seconds and is a longrange route. Fantastic utility for a class that seldom has any to begin with. Captive audience reveals enemies for four seconds and is good for scanning out potential areas and setting up for routes. Finally, curtain call has four shots, emulating his basic attack style, with the fourth one being a huge bullet that can potentially do thousands of damage. More than being a shining example of how taking their time leads to an incredible champion, Jyn's a shining example of divergent design. ADCs are characterized for their high DPS output and fast attacks. Jyn, on the other hand, specializes in quality over quantity, pumping out comparable DPS, but in a smaller number of shots. They were able to take that concept and add that to the rest of his kit, making it difficult to land each of his abilities to their fullest extent. But if you can do it, the reward is oh so sweet. That very execution check is why a few people complain about how strong Jyn really is. Dancing grenade can be one of the highest damaging basic abilities in the game. Amplified even further by the fact that a 150% ratio is way more deadly on a marksman with 800 AD than one with only 250 or 300. Deadly flourish is one of the longest range and longest lasting routes in the game. Captive audience traps can dish out more damage than team mushrooms and curtain call is basically four kalin ultimates in one. However, the conditions behind each of them make them just difficult enough to not be a guaranteed win button. And even if they are, it's still within the range of what a marksman is expected to do. One thing I haven't really talked about too much in Bard and Oriana sections, even though it also applies to them, is the satisfaction of playing a champion playing an integral role in their design as well. Jin is one of the most stylish champions in the game experientially speaking. Whether an AAM just messing around are playing in challenger solo Q, the feeling of executing a target with your fourth shot, landing a crucial W at max range, or lining up that final curtain called bullet perfectly will give any player a grin, especially when you see gigantic numbers to go along with it. There probably will never be a champion as brilliantly designed as Jyn for years more to come. Similarly to Jyn, no best designed champion list would be complete without giving mention to Thresh, the best designed support in history. At a time before Riot openly gave support champions actual carry potential in the form of DPS and whatnot, Thresh was seen as one of the few supports who could legitimately carry games. Considering the period in which he came out, he was honestly far ahead of his time, being one of the earliest case studies of a champion whose skin has equal impact in offense or defense, leaving the onus on the player to figure out how to best use it. Open-ended abilities are my favorite type of skill since there's an opportunity cost involved. Not only giving you more play style variety, but also placing a cap on how far you can take that ability. It's a win-win situation. The champion gets to do more things and the opposing team doesn't have to worry about that ability being frustratingly effective. Champions who can only do full offense full sent into offense and vice versa. In the case of Thrush, it's why he's one of the most engaging champions to play and play against. Death sentence can be used as both an engage tool and a lockdown option. Dark passage could be used to usher a teammate into a prime position to attack, or it can be used to help a teammate escape a bad situation. Blake can be used to push enemies away from you or knock them closer to you. Lastly, the box can be used to trap enemies, preventing them from running away, or it can be set up as a means of cover control if your team is trying to run instead. Precious kit's also designed to chain consecutive abilities one after another despite set abilities being functionable on their own. For example, death sentence can be used to engage into the enemy team. Then he can dark passage an ally to follow him where he can then flee anyone who tries to run away and then trap them in the box. Conversely, Thresh can use death sentence to stop enemies from attacking his team, throw dark passage to his teammate who is being attacked, then fling away any pursuers before setting down the box to impede them further. Alternatively, you can always use each ability separately whenever they're demanded of you. He's just a solid, well-rounded champion with good control and engage, but not too strong to where he feels obnoxious to deal with. Other supports might specialize more heavily in one aspect or another like Blisscrank is way better at singling out targets and locking them down or Alistar being a better front line, but having that kind of option select on a support champion especially is why everyone respects Thresh. There's a clear difference between a good and a bad one and he's without question one of those champions who can tangibly impact the game by himself. Last but not least, Fiddlesticks. Much like how Jin was a combination of thoughtful in-game design and great visual execution with a bit of promotion, Fiddlesticks was a well-received and advertised champion despite getting one of the smallest Vichus in League in terms of actual gameplay changes. On paper, his QWE and Ultimate had the same end goal after being changed. It's mostly how they reached the end goal that changed, but in more constructive and nuanced ways. Terravi, for example, can now be triggered on multiple targets if you remain hidden for a short period of time before attacking with an AoE ability like Reap or Crowtorm. Bountiful harvest is the same as drain, only you can now give the big suck to multiple targets, which was the biggest inongruity in his old kit. Finn was always about big team fight damage, and yet his main damaging ability can only really apply to one person. The jury's out on whether dark wind is better than Reap, considering one's more of a neutral tool with higher damage output against less enemies, not to mention being point-and-click, while the other is more immediate. But in the context of Viddle's current kit, immediiacy takes precedence, I would say, because ultimately that's what Fiddle's MO is. He doesn't share the same play style versatility as the other four I mentioned, which could lead to some people disagreeing with him being welldesigned as he's a onetrick pony and all, but the way he goes about achieving that one trick is what makes him so good. Piddle six is all about catching the enemy team offguard and overwhelming them before they get a chance to do anything about it. Every part of his kin is best used in a front-loaded manner, onslaughting his targets with a surprise crowtorm before chain CCing them and hopefully killing them during that time. Done correctly, he single-handedly wins team fights and by extension the game. But if the enemy team sees him coming andor can respond to his engage, in other words, they don't fear him, his pressure diminishes considerably, which is a point. Little six relies on literally jump scaring you and killing you in that time. His old kit wasn't very good at that considering how slow he was to go about it. In response, they ranged it to make him much more frontloaded at the expense of everything else. The way he plays carries a subtle yet doineering effect in any game he's in. players have to now position themselves and navigate the rift in ways that for his team give him the best angle of attack or for the opposing team stops him from doing so. It's that implicit macro pressure that adds to his character as a champion. He's all about fear both physically and psychologically. A team who doesn't respect him and plays accordingly gets punished and a team who does can deal with him very easily, making games involving him quite interesting. Satisfying for his team when he lands that killer threeman ultimate or emboldening for the enemy team when they successfully thwart his attempt at doing so. For that reason, I find Fiddlesticks to be a spectacularly designed champion design and experience-wise. Like I said, there's obviously way more than five champions, and I would be more than happy to make a follow-up episode on this topic again if you're interested. For the time being, though, be sure to let me know what your five best design champions are in the comments down below. And as always, if you enjoyed the video, a like and subscribe would be much appreciated. Don't forget to follow me on Twitter, Varsfire, join my Discord server, and why not check out my video on the worst design champions if you haven't yet. Until next time, thanks so much for watching and I look forward to seeing you again soon. Take care. [Music]

titre: Who is the LEAST Popular Champion in League of Legends?
transcription: 7 years ago, I made a video on the topic of the most and least popular champions in the game. Back then in 2018, the bottom five least popular champions were Corki, Scarnner, Aurelion Soul, Rexi, and taking the crown in last place was Ivvern. 7 years later, Riot has released 31 more playable champions, as well as multiple reworks. But despite that, the bottom of the most played champions list hasn't changed that much. Three of the five are actually still there. However, Ivvern is no longer the least popular champion. He's now the fifth least popular champion. And Scarnner, who was previously on the list, received a rework in 2024. So today, he's no longer the fourth least played champion. Instead, in the last 30 days, Escarnner is the absolute least popular champion in the game. Not only is he an unpopular pick right now, but he's also on a negative win rate. In the last 30 days, Escarnner has the lowest win rate in the game at 43%. We won't go into the weeds for every champion today, but for some context, the Scarnner rework was actually successful initially. However, over time, some aspects were a bit stronger than Ride would have liked, and it seems Scarnner became a victim to a sequence of nerfs that put him in a less than favorable position. Anyway, the other champion that's still in the bottom five seven years later is Rexi. A very consistent champion as all this time later, Rexi is still the second least popular champion in the game. And similar to Scarnner, Rexi also has a negative win rate, currently sitting at 48%. Rexi is one of those champions that's in a unique spot as in general they're not popular, but when she's in a strong position, she becomes a popular pick in professional games. So, Riot has to balance keeping her in a state that's fair for both pro games and your average solo Q games. And often the easiest solution for Riot in these situations is just to nerf the champion so nobody wants to play them at all. And I should mention a low win rate it doesn't always correlate into low popularity. One of the most popular champions in the game, Ezreal, is currently in a rough spot and he has a 47% win rate. Despite that fact, Ezreal is currently the 12th most played champion. But okay, the list of the bottom 10 least played champions are Olaf, Carthus, Bellevth, Cassiopia, Ranada, Ivvern, Taric, Kalista, Rexi, and Scarnner. Both Corki and Aurelian Soul were in the bottom five 7 years ago. But today, they're still pretty unpopular, but nowhere near the bottom. And when looking at the win rates of the least popular champions, only four out of the bottom 10 have a positive win rate. Ironically, this is almost the same ratio for the top 10 most popular champions as well. Unsurprisingly, this features champions like Lux, Jinx, Yasuo, and Misfortune, who are historically almost mascots for League of Legends in general. Additionally, in case you're curious about this kind of comparison, the top 10 most played champions at the moment have a total of 147 skins. This is including Yunara, who just came out. But if you replace her with the 11th most played champion, Lee Sin, this number goes up to 165. And now, if we come back to the bottom of the list, all 10 of these champions have a combined skin count of 65. Literally 100 less skins. And Olaf is actually carrying here as he has 11 of the 65 skins. It's probably just a coincidence, but the bottom two champions each have five skins respectively despite being decently old champions. So for the conspiracy theorists who think Riot only buffs champions for skin sales, it definitely gives you something to think about. Escarnner specifically not only has a few amount of skins, but he's also gone the longest time without receiving a skin. As of making this video, it's been over 1,700 days since Scarnner received a new skin. That's almost 5 years. I'm starting to think Riot hates this guy. The only other champion that's gone that long without receiving a new skin is Alar. However, it's not as bad because he has a total of 14 skins. But the metric of games played isn't the only way to determine a champion's popularity. Because a champion like Kaisa, who's incredibly popular and is currently the third most played champion, only has a ban rate of 2.3%. So, she's almost always available to be picked. Meanwhile, champions like Lux, Caitlyn, and Yasuo are both some of the most played champions in the game, but at the same time have very high ban rates. For an additional example, Shako is currently the 36th most played champion, but he's the number one most banned champion with a 40% ban rate. So, in 40% of all ranked games, Shako isn't even getting a chance to get played. Despite the fact that he sits at a 49.4% 4% win rate. He's not necessarily stronger than other champions, but he's kind of in a special case where his play style causes some players to decide that they never want to see Shako in their games ever again. Both on their team and the enemy team. Because we all know Shako on the enemy team is the most annoying, strongest champion ever. But suddenly when he's on your team, he's died six times 10 minutes into the game. Sometimes though, champions that are low win rate and unpopular actually end up shining regardless in professional games. Ironically, the opposite thing happens too. Popular champions like Teemo, Shico, Garen, just to name a few, barely ever get chosen in pro matches. This is of course because it's almost a completely different game when five players are communicating every move they make. Instead of five independent monkeys all pulling the levers down at the same time, hoping that things work out. For example, with Scarnner, despite his current state, he was still picked 13 times during the recent MSI tournament. He did, however, end up with a negative win rate as he only won six out of the 13 games. But other times, the solo Q numbers and the pro player stats actually line up. Kalista is currently the third least played champion and has the second lowest win rate in the game at 45% in the last 30 days. However, pro players swore that behind the scenes during scrimmages and trainings, Kalista was a better pick than other ADCs. This led to her being banned in 18 different games during MSI 2025. and was chosen in 10 games, including in the semi-finals between Geng and T1. However, it would seem the solo Q data was right because Kalista was played in 10 different games and she lost all of them. >> A good way to avoid losing is uh just don't pick Kalista. So, at the time of making this video, Scarnner is by far the least popular champion in the game and the most popular champion 7 years ago was almost objectively somewhere between Yasuo and Lee Sin. Today, it's not that clear. If you look at the infographics from League ofgraphs.com, the answer would seem to be one of Lux, Caitlyn, or Yasuo, but the answer is probably Lux. Lux has been in the top five most played champions for the past 82 consecutive patches. starting from October 2021 to today. I should mention Yasuo would probably still be the most popular champion if League didn't make a Yasuo 2. Currently, Yona is the 14th most played champion. So, you can probably assume that some players that would otherwise be running it down with Yasuo are now running it down with Yona instead. Anyway, thank you for watching. Send your condolences to Scarnner and I'll see you again soon. Take care.

titre: Kai’Sa Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
This... is Kai'Sa, Daughter of the Void. To survive among predators, Kai'Sa became one. The Daughter of the Void hunts with a symbiotic
second skin adapted from a living Void creature... Welcome to the Kai'Sa Champion Spotlight. Kai'Sa has two passives. The first is Caustic Wounds. Kai'Sa's basic attacks mark enemies with Plasma, dealing increasing bonus damage per stack. After enough stacks, the Plasma ruptures for a burst of damage based on the target's missing health. Nearby allies can donate Plasma by immobilizing enemies with crowd control but cannot rupture the plasma. Kai'Sa's second passive is Living Weapon: her symbiotic skin evolves her abilities based on permanent stats gained from items and champion levels. We'll come back to these evolutions with each ability. Kai'Sa's Q is Icathian Rain, which releases
a swarm of missiles evenly distributed among nearby enemies. Additional hits on the same champion or monster
deal reduced damage. Low health minions take extra damage. If you can catch an enemy up-close and alone,
they'll be pummeled with every shot for some unaVOIDable damage. Minions and other enemies will split the salvo, so remember to isolate your prey before making it Icathian Rain. With enough bonus Attack Damage, Icathian Rain evolves to fire significantly more missiles, dealing more damage. Kai'Sa's W, Void Seeker, fires off a beam
of Void energy that damages, reveals, and applies Plasma to the first enemy hit. With enough Ability Power, Void Seeker evolves
to deal more damage, apply additional Plasma, and partially refund the cooldown on champion hit. Put enemies on blast with a nasty poke, or
play Hide and Seeker with a fleeing enemy. Kai'Sa's E is Supercharger. First, Kai'Sa charges up, briefly increasing her movement speed but losing the ability to attack. After charging up, Kai'Sa whips out the big guns to increase her attack speed for a few seconds. Every basic attack reduces Supercharger's
cooldown. Additionally, bonus attack speed will reduce the charge up time and increase the movement speed gain. With enough bonus Attack Speed, Supercharger evolves to grant invisibility during the charge up. Your enemies hunting you will be in Vayne. The first half of Supercharger can be used
to create distance from a threat... and you can use the second half to turn and burn. Kai'Sa's ultimate is Killer Instinct. Kai'Sa dashes at extremely high speed to a
location near an enemy champion marked with Plasma, then briefly gains a shield. While Kai'Sa moves insanely fast during her ultimate,
it is technically a dash. Eagle-eyed opponents can interrupt you. Unlike other champions from the void with high mobility ultimates. Huh... Kai'Sa can use her ult to pick off a wandering enemy; Combo with a teammate to dive a carry hiding
in the backline; or reposition in the blink of an eye. Killer Instinct is exceptionally versatile
and has a long range, but it's a huge commitment. Don't be Rash with it: Once you're in deep,
you better be able to fight your way out. Kai'Sa's abilities and evolutions give her
multiple ways to approach a teamfight. One method is to rely on Supercharger, pinging
their frontline for constant cooldown refreshment and using Killer Instinct to dodge crowd control
or kite divers. Just make sure you don't abandon your team
to 1 v 5. That's ten points in Voidspotting. Alternatively, you can lurk on the outskirts,
waiting for a well-aimed Void Seeker; or some friendly crowd control to dive in and 
pop a squishy. Stay alert for opportunities to follow up
on an ally's engage and land that final stack of Plasma for the kill. After all, KS stands for Kai'Sa. Thanks for watching the Kai'Sa champion spotlight! Follow your Killer Instinct to dive deep and learn more about the Daughter of the Void.

titre: Do You Remember The Champion So Broken Riot Tried To Hide It? | A League Of Legends Movie
transcription: [Music] I would give up all my in modern day League of Legends we are used to players complaining about how broken the newest champions are we love to reminisce about the glory days of League though often through rose-tinted nostalgia goggles you see back in the glory days riot would never release champions like Senna keanu set or a Phileas these new champions are omnipresent in today's meta because more often than not they are given all of the tools to succeed they have overloaded in frustrating kits don't even get the conversation started on how many times it also feels like they don't even know what they want to do with certain champions let's just take Senna is she an ad carry a support it almost feels like even they don't know they just need to remove some of these champions from the free week rotation nerf them into the ground so that they're never viable and wait four years for them to be finally reworked alright okay maybe that's a little too far I mean they can't just do that or can they while it feels as if they only release toxic and unfair champions in 2020 historically there was a champion that rivaled these new beasts a champion that was so unfun to play against that even the developers were trying to hide him away from the rest of the game and discourage any new players from learning him it left him without a home in no place lonely and forgotten because of him being intentionally gutted out of viability it almost felt like you were forbidden to play a champion if you take a look at champions that people hate the most in season 10 such as this group of misfits you often see their champion spotlights like to dislike ratio not being the best but overall they're mostly receiving positive reception anywhere from 70% or higher even a filius who has become the new Yasuo and his reddit's new demon child still holds a pretty okay ratio I figured only one other champion could rival as much hate surely teemo's champion spotlight would have the worst like to dislike ratio and while it's not great it pales in comparison to York [Music] let's go back to 2011 when doing these champion documentaries we usually begin right into Champions concept and then we go into why they were flawed what problems they might have had and how the champion was reworked or changed later on down the road in order to fix these ever longing issues rarely though do we go into why some of these older champions specifically around the years 2010 and 2011 seemed to have so many different issues Wukong was released in 2011 with plenty of gameplay flaws and a lack of a true fluid design whether or not he's supposed to be a jungler or a top laner and finally nearly a decade later we get to see these brand new Wukong changes just recently nine years in the making when looking at a timeline of League of Legends and Riot Games as a company it is possible to piece together a potential reason for these flaws York was released in June of 2011 but earlier that year Riot Games was purchased by $0.10 the Chinese conglomerate for a price of 400 million dollars giving them a 93 percent stake in the company later on $0.10 would just go ahead and buy the rest of the company to have full ownership those fancy business words just made a company who is using its money and power to purchase other companies so they get their money and power they're like one big investor in Tencent is one of the biggest companies that has ever existed even though $0.10 video game division is not actually a developer they don't code for League of Legends or other games that they own they are still technically the biggest video game company in the world $0.10 owns a few games you might have heard of they own full control of Riot Games who makes that small indie game known as League of Legends Tencent also owns 80% of grinding gear the company that makes path of Exile they also own 40% of Epic Games I think they make some kind of shooter game pretty sure that's like a mobile game though and speaking of mobile games they also happen to own 84% of supercell the finished developer behind clash of clans oh yeah and they also have stake in all of these games to the point of this part of the story is not to tell you how much $0.10 owns and controls your life as a gamer as all of your favorite games are eventually going to succumb to their big dollar signs no no rather this is to give you an idea of the motivation and goals behind game developers after they see a massive increase in capital more specifically this is to tell you about $0.10 money and the fact that they want more of it when riot was bought for four hundred million dollars this was a massive move by $0.10 but it can still be seen as a huge risk what if they never get their investment money back after all this is a free-to-play game and if you told your friends that you're investing four hundred million dollars into a video game that the developers say hey come and play for free they might tell you this is the worst trade deal in the history of trade deals and maybe ever the nice thing for Tencent is that League of Legends does indeed have a business model and it has everything to do with the champions themselves in this game it's not technically required to spend any money in order to be competitive but the more good skins and champions that come out the more likely your customers will feel inclined and sometimes pressured into spending their money during this time it's very likely that the pressure from the devs at riot to make new champions was at an all-time high as the higher-ups at Tencent knew that this acquisition was brand new and they were looking to make as much money as possible more champions equals more money more money equals more growth and more growth equals better game this sounds great but in practice you're seriously going to miss the mark on quality let's take a time frame of when they released champions for example let's start around late 2010 even though the purchase wasn't made public until February of 2011 we can speculate that Riot knew of this offer for months if not way longer from before but of course the legal details contracting and funding all had to be figured out which of course can take a very long time let's then extend the time line until around six months after it went public to give us basically one year of champion releases from late 2010 until late 2011 the schedule for releasing new champions during these days was something that players probably liked at the time because we used to get a new champion every two weeks in fact the release of brand was only 11 days after releasing Lee sin to be fair we did get a few smash hits such as Orianna and lee sin but the number of rushed broken almost incomplete champions on this list is quite the trip irelia and leblanc have needed many different reworks over the years with LeBlanc's day one release being so overpowered that she was
