titre: Ornn Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
This… is Ornn, the Fire Below the Mountain. Ornn likes to do things himself. Like building items! Instead of bothering with shop owners, he
builds his own. And when the ram god decides it's time for
a teamfight... he moves mountains to start one. Welcome to the Ornn Champion Spotlight. Ornn has two passives. The first is Living Forge. It lets Ornn spend gold to build non-consumable
items for himself any time he's out of combat. Build items by opening the shop anywhere on
the map and spending gold like normal. Just for convenience, you can also build things
by clicking items shown in this menu. Ornn's second passive is Master Craftsman. It gives Ornn and his teammates access to
special upgrades for a few items in the shop. Each champion on Ornn's team is limited to
just one special item. Ornn's Q is Volcanic Rupture. Ornn slams the ground, damaging and slowing
enemies in a line. After a short delay, a magma pillar forms
at its maximum range, or right behind the first enemy champion struck, whichever comes
first. The pillar works like a wall but does not
knock-up enemies. Ornn's W is Bellows Breath. Ornn belches out flames that deal damage based
on the enemy's current health and shields himself for a short duration. While Bellows Breath is active, Ornn is unstoppable,
which means that any incoming crowd control won't have any effect until the ability completes. The final burst of Bellows Breath makes any
struck enemies become Brittle. This is a brand new status effect in League,
so let's explain how it works: When Brittle, enemies lose tenacity. Think of it like negative Mercury Treads. Things like stuns and slows last longer when
used on these targets. Brittle enemies that are struck by hard crowd
control take bonus damage—this consumes the debuff. There's one other effect that's unique to
Ornn: basic attacking a Brittle target knocks them back and consumes the debuff. Ornn's E is Searing Charge. Ornn charges, displacing and damaging enemies
in his way. If Ornn collides with terrain while charging,
he stops, creating a shockwave that damages and knocks up nearby enemies. This ability destroys terrain created by enemy
champions. It also destroys Volcanic Rupture's pillar. Pull off Ornn's basic combo by getting close
enough to lightly roast enemies with Bellows Breath. Once they're good and Brittle, turn up the
heat with a Searing Charge into nearby terrain for the knockup. If you can Charge into Volcanic Rupture's
pillar, it'll be a job extra well done. Ornn's ultimate is Call of the Forge God. Ornn summons a massive elemental that charges
toward him. Enemies run over by the elemental are damaged,
slowed, and made Brittle. Ornn can recast this ability to headbutt the
elemental, redirecting it. The rebounding ram will deal the same effects
as before, except knocking enemies up instead of slowing them. A mistimed head butt will leave Ornn feeling
sheepish. Your ultimate can be an amazing setup tool
for allied crowd control. Catch them in the forge god's stampede, then
tag 'em into a wall to make 'em feel extra Ornnery. You may want to max W first for the waveclear—it's
his most reliable ability. After you're able to blow down waves in a
single breath, focus on maxing E to make your Searing Charges extra spicy. If your enemies think they know your item
build, use Living Forge to pull the wool over their eyes. Here, Ornn sneakily crafts a Righteous Glory. He then activates it to hoof down Blitzcrank
and ram him into a wall, allowing his team to catch up and bring Blitz to the scrap heap. Use your passive to forge items in lane that
give an immediate advantage against your opponent. For example, forge an early specter’s cowl
into heavy magic damage. To hammer it home, think about forging a Warmog's
when you're low to stay on the map. As Ornn, it's your job to bring the teamfight. Call of the Forge God has a fixed distance,
so try to place it in a spot that cuts off your opponents' path. Then, stay away from enemy crowd control so
you can stick the redirect. Let's put it all together for a teamfight! You want to start out the fight with both
passes of Call of the Forge God. While the baddies are knocked up, drop your
Q's pillar and get ready to follow up with a Searing Charge. Stay in the fight with Bellow’s Breath and
trigger the Brittle debuff as often as you can to take down enemies of all sheeps and
sizes. Thanks for tuning into the Ornn Champion Spotlight. ORNN'T you glad there weren't more puns? I know, they're CORNN-y. Smash the hotlinks below to learn more about
the Fire Below the Mountain.

titre: Ashe: Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription: Bienvenue dans ce Focus sur Ashe, l'Archère de givre. Ashe est une tireuse d'élite qui se spécialise dans les coups critiques à distance et le kiting avec ses flèches de givre. Grâce à son contrôle de foule et de voie, Ashe peut infliger de gros dégâts tout en initiant les combats ou en assistant les alliés partout sur la carte. Le passif d'Ashe est Concentration. Quand elle n'utilise pas d'attaques de base, ses chances de critique augmentent sur la durée jusqu'à 100%, et plus vite aux derniers niveaux. La première attaque de base d'Ashe consomme le bonus de critique, mais lancer une compétence ne le réinitialise pas. Activé, Tir givrant ralentit les déplacements de l'ennemi à chaque attaque de base, ce qui consomme du mana à chaque coup. Activez Tir givrant pour tenir les ennemis à distance ou les garder à portée pour les tuer. Avec Salve, Ashe tire des flèches en cône sur la zone visée. Chaque flèche s'arrête au premier ennemi touché, lui infligeant des dégâts et le ralentissant. Un ennemi unique ne peut pas être touché par plus d'une flèche, et les cibles éloignées peuvent esquiver en passant entre les flèches ou se cachant derrière d'autres unités. Comme Salve hérite du ralentissement de Tir givrant, les ennemis ne seront pas givrés si Tir givrant n'a pas été amélioré. Rapace a un effet passif et actif. L'effet passif permet de gagner plus de PO quand elle tue un ennemi. L'effet actif envoie un faucon vers la zone ciblée, ce qui la révèle, hautes herbes y compris mais pas les champions invisibles, pendant quelques secondes. La portée est très longue, ce qui permet à Ashe de reconnaître des lieux clés ou voir les menaces arriver. L'ultime d'Ashe, Flèche de cristal enchantée, tire une flèche géante dans la direction visée. Elle voyage indéfiniment jusqu'à toucher un champion ennemi ou sortir de la carte. Si elle touche une cible, elle se brise, blessant et étourdissant le champion tout en blessant et ralentissant les ennemis proches. La durée de l'étourdissement augmente plus la flèche voyage loin sur la carte. Au début de chaque partie, Concentration garantit un critique tant que vous n'attaquez aucun ennemi en allant vers votre voie. Ashe est donc très puissante en combat d'équipes au niveau 1. Ici, Ashe utilise Salve avec le critique de Concentration pour enlever près de la moitié des PV de Tristana et ainsi garantir le premier sang. En début de partie, Salve est sa meilleure source de burst et permet de maintenir la pression sur les voies. Comme Salve n'est pas liée à ses attaques de base, elle le lance tout de suite après avoir touché Ezreal pour l'éloigner des sbires. Même sans le burst de Salve, Tir givrant est un excellent outil de harcèlement des champions ennemis. Ici, Ashe s'en sert pour dominer Graves sur sa voie, en lui infligeant des dégâts bonus alors qu'il tente de fuir. Tir givrant et Salve constituent une combo destructrice. Ici, Ashe ralentit Ezreal pour l'empêcher de poursuivre Thresh. Dès qu'il essaie de fuir dans les hautes herbes, Salve le ralentit suffisamment pour qu'Ashe le rattrape et le tue avant de passer à Zyra. Bien qu'il serve surtout en reconnaissance, Rapace est un outil important pour révéler un ennemi qui tente de nous semer. Ici, Ashe révèle Blitzcrank alors qu'il essaie de fuir dans les hautes herbes, ce qui lui permet de faire un Tir givrant afin de continuer la poursuite. Les capacités de kiting d'Ashe lui permettent d'être plus agressive sur les voies. Alors que Darius vient ganker, elle réussit à fuir en tenant les ennemis à distance avec Tir givrant et Salve. Ashe redevient l'agresseur et Tir givrant lui permet de poursuivre Darius quand il est allé trop loin. Ashe achève Darius grâce à son coéquipier sur la voie, puis commence à harceler le carry adverse. Après son Saut éclair, elle enchaîne avec Tir givrant et Salve pour empêcher Ezreal de fuir alors que Shen arrive. Au niveau 6, l'énorme portée de Flèche de cristal enchantée fait d'Ashe une menace globale, même quand elle va farmer. Ici, Ashe transforme un combat 2 contre 2 en 3 contre 2 sans quitter sa voie. Quand Katarina est touchée, nos alliés la tuent sans mal, forçant Diana à aller se retrancher derrière la tourelle. Quand vous utilisez Rappel, vérifiez l'état de vos équipiers. Depuis la base, vous avez un angle parfait sur les trois voies. Alors que Shen fuit Vladimir, Ashe tire sa flèche de loin pour l'immobiliser seul sous la tourelle. La durée de l'immobilisation permet à la tourelle de le tuer pendant que Vladimir fuit. Alors que ses équipiers tentent de tuer le dragon, Ashe utilise Rapace pour voir s'il y a des ennemis. Voyant arriver l'équipe adverse, celle d'Ashe a donc le temps de se replacer et initier sur Thresh alors qu'Ashe enchaîne avec Flèche de cristal enchantée. Le ralentissement de zone de son ultime bloque l'équipe adverse pendant qu'elle aligne sa première Salve. Ashe domine le combat en utilisant Tir givrant pour empêcher les évasions et en lançant Salve pour continuer à poursuivre chaque fois qu'un ennemi tente de sortir du champ de vision. Ashe tombe sur Vayne en tentant d'aider Fizz à voler le buff bleu. En ralentissant Vayne avec Tir givrant, Ashe s'attend à ce que Vayne fasse sa roulade pour tuer Fizz. Mais Vayne fait un Saut éclair sur le mur opposé, une jolie fuite en apparence. Grâce à Rapace et la vision des sbires alliés, Ashe aligne Flèche de cristal enchantée pour étourdir Vayne et approcher pour une Salve. En fin de partie, Ashe est une machine à kiter presque insaisissable. Avec assez de vitesse d'attaque, elle peut éloigner un ennemi presque indéfiniment. Ici, Trundle essaie de poursuivre Ashe et Orianna, mais est incapable d'avancer à cause de leurs ralentissements. Pour améliorer Ashe, associez les dégâts, la vitesse d'attaque et la mobilité pour complémenter ses sorts de givre. N'oubliez pas que le critique garanti par le passif d'Ashe fait de la Lame d'infini un achat très efficace grâce aux critiques augmentés. Côté runes : marques de dégâts d'attaque, sceaux d'armure, glyphes de résistance magique et quintessences de Vol de vie. Côté maîtrises : essayez 21/0/9 avec de la vitesse d'attaque, des dégâts et un bonus de critique en Attaque, et de la régénération de mana et réduction de délais de récupération en Utilitaire. Côté sorts d'invocateur : essayez Barrière ou Purge et Saut éclair, afin de survivre dans les combats d'équipes en fuyant ou annulant le contrôle de foule. Merci d'avoir regardé ce Focus sur Ashe. Abonnez-vous à la chaîne YouTube de Riot Games et laissez-nous vos commentaires sous la vidéo !

titre: How to Set up Target Champions Only - League of Legends
transcription: hello everybody and welcome back to another video today I'll be talking about how to set up Target Champions only let's start off with why do you want to have Target Champions only the reason why you want to have Target Champions only is because when you're fighting in big minion waves or Tower diving someone you don't want to accidentally click the minions or accidentally click the tower so how do we set this up go to escape go to hotkeys go to abilities and summer spells from here scroll all the way down to here from here you should see Target Champions only you need to set this to three keys one of three keys you can do to T which is what I personally do you can do it for tilde which is the key left of number one and above tab or lastly you can set this to any other key you prefer some people do mouse buttons some people do other Keys it's really up to you I personally use T and now as we can see here I'm gonna click a minion which is a normal Auto attack right but if I click T notice how my mouse turns white but I cannot click the minion it's not possible anymore it's because now my target Champions only is on so now that is how to hold T to use Target Champions only let's go with the other option go down to game here and go to this section right here treat Target Champions only as a toggle and the reason why I highly suggest you have this on is because you don't want to hold T while having to click other abilities it's a lot of working very hard so I highly suggest you put it onto to toggle to make your life a lot easier and as you can see here it's off let me turn it on now I'm only targeting Champions only I cannot click minions because it's currently on target Champions only now in order to attack minions again or Towers I need to turn it off and now I can attack them again and now the way this works is the reason why you want to do this is because when you are Tower diving or fighting in big waves you can now enter someone's Tower range and as you can see there's a champion here if I want to attack them with no target Champions only I sometimes might misclick the tower and it'll be very embarrassing but now with Target Champions only we activate it and now we can click the champion very easily this also applies to single Target abilities such as an EQ so anyq I can now Target the champion however I cannot accidentally an EQ of Minion it's not possible because my target Champion only is on so yeah guys thank you very much

titre: Building a better Janna || re-making a League of Legends champion [CC]
transcription:
This video was sponsored by Skillshare, the first 
1000 people to click the link in the description   can get a trial of Skillshare Premium for free.
Hello wanderers and wind spirits, my name is   TBSkyen, and… well, I’m sure some of you were 
expecting this video to be about Kai’sa, but,   ah… no. Not yet, not yet. But we’ll get to her.
Anyway, League of Legends crossed the 150 champion   barrier a while back, which means there are more 
League of Legends champions than generation 1   Pokémon. Which… forces me to realize that I know 
the lore of every League champion and the battle   cry of every gen 1 Pokémon, and I can’t help but 
wonder what useful life skills that information   is crowding out of my brain storage. Huh.
Now, this video series is called “Building   a Better” X, and before we get into it, just 
for context, that title means better… in our   opinion. As we’ll discuss in the video, there 
is no such thing as a perfect character design,   all design is a process of making tradeoffs, 
and our designs are not better as measured on,   like, ze objective karakter design goodness 
scale invented by Professor Design in 1987,   jaaaaa. They are better in our eyes and on our 
terms, and in this video we will argue for why   we think so. Anyway I’m making some references 
in the plural here, I should probably introduce   you… this is Apple Cork, my partner in artistic 
crime for this endeavour. The artwork you’ll see   in this video will be his, while I handle the much 
more difficult job of speaking into a microphone   and putting funny pictures 
on the screen. Like this one! You can find links to Apple Cork’s work in the 
description in case you want to give him a follow. Anyway, our vict--our subject for 
today is Janna, the Storm’s Fury,   a title that really doesn’t fit 
her anymore, come to think of it.   Huh, it’s almost as though she desperately needs 
someone to give her a visual update of some kind.  Long ago, in the far flung 
forgotten before times of 2009,   Janna was released as part of the original 
crop of 40 champions that formed the core   of League of Legends. And back then, 
she looked like this: *beta Janna*  Realizing their mistake, Riot soon corrected 
her colors to something that isn’t a war-crime,   giving her this--still eye-searing--but 
somewhat more familiar color palette.   Then, in 2011, she got a model update that 
looked like this, followed eventually by a   texture update that made her look like this.
So, what kind of character is Janna - what   sort of person was her design meant 
to express? What was the story there? Well, her earliest lore was pretty simplistic, 
as you’d expect from early League of Legends.   She was an orphan in the city state of Zaun - 
which, back then, was not a connected Twin City   with Piltover, rather they were rival states - who 
lived a pretty miserable life until discovering a   talent for wind magic, which gave her the means to 
secure for herself a better life and also improve   the conditions of the poor around her. In order to 
do that, she enters The League of Legends. Now as   we’ve noted on this channel before, originally, 
The League of Legends was, like, an actual thing   in the lore - it was a big international blood 
sports tournament that essentially replaced   mass warfare, and the champions of the game were 
champions in that tournament, fighting for the   political interests of themselves or their home 
nations. So Janna enters the League of Legends to   win money and fame and maybe, y’know, build some 
orphanages or something. And that was basically   the extent of her character as expressed. 
As part of the League of Legends tournament,   Riot also used to publish an in-universe 
newspaper called the Journal of Justice which was…   well, it was pretty rough by modern standards. 
Like, Riot writers would create self-insert   characters to write the articles and one of them 
wrote, like, official self-insert fanfiction where   his character got to date Nidalee? And I think at 
one point the Journal reported rumors that, like,   Janna’s nudes had been leaked? 
Which, like, *deep breath* Anyway, the Journal of Justice is gone 
now and it’s probably for the better. Her lore was updated a bit along the way, with one 
addition stating that her mastery of wind magic   actually began to change her physically, and 
that’s why she has elf ears, for example, but   the big shift with Janna’s character 
happens - as it does with most of the   older champions - in 2014 when Riot retcons 
the entire League of Legends universe,   removing the in-universe League of Legends 
tournament in favor of a more traditional   fantasy world. The fiction that League of 
Legends matches were technically somehow canon   is abolished, and Summoner’s Rift becomes 
a non-canon alternate universe playground. For Janna, this presents a problem, because her 
story kind of revolved around her being a champion   in the League of Legends. Some kind of change was 
necessary - and I suppose there were broadly two   ways Riot could have gone: They could either 
have changed the minimum necessary details and   kept her a Zaun sorceress trying to 
improve the social conditions of the city,   just without the League angle… 
or… they could make her a god. Or, well, because Riot loves their terminology, 
technically Janna is a wind spirit - a sort of   demigod of the winds. She is - or was - worshipped 
by the sailors of Runeterra, who would pray to her   for favourable winds and protection from 
storms. She would manifest usually as a   seasparrow bluebird, but sometimes in the form 
of a woman… who is mostly naked for some reason? Eventually, the sailors of Runeterra call upon her 
less and less as sailing ships improve, and Janna   fades out of memory, until a cataclysmic disaster 
in Zaun plunges much of the city below sea level,   leaving tens of thousands suddenly at the mercy 
of sea currents. People remember their old   faith and call out for Janna’s protection, 
which she offers in the form of winds and   gales that hold back the waters and sweep away 
the toxic smoke of the disaster. Since then,   Janna has become a household god in Zaun, acting 
as a sort of goddess of fresh air. Protection   both from the toxic smog of Zaun and to a lesser 
extent the violence of the, y’know, capitalist   exploitation that runs the place. So… how 
does all of that translate into her design? It doesn’t. It doesn’t translate into her 
design. Janna is an elf mage. That’s… what   she is. She is a stereotypical high fantasy 
elf mage who floats around in her underwear,   because League of Legends at launch was not a 
game especially concerned with… “originality.” As   we discussed in the Building a Better Ashe video, 
it was designed to be essentially a reworked port   of Defense of the Ancients with the serial numbers 
filed off so Blizzard couldn’t claim copyright,   and it was being created by what was at the 
time a genuine small indie company flying   by the seat of their pants. What 2009 Riot 
needed was a bunch of broadly recognizable   character archetypes to stuff into their game 
as fast as possible, and everything else had to   come after. Lore and story was, at least from an 
outside perspective, essentially an afterthought.  So Janna, who is ostensibly a hard-bitten orphan 
from a rapidly industrializing city state run on   mad science… looks like a stereotypical high 
fantasy elf mage who floats around in her   underwear. Her Hextech skin tries to imagine 
a slightly more lore-accurate look for her,   but from a design perspective, she was 
essentially only really meant to be   eye-catching, which arguably 
she succeeded at being. But League of Legends has moved on - gaming itself 
has rather moved on - and Janna’s design hasn’t   moved with it. When most people see this 
character, they see, again, a stereotypical elf   mage. They see a half naked fantasy babe 
who is entirely designed to be eye-candy.   They do not see an ancient wind-spirit / demigod 
worshipped by sailors and coastal dwellers, or   a goddess of fresh air guarding a 
choking city against toxic smoke.  Now, to be clear, the problem isn’t that this 
design couldn’t POSSIBLY be a wind spirit - a   generic high fantasy elf mage floating 
around in her underwear COULD be any number   of things. Show me this design and tell me it’s 
supposed to be a wind spirit and I’ll be like   “okay, sure, I guess?” Oh she’s a demigod 
of fresh air. Sure I guess. But you could   also tell me she’s a street magician, or a court 
mage. You could tell me she’s Demacian, Noxian,   from the Freljord, Targon or Ionia. You could tell 
me she’s from SMITE, and my answer would be “sure,   I guess.” She looks generic enough to be anything, 
which means her design says very little specific. She has no fashion that ties her to 
any region, no distinctive features   that mark her out as a special kind 
of being, much less a spirit or a god.   The closest she comes are her elf ears… 
which just make her look like an elf. Speaking of her fashion though, she’s not wearing 
very much of it. Now as we discussed, Janna   is half naked originally almost certainly just 
because it was supposed to be eye-catching, which…   okay. But that is basically the same design 
strategy as those old internet banner ads   with a hot lady on them saying, like, “PLAY NOW 
MY LORD!” It’s tacky, and kinda cringeworthy.  Also… she’s not even that hot. Like, if the idea 
is “oh yeah let’s attract some attention with a   smokin’ hot sexy fantasy babe,” I’m like… and 
that’s the best you could come up with? This   is the limits of your erotic imagination? 
That’s as spicy as you were willing to go?   Champion designs like Evelynn, Elise, Ahri and 
even Zyra have done a substantially better job   being sexually alluring in various ways, by 
comparison Janna is just some lady floating   around in hot pants and a strapless 
top. She’s not exactly scandalous. Now, in the story as written nowadays, 
Janna is a benevolent protector god,   who rarely manifests fully in her human form. 
Again, looking at Janna as presented in the game,   you don’t really get a sense of an ethereal, 
otherworldly mirage presence - especially showing   so much skin, she feels physical and present, like 
she’s made of flesh and blood. She feels human,   and vulnerable. She doesn’t really look like 
a supernatural interdictor or protector deity,   she looks - and I’m sorry to repeat 
myself so much - like a floaty elf mage. So, in summary, the problems with Janna’s design 
that we want to try and address are the following:  She looks like a generic elf mage.
She does not look clearly like a spirit or a god.  She has no visual connection to her 
region, Zaun, or her past as a sea goddess.  Her design is sexy for no 
discernable or useful reason.  If we can solve those problems, then hopefully 
we really will have built a better Janna.   Subjectively. Say hello to Natalia 'Verauko' Trykowska 
and their fan-made redesign of Janna… which   is really really good. They seem to have 
started from some of the same problems as   I identified in the original design - that 
it’s generic, doesn’t look supernatural,   lacks elements of wind magic and so on.
The solutions that Verauko have identified   are frankly excellent. Their version 
of Janna is centered around a single   extremely eye-catching feature: those 
THICC-ASS THIGHS--*static* a glowing,   magical core - a centre around which the rest 
of her body swirls like smoke. This one feature   instantly marks out Janna as a supernatural, 
possibly divine being, it instantly associates her   with air and the ethereal, and implies that the 
body we see is some sort of smokey projection or   construct surrounding the real core of who she is.
The airiness is accentuated by bringing the floaty   hair which is, admittedly technically present in 
the current Janna, but where Current Janna’s hair   is just kinda always pointing straight up in 
essentially a cone, Verauko’s Janna has a much   more distinctly floaty aesthetic to the hairstyle.
And returning to her thicc juicy thighs,   Janna’s general anatomy and look is substantially 
more mature than the original. Where Current Janna   is skinny, slight and very young looking, this 
Janna has - for lack of a better term - MILF   energy. She looks like a grown-ass woman, someone 
who’s been around a while. Not old, but mature,   an impression that’s aided by her visible, defined 
cheekbones and makeup - those red lips especially.   That sense of age and experience also gives her an 
air of confidence, and this has another benefit:   Janna is as we discussed a protector and 
guardian, a caretaker spirit. Having her   present as a mature woman gives her parent 
energy - the reassurance of being taken   care of by someone older and wiser, and it 
makes her feel more powerful and dependable. Verauko’s redesign addresses almost 
every single point of criticism   that we have raised, and as an added 
bonus, depending on your perspective,   even manages to improve Janna’s function as 
eye-candy. This is a very good-looking character,   with a mature beauty and appeal that League 
of Legends isn’t already overloaded with. Which begs the question, what the hell are me and 
Apple Cork doing then? If the design has already   been fixed, why bother making this video? Just 
do a video on Verauko’s design and call it a day.   And, well, actually, Verauko’s excellent design is 
exactly WHY I wanted to do this video. One of the   themes I want to have running through this series 
is that character design is not a settled science.   No creative endeavour is. Verauko’s design hasn’t 
“fixed” Janna because, well, because that’s not   how design works. You can’t fix it because there’s 
no ideal, flawless state to fix it to. What   Verauko has done, brilliantly, and what we are 
going to try to do, is examine the design and the   character story that is there, and say here’s our 
version, and here’s why we think it works better. And I stand by my opinion, that both me 
and Apple Cork’s versions and Verauko’s   are much better than the vanilla version 
currently in the game, but as I noted in   the intro to the video, this isn’t “ach ja ve 
haff measured ze karakter designs on ze karakter   design goodness scale und ve haff found zat ours 
is vay better, jah,” it’s a critical argument.   And the reason I’m hammering this principle 
home so hard is because I know if I don’t,   I have to deal with three months of people in 
the comments who absolutely refuse to understand   the idea that art is inherently subjective and 
that all arguments about art, every single one,   all of them, no exceptions, including the one 
you’re coming up with in your head right now,   yes that one too, are subjective as well.
So, Verauko has delivered this very good   version of Janna, which solves a 
lot of the issues we identified.   We are going to propose our versions of the 
character, and intentionally make them different   from Verauko’s to demonstrate the point that there 
is never just one way to solve a design problem. ANYWAY, let’s get into it. The early design process begins with rapid 
thumbnailing and ideation. We are at this   stage throwing everything against 
the wall, and seeing what sticks.   How much should her hair define about 
her design? Do we keep the streamers on   her hips from the original, or should we 
try for a cloak? A robe? Nothing at all?   Should we keep it simple? Make it elaborate? 
And what should her body shape be? Hourglass   shaped and classically feminine, 
or something more elfin and slight?  Personally, I begin to lean towards the idea 
of a version of Janna that is more ethereal,   less human, while Apple Cork decides to try for 
a more traditionalist version of the character,   keeping the general vibes of the original 
while making more conservative changes. Next, we start working on ideas for her face. 
One of my personal big bugbears with League of   Legends women is that so many of them look like 
their faces were copy pasted from an issue of   Generic Character Design Monthly. Eye shape and 
size, cheek bones, jawlines, noses, proportions,   these features are all - in my opinion - 
criminally underused in Riot’s design aesthetic.   So we try out a number of different variations, 
including - and I quite like this one - taking   AWAY as many facial features as possible to make 
her inhuman, or incorporating almost alien-like   features like big blank eyes and turning 
her tiara into a kind of forehead crest.  I push for an incorporation of androgyny - or 
more specifically a de-emphasizing of the usual   visual stylizations associated with femininity - 
because the design idea that’s emerging in my head   is one that pushes Janna towards a different kind 
of gender expression. Now androgyny is a complex   thing - in a design it can be achieved in a lot 
of ways. Here we tried to get there by getting rid   of things like strong eyeliner, emphasized lips, 
and other typical stylizations that are associated   with “femininity,” but you can also achieve 
androgyny by going the other way and applying   heavy makeup. Verauko’s design has this voluptuous 
high femme energy, so being a bloody contrarian,   obviously I’m running in the exact opposite 
direction. Apple Cork, meanwhile, gravitates   to a mix of the alien-like features we tried, 
with a more traditionally femme presentation. Then, there is the matter of the staff. It’s an 
iconic piece of kit for the original Janna - or,   well, I think it looks kind of a malformed 
mess, and in my opinion all it does for   Janna is make her look even MORE like a typical 
elf-mage by giving her a typical wizard staff.   Neither Apple Cork nor I feel especially like 
we want the staff to be part of our designs,   but we run it through an ideation phase just to 
see, mixing supernatural with material elements. Ultimately, we both decide that Janna’s 
story is that she IS the wind and therefore,   why would she need any special 
instruments to do the things she does?   Plus, we think that having her carry around 
a physical object like that makes her less   ethereal and more concrete. Less like a living 
wisp of wind, and more like a floaty person. Still, I am drawn to the idea of a version 
of Janna that carries around trinkets like   pocket watches, bird feathers and little 
offerings from people’s shrines to her name,   who perhaps uses fishing nets as 
clothes and a harpoon as a staff.   That, too, could be a good interpretation 
of the character, and would visually tie   her much more strongly to her region than 
either Verauko’s or our final designs do. Anyway, for me, I’ve decided on a more 
top-heavy silhouette. I decided to keep   a version of the streamers she already has, 
because they have the convenient ability to   float and billow in the wind that surrounds her, 
which is good for showing her nature in animation.   I liked the idea of her hair as essentially 
a wisp of cloud like you might see in the sky   on a clear day, and I liked the 
idea of hiding her body shape   somewhat within a cloak that could 
also be filled with clouds and mist.   I also fell in love with the idea of having 
her wear a literal fishing net as homage to   her nautical origins, and I decided to make her 
base presentation ultimately inhuman, with blue   skin and glowing, pupil-less eyes to make her 
look as much like a wind-elemental as possible. Where I am emphasizing her as an 
elemental, or wind spirit, Apple Cork   experimented with various ways to make her more 
alien or traditionally god-like - so his design   incorporates more gold ornaments, more 
distinct clothing pieces, and a more   specific gender presentation with more makeup 
and feminine stylization. He was looking for a   version of Janna which was alien to humanity - 
not malicious, but somewhat divinely distant. A   being who you would have to worship, because it’s 
not quite human enough to be a peer with you. Before we move to the final design phases, if 
you’re familiar with my channel you may have heard   the joke that a character would be S-tier if they 
were naked. The context for that was that during a   stream, I was complaining about Janna’s design, 
saying that if she’s supposed to be eye-candy,   she is BORING eye-candy, and if they really 
wanted to make her an alluring wind spirit   they should just have made her straight up 
naked. Like, commit to the bit, if you wanna   play the “sex sells” card, then a lady floating 
around in a bikini is a boring sales-pitch. So, since we’re here anyway, if you want 
to design a Janna that would actually   be a bit risqué, bam, bam, bam. Now you’re 
actually risking trouble with the censors,   now you’re pushing the envelope a little 
bit, now you’re taking a creative risk.   And if you want to justify it in the lore, 
well, hey, she was invented by sailors,   a group of people famously so horny 
that they mistook dewgongs for mermaids. Now to be clear, I don’t especially think 
being risqué works for Janna as a character,   it doesn’t fit with her lore that much, the game 
has plenty of hot skinny young women already,   it doesn’t need another one, but… if you wanted to 
go with the sexy lady angle, I think these are at   least more interesting versions of that than the 
rather anodyne floating bikini lady we’ve got. So, after a lot of back and forth, editing passes 
and changes, we arrive at our two proposals for   how to build a better Janna. Subjectively.
And we’ll start with mine, this time around,   I think. One of my major complaints with vanilla 
Janna is that for someone who’s meant to be a wind   spirit or demigod, who rarely if ever manifests in 
full form, in-game Janna looks way too much like…   just some guy. Like not in terms of her gender, 
but in terms of… you see this legendary being of   immense and mythical power and you’re like “it’s 
just some guy!” y’know? It’s just some… someone,   a person. And maybe that person does magic and 
they’re very powerful, but they just look like   some person. What I wanted was to create a design 
that is unmistakably supernatural, that you look   at for a quarter of a second and you realize, 
oh, this is NOT a person. This is something else. Now, in these artworks we’re going to be showing 
off the fully rendered character designs,   but a central idea that we’re both working from is 
that the vast majority of the time, you don’t see   this character. In the lore, it’s described that 
Janna only ever manifests fully when she unleashes   her full power, so most of the time, our Jannas 
would look more like this: shimmering mirages,   or indistinct shapes of moving mist and air, 
circled by a faithful blue-bird. Now, that   could create some problems with her readability 
in-game - League of Legends generally relies on   clear outlines and animations telegraphing the 
action of a character, and so these designs put   a lot of pressure on the visual effects to ensure 
that the character has a clearly readable presence   and hitbox. I don’t think that’s an unsolvable 
problem, but I did want to acknowledge it. So, major features of the character - first 
of all, I tried to lean into a certain level   of androgyny, or, again, at least the absence 
of the most typical gendered stylizations. My   Janna takes on a humanoid form, but not the human 
fascination for make-up or gender presentation.   With her long hair and slender features, she 
still reads more feminine than masculine, I think,   but what I wanted most was just to distinguish 
her from the typical feminine presentation of   most female League of Legends champions. This 
Janna is not meant to be, big air-quotes,   “conventionally attractive,” because being a wind 
spirit, that really wouldn’t mean much to her.   The idea of the cloak is, well first of 
all to give her a distinctive silhouette,   especially when she’s translucent, but also that 
it is essentially a billowing cloud of mist,   on top of which floats a fishing net, billowing 
gently as she moves. I’m still not sure the   fishing net really gels with this version of 
the character, but it’s one of those ideas I   just liked too much to be objective about.
The gold and jewelry on her figure, on the   other hand, is a compromise. In my mind, I wanted 
her to be completely free of those elements,   only composed of mist and air, but what I 
found thinking through it is that without   something more solid - some contrasting, colorful 
elements - to add structure to her character,   she would visually merge into an indistinct 
blue-and-white blob, especially from a distance.  Another way to solve the problem might be to 
compose her of different colors of clouds and mist   - you can do quite a lot of value variety with 
them - but the other thing is that I didn’t want   her to stray completely from the original Janna, 
I wanted to keep a couple of bits that carried her   visual… feel. Hence the four streamers emerging 
from under the cloak, and hence the gold and   orange, mirroring the gems on her original costume 
and in her staff. The blue wings on her tiara,   obviously, is to tie her a little more explicitly 
to the bluebird that acts as her symbolic avatar. The feeling I want this design to evoke is 
that you are encountering something non-human,   something supernatural, but also gentle 
and soft. A mild breeze on a summer’s day,   a gentle gust of fresh air that clears the smoke 
from your nostrils. She is a protector after all.  So, that’s my version of the design - now, let’s 
hear from Apple Cork to describe his process. Like last time, Apple Cork 
has written these words, but   I’ll be reading them to you. *clear throat*
Hey everyone, it’s Applecork. Going into the Janna   project, I wasn’t set on any particular direction. 
The only thing I really knew I wanted was to get   rid of the bikini thing. Eventually, I decided 
on doing something similar to what I did with the   Ashe concept and try to maintain as many parts of 
old Janna as I could while adding the new ideas.
   Let’s start with her costuming. The Wild Rift 
Janna model already does a lot to update her look,   so I used that design as my starting point. During 
the ideation stage, Skyen and I both liked the   idea of taking her away from the physical world 
and bringing her closer to the supernatural. Since   Janna is a wind spirit, she isn’t really human 
and so I could have fun with her tangibility.   In one of my sketches, I drew her legs as broken 
up gusts of wind spiraling into the shape of legs,   with wing-like shapes sprouting from her ankles, 
reminiscent of the Greek Messenger God, Hermes,   and I took that idea with 
me into the final design.  As I ideated, I found that I wanted to alter her 
shape language. In both League and Wild Rift,   Janna’s armor is very angular and pointed, with 
some arcs for texture. While it looked cool,   I thought it made her look a bit standoffish, 
almost a Noxian aesthetic, mixed in with a bit of   Kayle, and I wanted her to feel more approachable. 
In Janna’s lore, she is basically your friendly   neighborhood Wind Sprite - she brings 
breathable air to the people of Zaun and is   loved by children. So I rounded off the 
armor and introduced more organic forms,   similar to water or wind, which helped connect 
some of her design better to her themes. One thing you might catch is that I’ve 
significantly reduced her upper arm and shoulder   costume from my ideation stages. As I was working 
on it, I found it inevitably ended up resembling   Irelia too much. Other ideations were 
a bit too sci-fi, or too clean - they   looked more like something from a skin, rather 
than the base model, if that makes any sense.   The arcs and shapes that I really wanted 
to implement just wouldn’t look unique,   so I ended up mimicking the shape of her curved 
waist pieces and copying it onto the shoulders.
   Skyen suggested the idea of playing with the 
uncanny valley early in the project, and I really   wanted to find a way to incorporate that concept 
into my Janna. I wanted her to look beautiful, but   with something off - she’ll catch your eye with 
her looks, and you stare. But as you stare,   you begin to realize that the figure in front of 
you isn’t a beautiful woman with wind magic. It’s   something else, only pretending to be human.
To get that inhumanity, one of the first things I   wanted to change was her “skin texture,” so I made 
parts of her body mimic human skin, while the rest   fades into her true cloudy form. Since her “skin” 
is just wind magic, droplets of water refracting   light, she can take on whatever color she wants, 
giving her a lot of shapeshifting options.  Her hair was another fun thing to work 
on. Skyen and I both found her original   onion hair a bit ridiculous, and since she 
is effectively a goddess of wind and weather,   the idea of cloud hair was a straightforward 
choice, with the idea that the clouds can change   in appearance to suit her mood. Storm clouds when 
she’s angry, misty when she’s embarrassed, etc.  While working on faces, I sketched a version 
where feathers sprouted from where her ears   would be - Skyen found that a great idea for 
further separating Janna from humans and we both   implemented it. In my version, I 
removed Janna’s bangs so I could   better show how the feathers and her face blend 
into her true intangible cloud-like form.
   And then there’s the eyes. Eyes can tell you a lot 
about a person - humans have smaller pupils than,   say, dogs and cats, and it allows 
us to create a lot of expression.   Subtle glances, held gazes and flickers of the 
eyes can tell you something about the mood or   intentions of the person you’re looking at. So I 
removed that from Janna. I enlarged her pupils,   deleted her irises, and replaced them with 
a miniature sky, inspired by Gojo from   Jujutsu Kaisen. She kind of looks like 
a stylized anime girl with those eyes,   and since anime proportions would look off in 
real life, it helps make her feel more uncanny. The original idea of the orange eye shadow was 
that it was meant to bring in some of the color   palette of Janna’s symbolic animal, the bluebird. 
I ended up using a mountain bluebird, which has a   white belly. But at that point, the orange had 
grown on me and felt off when I took it away.   Combined with her new feather adornments, 
her appearance reminds me a bit of the Tooth   fairy from Rise of the Guardians, and I quite 
liked that fairy connection, however tenuous. I went into this project a bit unsure of where 
I wanted to go, compared to the Ashe video, but   in the end, I quite like where everything 
ended up, and I hope you do too. At the start of the design process, we 
identified four major problems with Janna   that we wanted to address, and we wanted 
to address them in a different way than   Verauko did in their redesign. So:
She looks like a generic elf mage.  I feel pretty confident in saying 
we dealt with that one. Apple Cork’s   design is the more human looking of our 
two, but generic elf-mage she is not.  She does not look clearly like a spirit or a god.
I feel pretty good about how we addressed this   one. Between the cloudy hair, inhuman eyes and 
swirling body, she reads pretty strongly as a   supernatural being of some sort. And by removing 
her staff, we remove the implication that she’s   a magic caster working through special tools 
or implements, so her form reads more like a   natural state, not a transformation spell.
She has no visual connection to her region,   Zaun, or her past as a sea goddess.
This one is mixed. We both decided   that we’d prefer not to have her carry too many 
physical objects around - whether a staff, fashion   or trinkets. I opted for the fishing net cloak to 
hint at it a little bit, while Apple Cork zeroed   in on making her look specifically like a god. 
The aesthetic of Zaun especially is based on punk   and technology, neither of which really fit with 
our concepts. A different design execution could   choose to focus on those elements instead, but 
we decided to prioritize other aspects instead.  Her design is sexy for no 
discernable or useful reason.  We showed briefly some of the ways you could 
make Janna a sexy or sexualized design,   if you wanted to, that would be more interesting, 
but we both went pretty hard in the opposite   direction. Apple Cork’s Janna is the more 
conventionally feminine of our designs and   very pretty, but she takes more after Wild 
Rift Janna, toning down the sexualization,   while using the costume to imply 
the non-human nature of the wearer. All in all, I think we did a pretty good job 
of proposing alternatives both to vanilla   Janna, and to Verauko’s very different rework. 
As I have stressed half a dozen times already,   but which I am going to stress again just 
in case, is that no design is perfect,   and none of this is meant to “fix” the original 
Janna, because looking at a design in terms of   a binary wrong or right, as though there is a 
set of perfect solutions for a given problem,   simply isn’t a useful way to understand design.  Having said that, what we’ve presented here 
is a set of arguments that we stand by.   We believe our proposed changes are better than 
what’s in the game already, that our designs do   a better job of telling the character’s story, 
and that they are more interesting, and that   they are, at least in the context of League of 
Legends, unique. These beliefs are subjective,   but they’re supported by arguments and analysis.
You are more than welcome to disagree,   and tell me so in the comments, but a good 
criticism of our designs should be backed up by   arguments and analysis as well. We 
believe we have built a better Janna. Thank you to the sponsor of this video, 
Skillshare, whose sponsorship helps me pay Apple   Cork for his time and skills. And if you'd like 
to improve or learn some new skills of your own,   the first one thousand of my subscribers 
who click the link in the description   will get a free trial of Premium Membership 
so you can explore your own creativity.   Skillshare is a learning platform, so it has 
no ads, and at an annual membership the price   works out to less than ten dollars per month.
Speaking of commissioning artists, back when I   was a freelance artist for a living, one of 
the most frequent commissions I took on was for   custom online avatars for people. Heck, I still 
draw them on occasion. I used to charge $30   bucks for one of these, but fortunately for you, 
professional illustrator Ryan Putnam has a series   on Skillshare called Digital Illustration: Design 
Your Avatar where he teaches you step by step   how to create appealing character 
avatars from photos and reference,   saving you the $30 you’d otherwise have 
to pay a guy like me to make one for you. Thank you again to Skillshare for 
their support of this channel. Hey, thank you very much for watching. Once again,   the artwork in this video was created 
by Apple Cork, whose portfolio and   instagram you can find linked down in the 
description in case you want to follow him. You can like, comment, subscribe, hit the bell 
icon and do all the other things on this video;   it’s helpful to me for algorithm 
reasons and it’s helpful to you if   you want youtube to show you when new 
videos are uploaded to the channel. This video is sponsored, so I won’t do the 
big self-promotion spiel, but I will say thank   you to Isa Bastian, Miruna Andreea, Zero, Lars 
Kuhnert, Nathan Price, Darrow Hall, Art of Paya,   Fergy560, Jacob Lin, ZenAsh, Katelyn O’Neal, 
Gwendragon and Heytun for joining my Patreon   in May, your help makes it possible for me to 
do this for a living and I am deeply grateful. Thank you all again for watching, please 
remember to wear your masks and wash your hands.   When the vaccine comes please take it - it 
can’t come fast enough for me - and try to   act with solidarity towards those who are 
working to make the world a better place.

titre: Bard: Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Welcome to the League of Legends Champion
Spotlight, featuring Bard, the Wandering Caretaker. Bard is a cosmic guardian who manifests on
Runeterra whenever the universe's equilibrium comes under threat. In game, Bard plays the
role of a support, and offers his allies incredible utility along with decent sustain and mobility
as he roams around the map. Because Bard's all mysterious and cosmic and
stuff he has two passives: Ancient Chimes and Meeps. Bard's presence causes various collectible
chimes to appear across the map. By wandering over them, Bard collects these relics and
gains a burst of movement speed, a portion of his missing mana back and a little bit
of experience.  Bard's movement speed buff stacks up to five times if he collects multiple chimes in quick succession, but falls off if he enters combat. Although Ancient Chimes
can appear throughout the map, they'll generally appear in areas near Bard. Finally, note that
chimes pop up on Bard's minimap as soon as they appear, while enemies will only see them
if they have direct vision. Head out to collect chimes whenever you're
running low on mana and your lane buddy's in a relatively safe spot. The speed boost
will help you quickly return to lane with that all-important magic juice. Bard's second passive is Meeps. As he wanders
and fights throughout the Rift, adorable little spirit creatures called meeps come to Bard's
side, following him and launching themselves at whomever Bard attacks before disappearing
back into the ether. BUT WAIT, THERE'S MORE. See, meeps become more powerful as Bard collects
his chimes, so while he only starts out with one lonely meep, once he's collected enough
chimes, he'll have frickin' tons of meeps at his back. They don't just grow in numbers
though – Chimes also improve each meep's attacks, eventually giving them awesome bonuses
like area of effect damage, slowing attacks, and muscles, so they deal extra damage. Finally,
as numerous as meeps can get, they're total homies to Bard, and will stealth when he's
hiding in brush. Use meeps to continually poke down your enemies
in lane. They provide decent early damage, and can help you win trades. Bard's Q is Cosmic Binding, a medium range
skillshot that slows and damages the first enemy struck. Once Cosmic Binding hits its
target, it continues on through them, seeking a second target, stunning them both if it
finds one, or stunning the initial target if Cosmic Binding hits a wall instead. Remember
that Cosmic Binding can tether to neutral monsters as well as player-made terrain, so
keep an eye out for Cataclysms, Pillars and barons. Line up Cosmic Binding during laning so your
Qs hit enemy minions and then their champions! Being able to strike the enemy marksman through
their minion wave can be really powerful, particularly when you coordinate with jungle
ganks or even just general trades. Up next is Bard's W, Caretaker's Shrine. Bard
places a small health pack on the ground that he and his allies can collect to restore health
and gain a quick burst of movement speed. Though Caretaker's Shrine can be collected as soon as it's placed,
if it's left alone for a few seconds, it'll improve, and give whoever grabs it extra health.  However, leaving health packs out puts them at risk - Bard's enemies can destroy Shrines by stepping on them.  Finally, Bard can only place three health packs at once, and they'll last until they're consumed or destroyed, or until Bard places a fourth
Shrine. Place Shrines in safe areas so they can safely
charge up. Enemies are much less likely to destroy them when they're out of the way,
and allies can let the packs improve for extra health when they want to keep fighting. Bard's E is Magical Journey, a short range
skillshot that creates a temporary portal on a wall. Once it's been created, Champions who click on the portal are taken on a -  yep! - magical journey to the far end of the wall, no matter how long the contiguous piece of terrain is. Here's the kicker though: it's
not just Bard's teammates that can use the portals - all champions can, though Bard's
allies do travel slightly faster than his enemies. Portals only operate in one direction, so
champions can't travel back through it once they've reached their destination. But, if
they're fast enough to return to the start, they can journey again.  Finally, champions traveling through portals are still vulnerable to damage and crowd control effects so watch your ethereal butt if you want to journey with enemies nearby. Use Magical Journey to surprise enemies. Bard
can get through terrain faster than most other champions and use his sudden positional advantage
to line up his Q. Bard's ultimate is Tempered Fate, a long range skillshot missile that places all units hit - literally everything that can move or attack - into temporary stasis.  While in stasis, units are immune to absolutely everything - all damage and crowd control effects -  until the effect wears off. There is one caveat here: Tempered Fate does not affect champions using certain abilities.  Uninterruptable moves like Vi's Assault and Battery and enemy spell shields and black shields will block this. Tempered Fate has a whole bunch of uses, one of which is to protect your allies from lethal ranged damage. Seriously though, it's a super versatile ability. Bard might be one of League's strongest roaming
supports, but he still needs to know when he can safely roam and when he should stay
with his lane pal. Basically, if you frequently wander off to collect your chimes regardless
of the state of your lane, your team's going to have a Bard time. Here are three things
to keep in mind before you go wandering off. Whenever you roam as Bard, you instantly give
up a ton of pressure in your lane, so make sure you always have a specific goal in mind
when you leave. Maybe you've spotted a few chimes that'll let you place deep wards in
the enemy jungle, or you want to roam mid to help a laner who's doing poorly, it doesn't
matter - just make sure you leave for a reason, and return as soon as possible. Before you leave to pick up chimes or take
care of other lanes, make sure that your own lane's in good health. Let your teammate know
you're leaving lane for a few, make sure they're in a place they can safely farm, and place
a few health packs down in case of trouble. Even once you leave, keep an eye on your lane
just in case of tower dives so you can hurry back or maybe lay down an ultimate. Bard doesn't bring much damage to a fight,
so optimize your roaming by teaming up with a hard-hitting buddy. The combination of Bard
and a teammate journeying behind the enemy laner will often lead to a devastating combination
of crowd control and murder. Cosmic Binding might not do a huge amount
of damage to a single target, but if you can hit two champions, the double stun is MASSIVE.
Here Bard waits until his enemies are heading straight toward him. This pretty much guarantees
the double stun and singlehandedly turns around the fight for first blood. Though Bard's health packs gain power the
longer they're left alone, you can cast it directly on allies in emergency situations
for a quick heal and movement speed boost. Here, Bard's quick reactionary shrine on Vi
helps her get out with just a sliver of health. Just to prove he's extra awesome, he then
turns to Janna, stuns her against a wall with cosmic binding and uses ignite plus a meeped
up basic attack to finish her off. MONSOON THAT, PUNK. Bard's Magical Journey can get allies into
fights and help secure objectives super quickly. That's great. However, just because you can
take a magical journey doesn't always mean you should. Here, the two squishy members
of the team journey right into Brand's face. After being bombarded, he's forced to ult
himself, flash away, and... well, yeah - it's bad. Look, just make sure you know what you're
actually getting yourself into when you take journeys like this. Remember that Tempered Fate has a huge number
of uses. After being caught attempting to sneak dragon, Bard uses his ult on both himself
and two enemies, freezing them as Pantheon drops in to turn around a losing fight. Though
Grand Skyfall doesn't deal any damage here, because SOMEONE CAN'T TIME THEIR ULT PROPERLY, Tempered Fate still wins the fight. Though Nami places a nice bubble, Bard sets up a
magical journey around it to grab another kill. Finally, another great way to use Tempered
Fate is to proactively set up your allies. Hitting three enemies with his ultimate provides
a “guaranteed” wombo combo. All the same, Bard lines up a perfect double Cosmic Binding
off of Orianna's Shockwave and they pick up two easy kills. Thanks for tuning in to the Bard Champion
Spotlight. Please subscribe to the Riot Games YouTube channel and leave us your comments
just below the video.

titre: Assassins: The Most Frustrating Champions In The Game | League Of Legends
transcription: I don't think I'm going too far out of line when saying if I were to ask every single person who ever played this game to tell me what their most hated class to play against is there would be an overwhelming majority voicing disdain for Assassins as a subcategory of the Slayer class in a staple playstyle in most competitive games assassins are a dominating force in League of Legends to the dismay of many of us when it talked about the cellular class over a year ago the title I gave it was the class of zero counter play implying there's no universally acceptable response to deal with them of course I plan to go over the sister classes skirmishers in a later date but when making that video I was mostly directing that statement towards assassins looking back I don't think I went to detail enough about what makes them such an irritating archetype if we go by the circumstances and leak every class in this game has some element of BS usually emphasized against another class in a sort of matchup chart where tank speed assassins assassin's big marksmen and Marksman V tanks in theory anyway but whether your Champion is well equipped to deal with them or not there's still a possibility that you don't enjoy taking them on for whatever reason so let's investigate more about why assassins are so frustrating while recent additions to the roster have stretched the class's definition assassins bear the same stereotypical characteristics as they do in other games they're fast on their feet and or stealthy unable to sustain a lot of damage in the normal sense and are specialized in dishing out huge amounts of damage in a short period of time usually against a single enemy although in League's case because of how every member goes by dispatching Targets in their own varied ways the only aspect that distinguishes you as an assassin is if the bulk of your damage is front loaded to recap there are three forms of pressure front loaded sustained and back loaded front loader pressure and tail stitching on most if not all of your damage and whatnot right when establishing contact with an enemy one of my favorite examples of this is Echo his standard combo is to set up parallel convergence face dive to surprise his opponent and hopefully stun them then toss that timeline into slow you and then smack you again to proxy Drive resonance afterwards Echo is for lack of a better word useless outside of his ultimate or if his opponent is low enough to activate his missing Health damage on Autos Echo can't do anything for a while although I'd be really surprised if a squishy could survive the brunt of his combo without being in a really bad spot afterwards front load it is generally the most desired out of the three forms of pressure as if it wasn't obvious it happens first just about every assassin is capable of layering their abilities on top of each other in quick succession and by surprise their usage of stealth blinding speed or a combination of both Evelyn's combo functions almost identically to Echo where she primes her main crowd control alert then tossing a hate Spike or Whiplash to detonate it before using the other to put on even more damage if she needs a bit more power to finish the job or to escape then less caress is there to help her out there's also this Chum the waters to reveal slow down his Target and get ready for big damage before using urchin strike and see Stone Trident to gun them down all while keeping playful trickster in his back pocket in the event he needs to escape retaliation the only others who specialize in front loaded damage are divers prioritizing explosive long distance engage in crowd control to start fights the key difference between them is that assassins usually for go crowd control in AOE for more concentrated force and discretion of speed with few defensive options they do possess are directed at avoiding incoming pressure rather than withstanding it such as how divers do not only are assassins able to dish out tons of damage in Rapid succession but the damage is accompanied by an element that inhibits the primary Target's counter-attack measures meaning they're able to avoid a certain type of attack altogether macaulay's Twilight schrad makes Auto attacks or targeted abilities impossible while quote said's death Mark and physics playful trickster render some untargetable letting the Dodge virtually anything during that short window other Champions can go invisible for much of the same results as akali like kha'zix and talent making their pressure all the more threatening as they can unload all of their strength on you but you can't do the same to them it's a high-risk High reward experience that can make you feel Unstoppable if you're able to successfully execute your game plan the fast-paced quick and dirty nature of the class gives them a natural advantage against targets with slower or more methodical Tempo and since no one is faster than assassins that comprises just about everyone else in the game League very much obeys the adage the best defense is a strong offense if you're able to inflict damage so severe to your opponent that they're dead or about to be they don't get to take their turn despite the game's increasingly faster pace of combat you can easily relate League of Legends to a game of chess by addressing your opponent into a dent if you do the end if you don't situation you've all but assured victory for your team assassins can easily dispatch your priority Target thus crippling the enemy team's chances of winning as they likely have lost one of their most important pieces that is the first reason why assassins are so frustrating unless your team coordination is on point and Evelyn kha'zix or Shaco can slip right past your supposed line of defense and catch you by surprise they're then able to monitor to zero you within seconds giving your team almost no time to respond whereas yet they're able to do this even if you have vision of your surroundings either through blinding speed like yone Kiana and echo or stealth like once again Evelyn kha'zix and Shaco completely defeating the purpose of vision in the first place about dilemma plaguing real-time games is the notion of turns in my Juggernaut Studio I talked about how the reason they do so much damage while having the luxury to itemize durability is due to the fact that they're almost always going to go second therefore they require sufficient resilience to withstand that first onslaught of damage and crowd control so they can then take their turn it's a lot more feasible to bounce speed and defense around each other in turn-based games poke Pokemon has their equivalent of juggernauts and assassins as well you have your big brutes with a hefty combination of attack and defense like Agro and viperia and gigolith what they lack in speed they make up for in physical resilience letting them usually tank the opposing Pokemon's first attack in order to strike back since each Pokemon gets only one move if a speedy assassin-like weevile fails to dispatch any of those juggernauts in its initial strike then it has a snowball's chance in hellos surviving that is unless you inflict a status ailment the concept of turns doesn't exist in leak theoretically a player can take multiple turns before their opponent gets to even if no crowd control a debuff is supplied when a Rengar jumps onto you he's allowed total freedom in casting all of his abilities simultaneously this means by the time you realize you're under attack Rengar and by extension most of their assassins have already put you in critical condition by that point you might only have enough time to lash out one or two Auto attacks or maybe an ability or two yourself before you explode it's not just 80 carries and mages either almost everyone in the game takes more time to use their abilities or attacks than assassins do hence why they're called assassins if they took the same out of time to unload their bursts as a Fiora or Lee sin they wouldn't make for very good assassins now would they but let's say you're playing someone who is adequately geared to take them on if we go by the aforementioned rock paper scissors format of Assassin's B Marksman Marksman B tanks and tank speed assassins Common Sense would dictate the easiest way to beat a team with an assassin is by carrying a tank on paper that's emphatically true in practice it's a bit more complicated than that Mobility is increasingly starting to become more and more viable as a defensive option especially in recent years thanks to almost everything being skill shots one thing ride has been very conservative about contrary to their Reckless ambition when it comes to 200 years is giving point-and-click abilities to a champion that's one positive result of the modern landscape of the game as much as we like to trash on new champions for being overloaded at the very least you can dodge most if not all their stuff given you have enough Mobility unfortunately which class is known for being very hard to catch assassins remember instead of tanking enemy counter-attack they prefer to avoid it all together either by sidestepping or making themselves completely immune this is also attributing factor to why assassins and The Slayer class as a whole have grown exponentially in popularity now that we're facing champions of far higher 1v9 capability dodging attacks is way more favorable over face tanking them doesn't matter who you're playing even if you're a super beefy tank getting hit by stuff puts you in a disadvantageous position for yourself and or your team I don't care if you're assigned with 10 000 HP it's always better to avoid getting struck by a Morgana Q as Shadow or chainsaw sometimes it's inevitable I get it but ideally speaking you want to avoid taking as many things as possible besides it feels like nowadays tanks are either impossible to kill or as squishies as Soraka depending on what Champions the enemy team has there's so much tank shredding and DPS in the game that the only truly safest option is to not get hit by stuff in the first place if we're assuming an assassin is facing a tank head-on then of course the tank is gonna win although unless your name is ramus Shannon or galio you can't force players to attack you you could be telling catch trying to beat up the enemy akali who's trying to kill your sivir and no matter how much you try to body block for your ADC akali could just walk right past you the result and kill server anyway you can't tag her with auto attacks because you can't click on her nor can use tongue lash because you don't know where she is mobility and Alternative forms of self-peel like untargetability or stealth are at times more effective since the end result is binary he either got hit by something or didn't there's no one between I'm not saying assassins have an escape option for everything sometimes there's too much AOE damage or crowd control to where no amount of stealth or Mobility will save you but that also applies to every other class in the game it's not like Pantheon stun is the only point and click on Assassins it's point-and-click on everyone the consequences of being engaged on vary based on who his Target is and considering assassins don't possess a whole lot of innate durability that's how you counterplay them right if only you were really that simple a game I used to play and log on to every now and then was Realm of the Mad God in that game you have different classes and those classes determine the type of weapons and armor you can wear since melee classes like Warrior Knight and Paladin have shorter range and are at risk of taking more damage they're given armors with more defense a Tier 15 heavy armor for example provides you with a staggering 28 defense was a Tier 15 light armor only gives 21 defense albeit with some dexterity and a Tier 15 robust 17. if we don't count the supplementary bonuses The Heavy Armor is the most practical choice for survival now imagine if you're a warrior who could equip a long-range weapon like a staff or above you get the best of both worlds right anyone who's picked up a game who knows equipment restrictions are imposed to allow classes their own set of privileges not just for the sake of balance but also to establish a sense of individuality there will be no point in playing a wizard or an Archer if a warrior could equip a staffer bow as well while having the extra defense or vice versa there'd be no point in playing a warrior if you could equip heavy armor as a wizard now where does this lack of restriction show up anyone I'll give you a hint it starts with an l and this where many people's dreams die correct League of Legends items in this game barring very very select exceptions are universally available if you possess enough gold you can purchase any item you want you can buy Trinity force on Soraka moonstone renewer on Gangplank Kraken Slayer on cho'goth ravenous cytron Sentra but the world is your oyster assassins are only assassins by playstyle and not actual in-game classification as a result there's no rule bar enough from accessing items or runes that are clearly at risk of being abused by them unlike an MMOs or other RPG games whereby you come across items that can only be equipped by a designated character or class this is somewhat kept in check by Champion's value in certain steps a Sentra could by ravenous Hydra but she scales off ability power so buying an AP item will be more efficient the differences in stats from one class to another has more to do with the items they purchased and not the champion itself they might lean slightly towards a specific stat but otherwise there's not that much variance tanks like skarner or cholga have more base attack damage at level 18 than TPS chance like Tristana or Draven I'm being serious by the way the form and two hover in the 140 range while the latitude are in the low 120s Tristana and Draven deal more damage through the items they bought but they could potentially be just as resilient as tanks if they decide to go full tank items play a way bigger part than what class you are most of the time assassins are the largest beneficiaries in this design structure as they don't explicitly have a necessary stat to itemize for if you're playing a tank you're pretty much obligated to chase after any combination of Health armor and match exist even if your abilities don't scale off of any of them directly if you're playing a marksman you're forced to build attack damage attack speed and crit chance if you're playing a Bruiser your first item number 2 can be damaged and then after that you should work towards going tanker at the very least tanky Bruiser enchanters have to buy items that boost his supportive capabilities assassins have none of these restrictions only an optional Choice physical damage dealers like Rengar kha'zix and Zed have an assortment of items that provide lethality formerly known as armor penetration thing is unless your name is Pike assassins don't directly scale off lethality all they thought he does is allow your existing damage to be less mitigated by armor granted every Champion carries to them based armor so it's not that litadi is never useful but if you deal enough damage to one shot your opponent and then an extra 50 left over you're effectively only benefiting from the attack damage not so much lidati why this matters is because there's nothing stopping an assassin from buying non-assassing items and they frequently do last year there was a time period period where every physical damage dealer in the game was buying Gore Drinker that included the likes of Talon Kiana Rengar Nocturne and kha'zix Beyond Mythic items it isn't rare for you to see a Bruiser oriented build like Black Cleaver death stance kampung chain sword even sterax this applies to AP Champions as well Katarina and akali Dabble with Divine Sunder every now and then I'm sure at one point you encountered the notorious Sunfire Diana Bruiser shadow assassin Kane or tank cassadin it seems like there's always an assassin who's building items that Riot probably wasn't intending for them to build or not to that degree anyway often I find items that are crucial for the success of certain Champions be nerfed because assassins are abusing them and one shotting you while being indestructible at the same time it's expected for concessions to be made in order to offset the class's inherent weaknesses a short range slow character should naturally have high durability and efficiency from building items like steric Skech the problem is that very same item can be purchased by flushed down character at no restriction with the majority of Assassins being melee they get access to the full range of benefits an item has to offer a solid range Champion so usually have weaker numbers like how divides under does three percent maximum bonus damage on Range versus six percent on melee case in point there are a lot of circumstantial elements that work in favor of Assassins explaining why they were terrorized in The Meta for years and it's only thanks to the recent changes to durability scaling that they're finally losing their Edge but what do you guys think do you believe there's a more frustrating class to play against than assassins or do you agree with my points feel free to share your thoughts in the comments down below that's it for today though so if you enjoyed the video please be sure to leave a like And subscribe consider following me on Twitter at varserver joining my Discord server and if you'd like to support me further check out my patreon by donating you get access to benefits for all three of my channels vars vars 2 and vars 3. but till next time thanks so much for watching and I look forward to seeing you again soon take care foreign [Music] [Music]
