But that initial laning phase can be very punishing if you don't know the exact extent of your limits. Kiana I'd highly recommend to people looking to make big plays and montage moments as long as you're willing to get over the hurdles that you may face initially. Akali is the most popular assassin amongst higher elo and professional players largely in part due to her stable early game. Most assassins are somewhat reliant on random skirmishes and the general chaos of solo queue in order to not fall behind. But Akali functions reasonably well even in a slower game. There are trade-offs for this safer laning phase, though, as Akali really lacks wave clear, and if you fall behind, you'll find it pretty hard to kill minions, let alone champions. Most assassins on this list tend to do very well into squishy and ranged champions. Akali is not only good into these, but also does really well into just about every other melee or assassin champion that you'd see in midlane. So, in other words, she's a great champion to have as part of a pool, but probably isn't as rewarding a one-trick as some of the other champions on this list. If you're looking to pursue a more fundamentalbased assassin, particularly if you're higher elo already, then Akali could be well worth your time. Katarina is the original montage champion of League of Legends, topping the charts for kills and pentacills for all champions. Katarina is the most mechanically demanding assassin and extremely punishing when it comes down to choosing your moments to engage. Learning this champion is not for the faint of heart and there are virtually no Katarina players outside of one tricks because the time investment required to become a good Katarina player is pretty significant. Katarina is reliant on a somewhat cheesy play style in that she must take risks and make some coin for plays in order to make up for her fairly weak 1v one laning phase. A great Katarina player can find ways to mitigate her weaknesses and maximize her skirmishing strengths in order to weight those coins in your favor and completely dominate games. If you want the most extreme embodiment of the assassin play style, then look no further than Katarina. Our final assassin is Leblanc. Leblanc is a very difficult champion to learn with not only an insanely high skill camp, but also a very high skill floor as well. She is amongst the most rewarding assassins to learn on this list, however, with very high outplay and carry potential. Similar to Akali, Leblanc could be considered a more fundamental assassin. And this is part of what makes her so difficult. You will not only need to have a fairly solid grasp of how to play mid lane in general, but also invest the time into learning Leblanc's many combos or matchups. Oftentimes, this can be very painful as Leblanc is completely useless from behind. Once mastered, though, you have the tools to carry almost every game. Watching a great Leblanc player is extremely impressive, and playing against one feels equally oppressive. Leblanc is one of my favorite champions to play and absolutely worth learning. Just don't expect it to be easy. Burst mages play somewhat similarly to assassins, but are typically ranged instead of melee and in compensation for this lack a little bit on the mobility side. They're still incredibly powerful in skirmishes and are often more so in the early laning phase compared to a true assassin. But they have to position much more carefully less they are caught out of position. Burst mages tend to also be much better in 5v5 fights than most traditional assassins, giving them one of the most straightforward win conditions of any class just by grouping up on objectives and winning key fights. This is a major reason why they find so much success in every elo. Vex is another champion I very often recommend to new players with a simple yet effective kit focused on blowing up key targets. She's particularly good in lower elos where you're unlikely to face as many range mage matchups or get punished for her fairly average laning phase. One of the biggest bonuses of easier champions like Vex is it's very easy to remain consistent from not only game to game but also just fight to fight which is one of the biggest factors for climbing. If you want to get inventive ability haste focused builds are also viable and this can push her towards more of a control mage style. Overall, Vex is an easy answer to melee champions if you struggle with those, and it's a solid choice to learn the mid lane fundamentals with. Annie has always had a reputation of being one of the easiest champions in the game, and for good reason. Her combos are easy to pull off, the game plan is pretty clear-cut, and she even has a built-in mechanic to help with farming. In spite of that, though, she has a fairly high skill ceiling centered mainly around bare micro and E usage. Additionally, Annie is actually really versatile despite having this simple kit. She can run a variety of runes and builds which allow her to deal with tons of different matchups and compositions. And the various roles that she can cover throughout the game make her one of the best champions for climbing solo queue, especially for beginner players. Aurora is about as close as you can get to a perfect champion for solo Q. She has mobility, huge team fight and skirmishing power, a strong laning phase, flexibility in draft and builds, strong side laning, and the list goes on. In essence, as long as Aurora's numbers aren't nerfed in the ground, she will always remain a very strong pick for solo cube. There can be quite a bit of difficulty to Aurora at first. The QE combo is rather slow and using your W and R bounces to their full potential isn't easy, but don't be deterred. If there is a single champion every midlaner should know how to play in today's League of Legends, it's Aurora. Zoe is one of the hardest champions in the entire game and has historically been a terror in both competitive and solo Q alike. She can be one of the most frustrating champions to play against, being able to inflict massive amounts of damage from extremely far away with little to no warning or counterplay. Her strong laning phase and incredible skirmishing make her an excellent pick for even the most chaotic of solo Q games. She can start to fall off a little later in the game, especially into tankier compositions. However, even so, Zoe is a fantastic one trick and may even be the most popular midlane one trick in GM and challenger elos largely for her ability to just annihilate solo Q. Learning Zoe can be a bit of an exercise and frustration at first. The options for every given situation can be pretty overwhelming and alting forward at the wrong time can easily get you killed. Once it clicks for you though, this champion is exceptionally powerful and at least for me, one of the most fun champions in the entire game. Control mages are the most common archetype of mid laner in both competitive play and in higher elo solo queue for very good reason. Control mages bring a lot to any composition, normally featuring great wave clearance team fight damage. In addition, they often feature high amounts of CC or very powerful laning phases. This makes them among some of the most well-rounded champions in the game and ideal for players who want to play the game on a more strategic level or if you want to play a more controlled and standard game instead of team deathmatch. Mahar is your most basic control mage, being one of the easiest champions to climb with up until you reach some of the higher elos. His super basic kit is especially good as a pick for newer players looking to take off some of the mechanical load of the game while they learn how everything else works. While Malahar can be basic, it's still pretty satisfying watching your dots and pets deal ridiculous amounts of damage in a team fight. And I always get a special tingling feeling making people miserable with his point-and-click ultimate. Overall, Malahar can be surprisingly fun and is a really good entry point to control mages. Another relatively easy to pull off mage is Vaggar. He's heavily focused on scaling and is particularly difficult for shorter range compositions to deal with. Vaggar is one of League's oldest champions and so may feel a little clunky by today's standards compared to some of the more fluid or updated mages. He also does tend to lack power in the early game, which has become increasingly important in modern day League of Legends. Still though, he is fairly fun to play, and if you can consistently make it into the mid game, you should see some success. Anivvia is the literal definition of a control mage with massive amounts of wave clear and zone control, which make her an absolute nightmare for melee heavy compositions to deal with. Ania is definitely on the mechanically easy side, but getting the most out of your wall and other CC abilities requires a little bit of foresight and game knowledge. As such, you might find Anivvia easy to pick up and play at first. But reaching high ranks with her is a whole different story. I'd really recommend Anivvia to people who want to one trick a control mage on the simpler side, but with enough skill expression to remain interesting. When people ask me for a champion to crush the laning phase with, I always say Victor. Amongst control mages, Victor's laning phase is second to none and has winning matchups into the vast majority of the midlane roster. He scales into a late game monster, too, being one of the few control mages that has the potential to delete an entire team in the late game. There are some drawbacks, though. Despite being strong in the 1v one, his early skirmishing in 2v2s or 3v3s is particularly lacking, and the lack of mobility and self- peel until he gets his upgrades can make him unforgiving to play. As such, Victor is a decent blind pick, but he's an exceptional counterpick champion, especially if you're struggling to deal with other mages. Over the last couple of years, Tia has become one of the most popular mid laners being played in higher MMRs. Her kit is extremely wellrounded, and she has the tools to navigate almost any situation. High wave clear, compounded by a global ultimate, provides her the ability to heavily impact the map, even in more difficult matchups. Her CC synergizes extremely well with any jungler that provides CC of their own and the combination of low cooldowns and high damage make her extremely strong scaler as well. While the variety of options in any given scenario can be a bit daunting, you can get a lot of power out of simply from her Q and E, which are relatively straightforward abilities. So, I'd encourage you guys not to be deterred by her high skill cap and give a try to one of the most fluid control mages out there. Boys are becoming increasingly meta and Riot really rose to the challenge with Hi. Quay is one of the most different champions Riot has ever released. Featuring a total of 10 different abilities. In spite of this, his difficulty is vastly overrated. Most of it coming from getting used to pressing two keys to cast an ability instead of one. His versatility allows him to be played in multiple position, namely mid, bot, and support. His high wave clear and self- peel tools can make him a fairly strong blind pick as well. Being one of the more recently released control mages, Wave's abilities are visually pretty spectacular, especially when compared to some of the older mages that have more bland spell effects, way would be an ideal choice if you're bored of normal champions and are looking for a truly unique way to experience League of Legends. Syndra is one of the best mages you could invest time into. Simultaneously having a very high skill cap from her combos and skill shots, but also being relatively forgiving due to her incredible scaling safety net. Synra is strong at virtually all stages of the game. Her high damage and low cooldowns make her a strong laner and easy access to CC and burst let her take over any early skirmish. In team fights, you can opt into a CC heavy build. Focus on making life as miserable as possible for the enemy front liners. Or you can build as a full glass cannon and one-shot any squishy that dares to enter your range. I'd highly recommend anyone looking to climb to higher eloers on control mages to consider having Synindra as part of your pool. If I had to choose one mage that every mid laner should know how to play, it would be Oriana. Oriana has been the queen of mid lane for many, many years. And if you're ever not sure what to pick in a game, you should probably just pick Oriana. She's good or even great at just about everything and fits into a variety of compositions. Large rune and build variety enables her to fulfill a ton of different roles, easily being able to go full damage and burst to one-shot squishies. Sustained damage builds make her into a strong sideler and capable of winning most 1v ones. And finally, haste builds make her a solid facilitator to strong bruises or hyper carries. If you're looking for a single mage on this list to one trick, I'd highly recommend you go for Oriana. Azir is amongst the hardest champions in the game as a champion that rests heavily on not only mechanical ability, but requiring immense game knowledge and fundamentals as well. It's very possible to put hundreds and hundreds of games into Azir and still not see the results you would see on other champions simply because he really requires you to be a very good mid laner as well as a very good Azir player. As such, I wouldn't really recommend him to players below master tier. If you aren't deterred by this, however, once mastered, his kit is massively overloaded, complete with mobility, engage, peel, DPS, push, basically everything you would ever need as a mid laner. You can be punished by certain matchups in the early lane, but first item onwards, you tend to be stronger than just about any other mid laner, and it only gets more and more out of control. Azir also has some of the most montage potential of any control mage, and many pro player highlight reels are bound to include several crazy Shereima shuffles or other outplays. If you can stomach a brutal learning curve, then Azir will return your investment many times over. Our final control mage is my favorite champion of all time. Casupia. Casupia is unquestionably the most mechanically demanding control mage. So, if you want a mage that really rewards mechanics and isn't so heavy on the brain requirement, she could be the one for you. Cass has incredible damage output with extremely low cooldowns and the ability to easily 1v2 or even 1v3 if she gets fed. She is very unforgiving though. Missing your poison will render you almost useless. And being so short range makes you an easy kill if you even slightly misposition. She can actually be a pretty good one trick though. She's very viable in not just mid lane, but top lane and bot lane as well. And there is even a QAX Casio build which can deal with some of the longer range mid lane counterpicks. If you watch the video to this point, you owe it to me to at least try one Casio game. And who knows, maybe you'll fall in love with the champion the way I did. Battle mages are pretty similar to control mages, but tend to lack crowd control in exchange for being a lot tankier and willing to dive deep into team fights. These sort of champions can be good for players who want to play more of a major spellcaster style, but maybe you're uncomfortable with the squishiness and positioning requirements that control me mages demand. Swain is an absolute elo hack of a champion that I recommend to players all the time. His game plan is very simple. Group up and 5v5. His kit is built around skirmishing and taking major objective fights. His innate tankiness means that you can be forgiven for a lot of the positioning errors lower elo players tend to make. And he's an absolute demon from ahead, pun intended, quickly becoming a 1v5 machine. If your current champion pool struggles with assassins or engaged champions, Swain can be an easy answer to plug the hole without needing to invest a ton of time into learning him. You could probably consider Aurelion Soul a control mage, but I decided to put him here for his very unique play style. Asol is one of league's premier scaling champions, boasting the highest win rate in the late game of any mid laner. You'd think that he sacrifices his early game for this, but good Aelian soul players can abuse his strong level one and ability to chase opponents down the lane with his W. Once you pick up Ries, his skirmishing becomes topnotch and will allow you to scale up even quicker. In higher elos, some relian souls has started to play him bot lane in order to avoid some of the more difficult mage matchups. So, if you decide to one trick him, you can easily play him in both roles. I think this champion is super underrated in general and if left unchecked is an absolute monster. Our final battle mage is Vladimir. Vlad is a very weird champion, utilizing health as a resource cost and having large amounts of sustain to go with it. It can be pretty difficult to start the snowball on Vlad, and you have to be very picky about the fights you choose to take in the early game. Laning against Vlad, though, is very different from laning against most other champions. So, in lower ELO, especially, you might be able to find yourself some easy wins, as people just don't know what you can do. This can become a lot harder as you rank up, however, as people get more familiar with your kit and how it plays. Most of Vlad's difficulty comes from navigating this weaker early game and taking advantage of the windows presented so that you can scale quickly into the team fights. His team fighting is very strong, but it can be a little complicated with a lot of reliance on flanks and timing to really pop off. Vlad is another champion I'd say is underrated, but more so as a counterpick into certain compositions or matchups that lack the ability to shut them down early. Facilitators are primarily built around pushing and making plays together with your support and jungle. They tend to have very high impact in the early game, but can sometimes fall short in later team fights if you haven't built enough of a lead, either for you individually or for your teammates. Most of the facilitating champions will only be able to neutralize or build slight leads in their 1v one. So instead, you want to focus on getting involved in as many skirmishes and river fights as possible. Gallio is the easiest facilitator to pick up and can also be a great option if you're struggling against either assassin midlaners or dive compositions. He is one of the best skirmishing mids in the entire game with solid base damages and an excellent CC chain, especially when you're paired with fighter junglers. A variety of builds means that Gallery can fill the role of anything from a tank to a full AP assassin, ensuring that no matter what champions you are paired with or against, you'll be able to have at least some impact. He can struggle against some of the stronger laning champions, and if you don't accelerate the early game, you can find yourself getting outscaled by most mages. A criminally underrated champion for solo Q is Landre, who despite being in our facilitator class, actually has a lot of individual power and carry potential. Landre is best used as a counterpick, either picked alongside engaged junglers and supports or into teams heavy on melee carries and assassins. Her extreme amount of CC makes her a nightmare to deal with, and she can combine this with either burst items to eliminate a single target straight away, or with tankier items to buy time for more CC rotations. I'd recommend Landre primarily to mage players who just want something to be able to deal with the champs and situations that often make their lives miserable. Ari has been the most valuable pick to learn in solo cube for many, many years now. She's incredibly well-rounded. Her sustain and wave clear allowing her to neutralize bad matchups. Her W movement speed and mobility with her alt bring even further forgiveness to her kit, making her particularly adept at dealing with solo Q's chaotic nature. If you're ever not sure what your champion pool needs or you just want a champion to cover as many situations as possible, you really can't go wrong with Ari. Despite being a jack of all trades, she's notably more powerful when you pair her with AD ganking junglers like Vires and Zo or alongside engaged supports like Reer Leona. However, this is absolutely not a requirement. She's a very solid blind pick, and these pairings just serve as a nice bonus. If you're looking for a champion that really rewards macro play, but is a bit lighter on mechanics, then you'd undoubtedly enjoy Twisted Fate. TF's abilities can be a little unintuitive at first, but are actually rather simple, and you'll quickly find yourself able to pilot the champion to a solid level. That being said, the difference between a good TF and a great TF is very noticeable with a large emphasis on knowing how to pressure the map and side lanes throughout the entirety of the game with your global ultimate. Whenever this champion is meta, he becomes an absolute solo Q terror and will enable you to climb the ranks with ease once mastered. Rise has a complex identity being a mishmash of facilitator, sidelaner, and battle mage. I put him in this category, though, because the majority of your early game will be focused on leveraging your push over mid into 2v2s or 3v3s with your support and jungler. Damage-wise, Rise scales pretty well into the late game, too. But his short range means that against longer range compositions, you can really struggle to actually deal your damage. As such, you're looking to snowball yourself and other lanes as Rise and then transition into a strong side lane threat to avoid team fighting on an even footing. Instead, you can look to create yourself a massive golden XP lead with your crazy farming capabilities, and you can easily secure numbers advantages for your teams through your push and then ultimate. Rise is definitely not an easy champion to play, but if you want to be able to pressure the map early without sacrificing your 1v one laning phase, then Rise could be the one for you. Nico isn't played much these days, but she's honestly super underrated. Strong laning phase and CC makes it easy to begin snowballing, and her powerful team fighting allows her to regularly seal the deal. The thing that keeps most people away from Nico is her very unique play style. A combination of control mage plus engager is weird enough, but the disguise mechanic complicates her gameplay even further. If you decide to invest time into Nico, you can also look into playing her in other roles. She functions very well in both the jungle and support positions, making her a valuable flex pick in the draft. Similar to Rise, Nico is a great facilitator to pick up if you want to be able to heavily impact early objectives or river flies without necessarily exchanging laning strength for it. Artillery mages place a heavy focus on winning fights before they even begin. These champions have immense range and high amounts of damage, and so when they do manage to secure a lead, they can become some of the most frustrating champions to deal with. If you fall behind though, it can feel like tickling a brick wall. Made even worse if the enemy team has high amounts of mobility, life steal, or engage tools. The most forgiving, and in my opinion, the most powerful artillery mage is Lux. I absolutely hate playing against Lux with a passion. Her high range and harass tools make laning against a good Lux very annoying. She can be difficult to punish, too. If she holds Q and W defensively, it can be really hard to aggress onto her. and her ulti offers a get out of jail free card every minute by instantly oneshotting a wave and resetting. Unlike most artillery mages, Lux features high burst as well, meaning that your poke before a team fight is often just catching someone out and one-shotting them. She does have some pretty major weaknesses though, namely sidelaning and immobility. But I'd say these aren't really noticeable outside of very high elos. And I think she's one of the best champions to pick up if you just want to climb quickly. Mel is Riot's newest artillery mage with all the bells and whistles you might expect from a new champion's kit. Her W allows her to reflect abilities, and so as a counterpick into certain champions or compositions can be very, very rewarding. In spite of this, Mel's abilities are very slow and easy to dodge, which can make her somewhat inconsistent from fight to fight. You can overcome this with skill or patience, however, holding your cooldowns to their opportune moments or following up other people's crowd control with your own. If you want to one-trick Mel, I'd actually recommend playing her bot lane as the matchups tend to be a little more forgiving and a second champion can help guarantee your combo. On the other end of the spectrum, we have Ziggs. Released all the way back in 2012, Ziggs is the artillery mage with the greatest snowball potential. His ability to blow up turrets accelerates the game and can skyrocket his gold lead. Low spammable coolowns means a Ziggs that gets ahead will leave little room for the enemy team to crawl their way back into the game. While he does lack CC, he possesses some mobility, which is normally a major weak point of longer range mages. Similar to Mel, Ziggs has easier matchups in the bot lane, and this is his most common role in high elo. He's still a very viable mid laner, though, especially in games where you know you can get some sort of lead and begin your snowballing early. Zerath is the artillery mage with the highest skill ceiling. He's even more skill shot reliant than other artillery mages and is by far the easiest to kill. Zerath also has the least actual skirmishing strength, so in early game and team fights, you will need to land poke on key targets, otherwise you won't be able to win the fight. There are some major upsides, though. A semi-global ultimate, allows him to easily snipe kills or land poke across the map. And his extreme range, even by artillery mage standards, means that even in the late game, you will still be able to ignore tanks and focus on more important targets. Zerith is a difficult champion to learn and can be quite unforgiving, but he's one of the most fun mages out there. So, if the Poke Mage play style really resonates with you, you need to try Zerah. Fighters are the bruisers of the mid lane, boasting strong 1v one laning phases and big sidelane win conditions. These champions tend to be a little weaker when it comes to bigger fights, and handshaking 5v5s without being overly fed will lead to a quick defeat. Most of your game will be about leveraging that 1v one strength in order to accelerate yourself and make the enemy mid laner's life as difficult as possible. One of the highest riskreward champions in the mid lane is Silus. From ahead, this champion is disgustingly strong, easily able to 1v2 or even 1v3 with his massive damage output whilst also being incredibly tanky. However, if he fails to get a lead or if he lacks powerful ultimates to steal, Silus can feel very underwhelming. He's best used as either a counterpick when game-changing ultimates are shown, such as Malight or Alistar, or into matchups that you know you can easily snowball. If you like high-risisk play styles but don't want to play something as squishy as an assassin or mage, then Silus offers a play style that's a perfect fit for snowballing solo games. If Silus is high risk, Yon is more like manage risk. This champion is insanely greedy and requires you to follow a very specific game plan, but his scaling is out of this world and can easily 1v9 games if left unpunished. For Yon, much of the early game needs to be spent building up a 1v one lead and choosing fights carefully. As before you hit two items, you're generally too squishy to be a part of any major fight. Instead, you'll have to use your 1 v one strength to prevent the enemy mid from joining fights. From one item onwards, a big focus needs to be on gathering XP and gold from the side lane. Once you do hit two items, though, it's time to start making montages, and it only gets crazier as the game goes on. Yona is a great champion to play if you like a high APM mechanically intensive play style that isn't quite as punishing as something like Aurelia or Yasua. Jace is a hybrid champion able to switch forms between melee and range. He combines the play style of a fighter with that of a poke mage and is one of the strongest champions in the game when truly mastered. These days, Jace is a bit more at home in the top lane as many of the mage matchups mid can be pretty difficult, but you can definitely still make him work. You're likely to enjoy Jace if you want to play the poke or artillery mage style, but still want to retain the ability to skirmish or secure kills when your opponents misposition or stay around for a bit too long and low HP. Jace isn't a champion I'd recommend if your goal is just to climb in the short term, but as a long-term main, I think he's one of the best. Yaso is a top three mid when it comes to difficulty. He's extremely mechanically demanding at every stage of the game, and there is realistically little to no room for error on the macro side either. Yasawo has the fighter strengths and weaknesses taken to the extreme, being an insane champion in the 1v one as well as the side lane, but sadly lacking in the 5v5 unless he's paired with specific combos like Diana or Re in order to provide him with some setup. If you're going to play Yasu, it pretty much has to be as a onetrick. He is just too difficult for most players to have as part of a pool. If you're still not deterred by now though, Yasu has almost infinite outplay potential. And the plays you can make on this champion make him one of the most enjoyable champions to watch at a high level. Our lucky last fighter is a Rally, who in my opinion is the most difficult mid laner in the entire game. This champion is insanely fun to play as you can regularly create massive CS leads or pick up solo kills. And once you hit Bladed Rune King, you're basically Thanos. You do still have to be careful of taking major fights, especially if your flash is down, as until you get tank your items, you can easily be CCd and one shot. Aurelia scales surprisingly well for a champion with such a good early game simply because she has so much access to extra gold from farming waves, creeps, and turrets super efficiently. She probably has the most 1v9 potential of any mid laner, compensated for by being immensely punishing if you do fall behind. Overall, she's a super sick champ. Insanely flashy with a nearly limitless skill ceiling and could easily provide you with a challenge for the rest of your league career. Mid lane AD carries are pretty uncommon these days, but I figured I should mention them just in case. ADC mids tend to be greedier picks and are often very difficult to pull off. ADCs naturally are pretty difficult champions to play, requiring precise positioning and to stay very high on farm at all times as you won't be bringing anything to the table if you fall behind. As such, it's best if you can pick ADC mid into easy or free matchups, or alternatively alongside an AP jungler to avoid the enemy team stacking too much magic resistance. Corki is going to be your most consistent midlane ADC. He does have some bad matchups, but overall, he's a pretty strong laner, a fairly good skirmisher, and has decent scaling. Corki is easy mechanically, but his difficulty comes from needing to accelerate and farm 10 CS per minute. Otherwise, you'll probably be outperformed in fights and skirmishes by mages. It's very important to know how to cheese people level one with E and look for key windows to be at a W4. His W and Triforce HP make him a little more forgiving than most AD carries in terms of positioning, but without the mega scaling that other AD carries have, he can really fall flat later in the game, particularly into tankier compositions. I'd recommend Corki mid mainly to people just struggling to find an AD mid for their pool. If you're looking for counter picks, Tristana is a really good ADC mid with a very strong early game and great scaling as well. The downside is that similar to Corki, Trist needs to accelerate. So, it's important to pick her into matchups that you can either spam push in or against champions that can't stop you from easily jumping forward. I'd recommend trying to stay away from picking into strong laning champions or mages with CC. Tristana is also pretty hard to play. It'll require a lot of APM to effectively deal damage with all her attack speed, and it's very easy to W in at the wrong time and die or W out too early and lose all your DPS. Make sure this is a champion you've practiced a lot of or are just picking in situations where you can easily snowball from. Another greaty ADC is Smolder. While he's fairly simple on the mechanic side, his scaling being highly dependent on farm and stacking makes him fairly difficult to pull off. Ideally, you want to pick him in matchups where you won't be pressured early, such as Gallo or Malahar, as this will ensure you reach your power spikes before the game is decided. If you do manage to get through the early game, the smaller champion becomes insanely overpowered with some of the strongest team fight power of any mid laner. Look towards Smolder if you want to consistently outscale your opponents and play for a late game win condition or if you just want something to punish greedy blind picks. Kale technically isn't an AD carry, but I didn't really know where else she should fit on this list. Kale's early game is very similar to Cassadins with a lot of focus on trying to neutralize the game for both you and your team. She's a great team fighter for two major reasons. Her massive amount of DPS makes her ideal into shorter range compositions, especially those that lack CC. Secondly, her ultimate is a very powerful tool for surviving engage or permitting your team to dive deep into the enemy backline. I think Kale works best as part of a pool where you can pick her alongside bruiser junglers like Hecrim or Zenzou or with AD carries with high damage output such as Jinx, Kaisa, or Aphilios. You can also use her as a counter to any mid laner who can't pressure the lane hard or is very reliant on bursting a single target. Axon is another weird champion that is partway between being an AD carry and an assassin, making him a great pick for people who enjoy playing marksman, but want to have a greater impact on the early game itself. Axon is almost exclusively played by one tricks, relying heavily on snowball in the early game. As from an even or losing game state, Axon will be next to useless. If you have a strong grasp of his limits and can master a somewhat unique and cheesy play style, you'll find Axan to be an excellent solo Q pick and one that can slingshot you through the ranks faster than almost any other. That's going to be it for this video. If you did like the content, don't forget to subscribe and let me know if you'd like to see more of this style of content in the future. As always, if you have any questions, please put them in the comment section below or ask me live on either YouTube or Twitch. Thank you guys so much for watching and I'll see you all in the next

titre: Thresh: Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription: Bienvenue dans le Focus sur Thresh, le Garde aux chaînes. "Nous les plongerons tous dans l'agonie." Thresh est un champion de soutien à courte distance et résistant, capable de changer sa position ou celle de ses ennemis et de ses alliés. Thresh peut attirer à lui un allié pour ganker, pour le sauver ou foncer au cœur du combat. Sa compétence passive est Damnation. Contrairement aux autres champions, Thresh ne gagne pas d'armure par niveau. Cependant, quand les ennemis meurent, ils lâchent une âme que Thresh peut collecter en passant dessus. En collectant des âmes, Thresh augmente définitivement son armure et sa puissance. Peine capitale a une propriété passive et une active. Passivement, Peine capitale permet aux attaques de Thresh d'infliger des dégâts bonus en fonction des âmes collectées, de ses dégâts d'attaque totaux et du délai depuis sa précédente attaque. En l'activant, Thresh projette sa faux, blessant et attirant vers lui le premier ennemi touché. Après avoir touché un ennemi, Thresh peut réactiver Peine capitale pour bondir vers la cible. Je conseille de mettre un point dans Peine capitale au niveau 1 et de la maximiser au niveau 13. Lien des ténèbres lance la lanterne de Thresh. Si un allié proche clique dessus, Thresh attire cet allié et la lanterne vers lui. Les champions alliés proches de la lanterne gagnent aussi un bouclier bloquant des dégâts pendant quelques secondes. Je conseille de prendre Lien des ténèbres au niveau 2 et de le maximiser immédiatement. Fauchage blesse et ralentit tous les ennemis sur une ligne, devant et derrière Thresh. Tous les ennemis touchés sont aussi envoyés dans la direction de la compétence : Thresh peut ainsi les repousser ou les attirer vers lui. Je conseille de vite mettre un point dans Fauchage, mais de le maximiser en dernier. La boîte est l'ultime de Thresh. Elle crée une prison de murs spectraux autour de lui. En traversant un mur, les champions ennemis sont blessés et grandement ralentis un court instant, mais ce mur est brisé. Voyons le laning de Thresh. Faites toujours votre maximum pour collecter les âmes des sbires ennemis sur votre voie. Certains joueurs peuvent toutefois avoir des difficultés. Taric et Tristana sont redoutables quand ils sont agressifs. Ne collectez jamais une âme si vous devez subir trop de dégâts pour ça. Contre Thresh, punissez-le toujours dès qu'il avance trop pour collecter une âme. Lorsque les sbires avancent, Thresh peut facilement prendre leurs âmes, mais celles-ci finissent par disparaître. Plusieurs compétences de Thresh peuvent sauver des alliés ou le sauver. Ici, Trundle vient ganker, alors Thresh lance Fauchage vers Trundle. Cela le repousse, ce qui permet à Thresh et Ashe de s'enfuir. Thresh est aussi capable de favoriser les kills sur sa voie. Bien qu'il ait raté Peine capitale, Thresh va au combat. Il blesse Taric puis lance Fauchage vers l'arrière, ce qui attire Taric et aide Graves à le tuer. Fauchage et Peine capitale se combinent bien. Thresh va vers Soraka et l'attire avec Fauchage. Le ralentissement facilite l'utilisation de Peine capitale, ce qui permet à Nocturne de la tuer. Nocturne étant affaibli, La boîte est activée pour empêcher Varus de poursuivre l'allié de Thresh. La combo complète de Thresh peut verrouiller une cible unique jusqu'à sa mort. Alors que Varus court, Thresh enchaîne Saut éclair et Peine capitale. Thresh bondit vers Varus, l'attire à lui avec Fauchage et invoque La boîte, ce qui le ralentit et le blesse encore immédiatement. Malgré la Purge et le Saut éclair de Varus, l'équipe de Thresh réussit à le tuer. Thresh peut utiliser Lien des ténèbres de différentes façons. En retournant sur une voie, si vous êtes devant un allié, lancez la lanterne derrière vous pour l'aider. Si un allié affronte un monstre, comme le dragon, sans soutien, lancer la lanterne par-dessus le mur peut lui permettre de se mettre à l'abri une fois le monstre mort. En combat d'équipes, vous pouvez rester derrière un mur et la lancer au milieu de votre équipe. Si quelqu'un se sent en danger, il peut bondir vers vous et fuir. Si quelqu'un se fait attraper alors qu'il fuit, comme Caitlyn, lancez la lanterne vers votre allié pour le ramener vers son équipe. Quand un allié vient ganker, dites-lui de rester hors de vue des ennemis. Lancez la lanterne derrière vous et laissez l'allié engager le combat après l'avoir attiré. Ici, cela permet de tuer rapidement Tristana. Plus complexe encore, vous pouvez la lancer derrière vous tout de suite après Peine capitale. Cela aidera votre allié à tomber directement sur la cible. Fauchage attire Taric et La boîte l'empêche de s'enfuir. Joué à la perfection, Thresh peut sauver ses alliés de situations apparemment sans issue. Lien des ténèbres met Graves en sûreté, loin de Taric et Ezreal. Ensuite, quand Evelynn arrive, Fauchage la repousse. La boîte capture les trois assaillants, ce qui sauve tout le monde sans avoir à utiliser de sort d'invocateur. En combat, Thresh peut être redoutable s'il coopère avec ses alliés. En combinant Lien des ténèbres, Saut éclair et Fauchage, il peut envoyer un Rammus en Démolisseur au milieu de l'équipe ennemie. Thresh lance Lien des ténèbres et va en avant. Dès que Rammus touche la lanterne, Thresh utilise Saut éclair, attire l'équipe ennemie avec Fauchage et profite du spectacle tandis que Trundle, Nami et Ryze se font rapidement éliminer. Pour le build de Thresh, je conseille de mélanger durabilité, réduction des délais et effets de soutien. Essayez des marques et des sceaux d'armure, des glyphes de résistance magique et des quintessences de PO. Pour les maîtrises : 1/13/16, avec de l'armure, des PV et la réduction des dégâts en Défense, plus de la régénération de mana, des PO et des consommables en Utilitaire. En sorts d'invocateur, faites votre choix parmi Fatigue, Soins et Saut éclair. Vous pourrez ainsi bloquer les ennemis clés et garder vos alliés en vie, ou vous repositionner pour profiter des forces de Thresh. Merci d'avoir suivi ce Focus sur Thresh. Abonnez-vous à la chaîne YouTube de Riot Games ci-dessus et laissez vos commentaires sous la vidéo !
